File.Name: h715b_h715-013.jpg
Notes: 35816031221 This female Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly has been brought down by a tiny Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). See my [Next] photo. The predator is visible on the left where the butterfly's head should be, but I don't know why it's facing away from the butterfly. These Ambush Bugs really are mighty predators that can bring down prey much larger than they are! (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2017)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Pacific Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat

File.Name: h715c_h713-044.jpg
Notes: 35906736716 I found this Pacific Azure aka Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) butterfly working summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). These butterflies were formerly known as the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon), but that name is now restricted to butterflies from the Appalachian and Ozark ranges. I believe this one is male with deep blue upper wings - but they are mostly hidden. This is one of our earliest butterflies, and summer seems an odd time to see one. I've found them as early as January in past years. I got my first photo this year on March 28, here. This must be a new summer brood. I got this photo a couple of days ago. I've been looking to get a better shot with no luck - so here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2017)
CommonNames: Pacific Azure; Echo Azure; California Buckwheat; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: h715d_h713-030.jpg
Notes: 35107485994 These are odd galls on a flower head of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family that is just about done flowering. You can see the remains of petals at the top of the galls, showing that they are really galled flowers. The galls are open at the top, like little volcanoes erupting from the plant - or tunicates in the ocean. They are not listed in my book on plant galls, and I don't know whose galls they are. I see similar galls on the old flower heads of Eriodictyon crassifolium down by the river, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2017)

File.Name: h716a_h716-043.jpg
Notes:
35972064975 Here is a handsome little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working flowers of native Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae). Look how accurately the butterfly's proboscis enters the flower! Apparently the butterflies are named for their blue color above and not for any affinity with the ocean. I've noticed these before this year, but this is my first photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2017)

My good news for day nine of the Whittier Fire is that Highway 154 was reopened this evening - the highway that lies between the fire and our home! That's a very good omen that we've survived another one. The fire is still active, and homes on West Camino Cielo are still under mandatory evacuation orders, but I'm breathing easier tonight. I went up into the higher mountains again today - there's not much choice. The fire still has some hot spots, but wind from the south/southeast helped today. It was still warm, in the low 90s. I have to go to the valley tomorrow or the next day anyway, so now I'm looking forward to the drive. It will be hard not to stop along the way - though I'm not supposed to!

File.Name: h716b_h716-035.jpg
Notes: 35971774835 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of native Red Thistle aka Venus Thistle or Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains today. This butterfly is a bit cream-colored, but there's no confusing it with a Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) like the butterfly in this photo. These gorgeous thistles have been flowering since the beginning of June, and they're still going strong. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2017)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Red Thistle; Venus Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; P. rutulus

File.Name: h716c_h716-001.jpg
Notes: 35800763882 I believe this is a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly working a non-native flower of Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis, Asteraceae) in the mountains. This butterfly is smaller and blacker than the similar Mournful Duskywing (E. tristis) butterfly that is more brown than black, compare this photo. That contrasting brown patch on the front wing is said to be a Funereal field mark. The Mournful uses Coast Live Oak as its host plant, but these Funereals use plants in the pea family (Fabaceae), and I found this one in a place where these were Coulter Pines and chaparral - but no oaks. The star thistle is an invasive weed in our mountains, but at least it's good for something! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2017)
CommonNames: Funereal Duskywing; Yellow Star Thistle; Mournful Duskywing
ScientificName: Erynnis funeralis; Centaurea solstitialis; Asteraceae; E. tristis; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Erynnis funeralis; Centaurea solstitialis; Asteraceae; E. tristis; Fabaceae

File.Name: h716d_h716-008.jpg
Notes: 35800337152 This is a grasshopper nymph's exuvia - the shed skin after molting. It's interesting that this one has "training wings" which go through the molt as well. I'm pretty sure from this that this was the final molt from nymph to adult. I found this on a stem of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). The nymph was originally head-down on the near-vertical stem, but it was windy enough that I couldn't get a photo as I found it, so I broke the stem and laid it flat. (The wind was frustrating, but at least it was from the south - favorable for our wildfire situation!) BTW, the plant should be flowering by now, but it's late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2017)

File.Name: h717a_h717-012.jpg
Notes:
35951688856 Fire on the mountain - but it's just about done!

After ten days, the Whittier Fire is subdued - but not quite done. There are still a few hot spots, e.g. in the north-west corner between Santa Ynez Peak and the Bee Rock quarry, but mostly it looked quiet today. Highway 154 was reopened yesterday evening, and I had to go to the valley anyway, so I got a look. We weren't supposed to stop along the way, but I couldn't resist making one stop. This is a place near the ranch land at the upper (east) end of the lake. The oaks are scorched, but some might even recover. The mountains in the background look barren. There are more interesting views along the way, but no place to stop. I suppose I could walk it, but there's no hurry. The fire burned over the mountains and part way down the coast side, but "the threat level on the east side of the fire has been reduced significantly" - as Inciweb succinctly puts it. That's where I live. The evacuation orders for West Camino Cielo, Kinevan, Stagecoach, and Paradise roads has been lifted. I may take another drive tomorrow! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2017)

File.Name: h717b_h717-005.jpg
Notes: 35604288130 I found this fantastic White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) trapped against the window of my truck this morning! The window on the other side was open, and the moth must have got in and couldn't find its way out again - like a big fly bottle. I opened the door to get a photo, and the moth immediately started fluttering its wings. I got this photo just as it was taking off. I usually see these fine moths right at dusk, though sometimes they are out in the middle of the day as well. See this photo for a clearer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2017)

File.Name: h717c_h713-003.jpg
Notes: 35992846775 This is another close shot of a petal of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family, showing the inside of the flower. You an see one of the nectar glands at the bottom that form bumps on the outside of the petals, see this photo. These glands produce nectar which attracts insects. These highly variable plants are still flowering in our summer-dry mountains. They are slowing down in the higher mountains, but they are just starting to flower in the canyon at home where I got this photo. This was my alternative take for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Texture" this week. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2017)

File.Name: h717d_h713-019.jpg
Notes: 35815364812 This is looking close at a detached petal of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family, showing the inside of the flower. You an see one of the nectar glands that form bumps on the outside of the petals, see this photo. These glands produce nectar which attracts insects. Yep, these fantastic plants are still lowering in our summer-dry mountains. They are slowing down in the higher mountains where I got this shot, but they are just starting to flower in the canyon at home. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Member's Choice: Texture" - HMM! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2017)

File.Name: h718a_h718-015.jpg
Notes:
36015283075 Here are two shots - above and below - of a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Or is it a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) - see this photo for some discussion. I don't really care what it is - it's a beautiful butterfly! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2017)

It was warm again today, but not too bad - in the mid-80s. There was some wind that seemed to be from all directions, north and south, but with our Whittier Fire subdued ... I don't really care! Thank you firefighters. Just a a few days ago the situation seemed dire, and tonight after eleven days it looks clear.

File.Name: h718b_h718-023.jpg
Notes: 36014903155 After consulting BugGuide, I believe this is a Cixiid Planthopper in the family Cixiidae, Fulgoroidea. It has antennae below the eyes and interesting wings. It looks a lot like this photo at BugGuide of a genus Melanoliarus hopper. It's only about 1100 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm, about 3/16 inch. I found this tiny insect on the calyx of a spent flower of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2017)

File.Name: h718c_h718-007.jpg
Notes: 36014606505 This looks like it might be a grasshopper nymph with those shot wings, but I believe it is an adult Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) grasshopper, and those are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. This one is quite dark, but others are light tan - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black specimen! They just are variable.) I found this one in the mint patch in the garden this morning. They might be our most common grasshopper. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2017)

File.Name: h718d_h718-022.jpg
Notes: 36014428175 Here is a new hatch of tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) spiderlings! Look at all the early molts at the bottom of the egg case. This is probably the same nest I showed in this photo last week, in which case they might be the Lesser Green Lynx (Peucetia longipalpis). It seems early in the year to find a hatch. I usually seem to find them in October/Arachtober - but maybe those are Peucetia viridans? It was curious that mama was not around, but maybe she was just hiding a ways off. I don't blame her! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2017)

File.Name: h719a_h719-003.jpg
Notes:
35645812930 I believe this is a white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) perched for a moment on a seed capsule in the garden this morning. This butterfly is larger and browner than the similar Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly that is more black than brown, compare this photo. We also have the Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly which lacks the white fringe, see this photo. This Mournful Duskywing uses Coast Live Oak as its host plant, and I may have a photo of the caterpillar here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2017)

It was pretty breezy today, mostly from the south/southeast in our canyon at least. It's been warm but not hot, in the 80s. The fire seems quiet despite the wind.

Notes: 35901273961 This is a mating pair of Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a new composite flower of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. These Ambush Bugs are mighty predators that can bring down prey much larger than they are (like this, but they have tender moments as well. Happy hump day! File.Name: h719c_h719-008.jpg
Notes: 35993246736 This is webbing and frass of social caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a plant of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). The caterpillars are gone now leaving nothing but their excrement. (See this photo for the caterpillars.) The little caterpillars spin their webs over the plant stems and consume the green tissue of the leaves leaving brown husks behind. They will feed until the shrubs go dormant for summer about now, then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they reemerge to finish feeding and pupate into adults next spring. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year! Yes, they do seem to kill a few branches, but usually the plants survive. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2017)

File.Name: h719d_h713-011.jpg
Notes: 35900961691 I'm pretty sure this is the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side, and you can see both in my photo, including the line that connects them. Maybe the white detritus on the web is ash from the Whittier Fire, but this is in the higher mountains which is another five miles away, so maybe not. The plant is not-quite-flowering Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Happy Web Webnesday! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 July 2017)

File.Name: h720a_h720-006.jpg
Notes:
35922208231 Do you ever have the feeling that you're being watched?

This is the still-spotted fawn of a Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus or O. h. californicus?) being invisible in the woods. I never would have noticed the motionless fawn, but mom made a move nearby, and then I spotted this one. I'm really not sure what kind of deer we have here. They are smaller than normal "muleys". Try a Google search on "What kind of deer live in Southern California" - maybe you can tell me! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2017)

I did drive up West Camino Cielo road towards the Whittier Fire today, but the burn area is still closed and I couldn't see anything. I can see more with some walking, but I reckon I'll wait until the actual fire-fighting is done. The fire now stands at 18,430 acres, 76% contained. The weather is still mild with high temps in the low-80s and mostly calm winds largely from the south. Nice!

It's confusing when the butterfly is on buckwheat - which is a host plant for Lupine Blues and a nectar plant for Acmon Blues. I guess I don't really care what it is - it's a beautiful butterfly! What a curious rear orifice it has! I instantly thought of another shot of a different kind of wild bee, see this photo. This is a good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2017)

File.Name: h721a_h721-037.jpg
Notes:
35906234172 Yipes! This is a real Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It's a kind of kissing bug - so called because they feed on blood during the night and prefer to bite humans around the mouth or eyes. Julie found this one on a book in the house. I moved it to a white plate outside, but it was a frisky little devil and at one point Julie grabbed a piece of mail to try to corral it. You can see a bit of its spike-like proboscis folded under it's head, compare my [Previous] photo of a different Reduviid.

This is one insect I don't want to find in our house! Apparently, they live and feed in Dusky-footed Woodrat (Neotoma fuscipes) nests, but they can move also into houses. I'm not surprised - the woodrats have moved into our house too at times in the past, see this photo! These insects are a potential problem because they are known to be a potential host of Chagas disease, caused by the parasite Trypanosoma cruzi. My book (Charles Hogue, The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin, 1974) says that "The Western Cone-Nose Bug often harbors a trypanosome parasite apparently identical to the one found in the disease. It may be a non-virulent strain." Is that still true?? There's more info at the CDC and Texas A&M University. We'll keep them out of our bed! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2017)

It was almost cool today as it barely got to 80∞. There was also a pretty steady breeze - from the south in the morning, then more from the north. I'm glad the Whittier Fire is contained, on our end anyway. I'm also glad that the "glass factory" shooting area near Arroyo Burro Saddle has finally been closed for the summer. The difference in traffic along East Camino Cielo road is remarkable, also better manners.
CommonNames: Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug; kissing bug; Dusky-footed Woodrat; Chagas disease; The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin
ScientificName: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi
associatedTaxa: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi

File.Name: h721b_h721-012.jpg
Notes: 36035229106 Here is a gorgeous nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera ) on a furry branch of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see the "training wings" of this late-instar nymph that will soon become an adult. Note that spike-like proboscis folded under it's head, and compare my [Next] photo. This may well be the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii), in which case they lose some of their charm as they mature - compare this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 July 2017)

File.Name: h721c_h721-023.jpg
Notes: 35905729902 I found this large green immature Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) munching a stem of non-native (and invasive)Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum, Fabaceae). Cool, its got a taste for this nasty weedy shrub! The grasshopper is pretty large, at least three inches long, and you can see that it still has the "training wings" of a late-stage nymph. My first thought was that it's a non-native insect on a non-native plant, but it might well be a Bird Grasshopper in the genus Schistocerca. It might even be a Gray Bird Grasshopper (Schistocerca nitens) despite the green color. The photos at BugGuide of this species include a number of green forms - especially nymphs. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 July 2017)

File.Name: h721d_h720-016.jpg
Notes: 36034668046 I found this handsome Bee Fly (order Bombyliidae, Diptera) working the summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Here it is perched for a moment on a twig which give a chance to see the twisty veins at tip of its wing. I believe these veins are the signature of the species Dipalta serpentina in the tribe Villini. The images at BugGuide are similar. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2017)

File.Name: h722a_h722-053.jpg
Notes:
35927845872 This golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) is going for nectar in a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's smaller than the bee in my [Previous] photo. Maybe it's the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni)? There more info at BugGuide and on p.94 of the excellent U.S. Forest Service Bumble Bees of the Western United States which can be downloaded here. This photo may extend the range a bit? It's a handsome bee in a handsome flower. The flowers in the higher mountains (where they first opened) are just about done, but they are in full glory in our canyon at home where I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)

It was a bit warmer today, in the low-90s, on the edge of being hot. It was breezy in the morning from the south and east, becoming calm in the afternoon. Isn't that the reverse of normal? Our weather might get interesting after tomorrow with some monsoon moisture!

File.Name: h722b_h722-044.jpg
Notes: 35927450032 This is a large Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) going for nectar in a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. These large bees make an impression on the flowers as they go round and round taking nectar from the glands at the base of the petals - see this photo and this photo. It's a handsome bee in a handsome flower. The flowers in the higher mountains (where they first opened) are just about done, but they are in full glory in our canyon at home where I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)

File.Name: h722c_h722-016.jpg
Notes: 35257053784 Here are photos of two different Damselflies (Zygoptera, Odonata) in the woods. The top one is hanging onto a leaf of not-quite-flowering Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). It has red mites (Acari) near its legs. The bottom one is hanging onto a composite flower bud of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. It appears to be munching some tiny prey. I don't know much about damselflies - these might even be male and female of the same kind? The most interesting thing about these might be that they are here in mid-summer when most of the creeks are dry. They have aquatic nymphs, so I wonder where the adults come from? (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)

File.Name: h722d_h722-001.jpg
Notes: 35256754594 I believe this is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae). It gets its common name for the size of its usual prey, but this one has something even smaller - a tiny red mite (Acari)! I have a shot of a different Reduviid you don't want to find in your house in my [Previous] photo, and I might have a photo of the nymph that this one comes from two photos back. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)

File.Name: h723a_h723-005.jpg
Notes:
36079277746 This is a striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working one of the first flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). The wasp looks "goggle eyed" - and indeed they are hard to photograph because they see so well. I'm concerned about these fine summer flowers this year. This one plant has flowers, but most plants have small buds that don't look like they'll mature. I hope I'm wrong about that. These summer flowers are all important in our summer-dry climate. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2017)

It was not too hot today, but quite breezy in the morning from the south again. We're still down for a 20% chance of showers and thunderstorms tomorrow. My grandkids are also coming down from Portland, Oregon tomorrow to stay with us for a week or more. That's cool - but it may change my photos for the week!
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: h723b_h723-021.jpg
Notes: 35987303711 I found this large Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on an old flower head of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae plant family. This one does have chevron markings on its abdomen, and I believe it is Peucetia viridans as opposed to (maybe) the Peucetia longipalpis in this photo. These Green lynx Spiders don't have to be green! This one is quite red, and compare this photo! There's an unusually interesting page about these spiders at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2017)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Trask's Yerba Santa

File.Name: h723c_h723-031.jpg
Notes:
35986943561 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) acorn. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa. That's a large acorn behind the spider and a small aborted acorn to the left. The large acorn is interesting because it's producing a flow of real "oak honey". It's sweet, I've tasted it. Insects like it too. It's not uncommon to hear a buzz of bees from the oaks in the summer, and this is why. It also makes this a very good place for the spider to set up shop. The flow is just starting, and there will be insect visitors, see this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2017)

I'm not sure what causes the honey flow. One possibility is that it's the work of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. Another possibility is that it's the work of Drippy Nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina, which I don't know anything about. There's some info on p.72 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. This is fascinating stuff!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Scrub Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy Nut
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina

File.Name: h723d_h720-046.jpg
Notes: 35279050614 These are brilliant red berries of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland, nowhere really common. The berries are juicy when ripe. They taste quite bitter to me, but maybe I haven't collected them when they are dead ripe? See this article for a different view and caveat emptor!. I got this photo a few days ago and never posted it. I was in the area again today, so I thought I'd get a fresh photo and ... every berry was eaten! I assume it's birds, and I guess they do wait until the berries are dead ripe! It's interesting that most of the plants have no berries this year - but this shrub was covered. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2017)

File.Name: h724a_h724-016.jpg
Notes:
35336653793 I found this handsome Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) on the chipseal road in our canyon today. I believe it's genus Trimerotropis in the Oedipodinae group of Band-winged Grasshopper. I thought it might be a Thalassica Grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica), but Allice tells me that it's a Fontana Grasshopper (Trimerotropis fontana) - see her comments below. Well, they are similar. This one has blue hind tibia and pale wings when it takes flight. Compare this photo of (maybe) the same thing, and see how different in color they can be. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2017)

It was downright cool today, only in the 70s. It was also quite breezy from both the north and south. The day started with a bit of marine layer fog spilling over the mountains into our canyon, accompanied by "fog winds". The fog quickly dissipated, but the wind stuck around. My grandkids are flying down from Portland, Oregon tonight to stay with us for a week or more. That's cool - but it may change my photos for the rest of the week!
CommonNames: Grasshopper; Band-winged Grasshopper; Thalassica Grasshopper; Fontana Grasshopper
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Trimerotropis; Oedipodinae; Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis fontana
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Trimerotropis; Oedipodinae; Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis fontana

File.Name: h724b_h724-011.jpg
Notes: 36009691761 This is a new flower of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family today in the canyon at home, the first I've seen this year. Actually, this is the first one I've seen since 2015. I guess the drought years have been hard on them. This seems late to be flowering, but the last one I saw was in September, see this photo. Yes, I noticed that there's a spent flower next to the open one, so it's really the second flower of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2017)

File.Name: h724c_h723-033.jpg
Notes: 35751672120 Here is a trio of developing fruit of a of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They look like pawn shop balls! In another month, the fruit will split open revealing real nuts inside, see this photo. This was my alternative take for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Three" this week. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2017)

File.Name: h724d_h720-041.jpg
Notes: 35295673854 Here is a trio of green acorns on a shrub of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like a pretty good crop on the scrub oaks this year, but less so on the oak trees. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Three" - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2017)

File.Name: h725a_h725-011.jpg
Notes:
36000011142 This is a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) about to take wing from a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden. These insects seem impossibly thin, but they are fully functioning insects! You can see the proboscis of this one folded under its head. They seem to suck plants, not other insects like the assassin bug in this photo. They seem to be quite common when you look for them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)

We have grandkids visiting! I stayed close to the house today, and I did find time to get some photos. The weather was much like yesterday - rather cool, in the 70s, and breezy. Like yesterday, the day started with a bit of marine layer fog spilling over the mountains, accompanied by "fog winds". The fog quickly dissipated, but the wind stuck around.
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: h725b_h725-042.jpg
Notes: 35361496603 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. Probably it is genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane. The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. BugGuide reports what I've often seen, that the flies will often bob up and down above a flower prior landing to nectar. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it! BTW, zinnias with single flowers are excellent garden plants for attracting butterflies and many other insects, but avoid the usual plants with doubled flowers. Insects can't get into them. That advice goes for most garden flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)

File.Name: h725c_h725-023.jpg
Notes: 35999272662 This is the nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) snuggled into an old leaflet of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae). Its antennae go on for about that much again! We have several different kind, brown and green and almost white. These crickets are the songsters of our woods on late-summer and autumn nights later in the season. This one already has "training wings" so it won't be long now. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: h725d_h725-029.jpg
Notes: 35775333950 Here are two shots of the same kind of golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) that I showed in this photo. It's working some kind of Lavendar flowers in the garden. Yeah, I know, they aren't the best shots - it was breezy and the bee was too fast for me. Try leaning back from your screen. I think this might be the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni). There more info at BugGuide and on p.94 of the excellent U.S. Forest Service Bumble Bees of the Western United States which can be downloaded here. It's a handsome bee. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)

File.Name: h726a_h726-033.jpg
Notes:
36055987921 This large Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera) has bought down a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera). Here they are on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The fly is quite large, at least an inch long and probably more. Nice whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2017)

It was warmer today, in the 80s, but just as breezy as it's been. It was breezy enough to be annoying. I took many photos of this one and my [Previous] photo to get that one shot that was satisfactory.

File.Name: h726b_h726-025.jpg
Notes: 35798525110 This tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has brought down a much larger wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The spider looks hairless so it's not another Mecaphesa, but it is quite small. The bee is definitely not a Honey Bee with that shaggy leg, maybe it's another Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora? The flowers are still going strong in the canyon at home, though they are just about done in the higher mountains where they started flowering. It seems odd that the spider has the bee by the face - isn't that a very hard place? (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2017)

File.Name: h726c_h725-028.jpg
Notes: 36021283002 Here is a mating pair of tiny true bugs (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) ... making more tiny true bugs! Maybe they are in the family Miridae, but that's a very generic guess. They are "doing it" on a spent flower head of native White Sage (Salvia apiana, Lamiaceae). These bugs are about 1060 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 4.2 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Happy hump day! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)

File.Name: h726d_h725-025.jpg
Notes: 36147317166 I found this fragment of an ash leaf caught in a spider web in a Wild Blackberry bramble yesterday. This is part of the ash fall from the nearby Whittier Fire which started on July 8, not quite three weeks ago. This ash leaf has been protected from the elements by its location in the bramble and web. It would fall apart with the slightest touch. The ash leaf must have been carried here from the Whittier Fire, several miles away - just try to imagine the chain of events that brought it here to this spider web and then kept it intact until I found it! I find something poignant about these remnants of a forest that is no longer there. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)

File.Name: h727a_h727-057.jpg
Notes: 36044926872 We took our grandkids to the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History today to see the "Bugs... Outside the Box" exhibit. It was OK, but the kids and I were most interested in the table outside with living critters, some of which you could pick up. My favorite was this caterpillar of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus, Saturniinae). I've found the large adult moths (here) and the silk cocoons (here - an old Fotolog shot, I need a new one!) so I know they're up here in the mountains, but I've never seen the caterpillars before. They are impressive. I've read that they get up to four or five inches long in their final instar! This one was captive in a plastic tub with leaves to munch on. They even had a docent there who really knew something about them. I really don't care much for critters in captivity, and I hope this one will soon go free. I got this photo hand-held with my normal lens - something I don't usually do any more. It was a fun day at the museum! (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 27 July 2017)
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History

File.Name: h727b_h727-013.jpg
Notes: 36076479771 This appears to be a small Orb Weaver spider in the genus Araneus on a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. It's only about 1/4 inch long! It looks like A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, but the habitat seems totally wrong - a garden flower rather than an oak tree in the woods. Maybe it is, but the abdomen also looks more patterned than either of those species. Could it be a juvenile of one of the large spiders like this one? Whatever it is, it's a striking spider. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2017)
ScientificName: Araneus; Zinnia; A. bispinosus; A. montereyensis
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Zinnia; A. bispinosus; A. montereyensis

File.Name: h727c_h726-010.jpg
Notes: 35406127883 I believe this really is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a summer flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) butterflies look similar, but they favor California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) - which doesn't occur near this one. See this photo.) The flowers are nearly done, but a few are hanging on. They have flat stamen filaments that remind me of those wide matches that some restaurants used to give out when I was a kid. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2017)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Lupine Blue; California Buckwheat

File.Name: h727d_h722-026.jpg
Notes: 36076232891 This is a large Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) butts up in a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. These large bees make an impression on the flowers as they go round and round taking nectar from the glands visible the base of the petals. It's a handsome bee in a handsome flower. The flowers are still going strong in the canyon at home, though they are just about done in the higher mountains where they started flowering. Look at the sepal at the base of the flower - can you spot the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae)? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)

File.Name: h728a_h728-042.jpg
Notes:
36231651265 This is the remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). What fantastic camouflage! They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the flowers. Buckwheat flowers are about 1/8 inch across, so they make a good ruler. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was. I've never found them on any other plant. See my [Previous] photo for another shot. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2017)

If you can't see the caterpillar, it's stretched out across the middle of my photo, with its head near the clear flower on the left and its tail near the right edge. Now that's camouflage! The weather has been more of the same lately - temps in the upper 80s, not too hot, and some breeze, especially in the morning. I can't complain.
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: h728b_h728-037.jpg
Notes:
35425691853 Here are photos of two different debris-carrying larvae that I found today. Both critters are so well camouflaged that they are almost impossible to see! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2017)

On the left is the remarkable "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, until they finally transform into the green moths on our window screens at night. This one is feeding on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), and has decorated itself with stamens and pistils of the flowers. Buckwheat flowers are about 1/8 inch across, so they make a good ruler. I only found this one because it was moving when nothing else was. I've never found them on any other plant. See my [Next] photo.

On the right is the remarkable debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae, Neuroptera) on a leaflet of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens, Polypodiaceae). You can see the critter at the bottom of the debris ball. It seems to have something between its long jaws. BugGuide has a nice page about these with lots of photos if you click the [Images] button. Apparently the debris-carrying larvae cover themselves with bits of litter, perhaps to deter predators. It is great camouflage. The next time you see a bit of lint moving along a leaf, look closer!
CommonNames: Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths; California Buckwheat; Green Lacewing; Western Bracken Fern
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: h728c_h724-004.jpg
Notes: 36097488291 This is some kind of Green Bottle Fly (genus Lucilia?, Calliphoridae, Diptera) on a leaf in the garden with a composite Zinnia flower behind. The fly is rather pretty as long as it's walking over flowers instead of your potato salad - HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2017)

File.Name: h728d_h722-057.jpg
Notes: 35397027584 This is a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) on the textured vinyl material below the window of my truck. Sure, I opened the door and window to give it a chance to fly off, but I got a photo first! It seems odd to find any crane fly in mid-summer as they are more common earlier in the year - but here it is. I don't believe it is a proper Large Crane Fly in the family Tipulidae family because it doesn't have the long palps that these flies have - see this photo. Maybe it's a Limoniid Crane Fly in the family Limoniidae ? I like this photo - the fly, the shadow, and the texture all work together nicely. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2017)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Large Crane Fly; Limoniid Crane Fly

File.Name: h729a_h729-012.jpg
Notes:
36252916925 This is another debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae,Neuroptera) sticking its neck out on a milkweed flower in the garden. See this photo> from yesterday - funny how you notice one ... and then you notice more! This one looks like a snail, but it's really sticking its neck (and thorax) out of a ball of debris that it carries around for shelter. I believe the milkweed is Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae) which is native to eastern North America and widely planted here. The flowers are remarkable, second only to orchids for complexity. I have an old photo at Fotolog that tries to explain them, but I need to redo it. Meanwhile there's always Google. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)

The weather has been more of the same lately - temps in the upper 80s, not too hot, and some breeze, especially in the morning. I can't complain.
ScientificName: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae
associatedTaxa: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae

File.Name: h729b_h729-028.jpg
Notes: 35447716893 These are open pupal cases of Braconid Wasps (Braconidae, Hymenoptera) on a leaf of a Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. These are the same pupae that I originally found on a still-living caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly - see this photo. The caterpillar fell away, leaving the pupae behind, see this photo. The two lower pupae have opened through a terminal lid, but the two upper ones have opened less neatly through the side. Have the parasites been parasitized by hyperparasites?? The pupae are about 900 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. The exit holes are about 200 pixel across, less than a millimeter. (See here for how I figure.)
Locality: Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly - see this photo. The caterpillar fell away

File.Name: h729c_h729-045.jpg
Notes: 36117751151 Here is another golden Bumble Bee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) going for nectar in a fantastic summer flower of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family. I believe it is the Morrison Bumble Bee (Bombus morrisoni), see this photo for more info and links. These bees go 'round and 'round in the flowers, butts up all the way. Sure, I saved another shot for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday! The flowers won't last too much longer, and I wonder what the bees will do then? Part of the answer is here. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)

File.Name: h729d_h729-035.jpg
Notes: 36210600616 I found this mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sack among the weeds along our road. These spiders are devoted mothers - this one might sit for a month waiting for the eggs to hatch, and then for another month until the spiderlings take off on their own. Like the spider in this photo, this one has red colors as well as green. It does have chevron markings on its back, so maybe it is Peucetia viridans as opposed to (maybe) the Peucetia longipalpis in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)
ScientificName: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia viridans; Peucetia longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia viridans; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: h730a_h730-042.jpg
Notes:
35471910993 This is a caterpillar of a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) on a plant of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae). Monarch caterpillars feed exclusively on milkweed-related plants, but I was still surprised to find this caterpillar almost in August today. The monarchs overwinter in the non-native Eucalyptus trees near the beach by the thousands - see this photo, and [Previous] to it - but they usually usually disperse in the spring and we don't see them again until the next autumn. This one is unusual, but not unprecedented - see this photo. BTW does my photo look odd, possibly because caterpillar appears to be walking backwards? The caterpillar was originally vertical with its head down, so I rotated my camera 90∞ counter-clockwise to make better use of my screen space. Is that cheating? (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 July 2017)

It was a little warmer today, into the 90s. No problem, but it's expected to get downright hot later this week. I'm not looking forward to it.
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Apocynaceae; Eucalyptus

File.Name: h730b_h730-024.jpg
Notes: 36143139691 Yeah, the eyes of this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) are not quite sharp - but look at its feet! This bee has been working the new summer flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae or Apocynaceae). Milkweed flowers are remarkable, second only to orchids for complexity, and they set a trap for pollinator insects to carry their pollen. This bee's legs have slipped between the "horns and hoods" of other flowers and picked up "pollinia" or pollen sacks by doing so. They are like little saddlebags. When the bee gets to another flower, the pollen sack might get stuck in the "stigmatic slit" of another flower - pollination happens. Milkweed flowers offer sweet nectar to pollinating insects, but they expect them to carry their pollen from plant to plant in exchange! I have an old photo at Fotolog that tries to explain milkweed flowers, but I want to redo it. Meanwhile there's always Google. Complexity aside, milkweed flowers are strange and beautiful! (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 July 2017)

File.Name: h730d_h729-014.jpg
Notes: 36108226482 Here is a little beetle (Coleoptera) munching stamens on the first flowers I seen in the mint patch of the garden. I believe this critter is genus Collops in the family of Soft-winged Flower Beetles (Melyridae), maybe species Collops quadriguttatus. I've been seeing similar beetles in the mountains, e.g. photos here and here - though the beetles in these photos vary in the amount of black on the wing covers and may represent different species. These beetles have short elytra - they hang out behind. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)

File.Name: h731a_h730-027.jpg
Notes:
35465089754 This is a single flower of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. See this photo for a whole plant. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get down on your hands and knees to really see the small flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring - though this one may have already been sprung, compare this photo. The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid. The flowers are related to our shrubby Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2017)

Yipes, we're under a NWS Heat Advisory, Wind Advisory, and a Red Flag Warning tonight! There's an ill wind blowing that feels like a real sundowner wind event blowing from the north towards the ocean. The sure sign of this is that the Santa Barbara foothills are warmer than up here in the mountains, but I haven't noticed this yet at the Weather Underground stations. There's some info (and a nice photo) in this pdf at InciWeb. Try a Google Search to learn more.

File.Name: h731b_h729-051.jpg
Notes: 36132474532 I noticed something interesting on the new flowers of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family that are still appearing in our mountains. This is looking at the base of the central pedestal of a flower - you can see the base of the three petals and the three narrow sepals between them. Of course the petals have glands near the base, see this photo. Now look closer at those yellow patches of hairs on the sepals - they are also glands with specialized hairs! Do they also attract insects? Do all Calochortus species have them? (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)

File.Name: h731c_h729-056.jpg
Notes:
36165878631 Here are two photos from different months showing rust fungus (Pucciniales aka Uredinales, Basidiomycotina) growing on native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae). I got the photo on the left on May 6 this year, and never got around to posting the photo. It shows a fungus making a making a hot-dog-on-a-stick-like gall on the petiole of a Poison Oak leaf. It might be the rust fungus Pileolaria effusa, but I don't know anything else about it. I got the photo on the right two days ago. It shows a rust fungus taking over a tripartite leaf of Poison Oak making color blotches and small raised wart-like bumps. What's curious is that these are on the same plant. Could they be two different forms of the same fungus? (San Marcos Pass, 06 May and 29 July 2017)

(And yeah, what a difference three months makes!)

File.Name: h731d_h731-003.jpg
Notes:
36129025632 This is the secret crown hidden in the throat of a garden flower of Rose Campion (Silene coronaria) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. I'm sure it's a queen's crown with those royal colors! This flower has a crown or corona - think "coronation" not beer. In botany, the corona is an extra ring of flower structures besides the usual sepals, petals, stamens, and pistils - e.g. in milkweeds and orchids. I'm not sure if the crown in this flower counts as a botanical corona, but it's good enough to give this plant its species name. This plant is one of those "plant once, have forever" plants. I think we got it decades ago from my mom, and it's been coming back in different places in he garden ever since. Note that it hasn't spread to the woods. We also have the native Mexican Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. laciniata, Caryophyllaceae) in our mountains which has a similar corona, see this photo. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Queen" - HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2017)

(I'm giving the Macro Mondays group one more try after my last photo was removed from the pool without comment ;=)

File.Name: h801a_h801-020.jpg
Notes:
35514911973 This is the spectacular caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a branch of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks like something from the ocean - like one of Jeff Goddard's nudibranchs! It's curious that the butterflies are so common in our oak forest, but I've only found the caterpillars once before. Maybe they're usually high up in the trees? This one has its head down, but see this photo of the other one I've found. I can't explain how yellow this caterpillar is - the other one is green, and this one looks green under the yellow. Maybe it's a molting thing? (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2017)

We actually had a bit of monsoon rain today! It wasn't enough to measure, but it did clear the air and smell wonderful. It was overcast and muggy today, and dead calm - so calm that it was almost spooky! The clouds helped cut the heat this morning, but it still got up into the high 90s in the afternoon. A neighbor's weather station recorded 102∞. We're still under a NWS Heat Advisory. Yipes - Portland is expected to be 107∞ tomorrow!

File.Name: h801b_h801-012.jpg
Notes: 35926958000 Here is a gorgeous Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with a small Crane Fly (Tipulomorpha, Diptera) for prey on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. Hah - it's a small crane fly, not a huge spider! Despite it's small size, I believe that the fly is a proper Large Crane Fly (family Tipulidae) with the "terminal (fourth) segment of maxillary palpus elongate, longer than first three segments combined." It seems odd to find any crane fly in mid-summer as they are more common earlier in the year - but here it is. I have no idea what kind of spider it is, there are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2017)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Crane Fly; Prickly Pear; Large Crane Fly
ScientificName: Salticidae; Tipulomorpha; Diptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Tipulidae

File.Name: h801c_h801-025.jpg
Notes: 36322932375 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its much larger prey on a "flower head" of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa. I'm not sure, but its prey sure looks like a winged ant. (Could it be something else??) That's odd because all the ants around our house finished swarming last spring - but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2017)

File.Name: h801d_h801-007.jpg
Notes: 36154423972 This interesting fly (Diptera) is sucking *something* from a leaf in the mint patch of our garden. I believe it's in the family Lauxaniidae, possible species Minettia flaveola. See the discussion with Thijs de Graaf at this photo of (probably) the same kind of fly. It has impressive mouthparts - see this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2017)

File.Name: h802a_h802-001.jpg
Notes:
35945020260 Be careful little bee - some of those flowers have claws!

Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey by a new flower in the mint patch of the garden. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa. The spider is about 650 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2.5 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2017)

It was actually a few degrees cooler today, only about 95∞, but I reckon the humidity made up for it. It was one of those days where you get covered with sweat just sitting in the shade. It only got up to 105∞ in Portland today, not the 107∞ that was forecast, but tomorrow is expected to be warmer. We didn't get any more rain today, though there's still a 40% chance tonight, and we're under a NWS Flash Flood Watch until later tonight.

File.Name: h802b_h802-015.jpg
Notes: 35532884503 This is another mating pair of Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera), this time on a flower cluster of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). Look how the lower one has its proboscis out! It's a weedy plant that is invasive in our mountains, but at least many insects utilize it and it doesn't get prickers in my socks. These little bugs seem to spend a lot of time doing this, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 August 2017)

File.Name: h802c_h730-018.jpg
Notes: 36204843821 I had to get close to these insects to see what they are actually doing... This is a mating pair of Small Milkweed Bugs (Lygaeus kalmii, Lygaeidae) on a seedpod of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). Uh, it's an interesting position for them - but they seem quite happy with it. They have a real ball and socket joint between them that is quite flexible. Note that one has its arm around the other as a token of endearment. Happy hump day! (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 July 2017)

File.Name: h802d_h728-021.jpg
Notes: 36296884306 This is a spider web with a funnel disappearing into what I assume is an old gopher hole. The white bits in the web are ashes from the Whittier fire a few weeks ago, they are still everywhere. Maybe this is the web of a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider? We have miniature versions of this on our window screens at home. I guess for some insects, this really is the entrance to the afterlife - Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2017)

File.Name: h803a_h803-016.jpg
Notes:
35962866570 Here are a pair of Grass-Skipper butterflies on a new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. I'm pretty sure the butterflies are Umber Skippers (Poanes melane). They were common in the spring, then there was a gap when I didn't see them, now here they are again - I believe this is their second brood. This is kind of a second flowering for these plants also. There's one colony is a sunny place that started flowering in June, but the plants in this colony in a shady place are just now starting two months later. It's one of my favorite summer flowers, and the butterflies appreciate it also. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2017)

It might have been a couple of degrees cooler today, but it was still too damn hot - in the mid-90s and muggy. Hah - it hit 108∞ in Portland, Oregon today. Is that a new record? Our grandkids are lucky to still be down here.
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Umber Skippers

File.Name: h803b_h803-007.jpg
Notes: 36191013092 Here is another goofy little Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera, on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's probably about 3/4 inch long. I'm not sure how to distinguish nymph and adult as both are wingless, but this one is larger than the nymphs I've been showing, so it might be an adult. This photo probably shows another adult with similar colors, but compare this photo of a nymph to appreciate how variable their color can be. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2017)

Notes: 36222781341 I found this adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods this morning. Here it is saying goodby to me - but they move so slowly that I had time for a photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2017)

File.Name: h803d_h729-019.jpg
Notes: 36314926866 Here is a little beetle (Coleoptera) munching new flowers in mint patch of the garden. I believe this critter is genus Collops in the family of Soft-winged Flower Beetles (Melyridae), maybe species Collops quadriguttatus - see this photo for the rest of the beetle. It's a beautiful beetle butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2017)

File.Name: h804a_h804-020.jpg
Notes:
36240710611 This is a Sulphur Butterfly (genus Colias) working new pollen-bearing staminate flowers of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants.) I suspect it's Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii), but I really don't know how to distinguish it from the Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme) other than by habitat. I found this one in the mountains miles away from any agricultural area that the latter one favors. There's also the Queen Alexandra's Sulphur (Colias alexandra)! OK, I'm confused. Whatever it is, it's an attractive butterfly - kind of a simpler California Dogface (Colias eurydice). (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 August 2017)

It was definitely cooler today with a high temp of about 90∞. It still felt hot and muggy, and it was a little breezier than its been. I suspect this will go down in the books as a record warm summer - if not year.

File.Name: h804b_h804-014.jpg
Notes: 35568923323 Here are two shots of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a new pollen-bearing staminate flower of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants.) The summer flowers are new, and this is the first of these butterflies that I've managed to photograph this year. I've been looking for them, they are late this year. The slate-gray upper wings are usually hidden - they are striking. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 August 2017)

File.Name: h804c_h803-039.jpg
Notes: 36332020906 I found this bee-sized Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on our upstairs deck late yesterday afternoon. It was injured, but still alive. I wonder if it's a female who has just finished ovipositing - laying her eggs - and is now "spawned out" like a salmon in the river that has laid its eggs and is now depleted? I have no time tonight try to identify this handsome fly, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2017)

File.Name: h804d_h730-044.jpg
Notes: 35980015140 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on a not quite flowering stem in the mint patch of the garden. Probably it is genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane. The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. BugGuide reports what I've seen, that the flies will often bob up and down above a flower prior landing to nectar. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2017)

File.Name: h805a_h805-009.jpg
Notes:
35997864060 I reckon this is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) growing in the canyon. These swallowtails look less pale as we move into summer - they get a cream color, and sometimes I confuse them with Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus). Both species have multiple broods, and this one looks quite fresh. Flowers that are suitable for these butterflies are getting scarce in our summer-dry mountains, but the plants in this colony are growing in a shady place and are just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2017)

Today was much like yesterday - still warm, but a few degrees cooler and breezier.

I'm pretty sure that's an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) above and a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) below. This is the first Woodland Skipper I've managed to photograph this year, though I've been seeing them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2017)

File.Name: h805d_h803-030.jpg
Notes: 35997382760 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the outside of a petal of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae) a couple of days ago. I would guess that it's a male because of its general proportions, but it doesn't seem to have particularly male-looking palps - compare this photo. I was back in the same place today, and the flowers are mostly done for the season. I wonder where this spider has gone? (Probably it hasn't gone far. The Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae) will be flowering in this place before too long.) (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2017)

File.Name: h806a_h806-003.jpg
Notes:
36018306250 This is the remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. It's odd that I found quite a few nymphs last summer - and not one adult. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

Today was much like yesterday, maybe a little breezier. The grandkids are finally heading home to Portland, Oregon with their dad. It was a good visit, but I'm ready for some peace and quiet after two weeks of company!

File.Name: h806b_h806-026.jpg
Notes: 35606979113 This grasshopper has an attitude! I'm sure it is an adult Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) grasshopper, and those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual! They just are variable.) This one is perched in a flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

File.Name: h806c_h806-016.jpg
Notes: 36246247852 Here are two shots of a tiny Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata, Araneidae) spider sitting on its web that goes across a single leaf of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I've seen these before, usually earlier in the year. This one seem uncommonly small. In the 1:1 macro shot on the left, the spider is about 485 pixels long, which comes to about 1.9 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) The shadow on the right is very cool! Note that this spider has eight spots on its abdomen instead of the usual six. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

File.Name: h806d_h806-024.jpg
Notes: 36017754060 These are new spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) - the mama spider in this photo! Mama is still watching out over her youngsters, but from a distance. That sound reasonable to me. I found this nest in the weeds along the road, mostly Tocolote (Centaurea melitensis, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

File.Name: h807a_h807-036.jpg
Notes:
36266594562 Here is a lovely Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly perched on mint flowers in the garden. This butterfly is a lady - see my [Previous] photo! The slate-gray upper wings are usually hidden - they are striking. These butterflies have been scarce so far this summer. This is the first one I've seen at home, and only the second one I've managed to photograph this year. They should become common in time - but not yet. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)

Today was much like yesterday - with peace and quiet!

File.Name: h807b_h807-043.jpg
Notes: 35626914083 This is a mama Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly ovipositing - laying an egg - on mint flowers in the garden. She caught me by surprise. She had been nectaring on the flowers - see my [Next] photo - and a moment later she was laying eggs! She just laid one or two, spreading them around I guess, unlike the Variable Ckeckerspot butterfly in this photo who lays them all at once. I have found their caterpillars before on the mint plants, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)

File.Name: h807c_h807-032.jpg
Notes: 35626754833 Here is a a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on it's web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. The buckwheat is flowering, making the white splotches on the background. The common name comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side. A better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. You can also see a bit of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white webs on the lower diagonals that look like extensions of the spider's legs. This is the first one I've seen this year. I hope it sticks around until October/Arachober like one did last year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)

File.Name: h807d_h806-033.jpg
Notes: 36031363970 This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Staying Healthy". It's actually a photo (my camera) of a photo (Julie's cell phone) of a photo (on Flickr) of a spectacular caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on a branch of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), see this photo for the original shot. Staying healthy has been an issue for since I had my heart attack and cardiac bypass surgery a year ago in May. My first photo taken outside after surgery was a different caterpillar - see this photo. I believe that getting outside everyday and taking photos of the life in our mountains is what's keeping me going. It's a healthy hobby that has also become a passion. BTW, Julie's cell phone is 2-1/2 inches wide, so I'm legal by the group rules. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

File.Name: h808a_h808-019.jpg
Notes:
36286360992 Here are two different color forms of galls of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) on stems of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls are instigated by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), and here are wasp larvae inside them. Most of the galls that I find are red, though I also find the green ones occasionally. These galls are particularly beautiful, but why would a gall - the induced home of tiny wasp larvae - be so darned pretty? Doesn't that just attract the attention of predators? (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2017)

It was breezy this morning so I started looking for something to photograph besides insects ... and found lots of wasp galls! Galls are just starting to appear. They partially fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms. The weather is still pretty static - "quiet and persistent" as the NWS forecast discussion had it this morning. It sound like it will get hot again later this week.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: h808b_h808-026.jpg
Notes: 36454180535 Here are two different mushroom-like galls on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Both of these galls are instigated by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). The gall on the left is the work of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) on the underside of a leaf. This gall looks remarkably like the gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in this photo, but that one grows on Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia) which hosts its own different gall environment. I think the gall on the right is the most striking one I found today - the gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) on a stem. The galls produce "oak honey", which attracts ants and other insects, who in turn keep the predators away from the larvae inside the galls. At least that's the idea, see this photo. But I didn't see any insect activity around this gall. It looks fresh enough, but maybe it's not quite ready to release honey? I know where it is, I'll keep an eye on it. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Antron douglasii; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Disholcaspis prehensa
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Antron douglasii; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Disholcaspis prehensa

File.Name: h808c_h808-024.jpg
Notes: 36285468982 Here are two different fuzzy galls under the leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Both of these galls are instigated by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). On the left is a single gall of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus). These remarkable galls typically form colonies that grow together to make a furry mass. They covered some trees last year, see this photo, but they've been very scarce this year. It seems odd to think of galls as having good/bad years ... but they do. I'm not sure about the gall on the right, but maybe it's the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Atrusca trimaculosa). That's cute the way it is cradled by the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Crystalline Gall Wasp; Woollybear Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus crystallinus; Atrusca trimaculosa
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus crystallinus; Atrusca trimaculosa

File.Name: h808d_h808-013.jpg
Notes: 36316406071 Here are two different disk-like galls under the leaves of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Both of these galls are instigated by Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). On the left is the "pinched form" galls of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae). They also occur in open forms that are like pen disks. I'm not sure about the gall on the right, but it may be work of the the Disk Gall Wasp (Andricus parmula) - the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) is similar. This one is glossy and hairless which suggests the former, see Joyce Gross's comment to this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Disk Gall Wasp; Saucer Gall Wasp

File.Name: h809a_h809-029.jpg
Notes:
36075253740 This is a nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae) stretched across the opposite leaves of a flowering mint plant in he garden. The nightly music jam has started ... but this one is still underage until it goes through its final molt. If you look close, you can see that it's missing its left hind leg - pretty important for a cricket. I've always wondered if they will get that leg back on their next molt?? (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2017)

The weather remains pretty static - "quiet and persistent" as the NWS forecast discussion had it yesterday. On the other hand, it's not too hot or breezy. What's not to like?

File.Name: h809b_h809-001.jpg
Notes: 36426226706 I found this tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods this morning. It's not quite 1/4 inch long. Maybe this is the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) like I showed in this photo last week? It looks like it's speeding along, but these critters actually move very slow - a good thing in the low light this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2017)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: h809c_h808-030.jpg
Notes: 36425830566 While looking for galls yesterday, I found this odd pile of little rocks in a shrub of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It was about head high in the shrub, cemented together into a solid mass. This might be the work of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, maybe genus Dianthidium or Anthidiellum - but it's so big! I occasionally find small nests that look like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, see this photo - but I've never found anything this big, maybe spanning two inches along the branch. I'm not sure what it is. That's a lot of rocks to carry up to this branch! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2017)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Megachilidae; Dianthidium; Anthidiellum

File.Name: h809d_h805-007.jpg
Notes: 36074505750 I believe this is a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider on its web which disappears into a tunnel on the left. The spider is actually under a thin sheet of webbing, which accounts for its slightly diaphanous quality. The web is still dusted with ashes from the Whittier fire a few weeks ago. The ashes vanish into the soil if they can get there, but not if they're caught up in a web. I wonder if that effects how many prey the spiders take? Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2017)

File.Name: h810a_h810-026.jpg
Notes:
36445411846 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) working a new summer flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. It seems to be working the wrong end of the flower, but it's doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. If you look close at my photo, you can see that another bee has punched a hole into the flower right below the bee's proboscis. Many insects seem to know this trick, and it's the only way large bee like this could reach the nectar in these flowers that are typically visited by hummingbirds. Look how worn the bee's wings are. The bees fly until they can fly no more. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 August 2017)

The weather remains static - "quiet and persistent" as the NWS forecast discussion had it a couple of days ago. On the other hand, it's not too hot or breezy. What's not to like? (The weather is static - so are my comments!)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: h810b_h810-005.jpg
Notes: 36491323135 Here is another nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in he woods this morning. It's larger and more developed than the one I showed yesterday in this photo, but I guess they will become the same kind of adults. If this one were an adult, I'd guess it was a female full of eggs - it's fat! This photo shows the interesting antennae and impressive spike-like proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2017)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
