File.Name: h810c_h807-040.jpg
Notes: 36491141505 This photo shows the tail end of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working mint flowers in the garden. Actually these butterflies have two rear ends! There's the real one - its abdomen sticking out below the wings. There's also the faux rear end - the painted eye-like spot and tails that give the impression that the head is at the tail. They help the illusion by keeping their hind wings in constant back and forth motion while not in the air. This is the same lady butterfly that I show ovipositing in this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)

File.Name: h810d_h806-010.jpg
Notes: 35656066624 This is the rear end of a handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden - probably genus Villa. It's funny that some insects always turn and face the camera when I get close, but these flies always seem to face away. The result is that I have lots of good butt shots, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2017)

File.Name: h811a_h811-002.jpg
Notes:
36465282406 This is looking close at a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working a new flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. This butterfly may be humble, but it has it's charms! The Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterflies in my [Previous] photo look similar, but they are slightly more ornate. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2017)

It was warmer today, so the weather is not quite static! It's still not too hot or breezy. What's not to like?
ScientificName: Pieris rapae; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Pontia protodice
associatedTaxa: Pieris rapae; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Pontia protodice

File.Name: h811b_h810-034.jpg
Notes: 35702671593 Here are two shots of a mating pair of Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterflies in the mountains. They started on a green stem of Deerweed (Acmispon glaber, Fabaceae) along Camino Cielo road, then moved to a weedy grass stem, and then took off completely when a truck came along that was too close. (I was partially laying in the road to get the photos - thankfully it wasn't too close!) At first I assumed these butterflies were the Cabbage White (Pieris rapae), but their markings are different. The female (below) is especially attractive with that yellow edging that follows her wing veins. She looks almost like the more-ornate Becker's White (Pontia beckerii) - see this photo. One obscure difference is that in Becker's White the double black bar on the forewing bends towards the wing tips, whereas in the Checkered White it bends towards the base of the wing. Really! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 August 2017)
CommonNames: Checkered White; Deerweed; Cabbage White; Becker's White
ScientificName: Pontia protodice; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; too; Pieris rapae; Pontia beckerii
associatedTaxa: Pontia protodice; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; too; Pieris rapae; Pontia beckerii

File.Name: h811c_h807-019.jpg
Notes: 35702297623 This is another little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. It's a handsome fly with those desert colors and patterned wings. I didn't recognize the fly at first, but then I noticed the twisty veins at tip of its wing which are the signature of the species Dipalta serpentina in the tribe Villini. I have another photo of this kind of fly here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Dipalta serpentina; Villini

File.Name: h811d_h807-010.jpg
Notes: 36510743335 This is a handsome little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working new flowers in the mint patch of the garden - probably genus Villa. I posted a photo of the rear end of a similar fly yesterday. We seem to have several different kinds of these flies in different sizes and slightly different colors - or is that a gender difference? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2017)

File.Name: h812a_h812-016.jpg
Notes:
36134864050 Be careful little bees - some of those flowers have claws!

I found this little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for prey on a flower cluster in the mint patch of the garden today. I investigated why it has its wings open, and it has a mint flower stuck inside where its wings should close. I think it will drop away when the critter next takes wing, so no surgery required. This isn't classic camouflage, but I bet it works just as well! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2017)

It was another warm day with not much breeze. What's not to like?
(The weather is static - so are my comments!)

File.Name: h812b_h812-046.jpg
Notes: 35722351983 Here are two shots of an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly taken in quick succession on a dry weed stem in the canyon. I'm pretty sure of this ID because the is no buckwheat growing nearby. (See this photo for another male on California Buckwheat that I'm not sure of - they look the same to me.) It's definitely a male as the females are brown above, compare this photo. It's a handsome butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2017)

File.Name: h812c_h811-004.jpg
Notes: 36484840666 This is a close shot of a Grass-Skipper butterfly working a new flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. The butterfly is probably a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). I was waiting for a different photo when this butterfly suddenly appeared way too close, but I got the shot. I got the [Previous] two photos in the same way. Sometimes we do get lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2017)

File.Name: h812d_h810-008.jpg
Notes: 36362672052 This is a close shot of a Duskywing butterfly working new mint flowers in the garden - probably a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius), but it's so worn that it's hard to be sure. That's an old rusty auto wheel in the background - an antique. I was waiting for a different photo when this butterfly suddenly appeared way too close, but I got the shot. I got the [Next] and [Previous] photos in the same way. Sometimes we do get lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2017)

File.Name: h813a_h813-061.jpg
Notes:
36555097405 This fresh-looking Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly is "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path. It looks a lot like the tattered American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly in my [Previous] photo. There are subtle differences in the color patterns, but this lady has four or five eyespots below and lacks the white dot on its forewing that the other one has. I usually have to catch a view below to know what I have. It would have been nice to also find a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) - I didn't, but see this photo from two months ago. This is the first Painted Lady I've seen since early November, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

I stayed in the garden today and got lots of photos close to home. Oh yeah, the weather today was more of the same. It sounds like it might change tomorrow, cooler with maybe some morning fog/drizzle. I'll admit that I've gotten spoiled by this static weather we've been having, pleasant as it is.
CommonNames: Painted Lady; American Lady; West Coast Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Vanessa virginensis; Vanessa annabella

File.Name: h813b_h813-018.jpg
Notes: 36386415242 This somewhat-tattered American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly is "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path. It looks a lot like the not-so-tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in my [Next] photo. There are subtle differences in the color patterns, but this lady has just two eyespots below and almost always has a white dot in the first orange cell on the trailing edge of its forewing. I usually have to catch a view below to know what I have. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

File.Name: h813c_h813-025.jpg
Notes: 36508036836 This Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) butterfly hasn't seen the fate that awaits it. Look at its two front feet and the end of its proboscis ... there is the little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that will grab the butterfly's proboscis with its claws and bring it down. (I'll make a note.) See my [Previous] photo. I didn't realize I had this photo until I saw it on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

File.Name: h813d_h813-027.jpg
Notes:
36554319335 This photo is downright brutal. You might want to skip it.

At first you don't see it ... but look at the butterfly's proboscis! A little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) has this skipper butterfly by the proboscis and is holding on tight while it reels it in. I'm pretty sure the butterfly is a second-brood Umber Skipper (Poanes melane), see my [Next] photo for the moment before it was captured. This might be the same Ambush Bug that I show in my [Previous] photo - it's in the same cluster of new mint flowers in the garden. I tried to get further pictures, but the little predator pulled the butterfly tight against the flowers so that I couldn't see it at all - and this is brutal enough. Like I've said before, these little predators can bring down prey much larger than they are! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Poanes melane

File.Name: h814a_h814-030.jpg
Notes:
36438531841 Here is another handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a flower of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra, Brassicaceae). It's definitely a male as the females are brown above, compare this photo. I got this photo in ranch country along Highway 154 today on my way to do some errands in the Santa Ynez Valley. This plant is a weed, but the butterfly was also working the new flowers of native Spanish Clover (Acmispon americanus, Fabaceae), photo here. There are other natives here too, like the milkweed in my [Previous] photo. It's not really peak butterfly season, but they will be here as long as there are flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2017)

The weather was different today. We didn't have any drizzle, but the marine-layer fog was lying just over the mountains on the ocean side and spilled over a few times. It was definitely cooler, only in the 70s. The fog and drizzle might reach us tomorrow.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Black Mustard; Spanish Clover

File.Name: h814b_h814-026.jpg
Notes: 36576258435 This is a clutch of nymphs of Large Milkweed Bugs (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae) on a seedpod of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). They look like they're made of wax or like Halloween candy! I found a number of these nymphs today but very few adults - see this photo. One of the fascinating things about milkweed plants is how they attract insects that you don't find anywhere else. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2017)
ScientificName: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: h814c_h813-042.jpg
Notes: 35740953184 I believe this is a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) on the mint flowers in the garden. The "double abdomen" of these wasps is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes? They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck! (See this photo.) I haven't found any nests yet this year, but the wasps are out there. I found one on almost the same date last year, see this photo. That one looks subtly different, more yellow and less black. Maybe it's a gender difference? Hah - the wasp is slightly pubescent so it could be a Hairy Potter Wasp! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

File.Name: h814d_h811-009.jpg
Notes: 35734281174 This is looking into a flower of ill-smelling Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing down by the river. The datura flowers were a favorite of the American artist Georgia O'keeffe - see here. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you! The flowers are large and beautiful, but they really do stink. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "High Key" - and I hope it counts as I have not eliminated all shadows and textures. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 August 2017)

File.Name: h815a_h815-001.jpg
Notes:
36429493292 This is a mass of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) foliage engulfing a young native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. Here it is showing some fall colors - in August! The clematis is deciduous which does give the madrone a chance to get some sun in the winter months. Compare this photo taken in October (in the rain!) when the leaves are mostly gone. This is a photo that I couldn't have gotten on a sunny day - too contrasty, too much light and shadow. The morning clouds were perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2017)

It did feel like autumn today. We were in a cloud for much of the day, with only partial clearing in the afternoon. The temperature barely got up to 70∞. This cool weather will probably be short-lived, but it is a nice change after such a long run of hot days.
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: h815b_h815-023.jpg
Notes: 35788437043 This is a fantastic seed head of the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Boraginaceae plant family. See this photo of the flowers that precede it. The calyx lobes of the flower make little cages with the seed pods inside. I have a closer shot of the seeds in this photo from a month ago. I don't think I've ever looked close at these before this year to see how interesting they are. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2017)

File.Name: h815c_h813-052.jpg
Notes: 36551435026 This is a fully-adult Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). It's a male with those broad wings, and these are the ones that sing on late summer and autumn nights. They produce their song by rubbing the ridges of their wings together, see this action shot! I've been hearing them at night, but this is the first one I've found. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: h815d_h809-017.jpg
Notes: 36551188936 I found this large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider sitting by her web which connects a couple of soon-to-be-flowering plants of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). Mugwort does look wind-pollinated, but it also attracts insects - which makes this a good place for a web. Maybe this spider is Araneus andrewsi or some kind of genus Neoscona? I believe these are one the spiders that make large webs under the eaves of our upstairs porch in fall - just in time for Arachtober. What a gorgeous pattern it has on its back. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2017)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus andrewsi; Neoscona
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Araneus andrewsi; Neoscona

File.Name: h816a_h816-001.jpg
Notes:
36619063405 I found both female (top) and male (bottom) Tree Crickets (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) today on different dusty leaves of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I guess from their colors that these are different species, and I know that we have at least two different kinds - a "thermometer cricket" that sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights, and another one that has a steady trill. It's the combination of the two that creates their wonderful song jams all night. Only the males sing, but they sing for the ladies. The males produce their song by rubbing the ridges of their wings together, see this action shot. (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2017)

We were in a cloud all morning, but it did clear up for in the afternoon. The temperature barely got into the 70s again, and it was breezy. This cool weather will probably be short-lived, but it is a nice change after such a long run of hot days - despite the breeze.

File.Name: h816b_h816-013.jpg
Notes: 36222188780 This photo shows where spider webbing comes from - it's a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on it's web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. There's a good view of its spinnerets, the source of all webbing - for spiders at least. The common name comes from the white/silver patch on the spider's top side - not visible here but see this photo. A better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Yeah, this would make a good "butt shot" for Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday, but I have other photos for that. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2017)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: h816c_h814-016.jpg
Notes: 36222084870 This is a mating pair of Small Milkweed Bugs (Lygaeus kalmii, Lygaeidae) on an old flower head of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). This is the conventional position for these true bugs, but compare this photo from two weeks ago! Happy hump day! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2017)

File.Name: h816d_h811-017.jpg
Notes: 36221956380 Here are little mating Datura Beetles (my name) in the insect order Coleoptera on a leaf of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure they are genus Lema in the family Chrysomelidae, maybe the Three-lined Potato Beetle (Lema daturaphila). When you get down in the foliage like this to get a photo, you really notice how ill-smelling the plant is! These are one of those insects that I can count on finding in mating position. I show a another such insect in my [Next] photo. Happy hump day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 August 2017)
CommonNames: Datura Beetles; Toloache; Lema; Three-lined Potato Beetle

File.Name: h817a_h817-046.jpg
Notes:
35830678073 Here are two shots of a striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working non-native mint flowers in the garden this morning. Yep, they have remarkable goggle-like eyes - but what gives with that amazing "third eye" between its two real eyes especially in the photo on the right - the mark of the Illuminati?? I've only found them working flowers, but this discussion at BugGuide suggests that they also collect prey. These wasps are hard to photograph as they really are all-seeing and hard to approach. It's a welcome challenge! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)

It was sunny today, but it was still on the cool side, in the 70s. There was some annoying breeze. I somehow threw out my lower back yesterday. I'm still getting around, but I'm walking easy and not bending over.

File.Name: h817b_h817-016.jpg
Notes: 36470492452 Look under the rear feet of this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) - there's a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that the bee is stepping on! I got this photo on mint flowers in the garden this morning. I was already focused close on the bug when the bee photobombed my picture. I guess this shows that Ambush Bugs don't like Honey Bees as prey. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: h817c_h817-027.jpg
Notes: 35830536213 This is a tough-looking Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. We seem to have both large and small ones, and they all seem to have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. They are red on each end! Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but they are handsome flies - albeit tough and gnarly. This photo shows the reddish butt, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)

File.Name: h817d_h812-001.jpg
Notes: 35830286583 Here is the rear end of the tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) trying to get away from me on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. Hah - it has a face on its butt! We seem to have a few different kinds of the nymphs in the woods: this one has speckled legs and spines on its abdomen, while this one doesn't. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2017)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: h818a_h818-006.jpg
Notes:
36521276601 Here is a Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) waiting for prey on a mint flower in the garden with a "tiny black ant" (my name) on its head - like a pirate's parrot companion! Nah, the ant was just passing by, but it's still a pretty cool shot. These photos show that the predators don't bother with ants, sand wasps (here), and honey bees (here) - but they do bother with skipper butterflies (here)! I wonder how they decide what they can capture - nature or nurture? (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)

It was sunny and warmer today, into the 80s, with some annoying breeze. My back is still bothering me. I'm not stooping down to photograph any grasshoppers, but I'm doing OK sitting in a hard chair by the mint plants.

File.Name: h818b_h818-033.jpg
Notes: 35850180653 Here is a striking Bembecine Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) sharing mint flowers with a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in the garden - though they have different purposes. The bug is waiting for prey and the wasp is getting nectar from the flowers. This is probably the same Ambush Bug that has the skipper butterfly in this photo a few days ago - at least it's in the same flower cluster. See this photo for another insect that these predators don't bother, and see my [Next] photo for another. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)
CommonNames: Bembecine Sand Wasp; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: h818c_h816-023.jpg
Notes: 36263076930 I believe from the antennae and proboscis and curled-under abdomen that this fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera. It's probably subfamily Myopinae and genus Zodion. The fly is about 1190 pixels long ion my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 4.7 mm long - about 3/16 inch. This is one of my favorite groups of flies - see this photo. This one is working flowers in the mint patch of the garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 16 August 2017)

File.Name: h818d_h813-003.jpg
Notes: 36489832982 I believe this odd fly is a Peacock Fly - species Callopistromyia annulipes in the Ulidiidae family of Picture-winged Flies. This one in in the grasp of a tiny spider, barely visible below the fly. It's interesting that there are no records of this fly in California at BugGuide, but here it is. I happened to notice this one in the mint patch of the garden. It's small. I can't see the fly's abdomen in this photo, but it's paddle-like wing is about 755 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 3 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) These flies get their common name from the way they hold their wings up from their body, like a peacock. I don't know anything else about them. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

File.Name: h819a_h819-005.jpg
Notes:
35844086064 This is a gorgeous Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) today. It's really guarding its willow tree by the creek a few feet away. Note that it's missing a bird-size chunk of its left wing. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border - compare this photo. They don't seem to visit flowers, but they just sit and wait - to jump out and chase away any other butterfly that comes close to their willow tree! I see them every time I go by the creek, but I don't often get a chance to photograph them - unless they're busy doing something else, like in this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2017)

It was another sunny but cool day, with some annoying breeze again. My back is still bothering me, but I did my usual walk this morning - without bending over much.
ScientificName: Basilarchia lorquini; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica
associatedTaxa: Basilarchia lorquini; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Adelpha bredowii; californica

File.Name: h819b_h819-014.jpg
Notes: 36632585186 I believe this is a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus, Reduviidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It has a row of four pointed spurs across the back of its thorax, but it's hard to make out in this photo. This might be the adult that goes with the nymph in this photo. The Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) in my [Previous] photo is a cousin. This is the first adult I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2017)
CommonNames: Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: h819c_h819-006.jpg
Notes: 36632344996 I found this adult Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a dusty leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. These are predators, and their common name apparently comes from the size of their typical prey - see this photo. These are our most common little assassins, but we do have others - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2017)
CommonNames: Leafhopper Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: h819d_h819-018.jpg
Notes: 35843480924 This is another remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a flower stalk of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. They seem to like the Basil. I noticed three of the tiny nymphs on this plant today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2017)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: h820a_h820-004.jpg
Notes:
35892504483 This is looking into a broken cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arhizus) "mushroom" on a garden path this morning. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules which are visible in my photo. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. I was (painfully) bending down to get a shot of the "compost fly" in my [Previous] photo, and I must have inadvertently stepped on this one. It looks gooey inside, but it feels quite dry. The whole mushroom looks and feels like a raw potato. There are rumors of it being used as a seasoning and a medicine, though I'm not tempted to try it. It's also said to be mycorrhizal on a wide range of trees - and the closest is a large shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). Mushrooms in August - can't complain, even if it's a weird one! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2017)

We were in a cloud for the first half of the day - the marine-layer fog was over 3,000 feet thick. I tried to drive up into the mountains to get above it - that worked, but the "fog winds" were not conducive to photography. My back is still complaining. The fog is expected to return again tonight - even deeper. That's not a good omen for viewing the eclipse tomorrow.
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: h820b_h820-002.jpg
Notes:
35892220133 This is another large Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a garden path this morning. It's maybe 3/4 inch long. These flies have antennae like an old-fashioned TV set and wonderful patterned eyes. (It was gloomy with marine-layer fog when I got this photo - the eyes look better with some sunlight.) These are our "compost flies" - see this photo of a maggot in our compost bin. They might actually do some good in the compost bin, see the article at Wikipedia. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2017)

(This fly has an odd circle of bumps on the vertex of its head, where the simple eyes (ocelli) should be. I'll make a note. I wonder what it is??)
Locality: This fly has an odd circle of bumps on the vertex of its head

File.Name: h820c_h817-023.jpg
Notes: 36563401381 This is a small net-winged Planthopper (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I'm pretty sure it's in the genus Neaethus of the family Tropiduchidae. This is the first one I've noticed this year. They're small, less than 1/4 inch long. Julie says they look like cartoon insects. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)

File.Name: h820d_h817-034.jpg
Notes: 36701349895 I believe this is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes) in the group of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera ). It's perched on a branch of a Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae) shrub. You can see just a hint of the brilliant red and blue colors on its rear legs - see this photo for a better view. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2017)
CommonNames: Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper; Spur-throated Grasshoppers; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: h821a_h821-014.jpg
Notes:
36585747561 This is a handsome Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) butterfly resting on a flower cluster in the mint patch of the garden. These butterflies are are quite plain below - see this photo. This photo shows that they are more interesting above. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2017)

The eclipse happened this morning, and I even got a photo through the clouds! It was curious that the traffic on the highway was terrible - and the internet traffic was also terrible. Was everyone on the mountain trying to stream the eclipse at the same time?

File.Name: h821b_h813-066.jpg
Notes: 36724576015 This is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. It's working mint flowers in the garden. It does have a bristly abdomen like many other tachinids. I have another recent photo here. I got this photo a week ago. I try to keep these photos more current, but sometimes I fall behind and don't want to lose a good shot. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2017)

File.Name: h821c_h821-008.jpg
Notes: 36328665050 I did my duty and got a photo of the eclipse this morning. I almost missed it as it was quite overcast and I was debating whether to try to drive up above the fog. But then the clouds started to clear just enough to make it out, and I got my photo. You can't see the fog at all, with the result that it's kind of a boring shot - too much like a corporate logo. I really wanted to get "eclipse shadows" like this photo and this photo, but it was too foggy for good shadows. We should have been in Oregon to see the totality - my kids went camping near John Day for the occasion, perfect! But we're already heading up there in mid-September, and two trips are just too much. Our eclipse here at home was said to be 63% of complete, good enough. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2017)

File.Name: h821d_h818-003.jpg
Notes: 36550531722 This is looking edge-on at a slice of very moldy bread. It's so moldy that you can hardly tell that it's bread, but it is. This isn't just kitchen mold - I dug this slice out of the compost bin so it's had some time to ripen in a moist and dark place. So many colors - yellow, cyan, white, black ... I'm sure these fungi all have names like Rhizopus, Penicillium, Aspergillus, and Fusarium, some of which sound like the antibiotics they give rise to. I won't try to identify these here, but it's interesting to see them all at once. Bread does indeed support life! This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Bread". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2017)

File.Name: h822a_h822-048.jpg
Notes:
35937181373 Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. Follow its proboscis - what perfect aim it has! The shrub has really finished flowering, but it continues to put our occasional clusters of flowers that the butterflies find irresistible. Yeah, my photo is a bit over-exposed - or is that just dramatic lighting? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2017)

The fog left us alone this morning, and it was warmer - in the mid-80s. It might get hot again over the next couple of days. My back seems to be getting worse. I did an easy walk today, but I didn't bend down at all. I wish I knew what I did to bring this on.
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae

File.Name: h822b_h822-028.jpg
Notes: 36746218015 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata that I found by our summer-dry creek today on a dead plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). It looks like a damselfly, but perches with wings open like a proper dragonfly. I guess this one is the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica), but I don't really know. I reckon this one is a male with those "claspers" at the end of its abdomen. Great eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2017)
ScientificName: Lestidae; Odonata; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Archilestes californica
associatedTaxa: Lestidae; Odonata; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Archilestes californica

File.Name: h822c_h822-032.jpg
Notes: 36746033005 This is a little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) carefully deconstructing a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Look how it's eating just the top layer of the leaf and the cell contents inside. We seem to have a number of critters that skeletonize the oak leaves, see this photo and this photo where the agent appears to be a fungus. This photo looks a bit fuzzy because the caterpillar is under a layer of webbing. I'll try to get another photo that shows the webbing better for Web Wednesday tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2017)

File.Name: h822d_h822-023.jpg
Notes: 36349778840 This is a summer-flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - actually showing some flowers. The flower petals are usually hidden in the bracts, but sometimes they are visible like here. It looks like it should be a very "buggy" plant - and sometimes it is, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2017)

File.Name: h823a_h823-009.jpg
Notes:
36597750192 I found this cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a ripening fruit in the cactus patch of the garden today. It's less than 1/4 inch long. I'm pretty sure it's genus Phidippus, maybe an immature Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni)? What amazing eyes they have - all eight of them, four on each side. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

Nice weather today, but my back is still complaining. It was another day without bending down.

File.Name: h823b_h823-039.jpg
Notes: 36767453835 This is the scene of a struggle on the web of an Orb Weaver spider (Araneidae) that I show in my [Previous] photo. Some critter met its end here, maybe a skipper butterfly judging from the bits left behind. Spider webs are interesting by themselves, but it's important to remember that the whole point of webs is to capture insects for prey. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h823b_h823-041.jpg
Notes: 36767260355 This is a fairly large orb web of an Orb Weaver spider in the family Araneidae. The web is two feet or more in diameter. I guess it belongs to a large spider in the genus Araneus or Neoscona, but I couldn't find the spider. They can hide pretty well. If you look close, you can see a place in the center-left about halfway out from the center that is rough with debris. That's what I show in my [Next] photo. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h823d_h811-026.jpg
Notes: 36597076982 Here are more mating Datura Beetles (my name) in the insect order Coleoptera on a large white flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure they are genus Lema in the family Chrysomelidae, probably the Three-lined Potato Beetle (Lema daturaphila). I posted a different photo of these mating beetles last week. Well I wanted a photo for the Insect Hump Day group, I didn't have one, and I never got around to posting this one, so here it is. Happy hump day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 August 2017)
CommonNames: Datura Beetles; Toloache; Lema; Three-lined Potato Beetle
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Datura wrightii; Solanaceae; Chrysomelidae; Lema daturaphila
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Datura wrightii; Solanaceae; Chrysomelidae; Lema daturaphila

File.Name: h824a_h824-010.jpg
Notes:
35977694663 Hah - I first thought this shiny black bug was a cockroach! After looking at BugGuide, I believe it is the nymph of a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae) in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - almost certainly Largus californicus. Compare this photo at BugGuide. The adults look quite different with more orange of their dull bodies, but if you compare this photo of an adult you can see a certain similarity. I found this one on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that is getting ready to flower. I reckon many critters in the woods are waiting for these shrubs to flower. Me too. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2017)

My back might be feeling better today, but I don't want to jinx it so I won't mention it. It was almost cool today with highs in the 70s. We did have some marine layer fog this morning that spilled over the mountains. The fog didn't last, but it stayed on the cool side.
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: h824b_h823-002.jpg
Notes: 36738970746 A yellow insect on a yellow flower is just too much yellow! I found this true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. Maybe this is genus Harmostes, a Scentless Plant Bug in the family Rhopalidae? Compare this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h824c_h823-031.jpg
Notes: 36389219100 I believe this photo shows the rear end of a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) on the mint flowers in the garden. The "double abdomen" of these wasps is distinctive. Maybe it's genus Eumenes? They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck! (See this photo.) I haven't found any nests yet this year, but the wasps are out there. I have another photo of these wasps that shows a little more of it. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h824d_h823-027.jpg
Notes: 36615927092 Here is another shot of the cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a ripening cactus fruit in the garden, see my [Previous] photo. I'm pretty sure it's genus Phidippus, maybe an immature Johnson Jumper (Phidippus johnsoni). One oddity is that it has an orange ring around the pedicel - that narrow place between the cephalothorax and the abdomen. I'm sorry it's not better imaged, but I wonder if it's mites? (I'll add a note.) This is also a pretty good shot of the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h825a_h825-008.jpg
Notes:
36409435010 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) barging into a scene with a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) and a little caterpillar of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on mint flowers in the garden. I was already getting photos of the Ambush Bug and the caterpillar - see my [Next] photo. Then the bee crashed the party and photobombed me. The wonder is that everything is in reasonably sharp focus! If you look close, you can see that the bee has its proboscis down inside a mint flower - displacement behavior? (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)

My back is feeling better today, but I don't want to hurry it - I'm mostly sitting in a straight-back chair in the garden. It was warmer today, in the high 80s. It will get downright hot over the weekend. I'm afraid that the lovely cool weather we've been enjoying is over.
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Pacific Ambush Bug; Gray Hairstreak
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Strymon melinus
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Strymon melinus

File.Name: h825b_h825-006.jpg
Notes: 35997029493 Here is a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on mint flowers in the garden. It's contemplating a little caterpillar of what I'm pretty sure is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly - with those chevron patterns of its back and general hairiness. I kept an eye on these two critters for an hour or more, and the predator never made a move towards the caterpillar. See this photo for more critters that they do not regard as food. Just after I got this photo, a Honey Bee decided to crash the party - see my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)
ScientificName: Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Strymon melinus

File.Name: h825c_h825-021.jpg
Notes: 35971500154 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden, probably genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane. The fly is about 1170 pixels long ion my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 4.6 mm long - about 3/16 inch. The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. BugGuide reports what I've seen, that the flies will often bob up and down above a flower prior landing to nectar. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)

File.Name: h825d_h824-016.jpg
Notes: 36635174632 I was focused on these brand new flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family when this little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) flew into my photo. Photobombed again! I believe the fly is genus Hemipenthes, possibly the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa), though there are other species that are very similar. The flowers are interesting, and this is almost the first flower I've seen this year. The plants are just starting to flower in our summer-dry mountains when most other flowers are long gone. These late-season flowers are important in the mountains to keep the pollinators alive for next year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2017)
CommonNames: California Aster; Bee Fly; Sinuous Bee Fly
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hemipenthes; Hemipenthes sinuosa
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hemipenthes; Hemipenthes sinuosa

File.Name: h826a_h826-015.jpg
Notes:
35994379284 Here is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) growing in the canyon. Most of these flowers are done for the year except for this one patch in a shady place in the canyon - and the butterflies have found it. These fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture the fantastic "dogface pattern" on their upper wings. I did get a photo today, but I'm not sure if it's good enough to post. See this photo from last year for a good try. These butterflies seem scarce this year, but maybe they're just starting a new brood? (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2017)

It was hot today, and it's going to be hotter tomorrow - 90s today and triple-digits for the next few days. We've been under a NWS Heat Advisory today followed by an Excessive Heat Warning though Wednesday. It's curious that these warning go right to the coast, something that I don't recall seeing before. Apparently the high pressure that's responsible for this heat wave is also responsible for stalling the hurricane in Texas causing lots of problems, see the discussion at the California Weather Blog.
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: h826b_h826-007.jpg
Notes: 36658175682 This is a fresh-looking Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new non-native flowers in the mint patch of the garden. I haven't seen these for a few weeks, and I guess this represents a new hatch of these multi-brooded butterflies. The mint patch sure is a food source for a lot of different insects! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2017)

File.Name: h826c_h826-006.jpg
Notes: 36658033712 This is another remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a flower stalk of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's thinking about jumping the gap to another stem. I never did see it jump, so I don't know if it used its long hind legs. If you compare te different nymph in this photo, you can see that they are different colors - maybe different instars or stages of their molt? These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They always remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2017)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: h826d_h825-022.jpg
Notes: 36657882592 Here are two more shots of the same tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden that I showed yesterday, here. The fly is probably genus Geron, which shares a root with the word "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane. The mint flower on the right makes a good ruler for the fly! The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)

File.Name: h827a_h827-006.jpg
Notes:
36684396892 This is another remarkable early-stage nymph of a Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's munching a stamen of the flower. If you compare the different nymph in this photo, you can see that they have different colors - maybe different instars or stages of their molt? These little nymphs are a favorite of mine. They always remind me of little ponies decked out with jeweled finery. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)

I reckon it was too hot for most insects today - and it was definitely too hot for insect photographers. It's a chance to show a few recent photos I don't want to forget about. We got up to 100∞ on the upstairs porch and 103.6∞ on a neighbor's weather station (here). Yipes - it was 109∞ down by the river today, and tomorrow is expected to be hotter! We have heat, but flooding would be much worse. I wish you well in Texas.
Locality: here). Yipes - it was 109∞ down by the river today

File.Name: h827b_h825-044.jpg
Notes: 36715337341 I found this lobster-colored true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. Checking at BugGuide, it might well be genus Arhyssus, a Scentless Plant Bug in the family Rhopalidae. Compare this photo at BugGuide. If this is right, then it's a plant bug, but not a Miridae. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2017)

File.Name: h827c_h823-015.jpg
Notes: 36683532092 This is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) taking pollen from a stamen of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. There are several different kinds of these little black bees so I won't try to guess what this one is. It's small, less than 1/4 inch long, and it does have pollen-collecting hairs on its rear legs. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2017)

File.Name: h827d_h822-005.jpg
Notes: 36457764020 This is an interesting little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden. It has been lapping pollen from the stamens and is about to go for another. This fly has Popeye-like enlarged legs! I believe it is genus Syritta or Tropidia in the subfamily Eristalinae. See this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2017)

File.Name: h828a_h828-015.jpg
Notes:
36710023922 This is a little White Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus albescens or P. communis) butterfly perched for a moment on mint leaves in the garden. They aren't especially common up here in the mountains, but I find few every year. This one i quite dark. They seem to be quite variable in the amount of darkness they have - or is it a gender thing? (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2017)

It's too damn hot ... listen. I reckon it was too damn hot for most insects today - and it was certainly too hot for insect photographers. It was another day in the 100s - up to 105.4∞ according to a neighbors weather station. It was also unnaturally calm today, almost creepy. It could be worse - like Texas right now!

File.Name: h828b_h826-012.jpg
Notes:
36484373620 Here is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly giving a peek at the dogface pattern on its upper wings. The butterfly is working new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca, Lamiaceae) growing in the canyon. Most of these flowers are done for the year except for this one patch in a shady place in the canyon - and the butterflies have found it. I have another photo of the same butterfly in here. These fine butterflies always close their wings at rest, so it's a challenge to capture their upper wings. This is our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2017)

(Hah - I just noticed a tiny wild bee in the air just to the right of the flower the butterfly is working!)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; wild bee

File.Name: h828c_h828-004.jpg
Notes: 36709216542 Here is the whole fossilized whale vertebra that I showed up close in my [Previous] photo for the Macro Mondays group. That was a small area about an inch in size on the top-right, I'll add a note. The ruler in this photo is 6 inches. The whole fossil is about 8x5x5 inches. I call it a whale - at least it's some kind of large marine mammal. You can see the matrix of the bone, transformed into dense crystalline stone. As I recall, I collected this fossil a couple of decades ago near Point Conception in Santa Barbara County. It's probably of Miocene age. I have several dozen of these fossils that I have collected over the years. Most of them are now happily lining garden beds. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2017)

File.Name: h828d_h824-001.jpg
Notes: 36732679861 This is looking close at the end-grain of a fossilized whale vertebra found locally - at least it's some kind of marine mammal. I believe this photo shows the matrix of the bone, transformed into tiny geodes. This whole scene is less than one inch across. I'll post a photo of the whole bone as my [Next] photo, later today. As I recall, I collected this fossil a couple of decades ago near Point Conception in Santa Barbara County - but it's been a few years! It's probably of Miocene age. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Member's Choice: Abstract Macro". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2017)

File.Name: h829a_h829-003.jpg
Notes:
36766204541 Here is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from damp soil on a garden path. As far as I know, these butterflies never feed, but they do come to take moisture. I guess they emerge with full bellies and fly until they're empty. They are our most common butterflies at home - after the little skippers. Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterflies look similar, but the orange on their wing tips goes right to the edge, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2017)

Hot weather continues - I saw 104∞ on the upstairs porch, and a neighbor's weather station at Weather Underground recorded 107∞ today. That's hot. It's interesting that that same neighbor's weather station had us over 100∞ from 9 to 5! We're under a NWS Excessive Heat Warning through Friday evening - and it looks just as hot going into next week.

File.Name: h829b_h829-015.jpg
Notes: 36905579235 I was focused on the Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) below this seed head of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Boraginaceae plant family. Then I noticed the little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) walking along the top of the plant in an unexpected two-fer! I have more photos of the interesting seed heads here and here. I like how both insects and the plant have autumn colors - the color of ripeness. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2017)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Caterpillar Phacelia; Stilt Bug
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Boraginaceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: h829c_h829-006.jpg
Notes: 36905413475 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flowering mint plant in the garden. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa. It's a cute little predator, only about 1/8 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2017)

File.Name: h829d_h829-012.jpg
Notes: 36905243645 Here are handsome multicolored berries of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. There are a pair of large seeds inside each berry that resemble coffee beans and give the shrub it's name - see this photo. The berries start green, then turn yellow, then red, and finally black when they are dead ripe. Birds love the ripe ones, but they usually wait until their time comes. I've never actually tried eating the ripe berries, as this plant is related to native Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2017)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus purshiana

File.Name: h830a_h830-011.jpg
Notes:
36757741262 This is a camera-shy Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly perched on a leaf in the garden. I've been seeing this butterfly in the garden for the last couple of mornings nectaring on the mint flowers, but it disappears as soon as I get close. It's a handsome butterfly that looks newly emerged. I hope I can get a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2017)

Hot weather continues - we had 104∞ on the upstairs porch, and a neighbor's weather station at Weather Underground recorded 107∞ today. It's interesting that that same neighbor's weather station had us over 100∞ from 9 to 5 again! We're under a NWS Excessive Heat Warning through Friday evening - and it looks just as hot going into next week. I'm getting tired of this constant heat - but it's better than flooding!

File.Name: h830b_h830-018.jpg
Notes: 36757536682 This is looking at the upper side of a fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider. This is a different spider from my [Previous] photo, though it's perched on the same California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) shrubs by the mailbox. (If you look close, you can see that they have slightly different patterns.) This one was off to the side of its web near one of the support strands. Yep, it's got a scary face on its abdomen like so many of these spiders that sit on their webs all day. It's made even better by the dreadlock-like "horns" around its abdomen. Now why would a spider have a face on its butt that looks scary to us humans?? (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2017)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: h830c_h830-021.jpg
Notes: 36532148100 This is a fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on its web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. The common name comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. You can also see two sections of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white zig-zag webs below. I've now found two of these spiders in the buckwheat, see my [Next] photo. I hope they stick around until October/Arachober like one did last year. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2017)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum

File.Name: h830d_h830-024.jpg
Notes: 36928100655 The large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider in my [Next] photo has wrapped its prey in a silken shroud. Maybe the prey is a honey bee, but I can't make out any details. I found this package a little to the right of what my next photo shows. Happy Web Wednesday - though it's not so happy for this bee. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2017)

File.Name: h831a_h831-016.jpg
Notes:
36778561962 Here is a handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a well-exposed flower of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). If you look inside the flower, you can see the butterfly's proboscis extending all the way to the bottom of the flower - but sometimes they stop in the hood, those little cups. See this photo for some more info about the anatomy of these remarkable flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 August 2017)

Hot weather continues - triple digits for most of the day. I'm getting weary of this constant heat.

File.Name: h831b_h831-019.jpg
Notes: 36778359342 This is the rear end of a Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae) on a flower cluster of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). See this photo for two different kinds of milkweed bugs. This is also a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 August 2017)
ScientificName: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: h831c_h831-009.jpg
Notes: 36761896956 This is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting a flower of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. There are several different kinds of these little black bees so I won't try to guess what this one is. It's small, less than 1/4 inch long, and it has a relatively large head. It does have pollen-collecting hairs on its rear legs, though this one only seems interested in nectar. I have seen them taking both nectar and pollen - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2017)

File.Name: h831d_h829-009.jpg
Notes: 36778082172 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera), which as I understand it has a more petioled abdomen than genus Polistes. This one is taking moisture from a wet garden path. The social wasps make their stalked paper nests under the eaves of our porch and roof. It's a paper wasp which looks threatening enough, but I've never been stung. It's a good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2017)

File.Name: h901a_h901-022.jpg
Notes:
36798191762 Here is a tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a stick in the woods. The nymph isn't much more than 1/4 inch long, though its antennae and legs make it look longer. I first found it and had to chase it for a while before I got the photo. This photo gives a good view of its mighty proboscis. It was fortunately above the retaining wall at home so I got the photo without destroying my back! This might be the nymph of a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus) because it doesn't have the usual spines of the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) nymphs. See this photo and this photo. It's not autumn yet, but it is the right month - and I like the autumnal colors of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2017)

Hot weather continues - but we got a break today. Monsoon clouds moved in during the afternoon, and we actually got a few drops of real rain! Thunder too, though I fortunately didn't see any lightning. The result was that the temperature started in the 100s, then it dropped into the 90s with the clouds, then it climbed back into the 100s as the clouds passed. It was cooler today, but I think the humidity more than made up for it. I'm getting weary of this constant heat - despite a few welcome drops of monsoonal rain. It actually got to 110∞ by the river today!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Zelus renardii

File.Name: h901b_h901-012.jpg
Notes: 36829192701 First you notice the skipper butterfly on the left that's in focus. Then you look at the other skipper butterfly on the right - but it doesn't look quite right. Then you notice the spider's claws! I believe the spider is a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes, Thomisidae), see my [Previous] photo of the same spider taken yesterday. The butterflies are Woodland Skippers (Ochlodes sylvanoides). The flowers belong to a non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I was focused on the spider when the other butterfly appeared. I swung my camera around and got the photo - though it's squeezed into the right edge of the frame. I wonder what the butterfly on the left thinks about the situation? (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2017)
CommonNames: White-banded Crab Spider; Woodland Skippers; Basil
ScientificName: Misumenoides formosipes; Thomisidae; Ochlodes sylvanoides; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: h901c_h831-004.jpg
Notes: 36572301220 This large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is guarding flowers of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe this is a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes). The Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, but the White-banded Crab Spider has a distinctive white ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes - a feature that the spider in this photo has if you look close. These large spiders are color-shifters, going from white to yellow depending their flowers. This spider has been an effective predator - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2017)

File.Name: h901d_h830-031.jpg
Notes: 36572204580 This is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the Diptera - probably genus Cylindromyia and maybe species Cylindromyia intermedia. It's working mint flowers in the garden. It does have a bristly abdomen like many other tachinids, and it likes to stick it up in the air and shake it around while feeding at the flowers. I have another recent photo here. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2017)

File.Name: h902a_h902-011.jpg
Notes:
36592084850 Here is a remarkable pair of predators apparently coexisting on flowers in the mint patch of the garden - I've never seen them so close together. That's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the left, probably genus Mecaphesa again, and a little Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica, Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the right. Hah - it looks like they're racing down a track, but they're actually quite still. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2017)

All of my photos today feature a crab spider. I figure that's appropriate because it's now September - not autumn yet or Arachtober, but getting close. Predators like these two have the job of picking off all the pollinators before winter comes and kills them anyway.

The season of the predators has begun - but it was still god-awful hot today! It's been very warm at night, 92∞ at 3:00 AM last night. It's hard to sleep. It got up to 109∞ this morning according to a neighbor's weather station ... but then it started to cool down as monsoon clouds started to move in. There's now a 40% chance of monsoon rain on Sunday afternoon and evening with moisture from what's left of Hurricane Lidia down in Baja. The clouds will cool us down some more, so this is hopefully the end of our triple-digit days for a while. Hah - it's already cooled down into the upper 80s this evening. That's the coolest its been for a couple of days!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Pacific Ambush Bug; crab spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Mecaphesa; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Mecaphesa; Phymata pacifica; Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: h902b_h902-003.jpg
Notes: 36848837591 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) on flowers in the mint patch of the garden. The hairs on the spider suggest that it is probably genus Mecaphesa. These spiders are common in our mountains - and so variable that I have no idea how many different species we have. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2017)

File.Name: h902c_h902-024.jpg
Notes: 36801172736 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our canyon today. The spider is posed like the diamond ring effect of the recent eclipse! The composite plants are kind of weedy, mostly growing along roads, photo here - but they are native. This is the first flower I've seen, at least in our canyon. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa because of its hairiness. I believe this one is male with those enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2017)

File.Name: h902d_h902-017.jpg
Notes: 36591485240 Here is a tiny wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying into flowers of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. There are several different kinds of these little black bees so I won't try to guess what this one is. Did you notice the well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding in the flowers? I believe it's a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), see this photo for a better view. Yep, the bee flew into the flower - and the spider didn't strike. Maybe the bee is too small or too hard-shelled for the large spider to bother with? (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2017)
CommonNames: wild bee; Basil; Crab Spider; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Thomisidae; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ocimum basilicum; Lamiaceae; Thomisidae; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: h903a_h903-031.jpg
Notes:
36203615583 Enough spiders! Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden. It's nice that this shrub is starting to flower again for the season - and we never did get around to dead-heading it. There are other insects in the woods, but it's just been too darn hot to go chasing them. And I'm afraid that the insects have taken a real hit from this week of triple-digit days. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2017)

Today started hot, about 95∞ in the morning. Then it cooled down to the 80s. We even got lots of thunder and a bit of rain - an honest 1/100 inch! Temps in the 80s were pleasant today, but there was also 80% humidity - if the neighbor's weather station can be trusted. That's "80 and 80" conditions - it feels hotter than it is. We're actually under a NWS Flash Flood Watch tonight with a 60% chance of more rain.
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Buddlejaceae

File.Name: h903b_h903-017.jpg
Notes: 36176109764 This is looking at the belly of a fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on its web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. The spider did make a move ... but the bee got away. Maybe the spider is just too big for these little bees, though I saw yesterday that it had caught a little Syrphid Fly with no problem. (And then she dropped it as I moved in for a photo.) The spider isn't going hungry - it also captured another skipper butterfly today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2017)

File.Name: h904a_h904-001.jpg
Notes:
36636367210 This large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is trusting to its camouflage under flowers of non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe this is a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes). The Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) is similar, but the White-banded Crab Spider has a distinctive white ridge between its chelicerae and anterior eyes - a feature that the spider in this photo has if you look close. These large spiders are color-shifters, going from white to yellow depending their flowers. This spider has been an effective predator - see this photo. It's been catching something every day - and the skippers butterflies are getting scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2017)

We did get a bit more rain and drizzle since yesterday, but only about 3/100 inch total. It's a bit of a let down because the Santa Barbara harbor got 1/4 inch pf rain - and even powerful thunderstorm winds that brought down trees and knocked out the power - and we got 3/100 inch just a few miles away. Well, it was that kind of storm. There are even reports of 3/4 inch in Cuyama, but sites like that are surrounding sites like us with little or no rain. The real news is that it was cool and cloudy today - only in the 60s. Hah - two days ago it hit 109 degrees - 40∞ higher!

File.Name: h904b_h904-007.jpg
Notes: 36891790711 I found this different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of native <Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. It's rather small, less than 1/2 inch long. I believe it is species Pselliopus spinicollis. I usually find a few of these from February to April, so it's interesting to find one now. I know this isn't the best photo. It was dark and gloomy today with a steady breeze. I increased my aperture and bumped up the ISO - and this was still the best I got. Oh well, it's a record shot of a handsome predator. Note the raindrops - that's a special treat for us in September! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2017)

File.Name: h904c_h901-017.jpg
Notes: 37032524935 Here is a tiny diurnal (daytime) moth (Lepidoptera) - "microlepidoptera" for sure - working mint flowers in the garden. I won't try to guess what it is. Hah, I have enough trouble identifying the large moths! Have a happy Moth Monday. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2017)

File.Name: h904d_h901-003.jpg
Notes: 37025907185 Here is a patchwork of different Crustose Lichens on the bark of a living Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. The scene is about an inch across. Every lichen is a partnership between an algae and a fungus, and that's why I chose them as my photo for the Macro Mondays group with the theme of "Connection" this week. Lichens are connected to a substrate, tree bark in this case, but the lichen itself is a connection between the algae and the fungus that make it up. The orange and white structures in my photo are not the lichen itself, but the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership. Lichens are at the top of my list of organisms I'd like to know more about. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2017)

File.Name: h905a_h905-003.jpg
Notes:
36884696882 Here's another photo of the large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the non-native Basil (Ocimum basilicum, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I believe it's a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes), see this photo. I've been photographing this spider ever day - and why not? It's large and spectacular and easy to find, though it did have to move over today as its current stalk ran out of flowers. This shot is look straight down a flower stem at the spider. If you look close, you can see the spider's fangs at the bottom of its chelicerae - I'll make a note. Compare my [Previous] photo of a different spider, the fangs seem to be a different shape. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2017)

The sun did come out after morning fog today, and it was very nice - in the 80s. I spent some time in the garden with my camera, but then I had things to do in town. I'll be increasing busy over the next few days - until a week from now when we're off to Portland, Oregon on the train. We'll be there until the end of the month. I'll try to keep posting until we leave, but there is a lot to do!

Notes: 36220402394 Here is another Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in with the mint flowers in the garden, probably genus Mecaphesa again. File.Name: h905c_h905-017.jpg
Notes: 37055071505 I believe this is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera on mint flowers in the garden. It's probably subfamily Myopinae and genus Zodion. It's small, only about 5 mm or 3/16 inch long. This is one of my favorite groups of flies - see this photo. The larger ones like this are remarkable wasp mimics. The small ones don't seem to be mimics, but I can recognize them right off by the combination of long antennae, stout proboscis which is bent, and the curled abdomen. I have another recent photo here which show the bent proboscis better. They might be common - but they are too small to see! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2017)

File.Name: h905d_h902-026.jpg
Notes: 36913417841 This is a remarkable golden Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working a new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. Hah - you can see the skinny fly inside that thick fur! I believe the fly is in the genus Lordotus, possibly Lordotus zona. These flies seem to have remarkable sexual dimorphism - the males look completely different, see this photo. I usually find these brilliant flies down by the river, but I have found then up here in the mountains as well, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2017)

File.Name: h906a_h906-018.jpg
Notes:
36679292950 Here is a pollen-dusted Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). The bee has nearly full pollen baskets! The shrub is just starting to flower - I found the first flowers a few days ago on a cloudy day, but I'll call these the first flowers for the record. Coyote Bush is dioecious with separate male and female flowers on separate shrubs. Both shrubs attract many different kinds of insects, especially different kinds of flies - though I didn't notice much diversity today. They pick an important time to flower - now at the end of summer when flowers are scarce. This humble shrub is kind of weedy, favoring disturbed places, but it is actually a pioneer species, one of the first plants to colonize disturbed ecosystems. Please show it some respect! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2017)

Today was a very nice day with temps in the upper 80s and not much breeze. Alas, I'm getting increasingly distracted with things to do before we head off to Oregon in a week, including preparing a newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. File.Name: h906c_h906-034.jpg
Notes: 37075411495 This is looking close at the rear end of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) sitting on her egg case - showing the spider's spinnerets. I can only see four spinnerets, but apparently most spiders (other than Mygalomorphs) have six - four large ones and two small ones that are hard to see. If you look very close, maybe you can see that there are six strands of silk coming from the spider. I found several of these spiders in a growth of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). This photo is substantially cropped. I didn't mean to get this shot, but I wish I had! Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2017)
