File.Name: h906d_h903-024.jpg
Notes: 36904805852 Here is another shot of the fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider on its web in a patch of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. (Those are white buckwheat flowers in the background.) You can see the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white zig-zag webs on the diagonal. It's function is still a subject of debate, see the Wikipedia entry for web decorations. The common name of this spider comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2017)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; stabilimentum

File.Name: h907a_h907-014.jpg
Notes:
36905615666 Here is another nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. It looks like it's made of glass. This one may be the nymph of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) because of the spines on it's abdomen, photo here. It looks fat, and I wonder if it's a female large with eggs even though it's only a late-stage nymph. Uh, is that even possible with incomplete metamorphosis? Anyway, it's a gorgeous portrait. I never get tired of photographing these little predators! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2017)

File.Name: h907c_h907-024.jpg
Notes: 36952054491 I found this female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) butts up in a green cactus fruit in the garden today. I reckon it's trying to hide "Ostrich style" in the belief that if it can't see you then you can't see it. (Of course that doesn't work with nosy photographers!) That red background is another ripe fruit - from last year? This is a female cricket with narrow wings, see this photo for a comparison. Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day - and only the broad-winged males sing. It's a nice shot of the lady's ovipositor. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2017)

File.Name: h907d_h905-037.jpg
Notes: 36696914490 This is a small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) visiting mint flowers in the garden. Sure it's a "butt shot" for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but look closer at the bee's abdomen - it's got a heavy infestation of translucent mites (Acari) lined up like pigs at a trough. I suppose that counts as multiple butt shots. Poor bee. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2017)

File.Name: h908a_h908-004.jpg
Notes:
36943107272 This is a little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). I say "little", but it's actually larger than the usual jumper. It definitely has punk hair on its head! It's not the best photo, but at least the eyes are sharp! It was breezy today, and I was distracted with things I need to do before we leave for Oregon next week - but I saw this spider and couldn't resist trying to get a photo. I did get a shot of the whole spider. It's not quite good enough to post, but I can see that it's maybe a female Pelegrina aeneola? Like I've said, the season of the predators has begun ... see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2017)

Yeah, I'm getting increasingly distracted with things to do before we head off to Oregon next week - but I did get this new photo today!

File.Name: h908c_h906-051.jpg
Notes:
36300196903 This tiny fly (Diptera) is even smaller than the tiny fly in my [Previous] photo! It's working the same new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). The humpbacked shape of the fly is distinctive. I'm pretty sure this is another Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera). It might be in the genus Geron, though it has a much shorter proboscis than our usual species, see this photo. It's smaller too. Compare the fly to the stamen and then see my [Previous] photo or the honey bee in this photo from a few days ago. I also counted pixels. The fly is about 540 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 2.1 mm long - less than 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) It's interesting that the upper half of its eyes seem to have larger lenses that the lower half. At least some dragonflies are like that too, see this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2017)

As Alice Abelia points out below, it may well be that this fly is a Micro Bee Fly in the family Mythicomyiidae, genus Mythicomyia. BugGuide says that they are traditionally treated as Bombyliidae, so I had a decent guess at least.

File.Name: h909d_h903-030.jpg
Notes: 36989757561 I got this photo a few days ago - a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae or Buddlejaceae) in the garden with not enough sunlight. File.Name: h910a_h910-038.jpg
Notes:
36759597170 This is a remarkable male Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a humble but native flower of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The spider is only about 1/10 inch long, but those front legs are much larger - 1/2 inch or more! If you look close at the spider's face, you can see a white ridge between its eyes and mouth parts. That suggests that this is actually a male White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes) - the same kind of large white crab spider I've been photographing in the basil plants in the garden at home! Compare this photo of a female - there is no comparison, except they both have a white ridge under their eyes. This is real sexual dimorphism - meaning that males and females look totally different. See this photo for another example with crab spiders. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2017)

File.Name: h910c_h910-018.jpg
Notes: 36758876140 This might be the smallest spider I've ever noticed - but it's clearly an Orb Weaver spider (Araneidae), both from its shape and the shape of its web right next to it. The web is about two inches across. The spider is about about 375 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 1.5 mm long - about 1/16 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) Small as it is, I suspect that it is a genus Araneus spider - A. bispinosus or A. montereyensis, see this photo of a spider from the same small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the woods. Maybe it's a juvenile, not much more than a spiderling. (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2017)

File.Name: h910d_h910-031.jpg
Notes: 36984840282 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding on her egg case in a flowering plant of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus, Orobanchaceae). If you look close at her spinnerets, maybe you can see that she really has six of them - four larger ones towards her head, bur also a pair of smaller ones behind, the "posterior median spinnerets". Compare this photo. She really is guarding her eggs. I tried nudging her a bit with a stick to better show her back, and the more I nudged the more she hugged her egg sack! I let her be. I hope she's still here when I get back from Oregon - in Arachtober! (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2017)

File.Name: h911a_h908-010.jpg
Notes:
37175986565 Here is one more shot of the rear end of the fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider by our mailbox. This photo show the spider's spinnerets - where spider silk comes from. If you look close, you can see four larger ones towards her head (up) and maybe the base of the smaller "posterior median spinnerets" behind (down). (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2017)

We did have some excitement last night - thunder and lightning and even 1/3 inch of rain! Summer monsoon rain is uncommon in the Santa Barbara mountains, but it does happen. And the newsletter is off to the printer and out of my hands. Just this one photo this evening, I've got packing to do. Julie and I are taking the train up to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to spend some time with our grandkids. This is more of a babysitting trip than a photo trip, but I am taking my camera. I don't expect to post any pictures while I'm gone - it's a vacation! We'll be back on October 1, just in time for the start of Arachtober. I've been getting in the mood lately with lots of spider photos!

File.Name: h911b_h910-012.jpg
Notes: 36998027352 This is an old rusty hook around the old metal rim of some sort of wheel. We found the hook on a chain buried along our dirt driveway many years ago. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Rust". By the way, the gap in the hook is about 1/2 inch - which makes the whole scene about 3 inches across. I figure that's not really macro, but it is just barely legal for the group. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 10 September 2017)

File.Name: ha02a_h930-540.jpg
Notes:
37205493710 This is a tattered California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly that I found last week on Mount Tabor in Portland, Oregon. It's also the only butterfly that I saw in Portland, though I did see a few more after I got this photo. I believe that they migrate up and down the coast. I usually find them at home in the Santa Barbara mountains in mid-winter, for example this photo from last December. This one seems to be taking moisture among cut grass stems. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 September 2017)

We got back from almost three-weeks in Portland, Oregon on the train last night. This was more of a babysitting trip for our grandkids than a photo trip for me, but the kids went to school most days and I went for long walk on nearby Mount Tabor with my camera. The park had a nice "Trees of Mt. Tabor Park" leaflet with a map and all the trees marked with yellow stakes. I took it as a quest to find and photograph all the trees and found all but one! I'm not sure if I'll share all my photos here on Flickr, but it was a fun make-do project.

File.Name: ha02b_h930-561.jpg
Notes: 36753512344 I was surprised by this large Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) on my path on Mount Tabor in Poit'rtland, Oregon. I'm always surprised to find them! Maybe this one is a Chinese Mantis (Tenodera sinensis)? They look so aware! Sure, it's an urban setting. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 September 2017)

File.Name: ha02c_h930-299.jpg
Notes: 36753073814 This critter looks like a wasp, but it has bee-like eyes and hairs on its hind legs. I believe it is a wasp-like Cuckoo Bee in the genus Nomada, Apidae, Hymenoptera. These bees are kleptoparasites which lay their eggs in the nest cells of other bees, primarily genus Andrena. This one is licking nectar from a spent flower of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae). I'm glad is invasive plant is good for something besides covering walls! I found some interesting insects on these flowers during our time in Oregon. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 22 September 2017)
ScientificName: Nomada; Apidae; Hymenoptera; kleptoparasites; Andrena; Hedera helix; Araliaceae
associatedTaxa: Nomada; Apidae; Hymenoptera; kleptoparasites; Andrena; Hedera helix; Araliaceae

File.Name: ha02d_h930-439.jpg
Notes:
36791890563 Here are two shots of a Cross Spider (Araneus diadematus) in the Araneidae family of orb-weaving spiders in Oregon. I'm sure I saw more of these spiders than any others in Portland for the last few weeks - and walked through more of their webs! These spiders are apparently introduced from Europe, see here. They sure have spread across the northern states, though I've never seen one at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. They seem to be quite variable in both color and size. Arachtober 2. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 24 September 2017)

We got back from our trip to Portland, Oregon on the train last night. I even started to upload my photos to find a shot to post for Arachtober 1 ... but then I thought that's nuts! I'll share a few more Oregon spiders over the next few days, and then I'll try to finish Arachtober with October spiders from home.

File.Name: ha03a_ha03-007.jpg
Notes:
36776317954 This is looking closer at the dragonfly in my [Previous] photo, which I believe is a Common Green Darner (Anax junius, Aeshnidae, Odonata). The green color is remarkable, and so is the "bulls eye" pattern on its face in front of its eyes. This one is perched in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree near our summer-dry creek. It's hanging motionless on a cool and overcast autumn day, a nice find on a day that didn't seem promising for any photo. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the whole dragonfly and some info. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2017)

Flickr has been cranky for the last couple of hours, but it seems to be working again now. This was a good day for finding resting dragonflies sitting out a cool autumn day. When it's too cold for active insects, there's always something else to find!
CommonNames: Common Green Darner; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Anax junius; Aeshnidae; Odonata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Anax junius; Aeshnidae; Odonata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ha03b_ha03-016.jpg
Notes: 37227715420 I was staring at this small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree ... and suddenly this beautiful green dragonfly appeared! It's not magic, but the dragonfly was so well camouflaged that I could look right at it without seeing it. It's hanging motionless on a cool and overcast day. I believe it's a Common Green Darner (Anax junius, Aeshnidae, Odonata), but there are other similar species like the Giant Darner (A. walsinghami). It has a remarkable "bulls eye" pattern on its face below its eyes. I don't recall ever seeing one of these in the summer-dry mountains away from any open water, but maybe it's part of a migration? It was a good day for resting dragonflies, see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Common Green Darner; Giant Darner
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Anax junius; Aeshnidae; Odonata; A. walsinghami
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Anax junius; Aeshnidae; Odonata; A. walsinghami

File.Name: ha03c_ha03-010.jpg
Notes: 37483572291 It was cool and overcast today - a real early autumn day. I found this dragonfly motionless at an odd angle in a low shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). The plant looks quite dead after our long dry summer, but it will green up when the rains come. I got this photo shooting almost straight down. (I actually poked the dragonfly after I got my photos just to make sure it wasn't spider-prey - it's not!) I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. With luck, I'll find them all winter long hanging on to plants on cold days. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2017)
ScientificName: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata
associatedTaxa: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata

File.Name: ha03d_h930-029.jpg
Notes: 37483180021 I'm pretty sure this photo shows female (left) and male (right) Common House Spiders (Parasteatoda tepidariorum) in the spider family Theridiidae. You can see an old egg sac behind the female and the enlarged palps of the quite-different male. This photo has a definite sexual tension! I watch them for a while, and the male would advance a bit and then retreat - again and again. I finally left them to their affair. Maybe the male's caution is justified. There is well-known story about spiders killing and eating their mates, and see this photo! I got this photo on an outside wall at my kids' house near Mount Tabor in Portland, Oregon where Julie and I have been babysitting the grandkids for the past few weeks. Arachtober 3. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 16 September 2017)

File.Name: ha04a_ha04-006.jpg
Notes:
36793403404 Here is another nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods today. I'm pretty sure it's another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). Hah, I also found one of these in the ivy patch during our time in Portland, Oregon - they get around! This one has a tiny wasp (Hymenoptera) for prey, probably a Chalcid Wasp (Chalcidoidea) with those enlarged hind legs. It's not a spider like my other photos today, but it is a spider-like predator. That proboscis is a wicked dagger! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2017)

I had my 70th birthday today - old enough in California that I had to actually go to the DMV to renew my driver's license. I passed! On my birthday sixty years ago, Russia launched Sputnik 1 - the first artificial Earth satellite. I remember seeing it pass overhead just after sunset. I heard it go "beep beep beep" in a neighbor's ham radio shack. Ah, to be ten years old on my birthday at the dawn of the space age...
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug; wasp; Chalcid Wasp; Sputnik 1
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus renardii; Hymenoptera; Chalcidoidea
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Zelus renardii; Hymenoptera; Chalcidoidea

File.Name: ha04b_ha04-013.jpg
Notes: 36834027493 I found this spiny spider on a spiny cactus plant today! The plant is a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden, and the large spider is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae). The spider does have chevron markings on its back, so maybe it is Peucetia viridans as opposed to the Peucetia longipalpis in this photo? These spiders are highly variable, especially at mating time - which is now. This one is especially beautiful - jade, porcelain, and carnelian! It's nice to have both Web Wednesday and Arachtober on the same day, spiders galore! Arachtober 4. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2017)
ScientificName: Opuntia; Cactaceae; Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia viridans; Peucetia longipalpis
associatedTaxa: Opuntia; Cactaceae; Peucetia; Oxyopidae; Peucetia viridans; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: ha04c_h930-493.jpg
Notes:
37502846081 Here is a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the funnel opening of its web. It sure looks like it has a prey, but I can't make out what it is. The spider looks like it might be genus Agelenopsis by the eye arrangement, see this useful page at BugGuide on Spider Eye Arrangements. I found this one last week in a patch of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in Portland, Oregon. Happy Web Webnesday! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 September 2017)

Sure, I could have also posted this one to the Arachtober group, but I happened to find the beautiful Lynx spider in my next photo today. It's nice to have both Web Wednesday and Arachtober on the same day, spiders galore!

File.Name: ha04d_h930-518.jpg
Notes: 23650258448 This looks like fireworks! It's a spider on its web in a beam of sunlight in the shady woods on Mount Tabor in Portland, Oregon. I can't remember, but maybe there was a puff of wind or I bumped my tripod with my elbow? This web is the work of a small Cross Spider (Araneus diadematus, Araneidae), see this photo for a less dramatic shot. You can actually see the spider in my photo as that brown blur in the upper left. It's a neat effect that would be worth playing with deliberately to see what you could get. Happy Web Webnesday! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 September 2017)

File.Name: ha05a_ha05-041.jpg
Notes:
37475208996 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a humble but native flower of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The general hairiness of the spider suggests that it is genus Mecaphesa, and the enlarged palps suggest that it is a male. Compare the very different male crab spider in this photo on the same kind of flower! The composite plants are kind of weedy, mostly growing along roads, photo here - but they are native. I don't think insects find them very attractive as a food source, but there must be a few. Arachtober 5. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2017)

It was warmer today, in the upper-70s rather than the 50s like its been all week. We're forecast to be in the 90s tomorrow. That's how it is with our autumn weather. It swings...back and forth!

File.Name: ha05b_ha05-047.jpg
Notes: 36853685873 I found this broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on a fading composite Zinnia flower in the garden today. Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. Hah - this one isn't trying very hard to hide on that dingy flower! See this action shot of a male doing its thing! The females have narrow wings and don't sing, compare this photo. The crickets have been quiet since we got back from Portland a few days ago, but I expect they will pick up the evening music jam again this weekend as the forecast is to warm up. I like the sheen of its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2017)

File.Name: ha05c_h930-457.jpg
Notes: 36853404483 This photo shows the rear end of a striking fly on an Aster flower in a Portland, Oregon garden in the neighborhood near Mount Tabor. I believe the fly is a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), probably genus Helophilus or Parhelophilus in the same Eristalini tribe that includes many more bee-mimic flies in the genus Eristalis. HBBBT! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 24 September 2017)
ScientificName: Aster; Syrphidae; Diptera; Helophilus; Parhelophilus; Eristalini; Eristalis
associatedTaxa: Aster; Syrphidae; Diptera; Helophilus; Parhelophilus; Eristalini; Eristalis

File.Name: ha05d_h930-432.jpg
Notes: 36853160633 This photo shows the rear end of a Potter Wasp aka Mason Wasp (Eumeninae, Vespidea, Hymenoptera) on odd flowers of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in Portland, Oregon. An assortment of wasps, bees, and flies were working the flowers by licking nectar from he base of the flowers - the most insects I saw anywhere during our time in Portland. These wasps have that distinctive "double abdomen". Maybe it's genus Eumenes? They make neat little mud nests mostly on plant stems. We have these wasps at home too, see this photo of maybe the same kind of wasp in our home garden. A couple of years ago, one of them made a nest on the antennae of my truck, see this photo. HBBBT! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 24 September 2017)
CommonNames: Potter Wasp; Mason Wasp; Common Ivy
ScientificName: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Eumenes
associatedTaxa: Eumeninae; Vespidea; Hymenoptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Eumenes

File.Name: ha06a_ha06-004.jpg
Notes:
37510877392 The Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on the cactus plant in our garden became a mama yesterday! I showed her two days ago in this photo. Then she moved from the closer cactus pad to a more distant one that's a pretty long reach for my macro lens, maybe eight feet off the ground. I got the above photo on the left yesterday, then I got the photo on the right this morning. She laid an egg - actually lots of eggs, in a neat silk sac. Look how deflated her abdomen is! It's hard to get any closer in this patch of spiny Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus plants, but I might have to rig up a ladder. Now she will guard her eggs until they hatch - hopefully before the end of the month. Arachtober 6. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2017)

It was warmer today, up to about 90 degrees. Tomorrow will be another warm one, then it is expected to cool down some next week. That's how it is with our autumn weather. It swings...back and forth!

File.Name: ha06b_ha05-014.jpg
Notes: 37541734311 This is a gorgeous fly (Diptera) grooming itself on a leaf of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree near our summer-dry creek. I thought at the time that it's a Golden Dung Fly (Scathophaga stercoraria, Scathophagidae) - or rather I didn't think about it! The antennae are totally wrong for that kind of fly. Now I think it might be a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae. BugGuide has several photos that look like mine, but not identified to species. One possibility is Thereva fucata, but that species doesn't look as yellow/orange as mine, see this photo at BugGuide. Another possibility is Thereva flavipilosa, but there's not much info about it. Whatever it is, it's a handsome fly that I don't think I've seen before. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Golden Dung Fly; Stiletto Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scathophaga stercoraria; Scathophagidae; Therevidae; Thereva fucata; Thereva flavipilosa
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Scathophaga stercoraria; Scathophagidae; Therevidae; Thereva fucata; Thereva flavipilosa

File.Name: ha06c_h930-040.jpg
Notes: 37510318632 This is just a fly - but I think it's a gorgeous portrait. I got this photo in the late afternoon on a gray day with some light rain in Portland, Oregon. The fly was motionless on a leaf, so I set up my tripod and did a long 1/8 second exposure. I guess its a Muscid Fly (Muscidae) or maybe a Root-Maggot Fly (Anthomyiidae). HFDF! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 18 September 2017)

File.Name: ha06d_h930-152.jpg
Notes: 37510060932 I guess this is a Bald-faced Hornet (Dolichovespula maculata, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on odd flowers of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in Portland, Oregon. We don't have these wasps in Southern California, and I didn't recognize it. I thought maybe it was a large bee that's not very hairy. Well, live and learn! These are the wasps you see in cartoons that make the large paper nest up in trees that some cartoon-kid always knock down. The are large and handsome. This one didn't seem aggressive to me - but I didn't knock its nest down either! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 21 September 2017)
ScientificName: Dolichovespula maculata; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae
associatedTaxa: Dolichovespula maculata; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae

File.Name: ha07a_ha07-019.jpg
Notes:
37301960670 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). Coyote Bush is dioecious with separate male and female flowers on separate shrubs. These flowers are male - with stamens. It's interesting that the spider has a little dollop of pollen on the end of its palp. I've seen this before, e.g. here and here - bait? Arachtober 7. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2017)

Today was another nice day with highs up to about 90 degrees. Tomorrow is expected to be cooler and breezier. At least we don't get those nasty Santa Ana winds like locations south and east of us. That's because we don't have a direct path to the desert in Santa Barbara County. We do get more than our share of wind, especially the Sundowner Winds, but not this time.

File.Name: ha07b_ha07-001.jpg
Notes: 37528607272 I believe this is a single stalked egg of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae,Neuroptera) insect on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that has been skeletonized by some fungus or insect larva. Presumably this reduces cannibalism among newly hatched siblings, but I usually find them as just single eggs like this one, so that wouldn't apply. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2017)

File.Name: ha07c_ha06-028.jpg
Notes: 36890405693 This is a fasciated stem of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family. The stem is flat like a ribbon, but the plant is still flowering beyond. You can see normal round stems in the background. Many plants get fasciated, some often and others (like this one) rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. They are unusual and worth collecting - in photos at least. There is even a low-traffic Flickr group on Fascinating Fasciations to which I'll add this one. Even without the fasciation, it's an interesting native plant that picks the driest time of year to flower - unlike its wetland kin. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2017)

File.Name: ha07d_ha06-018.jpg
Notes: 37511865556 This is an odd cap of a Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius aka P. arhizus) "mushroom" in the woods along West Camino Cielo road yesterday. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - the fungus gets its scientific name from the pea-shaped peridioles or spore capsules which are somewhat visible in my photo - and more so in this recent photo of a fresher specimen. They very slowly break down to release the spores. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. It's not really fleshy, but it is a fungus at an odd time of year. Our real mushroom season won't begin until next month when the real rains hopefully start. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2017)
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; P. arhizus; peridioles; tinctorus

File.Name: ha08a_ha08-002.jpg
Notes:
36913305883 Here is another little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the tunnel of the funnel-opening of its web. Hah - this little spider is on the window sill in our bathroom, right next to the throne! It's been here for months catching the occasional fly and not bothering us. Its web comes and goes depending on when we last opened the window, which disturbs it. Usually it's hiding down in the hole during the day - I got lucky this morning! Arachtober 8. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2017)

Is it my imagination or is Flickr suddenly running a sharpening filter on all photos? Some of my photos now look over-sharpened. I don't like it. Today was indeed 15+ degrees cooler than yesterday, in the low 70s instead of the 90s. Like I've said, that's the way it is with our autumn weather - it swings...back and forth!

File.Name: ha08b_ha08-031.jpg
Notes: 36872312724 I don't recognize this Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly which I found today perched on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Is it just a very worn Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides)? I have a photo of a fresh specimen here. At first the butterflies look completely different - but there is a correlation between the colored areas. Or is it something else? (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2017)
ScientificName: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperiinae; Hesperiidae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: ha08c_ha08-021.jpg
Notes: 37550634152 Here are ripening fruit of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, with lots of bay leaves. There are also new flower buds on the same stem, though they won't open until next January. The trees are related to avocados, and you can see the resemblance in the fruit. These green fruit will turn black as they ripen and reveal the ripe bay nuts inside. Have you ever accidentally eaten the "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so - and the fruit are even more so than the leaves. But hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when dark roasted - rich, almost like bitter chocolate or coffee. They are really good, habit forming. This might be a decent year for them after several failing drought years. I'll post a recipe here when the time comes, but if you can't wait, see this YouTube video! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2017)

File.Name: ha08d_ha07-015.jpg
Notes:
37550503402 Seeds of life...

This is looking into a ripe seedpod of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). I opened it up a bit to let the light in and make a natural cornucopia. There is new life in there! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2017)

File.Name: ha09a_ha09-032.jpg
Notes:
23753252618 This is a different Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by the creek. Compare my photo of the more colorful fly in this photo from a few days ago in the same tree. There are similarities but also differences - e.g. the spotted wings of this fly. This one seems to match the species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I believe from the separated eyes that this is a female. I wonder what those shinny black things are between its eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)

It was a nice enough day today, not too hot - but the big news is the terrible fires north of San Francisco. I wish you well. "There but for fortune...
ScientificName: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata

File.Name: ha09b_ha09-025.jpg
Notes:
37573345582 This is a fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider wrapping its prey on its web. Its prey seems to be a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe genus Chinavia or Thyanta, see this photo. I wonder how the spider caught it? You can see patches of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white zig-zag webs on the diagonal. The common name of this spider comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Look close - this one seems to have a safety line! Arachtober 9. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)

I was lucky to find this spider today. I would normally have spiders located before Arachtober, but we were in Portland, Oregon this year. It's pretty remarkable how you can be standing right in front of a spider web and not see it until ... *wham* there it is! The spiders I had staked out before we left (like this one) are gone now. This is a new one.
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Thyanta; stabilimentum
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Thyanta; stabilimentum

File.Name: ha09c_ha09-022.jpg
Notes: 36894854074 Hah - this photo is kind of a botanical joke. A new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family has somehow grown into a seedpod of Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus, Liliaceae). Of course he seedpod should be full of seeds not flowers, see this photo from yesterday. That's the joke. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: ha09d_ha06-022.jpg
Notes: 37344228580 Here is a plump green acorn on a shrub of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's lit by natural light from the side, so I guess this counts as side lighting for the Macro Mondays group this week with the theme of "sidelit". It's interesting that the terminator line of the acorn - the line that separates the illuminated side and the shadow side - isn't sharper than it is. Is this the effect of light scattering by the atmosphere? These shrubs seem to be having a pretty good year for acorns, good for wildlife. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2017)

File.Name: ha10a_ha10-012.jpg
Notes:
23773739418 This is looking close at an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn. They get everywhere, including the pistil of the flower. It does the soul some good to find these flowers at the very end of our season. I reckon they are important for keeping the hummingbirds alive during this harsh time of year - at least until the manzanita really kicks in. These plants have been flowering for a while, but this is their main time - right now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2017)

It was another pleasant autumn day, but it was windy. It was curious that the wind was from the southeast whereas our usual wind is from the northwest. The temperature is mild, but we're under a NWS Fire Weather Watch later this week because of the wind.

File.Name: ha10b_ha10-024.jpg
Notes: 36955735563 Here are - already! - the first new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today - well before the first winter rains. This particular shrub seems to flower very early most years, see this photo from 25 October 2015. Plants are individuals, and some just are early. The rest of these shrubs won't flower until sometime next spring. See my [Previous] photo for another early bloomer. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2017)

File.Name: ha10c_ha10-009.jpg
Notes: 23773271448 Here is the first new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along East Camino Cielo road today - well before the first winter rains. This one particular shrub is early-flowering every year, see this photo from 6 November 2016 and this photo from 8 October 2015. The shrub just flowers very early, though most flowers won't appear until after the rains come. Hah - I heard a hummingbird nearby - maybe already claiming the early flowers, see this photo! I'll have to check the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) which may also be flowering, and see my [Next] photo for another early bloomer. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 October 2017)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; before; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; before; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: ha10d_h930-502.jpg
Notes: 37577454396 This cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has climbed up onto a spent flower of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in Portland, Oregon last month. Hah - see this photo and this photo to get a sense of scale! Is the spider missing one of its palps or is it just the angle of the shot? I got this photo during our recent trip to Oregon. I'm posting it today in support of International Jumping Spider Day on 10/10. Really! Arachtober 10. (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 27 September 2017)

File.Name: ha11a_ha11-008.jpg
Notes:
23792396748 I found another broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) sprawled along a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. As wide as this photo is, I still had to cut off the long antennae! Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day, but only the males sing. See this action shot of a male doing its thing! The females have narrow wings and don't sing, compare this photo. The crickets have been pretty quiet with the cooler weather lately, but they are still out there. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2017)

It was another pleasant autumn day, but it was windy again in the morning - when I was out getting my photos. The wind was from the southeast again whereas our usual wind is from the northwest. The temperature is still mild, but we're under a NWS Fire Weather Watch starting tomorrow evening because of the wind.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: ha11b_ha11-027.jpg
Notes: 37386981060 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting with open arms for prey on a new autumn flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers are smaller than garden asters, only about 3/8 inch across. The general hairiness of the spider suggests that it is genus Mecaphesa - again. Arachtober 11. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2017)

File.Name: ha11c_ha10-047.jpg
Notes: 23791947988 This is a large spider web in the shady woods at home that's catching a beam of sunlight. If you follow the bright strand from the web across the center to the right, you can see the spider! It's a large Orb Weaver spider in the family Araneidae, maybe Araneus gemmoides or Neoscona crucifera. I tried to go after the spider, but it retreated to its lair and I lost it. I'll keep trying to get a photo for Arachtober, but this is a nice web shot - Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2017)

File.Name: ha11d_ha09-036.jpg
Notes: 36973790773 This is a neat little spider web in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's just about done flowering. The whole web is just a couple of inches across. Hah, compare the web in my [Next] photo that's a couple of feet across! I guess this the work of a little Orb Weaver spider in the family Araneidae. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)

File.Name: ha12a_ha12-018.jpg
Notes:
36953839614 Here are pretty new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - the first shrub I've seen with flowers in the new water year. If you look close, you can see that insects have chewed into a few flower buds to get to the nectar earlier. This is another early bloomer that flowers even before the winter rains start - which is remarkable given our summer-dry Mediterranean climate. I've also found flowers on Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae and Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia, Rosaceae), photos hereand here. It's a new year! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2017)

It was another pleasant autumn day with mild temperatures and not much wind. It's calm so far, but we're under a NWS Red Flag Warning for the next two days with sundowner winds expected. Unfortunately, similar downslope winds are also in the forecast for the fire areas north of San Francisco. It's a dangerous time of year before the rains. I hope the winds don't appear.
CommonNames: Chaparral Currant; Bigberry Manzanita; Holly-leaved Cherry
ScientificName: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae

File.Name: ha12b_ha12-041.jpg
Notes: 36993293283 This is a large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider, probably Neoscona crucifera and not the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) which looks similar. (I'm sure I've confused them in the past.) It's the same spider that I showed yesterday moving away from its web in this photo. Lucky me, I went back this afternoon and found it sitting in its web. Light is an issue in this place on a north-facing slope that loses the sun in winter, but at least I had some sunlight filtered by trees. Arachtober 12. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2017)

File.Name: ha12c_ha09-027.jpg
Notes: 36993144143 This photo shows the rear end of the same fairly large Silver Argiope (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider that I showed in this photo. It's got prey, maybe a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera. The common name of this spider comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side which is not visible in this photo, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Look close - this one seems to have a safety line! It's a good butt shot so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: ha12d_h930-402.jpg
Notes: 23810940178 I believe this is the rear end of a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera), maybe in the genus Eristalis that includes many pretty bee-mimic flies. It's working the odd flowers of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in our neighborhood in Portland, Oregon. I figure I'm running out of time when I can reasonably post photos from our Oregon trip last month, but this is such a good butt shot ... so HBBBT! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 23 September 2017)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Hedera helix; Araliaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eristalis; Hedera helix; Araliaceae

File.Name: ha13a_ha13-005.jpg
Notes:
23828814698 Here is the husk of a nymph of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera). Maybe it's the shed skin or exuviae of the final molt - or is it the work of a spider or other predator? I found it on a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. That seems an odd place for an assassin bug, but I've found other unexpected insects here, for example see this photo. I'm pretty sure it's another Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2017)

We're under a NWS Red Flag Warning today and tonight so I've been expecting hot, dry winds. So far they haven't materialized, so maybe we'll be lucky this time? This is a dangerous time of year before the rains come.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Prickly Pear; Leafhopper Assassin Bug

File.Name: ha13b_ha12-004.jpg
Notes: 37010698223 Spider striptease! Hah, this is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) exposing its legs from behind a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. It seems to be making a flirty come-hither gesture with its top leg! You can see just a bit of the spider's palps, which gives a hint about its orientation. The general hairiness of the spider suggests that it is genus Mecaphesa - again. Arachtober 13. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2017)

File.Name: ha13c_ha09-034.jpg
Notes: 37010610103 This is looking at the whiskery face of the fly in this photo. I believe it is a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, Diptera in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by the creek, maybe species Thereva fucata, see this BugGuide info page. I believe from the separated eyes that this is a female. I wonder what those shinny black things are between its eyes? HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2017)
ScientificName: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Thereva fucata

File.Name: ha13d_h930-400.jpg
Notes: 37423277610 This is a gorgeous bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) working the odd autumn flowers of Common Ivy (Hedera helix, Araliaceae) in our neighborhood in Portland, Oregon. I figured that it look like genus Eristalis that includes many pretty bee-mimic flies - at least it is Eristalis-like. Turns out that it is species Myathropa florea in the tribe Eristalini which includes the genus Eristalis. Thanks Thijs! It sounds like this is a relatively new introduction from Europe and North Africa dated 2002. I figure I'm running out of time when I can reasonably post photos from our Oregon trip last month, but this is such a handsome fly ... so HFDF! (Mount Tabor, Portland, Oregon, 23 September 2017)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Eristalis; Myathropa florea; Eristalini
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Hedera helix; Araliaceae; Eristalis; Myathropa florea; Eristalini

File.Name: ha14a_ha14-012.jpg
Notes:
37030585533 I found this tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on a flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). The spider is about about 630 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 2.5 mm long - about 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) It's hardly more than a spiderling, yet here it is all alone at the top of a five-foot plant! There was a small but steady breeze today, and the plant was rocking back and forth. I had to take *many* photos to get this keeper. This isn't far from the spiderlings in this photo a month ago - could they be the same? Arachtober 14. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2017)

Like I said, there was a breeze today - but the dangerous winds in our Red Flag Warning forecast never materialized. It was warmer today, in the low 80s.

File.Name: ha14b_ha14-016.jpg
Notes: 36990583204 I'm sure this is an adult Oedaleonotus phryneicus (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera) grasshopper, and those little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. This one is tan, but others are quite dark - compare this photo. Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual! They just are variable, but they all have little stubby wings. This one is perched in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I've been finding a fair number of grasshoppers lately. Today I'm showing three different ones from the last three days. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2017)
ScientificName: Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Oedaleonotus phryneicus; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ha14c_ha13-002.jpg
Notes: 37652051106 I believe this is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes) in the group of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera ). It's sprawled across a none-too-fresh leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It appears to be large with eggs. You can see just a hint of the brilliant red and blue colors on its rear legs - see this photo for a better view. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2017)
CommonNames: Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper; Spur-throated Grasshoppers; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: ha14d_ha12-002.jpg
Notes: 37651771476 This might be a Thalassica grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) grasshopper. The root of the unfamiliar word "thalassica" means relating to the sea. I'm not sure what the connection is, but it's a nice name. Another possibility is a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). It's definitely a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae of the Acrididae, Orthoptera. I found this one on the ground near our mailbox. It seemed to have clear wings when it took off, but they might have been pale greenish. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2017)
CommonNames: Thalassica grasshopper; Pallid-winged Grasshopper; Band-winged Grasshopper
ScientificName: Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis pallidipennis; Oedipodinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera
associatedTaxa: Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis pallidipennis; Oedipodinae; Acrididae; Orthoptera

File.Name: ha15a_ha15-012.jpg
Notes:
37691163882 This is probably a Baja California Chorus Frog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca), but I can't really tell from this photo. Compare this photo from last spring. These frogs come in both green and brown forms, and I believe they can slowly change color. Julie found this one in our hot tub today and moved it to a leaf in our "water garden". (We mostly keep our hot tub as a cold tub in the summer.) The water garden is nothing fancy - just a tub filled with water and plants, but these tubs are important for the frogs and other critters. Five years of drought has taken its toll. I don't know how they could survive our dry summer without help. These are chorus frogs, and they make a racket in the spring! There are sound recordings at CaliforniaHerps.com. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)

It was warm again today, in the low 80s, with only slight breeze. It's forecast to stay warm for a few days - and then there's a chance of rain on Thursday/Friday. Like I've said, that's the way it is with our autumn weather - it swings...back and forth!

File.Name: ha15b_ha15-008.jpg
Notes: 37052742503 I think of these as "Sink Spiders" because I usually find them stuck in the kitchen sink - they get in overnight and then can't get up the steep sides until I rescue them in the morning. It's really a Wolf Spider in the family Lycosidae. This one is still in the glass that I caught it is. Look at those enlarged palps - it's definitely a male! After I got my photos outside on the porch, I let him go on the railing ... and he quickly disappeared. I wonder if he'll be back in the sink tomorrow! Arachtober 15. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)

File.Name: ha15c_ha15-019.jpg
Notes: 37052490993 Here are male (top) and female (bottom) Tree Crickets (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) that I found today and yesterday. The males have the broad wings that they use to produce their song, see this action shot. Only the males sing, but they sing for the narrow-winged ladies. I found the male yesterday on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) and the female today on a cactus pad in the garden. (You never know what you'll find in the cactus patch!) Both males and females work the night shift and try to hide during the day. It seems like I've been finding quite a few males lately, but this is the first lady I've seen for some time. I have another two-fer photo here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: ha15d_ha15-023.jpg
Notes: 37722285511 This is an "ootheca" or egg mass that I suspect are the eggs of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in an unlikely place - a ripe fruit on the Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. Well, maybe it's not so unlikely since I recently found the husk of an assassin bug nymph in the same cactus patch - see this photo. These are probably the eggs of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) which are so common in the woods right now. The individual eggs are about 500 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, which I figure is about 2.0 mm long - less that 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) The eggs have been covered by a spider web, but I don't think that will slow down the infants when they hatch! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Prickly Pear; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: ootheca; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: ootheca; Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: ha16a_ha16-002.jpg
Notes:
37486390250 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) is still waiting for her eggs to hatch on the cactus plant in our garden. It's not time to start pacing the floor yet as she only made her egg sac on October 5 or 6 - see this photo of before and after. I'm hoping her spiderlings will emerge before the end of Arachtober so I can share a photo. What devoted mothers they are! She is hard to reach at about eight feet high in this spiny Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus plant. I got this photo by extending my tripod all the way up and standing on a box. Not very steady, but it worked. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2017)

It was surprisingly hot today - up to 90∞, more than forecast. There was a breeze. I'm trying not to think too much about that F-Word that rhymes with "ire". It sounds like our chance of rain later this weak has dissipated, but at least it will cool down.

File.Name: ha16b_ha15-017.jpg
Notes: 23891332688 Here are two shots of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found in the cactus patch of our garden yesterday. I believe it is the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes which makes them look much larger. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. Arachtober 16. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)

File.Name: ha16c_ha15-031.jpg
Notes: 37695607896 Here's another shot of the sandstone rock with shell fossils that I showed up close in my [Previous] photo. The whole rock is about a foot across. I found in the summer-dry bed of the Santa Ynez River, but it has obviously been grinding away in the river when it's high. This rock now lives in our garden - like many of my souvenirs from past hikes and trips. Good memories. I wish I could say I remember every rock - but I don't. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)

File.Name: ha16d_ha15-028.jpg
Notes: 37479473920 This is looking close at shell fossils in a sandstone rock I found in the summer-dry bed of the Santa Ynez River. I don't buy postcards or coffee cups as souvenirs to remember my travels, but I do like to collect interesting rocks and fossils when I find them. This photo shows an expanse of an inch or two, but I have another photo of the whole rock here. It now lives in our garden. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Souvenir". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2017)

File.Name: ha17a_ha17-012.jpg
Notes:
23911992258 Here is a closer shot of the spider-like hatchlings in my [Previous] photo. They are tiny Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in an unlikely place - a ripening fruit on the Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. They weren't here yesterday, so they just hatched last night. The individual hatchlings are about 600 pixels long at 1:1 magnification, which I figure is about 2.4 mm long - about 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) They have pumpkin colors for Halloween! They look like spiderlings, but look closer - they have six legs and a beak-like proboscis folded under their tiny heads. Look how their antennae have brown tips, like eyeglasses. As with true spiderlings, I wonder what they eat?? (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2017)

We had some interesting weather today - overcast and breezy. It almost felt like rain at times, but it was also warm and tropical. It was a nice change - except for the breeze. I closed my car windows just in case.

File.Name: ha17b_ha17-011.jpg
Notes: 37055723864 Two days ago I got this photo of a group of eggs of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in an unlikely place - a ripe fruit on the Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. They hatched last night! You can still see the egg mass in the upper-left. I suspect these are the nymphs of a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii) which are so common in the woods right now - though they look nothing like them. The individual hatchlings are about 600 pixels long at 1:1 magnification, which I figure is about 2.4 mm long - about 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) They look like spiderlings, but look closer - they have six legs and a beak-like proboscis folded under their tiny heads. As with true spiderlings, I wonder what they eat?? See my [Next] photo for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2017)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Prickly Pear; Leafhopper Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Zelus renardii
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Zelus renardii

File.Name: ha17c_ha17-004.jpg
Notes: 37764519851 I'm sure this is a Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) because of leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. It might well be the Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis). The Western Leaf-footed Bug (L. clypealis) is similar, though it has a small spine at the tip of its head and more of a white line across its wings. (Thanks Sergii.) We do have a small forest of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri, Pinaceae) just up the hill from us. I found the bug this morning on an outside window that is actually vertical and not horizontal as it looks. The window is reflecting the cloudy-bright sky today, which makes for nice light. They look a bit like large assassin bugs (Reduviidae) but they are strictly herbivores that use their sharp beaks to suck plant juices instead of other insects. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2017)
CommonNames: Leaf-footed Bug; Western Conifer Seed Bug; Western Leaf-footed Bug; Coulter Pine
ScientificName: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis; L. clypealis; Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Reduviidae
associatedTaxa: Coreidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Leptoglossus occidentalis; L. clypealis; Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae; Reduviidae

File.Name: ha17d_ha17-006.jpg
Notes: 37732368112 I believe this is a Long-bodied Cellar Spider (Pholcus phalangioides) in the family Pholcidae in our house. I usually find these little long-legged spiders in the corners of our windows. Their legs look like they're made of capillary glass tubing. Apparently they are introduced from Central Europe - but they've sure made themselves at home. I got lucky with the light on this one - my 1/2 second long exposure worked. Arachtober 17. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2017)

File.Name: ha18a_ha18-027.jpg
Notes:
37075187164 Look close - a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has brought down a much larger broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) in a still-flowering shrub of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I believe that the spider is genus Mecaphesa - again. Truth to tell, I rotated this photo from vertical to horizontal so it fits the screen better. It's also a pretty tight crop, tighter than I like - it was breezy, and I had no choice but to stand back. It's still a dramatic photo! Does anyone else remember that book by B. Kliban "Never Eat Anything Bigger Than Your Head"? I guess spiders follow different rules! Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)

It was sunny again today, but cooler and breezier. We lost our chance for rain on Friday, but cooler and breezier just about covers it. Then it will swing back to the warm side early next week.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Tree Cricket; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Never Eat Anything Bigger Than Your Head
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Mecaphesa; Never Eat Anything Bigger Than Your Head

File.Name: ha18b_ha18-023.jpg
Notes:
37735233876 A couple of days ago I posted this photo of a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac on a cactus plant. This new photo shows an old spider nest which has done its job and is now abandoned - see this photo from September 10. The spiderlings have gone, but if you look close you can see that they have left behind their exuviae - shed skins after molting. (I can't resist - see this photo of tiny spiderlings molting!) The shed skins persist for a long time, but I reckon that other insects like Carpet Beetle (Dermestidae, Coleoptera) regard them as delicacies. Hah - those beetles are also the bane of insect collections. Arachtober 18. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)

Here's a question - is this photo appropriate for the Web Wednesday group? It does show the haphazard web of a Green Lynx Spider, and isn't that egg sac really made out of spider silk from the same spinnerets as normal webbing?? I'll do it, the SysAdmin can always remove it ... so Happy Web Webnesday!

File.Name: ha18c_ha13-026.jpg
Notes: 37784200011 This is part of a "Halloween Web" - the web of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in this photo, probably Neoscona crucifera. I have another photo of the same spider's web in this photo. Light is an issue in this place near home on a north-facing slope that loses the sun in winter, but at least I had a bit of sunlight filtered by trees. I haven't found any of these impressive spiders this year that are actually in the sun for more than a few minutes of a day. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2017)

File.Name: ha18d_ha18-017.jpg
Notes:
37525653550 Here are two photos of a gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a stem of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This photo shows how quickly the galls mature - I got the photo on the right today and the photo on the left two days ago! These galls are about the size of a blueberry. The green galls turn brown and then drop to the ground - unless they are trapped like the gall in this photo. Autumn seems to be their time. (San Marcos Pass, 16+18 October 2017)

This is a unisexual gall, which contains only female wasps. The lady wasps will emerge next spring and parthenogenetically produce a crop of tiny bisexual galls that contain both male and female wasps. The wasps emerge, mate, and induce another crop of unisexual galls that contain only females, and the cycle repeats. Sorry, but galls are complicated like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ha19a_ha19-005.jpg
Notes:
23950124418 These photos show the upper and lower wings of a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on flower clusters of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I've been seeing a fair number of these butterflies lately - and not many other butterflies. I suspect this is part of their migration pattern. In some years, we have large spring migrations of these butterflies heading north from Mexico, so maybe these butterflies are heading home? The buckwheat flowers are on their way out, but there are still open flowers in there. The finished flowers turn a rich brown. In time, the flower heads will be all brown. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2017)

As expected, it was cooler and breezier today - becoming downright windy in the afternoon with 30+ mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory this evening. It should remain cool over the weekend, then heat up dramatically next week.

File.Name: ha19b_ha19-013.jpg
Notes: 37754462606 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly looking away from me on flower clusters of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I've been seeing a fair number of these butterflies lately - and not many other butterflies. I suspect this is part of their migration pattern. In some years, we have large spring migrations of these butterflies heading north from Mexico, so maybe these butterflies are heading home? See my [Next] photo for separate shots of their upper and lower wings. Meanwhile, this is a nice butterfly butt shot so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2017)

File.Name: ha19c_ha18-018.jpg
Notes: 37754309056 This another tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I honestly don't know what prey it expects to find here, but I've seen several of these spiders on oak leaves lately - there must be something. The general hairiness of the spider suggests that it is genus Mecaphesa - again. The spider is about about 900 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 3.5 mm long - a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) Those odd hairs on he leaf are the star-like stellate hairs that these leaves have. Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; stellate hairs
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; stellate hairs

File.Name: ha19d_ha18-001.jpg
Notes: 37802201691 I was sitting on the throne in the bathroom admiring this little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider sitting in the tunnel of the funnel-opening of its web, see this photo. This little spider lives on the window sill in our bathroom - we don't bother it and it doesn't bother us. But something seemed wrong with the spider, so I took a photo to see it better. It wasn't until I looked at the photo that I realized the spider was pointing away from me - I was looking at spinnerets not eyes! It's a nice spider butt shot so HA and HBBBT! Arachtober 19. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)

File.Name: ha20a_ha20-023.jpg
Notes:
37111299874 This is an especially gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It may be the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes). I noticed this critter climbing along a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) on a windy day. I took many photos to get this keeper. It's another of our autumn predators. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2017)

It was gloomy enough this morning that I thought we might get some drizzle at least - but wind is all we got with 30+ mph gusts. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and a Red Flag Warning tomorrow. I guess I can't complain too much as we've really had very little wind this autumn and it's coming on a cool day. That will change on Monday/Tuesday next week which are expected to be quite hot - and hopefully calm.

File.Name: ha20b_ha20-002.jpg
Notes: 37821373161 Here's another spider-like hatchling of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in an unlikely place - a ripening fruit on the Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. It's only a couple of days old, see this photo for more info. They have pumpkin colors for Halloween! I assume they will scatter in time, but I'm happy that they're hanging around for a while at least. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2017)

File.Name: ha20c_ha18-009.jpg
Notes:
37110865294 I found this dead fly on a board near a spider web, probably a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera). It's probably spider prey. I thought of an old song by Bob Dylan from 1962/3 "Only a Hobo" - listen here. With one change, this becomes "Only a Fly":

Only a fly, but one more is gone
Leaviní nobody to sing his sad song
Leaviní nobody to carry him home
Only a fly, but one more is gone

Autumn is the time for predators to pick off the pollinators before they're gone for good as winter approaches. "Nature red of tooth and claw" as Tennyson wrote, but it's also making good use of a resource. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)

File.Name: ha20d_ha18-025.jpg
Notes: 37150666223 Look above the cricket for the spider! This is another shot of the small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with the much larger broad-winged male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) for prey that I showed a couple of days ago, see this photo. I found this in a still-flowering shrub of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). I believe that the spider is genus Mecaphesa - again. For that other shot, I rotated my camera counter-clockwise to make the photo more horizontal, but this photo shows the scene as I found it. It's not as dramatic, but I like the way it shows the spider and the unfortunate cricket. The cricket looks like The Hanged Man in the classic tarot cards. Arachtober 20. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2017)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Tree Cricket; Sawtooth Goldenbush

File.Name: ha21a_ha21-042.jpg
Notes:
37133349744 Here's another shot of the same small Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that I show in my [Previous] photo, taken an hour later as I came back on my walk. It's curled up in a dandelion-like seedhead of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). This might be the same spider as I showed a week ago in this photo - at least I found it in the same group of plants. If it is the same, it's been growing! Most of these flowers have withered in the last week. It must be tough for these spiders when their flowers are done - what do they do? Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

Today was very nice with temps in the 70s and not much breeze. We're under a NWS Red Flag Warning until Sunday morning, but there doesn't seem to be any particular reason for it. Tomorrow it will start to warm up. I hope it stays calm as forecast.

File.Name: ha21b_ha21-011.jpg
Notes: 37585349720 This is a small Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) traveling along a stem of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae). This might be the same spider as I showed a week ago in this photo - at least I found it in the same group of plants. If it is the same, it's been growing! My [Next] photo is another shot of the same spider taken later in the day. Arachtober 21. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

File.Name: ha21c_ha21-002.jpg
Notes: 37811326102 I believe this is a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes) in the group of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera ). It's curious that it only has one antenna - I wonder what happened? It's sitting on dusty leaves of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) that are waiting for a rain to wash the dust away. Most insects are getting scarce now as autumn gets closer to winter - except for the predators. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

File.Name: ha21d_ha21-044.jpg
Notes: 37794478356 This looks like a nut or a gall, but it's actually an odd insect - a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It feels hard like it has a shell. I found this one on a leafy stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This one seems to match the species Allokermes essigii in these photos that I found on the web. Unlike some other scale insects, I usually find these alone or in small groups. They don't seem to do any damage to the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

File.Name: ha22a_ha22-014.jpg
Notes:
37837986512 I found this large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the woods today. I believe it is genus Araneus - maybe Araneus gemma but cp. Araneus andrewsi because of its dark colors. It's on a dead oak branch above her web - about 8 ft. high so I had my tripod extended all the way. Like many of our large spiders, it seems to have a face on its back with soulful eyes looking up. These spiders make webs that can span a road. You don't want to walk into one at night - *Where's the spider!?* This spider's web was pretty tattered when I got this shot, but I'll keep trying for a photo now that I know where it is. I don't know why, but I'm suddenly noticing lots of their webs. I guess it really is the month of Arachtober 22! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2017)

Today was a lovely autumn day - highs in the low 80s, but it still felt cool when you stepped into a shadow. It was uncommonly calm. Almost perfect, but I would appreciate a few more insects to photograph, all bugs except predators are getting scarce as winter approaches. On the other hand, I did see both Red Admiral and Mourning Cloak butterflies in the canyon today - positive ID but no photos. Check the dates of the photos in those links. These are our usual winter butterflies so we're heading in that direction.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Araneus andrewsi
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Araneus andrewsi

File.Name: ha22b_ha22-045.jpg
Notes: 37820005896 I found this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) in the woods today. I believe that the spider is genus Mecaphesa - again. Don't tell the spider, but the flower has probably lost its attraction for insects - it's a female, pistillate flower on this dioecious shrub that is already going to seed. Still, look how intent the spider is! Arachtober 22. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2017)

File.Name: ha22c_ha22-020.jpg
Notes: 37837384262 I found this lobster-colored true bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) working the fading flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. I believe that I've seen this critter before, see this photo from the end of August. It might be in the genus Arhyssus, a Scentless Plant Bug in the family Rhopalidae. The bug is almost the same color as the faded flowers of the buckwheat - great camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2017)

File.Name: ha22d_ha20-012.jpg
Notes: 37819340966 This is a fierce-looking Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) among the spines on a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden. I thought for a moment that it might be skewered by a cactus spine - it happens, see this old photo from Fotolog in 2008! But no, I made my way around to the back and the spider moved away. The wasp was on the far side of our cactus patch, I couldn't get any closer, the nice light wouldn't last ... this is a pretty tight crop, but it's a nice photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2017)

File.Name: ha23a_ha23-005.jpg
Notes:
37843410786 Here is another shot of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider my [Previous] photo. I believe it is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma. If you look close, you can see that it's missing its first leg on the spider-right/screen-left. It looks like a clean stump. These are real Halloween spiders that come out in the woods and under the eaves of our roof. Like many of our large spiders, it seems to have a face on its back with soulful eyes looking up. Now why would a spider have a face on its butt that looks scary to us humans?? These spiders make webs that can span a road. You don't want to walk into one at night - *Where's the spider!?* Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2017)

It was downright hot today, up to about 95∞, and it was almost unnaturally calm. What's curious is that it was hotter down in Santa Barbara. This is usually the work of downslope sundowner winds, but there was no wind today. Maybe this is spillover from the Santa Ana winds down south/east in Ventura? Whatever the reason, Santa Barbara on the coast is under a NWS Heat Advisory while we folks in the mountains are not. Curious!

File.Name: ha23b_ha23-008.jpg
Notes: 37171441443 Here are two shots of the same large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the woods. This is different from the one I showed yesterday in this photo, but I believe it is the same kind - genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma. I got the photo on the right yesterday and the photo of the web today - with the spider sitting in the center! The web is in a summer-dormant plant of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), and that's where sh'e sitting on the right. Like many of our large spiders, it seems to have a face on its back with soulful eyes looking up - see my [Next] photo. These spiders make webs that can span a road. You don't want to walk into one at night - *Where's the spider!?* Arachtober 23. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2017)
