File.Name: ha23c_ha22-019.jpg
Notes: 37842990946 I guess that this is another Thalassica grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) grasshopper on the ground near our mailbox. The root of the unfamiliar word "thalassica" means relating to the sea. I'm not sure what the connection is, but it's a nice name. It seemed to have light green wings when it took off. It has blue tibia. At first it appears to have two dark bands on its inner femur (on the back leg) but I think I see a large black patch in between which is indicative of Thalassica. Alice Abelia has a nice identification page for these grasshoppers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2017)

File.Name: ha23d_ha22-004.jpg
Notes: 37853598642 This is the shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda), probably a Shoulderband Snail, genus Helminthoglypta in the family Helminthoglytidae showing its spiral shape. It's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails that are so common in the lowlands. These are creatures of the winter rainy months and not the end of our dry summer, but I remembered this one from last year, and there it was right where I remembered it. (I may have another photo of the same shell from January 2.) The whole snail is about an inch across. I do find living snails in the mountains now and then, see this photo. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Spiral". I agree, it's kind of an obvious response to the assignment, but I've been preoccupied with spiders lately! HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2017)
CommonNames: Land Snail; Shoulderband Snail; Garden Snails

File.Name: ha24a_ha24-016.jpg
Notes:
37882562012 Here is a different mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) guarding her newly emerged spiderlings in the woods today. Hah - I tried to get both mama and spiderlings in focus and failed - at least this photo has the spider sharp! This is a new spider for me - not the species, but the individual. I was looking for the Silver Argiope spider in my [Previous] photo who strayed from her web ... and found this one instead. Sure, I'll go back to get a better shot of the cute spiderlings. I would this one in the grass/weeds along the road in the canyon. Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2017)

Today was much like yesterday, about 95∞, and almost unnaturally calm again. A neighbor's weather station had us at 102∞ but that station seems to run warm. It's curious that it was hotter down in Santa Barbara again. This is usually the work of downslope sundowner winds, but there was no wind today. Maybe this is spillover from the Santa Ana winds down south/east in Ventura? Whatever the reason, Santa Barbara on the coast is under a NWS Heat Advisory while we folks in the mountains are not. Curious! Tomorrow should be a few degrees cooler.

File.Name: ha24b_ha24-024.jpg
Notes: 37912906441 Here is another shot of the Silver Argiope aka Silver Garden Spider (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider the woods today. I was worried at first because she wasn't sitting in her web where she usually is, but I found her hanging from a perch off to the side. She always seems to be hanging with her underside exposed (like this), so this is actually the best photo of her upper side that I have. The common name of this spider comes from the white/silver part on the spider's top side, but a better characteristic for identification is their remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. What beautiful spiders they are! Arachtober 24. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2017)

File.Name: ha24c_ha24-004.jpg
Notes: 37864091886 This is an odd colony of wool-producing critters on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe they are Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. But I'm confused because there seem to be two different organisms in here - the fat ones that are mainly responsible for the wool and look like mealy bugs, and the smaller ones that really do look like aphids. You can see them both in the photo on the left, and there are closer crops of each on the right. (These are from three different photos.) If you do a search for "Stegophylla" at BugGuide you get a nice photo by Alice Abelia (among others) but no links to more info. I'm not at all sure about this, and I would appreciate any guidance. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Woolly Oak Aphids

File.Name: ha24d_ha23-028.jpg
Notes: 26136153009 I'm pretty sure this is the gall of a Dried Peach Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The galls really do look like little peaches! I've been neglecting galls while I look for spiders this month, but the time of Arachtober will soon be over, and I'll have more time for them. This is probably the peak time for galls - which is convenient because insects and flowers are getting scarce. There's always something to photograph in our woods, though sometimes you have to hunt for it! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2017)
CommonNames: Dried Peach Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ha25a_ha25-012.jpg
Notes:
24086047838 Here is one more shot of one of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spiders that I've been showing lately - here, here, and here. Well, why not - "If you got emí, flaunt emí!" Look how its screen-right leg is touching the line that runs to her large web - like using your index finger to sense the line while fishing. I believe it is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma. I said before that she's on a dead oak branch, but I misspoke. The spider is on a dead stem of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) - not that it matters. These are proper Halloween spiders! Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2017)

Today was much like yesterday, in the 90s and most;y calm. It was a few degrees cooler than it's been, but when it's this hot who cares. It was hotter down in Santa Barbara again, close to 100∞ or more. Santa Barbara and Santa Maria have been setting heat records. Tomorrow should be about 10 degrees cooler.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: ha25b_ha25-008.jpg
Notes: 37884003846 Here is the beautiful web of a tiny Trash Line Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in the woods. You can barely make out the spider sitting in the center of the web. The whole thing is only about the size of a CD/DVD disk. Hah, it seems to have the same kind of interference patterns too! This one doesn't seem to have any sign of its "trash line" that these spiders make with the dead bodies of their prey, but I believe my ID because it has the typical odd bump on its abdomen, see this photo. HA and HWW! Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2017)

File.Name: ha25c_ha21-059.jpg
Notes: 37905996842 Spider engineering! This photo shows the end of the main structural "bridge thread" of the web of a Silver Argiope aka Silver Garden Spider (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) spider - a different one from the spider I've been showing lately, who has now vanished. This is the thread between two anchors that supports the whole web. The bridge seems to be made of multiple strands of silk that are individually anchored to the faded flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae), see my [Previous] photo. I do believe this is strong! As I said, the spider vanished while we were in Oregon last month, but it left its web behind. In time these forgotten webs can become something like a "ghost net" - a fishing net that has been left or lost in the ocean by fishermen and keeps on killing after it is lost, see Wikipedia. HA and HWW! Arachtober 25. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)
CommonNames: Silver Argiope; Silver Garden Spider; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: ha25d_ha25-016.jpg
Notes: 37905789672 Here are old and new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I've been showing this plant quite a bit lately, so it seems time to feature it in its own photo. It's a remarkable plant that flowers all summer long and well into autumn. The small flowers turn to a rich brown as they fade, but this one still has fresh flowers to replace them. I believe that only the flowers with pink stamens are still producing nectar. This is one of those plants that keeps the insects alive through our long hot summer. I've been looking for crab spiders in these flowers, but I'm not finding many. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2017)

File.Name: ha26a_ha26-021.jpg
Notes:
37246340384 This is another shot of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that I found guarding her newly emerged spiderlings a couple of days ago, see this photo. I went back to her today to get a photo of her spiderlings, but they retreated to the shady side of her egg sac, and I couldn't draw them out into the sun - even with a grass stem "nudger". I did draw out mama spider though. Look how emaciated she looks. I have seen this before, see this photo of a spider before and after laying eggs, but the current photo looks exceptional. These mama spiders sacrifice everything for their offspring. I don't think I ever realized how hairy these spiders are. Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2017)

It was indeed 10 degrees cooler today. It was breezy this morning, but it calmed down to become a very nice day. The little map on the NWS weather page is refreshingly empty of alerts and warnings!

File.Name: ha26b_ha21-017.jpg
Notes: 37246103294 This is looking close at the abdomen of the Silver Argiope spider aka Silver Garden Spider (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) that I was first showing from the canyon at home. They have a way of hanging on their web with their tail sticking out just like this, see this photo of the same spider. These are the spiders that have remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Here you have a good view of the spider's spinnerets. You can also see her epigynum and one of her book lungs on the other end of her abdomen. HA and HBBBT! Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

File.Name: ha26c_ha21-056.jpg
Notes: 37955145451 Here is the hairy behind of a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on fading flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I believe the spider is genus Mecaphesa - again. This spider didn't want to pose - I got this shot and it was gone. I'm not sure why, but these spiders have been relatively scarce this year. HA and HBBBT! Arachtober 26. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2017)

File.Name: ha26d_ha26-002.jpg
Notes: 37924102802 Never mind the (lack of) white balance - I'm pretty sure this is a nocturnal Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, Arctiidae, Lepidoptera). I found her laying eggs on the door screen before I went to bed last night, and got the shot on the right with flash. The eggs were still there this morning, so I got the shot on the left with no flash but deep shade. I wonder what happened to bring about this misfiring? I have another photo of the pretty moth here and another photo involving eggs here. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2017)

File.Name: ha27a_ha27-020.jpg
Notes:
24126649128 While I was getting a close photo of the spider in the autumn flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family, this little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) flew into my photo on the same flower! The spider disappeared. The feet of the fly are touching the flower, but it's really just hovering. The fly might be genus Hemipenthes, possibly the Sinuous Bee Fly (Hemipenthes sinuosa), though there are other species that are very similar. See this photo for another shot of the same kind of fly on the same kind of flowers from the end of August. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2017)

Today was much like yesterday - with the difference that there was a thin layer of fog covering the ocean and lapping up on the shoreline. That means that our offshore winds are done, so it should finally start cooling down - more than it has already. It was still up to 90∞ today, too warm for this time of year.
CommonNames: California Aster; Bee Fly; Sinuous Bee Fly
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hemipenthes; Hemipenthes sinuosa
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Hemipenthes; Hemipenthes sinuosa

File.Name: ha27b_ha27-016.jpg
Notes: 37947210572 Look close at this autumn flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family - there is a tiny spider on the yellow disk flowers in the center! The flowers are smaller than garden asters, less than 1/2 inch across so that spider is really tiny. I believe it's another Crab Spider (Thomisidae), but it looks different from the genus Mecaphesa spider in my [Previous] photo. Compare this photo of a much larger crab spider on he same kind of flower. Arachtober 27. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2017)
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Thomisidae; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Thomisidae; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ha27c_ha25-022.jpg
Notes: 37946862042 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with a fly (Diptera) for prey. It's on the shady side of a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). It was too bright and contrasty to get a photo, so I reached out with my left hand to make shade, and that worked well to reduce the contrast. The fly looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera), maybe genus Peleteria which I've found at this time of year before, see this photo. I'm pretty sure the spider is genus Mecaphesa again. HA and HFDF! Arachtober 27. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2017)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; California Buckwheat; Tachinid Fly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; Peleteria; Mecaphesa
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Tachinidae; Diptera; Peleteria; Mecaphesa

File.Name: ha27d_ha27-026.jpg
Notes: 37923522466 This sure appears to be two different galls on one leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). That's unusual. Both are the work of different tiny Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I guess that the light-colored gall on the left is the work of a the work of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) and the darker gall on the right might be the gall of a Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa. Both are small, about the size of a fuzzy pea. Autumn is the time for galls as well as spiders! (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp; Woollybear Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus fullawayi; Sphaeroteras timaculosum; Atrusca trimaculosa

File.Name: ha28a_ha28-021.jpg
Notes:
37947422906 Here is another shot of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) with a couple of her tiny newly hatched spiderlings. I like that one spiderling climbing on mama's belly! This is the mama spider that looks emaciated, see this photo. These mama spiders sacrifice everything for their offspring until there's nothing left. I've been trying to get a group photo of the spiderlings, but it's hard - they gather on the shady side of their egg sac, and I haven't been able to draw them out into the sun - even with a grass stem "nudger". See my [Previous] photo. I'll keep trying. Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2017)

It was still warm today, but I think it's finally cooling down - it's already in the 60s which is actually the coolest it's been all week. There's a chance of drizzle tomorrow night and even a chance of real rain next weekend - but I know not to get too excited about forecasts made a week away!

File.Name: ha28b_ha26-014.jpg
Notes: 26224517279 This is one of the spiderlings of the Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that I've been showing lately on the stem of a fallen oak leaf that's been incorporated into the nest. The spiderling is about 600 pixels long at 1:1 magnification in this macro shot, which I figure is about 2.4 mm long - about 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) It's interesting that it's the same size as the tiny hatching assassin bug in my previous photo - two tiny predators trying to make their way in a wild world. It's also interesting that these spiderlings already show signs of color variation - this one has an orange abdomen while others are green. It's been hard getting a group photo of the spiderlings - they gather on the shady side of mama's egg sac, and I haven't been able to draw them out into the sun - even with a grass stem "nudger". I'll keep trying. Arachtober 28. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2017)

File.Name: ha28c_ha26-001.jpg
Notes: 38000284791 On October 16/17 we had a hatch of tiny Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in our garden, see this photo. The hatchlings have since scattered, but I happened too notice this one today on a different cactus in the same cactus patch. It's interesting that it seems to have the same defensive pose as the adult in this photo - front legs and head up in the air. It is about 600 pixels long at 1:1 magnification in this macro shot, which I figure is about 2.4 mm long - about 1/10 inch, really tiny. (See here for how I figure.) Hah - if you think that's tiny, follow the spider web down from the upper-left to the bundle of cactus spines in the lower-right. Now there's a really tiny spider - 1/100 inch long? I wonder if it has an even tinier mite on it! I won't add this to the Arachtober group, but it shows that you see spiders everywhere when you really look! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2017)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; really
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; really

File.Name: ha28d_ha25-003.jpg
Notes: 24148298398 This is a tiny Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on an old stalk of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae), probably genus Jalysus. You can see its proboscis folded under its head. They seem to suck plants not other insects like the assassin bugs they resemble, see this photo. They seem to be fairly common when you look for them, but it's getting late in the season when all insects are becoming scarce - and notable. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2017)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Jalysus
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Jalysus

File.Name: ha29a_ha29-008.jpg
Notes:
37996862312 This photo shows a remarkable meeting between two orthopterans - a female Tree Cricket (Oecanthus, Gryllidae) on the left and a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes, Melanoplinae, Acrididae) on the right. You know how it is - when you work the night shift (cricket), some fool always comes along (grasshopper) to disturb your daytime sleep! In this case, the grasshopper is munching a none-too-fresh leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I didn't wait around to see what happened, but I suspect the cricket made a hasty exit. This is a nice shot of the colorful legs of that grasshopper. I did take one liberty with this photo - turning my camera sideways to make it horizontal. Turn your head counter-clockwise if you prefer. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)

Today started sunny and clear - but there was a 1500 foot thick marine layer that never did rise up over the mountains but did stir up some "fog winds". There was also fog in the Santa Ynez Valley as far inland as Lake Cachuma. Then a veil of real clouds slowly moved in from the west, and it was overcast for most of the rest of the day. Increasing clouds and patchy fog are in the forecast for the next few days - and then RAIN LIKELY on Saturday night! Yeah, I know about forecasts made a week away...
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper; Canyon Sunflower
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Melanoplinae; Acrididae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: ha29b_ha29-028.jpg
Notes: 26251374259 This is a Green Stink Bug in the family Pentatomidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe genus Chinavia or Thyanta, see this photo. It's posing for me between two autumn flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum, Onagraceae), see this photo for a better view of the fine flowers. Nice colors! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Thyanta; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Thyanta; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae

File.Name: ha29c_ha29-043.jpg
Notes: 26250912959 Here is another little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on spent flowers of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). Look close - this unfortunate spider is missing 3 of its 4 front grasping legs making it hard for it to grab anything. I reckon it tried to grab something too big and strong for it to hold - and it got away with the spider's front legs. Good luck little spider, life is resilient! I apologize for the way this photo is cut off. I got this shot hunkered too close to the ground, squinting at my viewfinder from a ways back. This was the best shot I got, near the edge of the frame. Photographers have to be resilient too! Arachtober 29. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)

File.Name: ha29d_ha29-003.jpg
Notes: 24174525398 Here is one more shot of the still-waiting-to-be-a-mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on a tall cactus plant in the garden. She made her egg sac on October 5 or 6 and I suspect it will be another couple of weeks until the spiderlings hatch. What a great Halloween mask she has on her abdomen! And it's interesting that her abdomen has nicely filled in - see this photo showing her just after making her egg sac. She is now hard to reach at about eight feet high in this spiny Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus plant. I got this photo by extending my tripod all the way up and standing on a box. Not very steady, but it worked. Yeah, the white balance seems off in this photo, but that's just the way it looked this morning before the sun got to it. We live on the north-facing slope of the mountains, and we mostly loose the sun behind the hills in winter. That's not so good for insects and spiders, but it's excellent for mushrooms! Arachtober 29. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)

File.Name: ha30a_ha30-013.jpg
Notes:
26275974329 I was staring right at this for several moments before I saw the spider!

I'm pretty sure this is a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) tucked into its thimble-shaped retreat that is decorated with the bodies of its prey. These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. There is usually a line or two that connects them, and you can see that the spider in my photo has a finger on a couple of lines. I found this one in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's done flowering. I've been trying to get a photo of these spiders, but I think it took a cloudy day with soft light to make it happen. Hah - I see a cat face on its abdomen! Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2017)

It was overcast all day. It sounds like it will stay overcast with marine layer fog and clouds for the next few days - and then we get the "first widespread rainfall of the season" next weekend! At least that's the gist of it from the NWS.
ScientificName: Metepeira; Araneidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; and
associatedTaxa: Metepeira; Araneidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; and

File.Name: ha30b_ha30-001.jpg
Notes: 37998684146 Here are three shots of a tiny Trash Line Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider in our cactus patch this morning. These spiders are ridiculously small - just 720 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot , which comes to 2.8 mm, about 1/10 inch. (See here for how I figure.) They always have that odd bump on their abdomen like a bottle gourd. These spiders spin perfect orb webs that are about the size of a CD/DVD, see this photo. I know, these photos aren't quite sharp, but they show the spider. It will help to sit back a few feet. Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2017)

File.Name: ha30c_ha30-018.jpg
Notes: 38051782931 This is looking straight down at a Silver Argiope spider aka Silver Garden Spider (Argiope argentata, Araneidae) hanging from its web and wrapping a honey bee as prey. This is a different spider from the one I've been showing - one that was here before we left for Oregon in September and was gone when we got back - except for her web, see this photo. This spider is in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox, so either she's come back or been replaced. They have a way of hanging on their web with their tail sticking out just like this, see this photo of the other spider. These are the spiders that have remarkable "horns" around the abdomen which gives them a cartoon look. Here you have a good view of the spider's spinnerets. What a handsome mask she has for Halloween! Arachtober 30. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2017)
CommonNames: Silver Argiope; Silver Garden Spider; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Argiope argentata; Araneidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: ha30d_ha27-006.jpg
Notes:
37335032154 This Halloween spider came from the Rite-Aid pharmacy, but I put it on the real web of this spider for my photo. The spider is actually a plastic ring, and I tore up the web some getting it into position. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Halloween". It's only marginally a macro photo - the spider is 1-1/2 inch high, so I figure the whole field of view is about 2-3/4 inch, barely legal for the group. I first thought shooting a real spider would be a natural for this assignment, but then I read the admin j.towbin's claim that "posting a plain old spider photo won't cut it this week--all photos MUST BE related to Halloween." I figure this works - and I hope it also works for Arachtober 30! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2017)

(Interesting news today!)

File.Name: ha31a_ha31-032.jpg
Notes:
24224419628 Here are two shots of the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) that looks emaciated, see this photo and this photo - now deceased. She was looking after her spiderlings as recently as October 28 in the first link - and now she's dead, with legs folded across her chest. I said before that "these mama spiders sacrifice everything for their offspring until there's nothing left" - it's true. Look, there's one of her spiderlings walking on her legs on the right - now you're on your own! Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2017)

Today is the last day of Arachtober 2017. It's been great fun hunting for spiders every day as a kind of scavenger hunt, and I've found them most days at least. I've learned a lot too, mostly that there are more spiders in the world than I find at home! Now to get back to all the other creatures in the woods.

It was another gloomy day - with an annoying breeze that was just enough to keep everything in motion. It sounds like it will stay overcast with marine layer fog and clouds for the next few days - and then we're still on track to get our "first widespread rainfall of the season" next weekend!

File.Name: ha31b_ha31-038.jpg
Notes: 38045736012 Hah - I found this little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) inside a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden today. I'm not sure if it's really waiting for prey or just snuggling on a gloomy day! I actually saw a few of these, but they all skedadalled when I got close - except for this one. Maybe it was just too comfortable to consider moving? It was cloudy with a breeze today, and I took *many* shots to get this keeper. Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2017)

File.Name: ha31c_ha31-034.jpg
Notes: 38023507516 Here is a really tiny spider doing a tightrope walk between two spines of a prickly pear cactus in our garden. Most of those cactus spines are really "glochids" - those irritating small tufted spines that are nearly impossible to remove. The spider is only about 290 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.1 mm, less than half of 1/10 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) Small as it is, it's a gorgeous spider with an iridescent body like it's made of copper. I don't know what kind of spider it is - other than a tiny one! Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2017)

File.Name: ha31d_ha30-012.jpg
Notes: 37366926134 Here is one more shot of the large bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider I've been showing lately, e.g. in this photo. I believe it is genus Araneus, maybe Araneus gemma. The spider is perched in a summer-dormant plant of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), and her web is below her. I've shown this spider before, but I got this photo yesterday, and I wanted to have at least one impressive Halloween spider today! Arachtober is almost over, but I'll be keeping my eye (and my camera lens) on these spiders. Arachtober 31. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2017)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus; Araneus gemma; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: hb01a_hb01-020.jpg
Notes:
26324842169 You have to study this photo for a while to see what it is... Look close, and you can see two legs going forward and two more going backwards - that's the clue!

This is a remarkable little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging on its web with perfect camouflage. The web is attached to a stem of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae). I'm sure this spider is genus Tmarus, probably Tmarus angulatus. This might be the best camouflage I've ever seen - or is it the caterpillar in this photo? It's good enough camouflage that I've only found these spiders twice in many years - here and here. I only noticed this one because it was bouncing up and down on its web. Now that attention-getting behavior is NOT good camouflage! Hah - just because Arachtober has come to an end doesn't mean that I can't keep posting spider photos! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2017)

It was another mostly gloomy day - though the sun did appear after noon. There was some breeze. We have drizzle and rain in the forecast through next Monday, though I notice the NWS is no longer saying "rain likely" for this weekend. *Sigh*
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus

File.Name: hb01b_hb01-006.jpg
Notes: 24248755098 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a tuft of spines on a prickly pear cactus in our garden. The short cactus spines are really "glochids" - those irritating small tufted spines that are nearly impossible to remove. (There's a pretty good discussion at Wikipedia.) The spider is only about 570 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) The spider might be a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, but it might also be genus Ozyptila. The difference seems to be that the latter only has two bristles on tibia I - which seems to apply to my spider. Whatever. It's another tiny spider in the cactus patch - see this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2017)

File.Name: hb01c_ha31-007.jpg
Notes: 24248646928 Here's another shot of a little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) on flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia x 'Waverly', Lamiaceae) in the garden. See this photo of a similar spider inside the flower! I'm not sure if it's the same spider or different. I got this photo first, then went off for a few hours, and then came back and got the other photo. In or out, it's a nice photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2017)

File.Name: hb01d_ha23-011.jpg
Notes: 38047678486 I believe this photo shows the two webs of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae). These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side with a line or two that connects them. If you look close, you can even get a peek at the spider on the top-right of its house - I'll make a note. I have another recent photo of the spider here. I found this one in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's done flowering. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2017)

File.Name: hb02a_hb02-030.jpg
Notes:
38094875232 Here are ripe seeds of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). The flower stalks get 6-12 feet tall, but this one has fallen over. They too seem to have neat stacks of flat seeds like the Humboldt Lily in my [Previous] photo. Like our lilies, they are sometimes harvested for the flower stalks. It would be interesting to try to grow yucca plants from seed. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2017)

I got this photo during a rare moment of sunshine today, but it was mostly overcast and gloomy. It was also pretty breezy, making for a difficult photo day. It did kind of look like rain at times today, but it didn't feel like rain - not much humidity. It's not looking good for our chances of rain this weekend - but there is still a chance.
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; feel
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae; feel

File.Name: hb02b_hb02-015.jpg
Notes: 37416961844 This is a ripe seedpod of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) - full of flat seeds in neat stacks. They are one of our most impressive wildflowers, see this photo from last June. Thoughtless "drive-by pickers" get some of the flowers because they are so beautiful - see this photo of the result. I'm glad that some survive to make seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2017)

File.Name: hb02c_ha29-027.jpg
Notes: 38094166462 Here are new seeds on a plant of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum, Onagraceae). I still think of these plants by their old name Zauschneria californica, but even I have to admit that they sure look like genus Epilobium when they are in fruit like this! They look like dandelion seeds with wings to fly - but I reckon the actual seeds are too large for that. See this photo for the fine flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Epilobium
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Epilobium

File.Name: hb02d_ha25-030.jpg
Notes: 38071329266 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working the new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family that have appeared on plants growing along East Camino Cielo road - well before the first winter rains. This one particular shrub just flowers early every year, though most plants won't flower until after the winter rains come. It's also a nice butt shot, so HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 October 2017)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; before
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; before

File.Name: hb03a_hb03-008.jpg
Notes:
38120540232 Here are two shots of a small wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) that I found perched on a spent seed capsule of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum, Onagraceae). I was looking for more stilt bugs, but I found this bee. Maybe this one is genus Andrena? These aren't perfect photos, but this was just about the only insect I saw on this gloomy day! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2017)

Today was mostly chilly, overcast, and breezy - not a good photo day, but I was happy to get photos of this bee. We actually had a few drops of rain today. It's not looking good for our chances of rain this weekend - but there's still a 30% chance on Saturday night.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Andrena; only
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Andrena; only

File.Name: hb03b_hb01-010.jpg
Notes:
38096196926 I found this tiny winged aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the same prickly pear cactus in our garden where I've been finding tiny spiders - see this photo and this photo and use the tufts of spines as a ruler. Once I found his one aphid, I looked close and found more. I've been wondering what the spiders are looking for on the cactus pads, and this may be the answer. The orange aphid is probably a Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii?) that has flown over from the milkweed plants on the other end of the garden. See this photo for proof that spiders really do go after aphids! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2017)

After posting this, I noticed a really tiny aphid nymph on the far side of the tuft of spines, I'll make a note. Counting pixels, it's only about 1/2 mm long. This puzzles me. Are aphids really attracted to the cactus as a food source??

File.Name: hb03c_ha29-021.jpg
Notes: 38119646692 Look close. Here are a pair of small Stilt Bugs (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on opening seed capsules of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum, Onagraceae). There's also the husk of another insect in the top-right that may be a third one. This stilt bug looks subtly different from our usual ones in this photo with a broader abdomen, but maybe that's just the angle of the shot. Once I noticed these, I noticed more - this seems to be a place they like. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2017)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Epilobium canum; Onagraceae

File.Name: hb03d_ha28-024.jpg
Notes: 24298889948 This is another tough-looking Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. We seem to have both large and small ones, and they all seem to have reddish eyes and an orange or red butt. This photo shows the eyes, see this photo for the other end of a different fly that looks the same. They are red on each end! Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but they are handsome flies - albeit tough and gnarly. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2017)

File.Name: hb04a_hb04-027.jpg
Notes:
38119733456 I noticed this small spider crawling along a line to this dead-looking stem of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae). Actually the plant is alive and you can see new green growth even though we're still waiting for our first proper rain. The spider sure looks like an Orb Web Weaver (Araneidae), but it's really tiny. The spider is about 580 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.3 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) It sat here for some time, and I finally left. I'm not sure if it's a spiderling or just tiny. I've had good luck finding tiny spiders lately. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2017)

Today started sunny, then turned cloudy, then turned sunny again. We were initially down for a 50% chance of rain tonight, but that turned into a 20% chance. And now it's cloudy again. That's our swinging (back and forth) autumn weather in a nutshell!

File.Name: hb04b_hb04-038.jpg
Notes: 26398891949 I found another tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on a seedhead of of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) along the road. Compare this photo and this photo of the same kind of small spider on the same plant. The spider is about 830 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3 mm, about 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) That's more than the 1/10 inch of my previous spiders - they've been growing! I've had good luck finding tiny spiders lately. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2017)

File.Name: hb04c_hb04-005.jpg
Notes: 26398498639 I found this mystery nest under a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) today. It feels rough, like excelsior, less than an inch long. One possibility is that it's the cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) - which I need to get a better photo of. On the other hand, my nest seems small for a moth. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2017)
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: hb04d_ha31-012.jpg
Notes: 24322495278 This is another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in plain sight on a dead leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). These crickets have such lobster-like faces. This one is missing its hind leg on the right (screen-left) side. Only the broad-winged males sing at night, but the narrow-winged ladies are there to be enchanted. They're still going at it. but I've only been hearing the crickets with a steady trill, not the pulsing "thermometer crickets" whose pulse rate depends on the temperature. Maybe it's too chilly for them? (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2017)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: hb05a_hb05-013.jpg
Notes:
37490859054 Here's a close shot of a remarkable little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging on its web attached to a spent leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), see my [Previous] photo and this photo. This spider is genus Tmarus, probably Tmarus angulatus. It's about 1/4 inch long fully extended, with a body about half that. It's also remarkable that its feet are blue, and what interesting eyes it has. This might be the best camouflage I've ever seen - or is it the caterpillar in this photo? (Or is the best camouflage that of all the critters I've never seen!) As before, I only noticed this spider because it was bouncing up and down on its web. Now that attention-getting behavior is NOT good camouflage! See my [Next] photo for a more distant shot. For some reason this photo reminds me of rabbits. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)

We did get a bit of rain last night, less than 1/10 inch of rain, mostly as drizzle. It's a start, but not enough to stir up the termites!
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Keckiella cordifolia; Plantaginaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus

Notes: 26424613289 I had another encounter with the remarkable little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging on its web attached to a spent leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Plantaginaceae), see this photo. This spider is genus Tmarus, probably Tmarus angulatus. This might be the best camouflage I've ever seen - or is it the caterpillar in this photo? (Or is the best camouflage that of all the critters I've never seen!) As before, I only noticed this spider because it was bouncing up and down on its web. Now that attention-getting behavior is NOT good camouflage! See my [Next] photo for a close shot. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)

File.Name: hb05c_hb05-022.jpg
Notes: 38145103916 This is a little Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) tucked into a sheltered place between leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it's a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata) and not the more common Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens), see this photo. It's been hiding out here for several days, and why not? It seems like a good shelter against rain and predators. I wonder if it will stay here all winter. These ladybugs were apparently introduced from Europe fairly recently (1973) to control aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)
CommonNames: Lady Beetle; Coast Live Oak; Seven-spotted Lady Beetle; Convergent Lady Beetle

File.Name: hb05d_hb05-010.jpg
Notes: 38144782176 Our native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) is dioecious with separate male (staminate) and female (pistillate) flowers on separate shrubs. This photo shows female flowers with raindrops from our small storm last night. Or rather they used to be female flowers, but now they are going to seed with the pappus hairs becoming the dandelion-like wings of the seeds. I like the way the raindrops act like little magnifying lenses. We got less than 1/10 inch of rain, mostly as drizzle. It's a start. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)

File.Name: hb06a_hb06-020.jpg
Notes:
38228966301 These are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They are proper tree nuts with a husk on the outside, then the hard shell, which you can crack to expose the nut meat inside. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in both woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers that smell like perfume, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting edible nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2017)

Today was pretty gloomy and breezy, though there were some moments of sunshine. Hah - there were also a few raindrops! There was some breeze today - not the kind of big wind that makes the trees dance, but the sort of frustrating breeze that wiggles the leaves and keeps everything in motion. That makes it hard to get my kind of photos.

File.Name: hb06b_hb05-023.jpg
Notes: 37517645034 Here are two shots of seedheads of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora, Asteraceae) taken before and after the small rain we had yesterday night. The composite plants are kind of weedy, mostly growing along roads, photo here - but they are native, and they do have their charms! Note that there is a difference in the seeds on the left. I believe the outer ring of seeds comes from the ray flowers - nearly glabrous and without the dandelion-like hairs that help them fly. The inner seeds come from the disk flowers - quite hairy and with the dandelion-like hairs. Spiders like these seedheads too, see this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)

File.Name: hb06c_hb02-021.jpg
Notes: 26451745379 This is a small (1/4-inch) twig of native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea) in the Adoxaceae plant family. I sliced the stem to show the pithy interior, and this is true of larger stems as well. These shrubs have always been associated with music because the pithy stems are easy to hollow out, and they make good flutes and clapper-sticks. The Greek name "sambuca" (like the genus name) apparently refers to a musical instrument made from this wood, so this association goes way back. This was my first thought for the Macro Mondays theme this week of musical instruments. It's more in keeping with my usual kind of photo, but then I had second thoughts and took a photo of my guitar instead, see my [Previous] photo. After all, it wouldn't do to show a spruce tree just because guitars have a spruce top! It's still interesting how the elder branches big and small have this pithy interior. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2017)

File.Name: hb06d_hb05-040.jpg
Notes: 24366504688 This is the headstock of my classic Gibson LG1 acoustic guitar. I've had it since the 1960s, and I'm pretty sure it dates back ten years before that. I like the cracks in the varnish - like an old oil painting. Sure, it's seen a lot of use through the years, though it sounds better than ever. It's a small-bodied guitar with a narrow neck and a bluesy sound. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Musical Instruments". The "Gibson" logo is 1-1/2 inches across. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2017)

File.Name: hb07a_hb07-033.jpg
Notes:
38222976652 This is a gorgeous little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm sure it's genus Phidippus, but I won't try to guess which of the 19 species in California it is. One mark of this genus is the "iridescent scales on their chelicerae" - green in this specimen. I'll have another photo of its abdomen for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group in a couple of days. This spider seems unusually large for a jumper, maybe 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)

It was mostly sunny today, with some breeze. We were originally forecast to get some more rain later this week, but no more. *Sigh* It's not looking good for our rainfall this season, see the report at the California Weather Blog.
ScientificName: Salticidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Phidippus
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Phidippus

File.Name: hb07b_hb07-052.jpg
Notes: 37543578954 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on an old seed capsule of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe the fly is genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane! The fly is about 1050 pixels long in my original photo at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 4.1 mm long - a bit over 1/4 inch. The larvae are said to be parasites of other insects, especially moths. BugGuide reports what I've seen, that the flies will often bob up and down above a flower prior landing to nectar. (Other bee flies seem to do this too.) I was surprised to find this fly at this time of year, but here it is. It's an attractive fly - if you get close enough to actually see it. Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Geron
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Geron

File.Name: hb07c_hb07-072.jpg
Notes: 38253703411 We had bit of rain a couple nights ago, and sure enough there are termites today! We had a hatch of these winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this afternoon. I found this one on a succulent Aloe leaf in the garden, about to take wing. Maybe these are the Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae)? They were emerging from the base of an almost-dead Ceanothus spinosus shrub that's pretty close to the house - but I've also been seeing flying termites elsewhere in the woods today, so I'm not too worried. It was a small rain, less than 1/10 inch. I reckon that means it's not so much the rain as the idea of rain that brings out the termites! We see these termites every year, see this photo from last year for an especially large hatch. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)

File.Name: hb07d_hb07-068.jpg
Notes: 38222006422 This is a still-green gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a stem (and bud?) of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). These galls are almost too perfectly round! These galls very quickly turn brown and drop to the ground, see this photo. These galls are about the size of a blueberry. Autumn seems to be their time. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: hb08a_hb08-005.jpg
Notes:
38243679672 This is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) working a new autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. It seems to be working the wrong end of the flower, but it's doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick, and it's the only way large bees like this could reach the nectar in these flowers that are typically visited by hummingbirds. Now look closer just to the right of the bee's face... There's a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) that also has its proboscis stuck into the base of the flower. Hah - there are a couple of aphids in there too, probably too small to see. There aren't a lot of insects in the woods right now as we move towards winter, but they are out there if you can find the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2017)

It was mostly cloudy this morning, and even looked like rain at times. We were originally forecast to get some more rain later this week, but no more. *Sigh* It's not looking good for our rainfall this season, see the report at the California Weather Blog.
CommonNames: Large Carpenter Bee; California Fuchsia; Stilt Bug
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: hb08b_hb08-018.jpg
Notes: 26498956329 Here is a bee-sized wild bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) flying into a late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). Maybe the bee is a Mining Bee in the genus Anthophora - maybe even species Anthophora californica? It's a handsome bee on a handsome flower. These plants are just about done for the year, but there are still a few flowers. These summer flowers are all important in our summer-dry climate because they keep the insects like this bee alive during the hardest time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2017)
CommonNames: wild bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Anthophora; Anthophora californica
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Anthophora; Anthophora californica

File.Name: hb08c_hb08-014.jpg
Notes: 38220290206 These are the odd new flowers of a native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. What look like skinny petals are really club-shaped style branches. They've been flowering for some time now, and some plants have gone to seed, but other plants are just starting. It's another of those late-season composite flowers in our woods that are are important for keeping the insects alive. I find it so interesting that there's always something flowering in our woods. The late-season composites and others like California Fuchsia overlap the early flowers of Chaparral Currant and Bigberry Manzanita in a way that almost seems like design to keep the pollinators alive. Could this be an adaptation of the ecosystem? (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2017)

File.Name: hb08d_hb07-011.jpg
Notes: 37563673004 Here are two different branches of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) greening up in the canyon. The plants look quite dead at first, but they are starting to show signs of new life. They are also bedecked with spider webs that looks something like rigging on a ship of the line. I don't know what kind of spider does this, but I do have another photo of a tiny spider on the same plants a few days ago here. Happy Web Wednesday! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)

File.Name: hb09a_hb09-009.jpg
Notes:
38265990702 I believe this is our usual winter dragonfly, a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) in the family Libellulidae, Odonata. It's perched on old seed heads of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I usually find these dragonflies hanging onto plants on cold days in winter, grounded by the weather. It wasn't that chilly today, but it did cool down at one point as clouds moved in, and I guess that was enough. Except for the days when they are grounded, these predators seem to be active all winter long looking for prey in sunny places. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2017)

As I said yesterday, there aren't a lot of insects in the woods right now as we move towards winter, but they are out there. Everything in the woods is waiting for real rain right now.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae

File.Name: hb09b_hb09-021.jpg
Notes: 38242493216 Here are two shots of a tiny Trash Line Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider with prey on its web. Its unfortunate prey appears to be some sort of Plant Bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), though not necessarily family Miridae - see this photo. These spiders are ridiculously small - just 745 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot , which comes to 2.9 mm, not quite 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figure.) They always have that odd bump on their abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd. These spiders decorate their webs with the bodies of their prey to make up the "trash line" of the web, which is surely the fate of this insect. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2017)
CommonNames: Trash Line Orb Weaver; Plant Bug

File.Name: hb09c_hb07-027.jpg
Notes: 37585541894 This is the bright orange rear-end of the gorgeous little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I show in this photo. It's on a stem of California Coffeeberry (Frangula californica aka Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm sure the spider is genus Phidippus, but I won't try to guess which of the 19 California species it is. This one actually has a "safety line" of webbing going from its spinnerets to the twig, but it's hard to make out. This spider seems unusually large for a jumper, maybe 1/2 inch long. This photo certainly counts as a butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)
ScientificName: Salticidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Phidippus
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Frangula californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Phidippus

File.Name: hb09d_hb04-043.jpg
Notes: 26521744749 This is a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in plain sight next to a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I think I recognize this cricket as the same one in this photo taken a few days before that is missing its right hind leg. It's in the same shrub, and I guess it's not traveling too far. The cricket won't fit in the boat but other critters do - see this photo and this photo. This is a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2017)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: hb10a_hb10-010.jpg
Notes:
24451152808 I'm pretty sure this is a bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) on late-season flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. Maybe it's genus Peleteria or Deopalpus? This fly is not much larger than a house fly, but it's much more interesting - what a bristly little beast it is! I expect to see lots of tachinid flies in Autumn, but they've been pretty scarce this year. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2017)

As I've been saying, there aren't a lot of insects in the woods right now as we move towards winter, but there are some. Everything is waiting for rain right now. Me too.
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Peleteria; Deopalpus
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Peleteria; Deopalpus

File.Name: hb10b_hb10-013.jpg
Notes: 26547479779 This is another small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging from a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. The general hairiness of the spider suggests that it is genus Mecaphesa - again. These spiders were common, but they are becoming less so as flowers fade with winter approaching. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2017)

File.Name: hb10c_hb10-015.jpg
Notes: 26547224819 Here are fluffy new seeds of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae) blowing in the wind - with a couple of Small Milkweed Bugs (Lygaeus kalmii, Lygaeidae) on the seed pods. I had my usual errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today so I stopped along the way to see how the milkweed is doing in the ranch land. The seeds are just about all dispersed, and the plants look quite bare. These were the only milkweed bugs I saw. I'm not sure what they're doing there unless they're looking for a ride! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 November 2017)
ScientificName: Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Lygaeus kalmii; Lygaeidae
associatedTaxa: Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Lygaeus kalmii; Lygaeidae

File.Name: hb10d_hb07-051.jpg
Notes: 24450439648 Here are two shots of the same tiny humpbacked fly that I show in this photo. I found it on the flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia, Asteraceae) on the right, then it moved over to an old seed capsule of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata, Rosaceae). It sat there for a while blowing bubbles! I believe the fly is a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) in the genus Geron. The genus name sounds like "gerontology" - imagine a humpbacked old man with a cane! Julie calls it a "Kokopelli Fly" - which is perfect. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)
CommonNames: California Aster; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Geron

File.Name: hb11a_hb11-024.jpg
Notes:
37632876054 I found another gorgeous Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) today - compare this photo of the spider I found last week. I'm sure both are genus Phidippus, but I won't try to guess which of the 19 species in California it is. One mark of this genus is the "iridescent scales on their chelicerae" - blue in this specimen, though iridescent colors sometimes switch between blue and green depending on your position. As before, this spider seems unusually large for a jumper, maybe 1/2 inch long. Hah - it seems to have blue toes, like tiny ballet slippers! See this photo for the bright orange rear-end of the other spider I found. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2017)

It almost feels like the days have been run off on a copy machine lately - sunny in the morning, then clouds move in with some breeze, then sunny again in the afternoon, highs in the mid-60s, lows in the 40s - day after day. That's pretty much our forecast for all next week, except for a chance of drizzle on Wednesday night. Everything is waiting for real rain right now. Me too.

File.Name: hb11b_hb11-032.jpg
Notes: 26571821429 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on flower clusters of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I'm still seeing a fair number of these butterflies - and not many other butterflies. I suspect this is part of their migration pattern. In some years, we have large spring migrations of these butterflies heading north from Mexico, so maybe these butterflies are heading home? The buckwheat flowers are on their way out, but there are still open flowers in there - just follow the butterfly's proboscis down to one of the few flowers that still has stamens. You can also see pollen stuck to the butterfly's proboscis from other flower visits - thus pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2017)

File.Name: hb11c_hb11-006.jpg
Notes: 38315437632 This is a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - crawling through dead leaves of a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This one seems unusually pale, compare the specimen in this photo. This one also didn't want to pose for me - it immediately turned tail and got away. Hah - it's got bug eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2017)
CommonNames: Bordered Plant Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: hb11d_hb11-009.jpg
Notes: 24475017778 This is a beautiful gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. It's my understanding that the galls start to grow in late spring and early summer, and adult wasps emerge in November and December - right about now. I've only seen a few of these pretty galls this year. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad years, but the wasps that induce them do. These galls are particularly beautiful, but why would a gall - the induced home of tiny wasp larvae - be so darned pretty? Doesn't that just attract the attention of predators? (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2017)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: hb12a_hb12-026.jpg
Notes:
38345608052 This is a cluster of late-season flowers on the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) shrubs by the mailbox showing that there are still nectar-bearing flowers. (Look for the pink stamens.) Did you notice the well-camouflaged little Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) guarding that one open flower just to the left of center? These spiders seem to be getting scarce now, but maybe I'm just missing them. They are that well camouflaged! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2017)

After saying yesterday that all the days are the same, today was different - overcast all day. There weren't a lot of photo opportunities today, but as I've said before: there's always something to photograph in the woods!

File.Name: hb12b_hb12-023.jpg
Notes: 38345359742 This is another small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) guarding flower clusters of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Like it's cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), this shrub is dioecious with separate male and female plants - this one is male.) The flowers seem to be all done. It must be tough being a crab spider when your shrub finishes flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2017)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; B. pilularis; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Mecaphesa; Thomisidae; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; B. pilularis; dioecious

File.Name: hb12c_hb12-015.jpg
Notes: 24504470208 I believe these are tiny scale-like pupae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. Or are they larvae? Or one pupa in the center with a white fringe surrounded by larvae? This is more than I usually find at one time. One possibility for ID is Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi), see p.44 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. You can also see the "hairy armpits" of this oak leaf where the main vein meets the side veins. I believe this is one sure-fire way to always recognize Coast Live Oak. I'll try to get back to this leaf to get a closer 1:1 photo, but see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2017)
CommonNames: Whitefly; Coast Live Oak; Stanfordís Whitefly
ScientificName: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi
associatedTaxa: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi

File.Name: hb12d_hb12-012.jpg
Notes: 38344544542 This leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) is rolled up like a fine cigar! It's somebody's home or nest, but I'm not sure whose. I didn't disturb it. It's just another little mystery in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2017)

File.Name: hb13a_hb13-002.jpg
Notes:
26628706299 I'm sure this is a group of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from the stump of a fruit tree in the garden at home. Here they are before the winter rains in this (so far) dry year. I guess these early mushrooms are like a preseason football game - they get your interest up! These mushrooms haven't opened yet to release their spores, but I have no doubt what they are - they were here in the same place last year too, see this photo. It's interesting that for gardeners, these mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "Oak Root Fungus". They seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2017)

It was downright windy this morning when I was out getting my photos. There's been hazy sunshine all day - not quite overcast, but not quite sunny either. We do have a chance of light rain on Thursday which will make these mushrooms happy!
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains

File.Name: hb13b_hb02-001.jpg
Notes: 37689551114 I believe this is some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushroom coming up in the garden, which is also close to Coast Live Oak trees. It has no particular odor. I've seen these early mushrooms before, see this photo. They certainly qualify as "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) - of little interest except here it is before the winter rains in this (so far) dry year. I guess this early mushroom is like a preseason football game! I got this photo over a week ago, and never got around to posting it. It goes with my [Next] photo, so here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2017)

File.Name: hb13c_hb13-013.jpg
Notes: 24532800248 Here are more late-season flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers are a bit less than 1/2 inch across, smaller than my thumbnail, see my [Previous] photo. The flowers are pretty enough, but they are usually scattered along the stems, so it's hard to get nice groupings. You can see on the left that the stems are densely white-tomentose with patchy white fuzz that wears away revealing the smooth stems underneath. The plants are highly variable, and apparently they have been partitioned into a number of distinct species. It's remarkable when they flower - at the end of our long dry summer and maybe even into the new year. They help keep the pollinators alive during a hard time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2017)

File.Name: hb13d_hb12-020.jpg
Notes: 38363829092 Here is a late-season flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family - in my grasp. The flowers are a bit less than 1/2 inch across, smaller than my thumbnail. See my next photo for more of the plant. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Fingertip(s)". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2017)

File.Name: hb14a_hb14-004.jpg
Notes:
37713280934 I found this odd capsule on a dead leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's about 1250 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.9 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I believe this is the pupal case of an insect, but I'm not sure what kind. (A little parasitic wasp comes to mind.) It's interesting that there are two exit holes where something got out - or is one of those an entrance hole where something got in?? I've found something similar before, see this photo. I didn't find anything large and exciting today, so I was forced to look close - and I did get my photos for the day. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2017)

It was sunny and calm this morning, but the wind kicked up in the afternoon. Our chance of rain on Thursday night is looking better, now "rain likely" - but this will be a small storm, not the real rain we need to reinvigorate the woods. It sounds like most of our rain is staying north in the wine country - where they don't need it after the terrible wildfires.

File.Name: hb14b_hb14-007.jpg
Notes: 37712842384 This is a gall on a small leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae). I'm not sure what critter makes these galls, as they are not mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). They do look something like the galls of Gall Midges (Cecidomyiinae, Diptera) in the families Asphondylia or Contarinia. I don't find a lot of these galls, but they are something you have to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2017)
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiinae; Diptera; Asphondylia; Contarinia
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiinae; Diptera; Asphondylia; Contarinia

File.Name: hb14c_hb14-018.jpg
Notes: 24556030788 Look in the lower-right. Here is an odd gall on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe it is a gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii aka Cynips douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but it sure doesn't have the typical mushroom shape of the galls in this photo. They are said to be quite variable. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Spined Turban Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Cynips douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: hb14d_hb13-006.jpg
Notes: 24555808488 Here are a pair of classic "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). (There is a also branch of California Bay in the background.) The galls are about the size of a golf ball. These galls start succulent and red, but they are starting to turn dry and brown as they age. The wasps that make these galls are apparently completely parthenogenetic, and neither males nor an alternate generation is known. These what most people think of when you mention galls, but there are many different kinds - like in my next two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2017)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: hb15a_hb15-005.jpg
Notes:
24576028238 Here is a Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Hymenoptera) doing "nectar theft" on an autumn flower of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat so that pollination might happen. Many insects seem to know this trick, and it's the only way large bees like this could reach the nectar in these flowers that are typically visited by hummingbirds. If you look close, you can see another slit in the base of the flower where another bee got in first. Now look closer at the top of the plant to the left of the bee's face... There's another little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) with its proboscis stuck into the ovary of another flower that will become a seedpod. Curiously, I got a very similar photo of both insects doing the same thing a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2017)

It stared calm and cloudy this morning, but the wind kicked up again. It was unusually warm, in the mid-70s at least. We still have an even chance of rain on Thursday night - but this will be a small storm, not the real rain we need to reinvigorate the woods. Most of the rain is staying north in the wine country - where they probably don't want it after the wildfires.
CommonNames: Large Carpenter Bee; California Fuchsia; Stilt Bug
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Hymenoptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera

File.Name: hb15b_hb15-016.jpg
Notes: 38446988831 Here is a little Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) with its proboscis stuck into a seed capsule of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. This one seems subtly different from the ones I find in the woodland at home, compare this photo - it has wider wings with speckles. It also has an interesting proboscis - jointed, like another leg. I'll see if I can get a closer crop to post tomorrow, but this photo shows something similar. Hah - there's a little green aphid in front of the bug, both are stem suckers. See my [Next] photo for another unexpected stilt bug. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2017)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: hb15c_hb15-030.jpg
Notes: 24575504848 This is another Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae, Hesperiidae) butterfly that I don't recognize working a late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae). See this photo for another one that looks similar. Maybe it's a very worn Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides)? I have a photo of a fresh specimen here. At first the butterflies look completely different - but maybe not. This is the first skipper butterfly I've seen for some time. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2017)
