File.Name: hb15d_hb14-014.jpg
Notes: 37559668265 This is webbing on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, but I don't think it's the work of a spider. I believe it's the same little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) that I show in this photo - at least they both skeletonize the leaf in the same way. (Look under that thick white webbing to the left of center.) We seem to have a number of critters that skeletonize the oak leaves, see this photo and this photo where the agent appears to be a fungus. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2017)

File.Name: hb16a_hb16-029.jpg
Notes:
24601148858 This is another small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I'm not sure what prey it expects to find on an oak leaf instead of a flower, but it is perched right in front of oak buds - and maybe that helps? These spiders seem to be getting scarcer now, but they are still out there. (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2017)

I reached a small milestone today - I've now posted 10,002 photos if Flickr's count is right! I started my Flickr account way back in 2004, but I've only been using it since February 13, 2013 - here is my first Flickr photo. Before that, I posted my almost-daily nature photos to Fotolog where I have another 9,250 photos dating back to June 21, 2003. I've been doing this for a while!

We did get a few drops of rain today - just a whisper of rain, like the damp edge of virga, that rain that doesn't quite make it to the ground. I'm hoping for more tonight - but not holding my breath.

File.Name: hb16b_hb16-004.jpg
Notes: 38441190892 A few weeks ago, I noticed a nocturnal Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, Arctiidae, Lepidoptera) laying eggs on the door screen before I went to bed, see this photo. The eggs hatched last night! These are actually tiny woolly bear caterpillars still on the door screen. They are about 500 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2 mm, less than 1/10 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) They can't live here, so Julie and I carefully got the screen free and leaned it against an oak tree that hangs over the porch. Maybe it worked - when I came back a few hours later, the caterpillars were all gone. I'll be looking for them next summer! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2017)
CommonNames: Painted Tiger Moth; woolly bear caterpillars

File.Name: hb16c_hb15-027.jpg
Notes: 37585576795 This is a tiny parasitic wasp showing its stinger-like ovipositor on a flower petal of native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. Not including its ovipositor, the wasp is about 850 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.3mm, about 1/8 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I number of these wasps were on the tubular flowers, mostly on the closed ovary-end. I'm not sure what kind of wasp it is. It's a pretty good butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2017)

File.Name: hb16d_hb11-008.jpg
Notes: 24600645498 A few days ago, I posted this photo of a Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - almost certainly Largus californicus - crawling through dead leaves of a stem of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Here is the same bug after it decided not to be photographed and ran for cover! It's a fine butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2017)
CommonNames: Bordered Plant Bug; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Largidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Largus californicus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: hb17a_hb17-011.jpg
Notes:
37776363914 This is another female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) hiding in plain sight next to a boat-like seed capsule of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). She looks large with eggs. This is not the same cricket I showed last week on the same plant in this photo - that one was missing its right hind leg. Only the broad-winged males sing at night, but the narrow-winged ladies are there to be enchanted. They're still going at it. but I've only been hearing the crickets with a steady trill, not the pulsing "thermometer crickets" whose pulse rate depends on the temperature. Maybe it's too chilly for them now. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2017)

Sure, this would be a nice photo for the Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group, but we're heading off to Oregon on Sunday to spend Thanksgiving with our kids and grandkids. We'll find some mushrooms too - maybe for the table, see this photo and this photo from a few years ago! We're still waiting for real rain here. Our little "storm" produced some wind today but no rain to speak of. Well, if the rain won't come to us, we'll go to the rain - in Oregon!

File.Name: hb17b_hb17-019.jpg
Notes: 37776144304 Here is another small Crab Spider (Mecaphesa, Thomisidae) lurking behind a flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae)! It's nothing lascivious, the spider is just waiting for some unsuspecting prey to come along. Most of the buckwheat flowers are finished now, but this spider has chosen its place well - note the pink stamens that show that the flower it is guarding still has something to offer insects. These spiders seem to be getting scarcer now, but they are still out there. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2017)

File.Name: hb17c_hb15-019.jpg
Notes: 38435336956 This is a very tight crop showing the mouthparts of the Stilt Bug (Berytidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) in this photo a couple of days ago. It's actually a 1:1 1066x800 pixel crop from a different 6000x4000 pixel photo of the insect. The mouthparts are remarkable - jointed, like another leg. Going from top to bottom, first there is the labrum, a small piece of skin that covers the top of the rest; then there is a bundle of thin stylets which contain food and salivary channels, but need support; finally there is the segmented labium which is jointed like a little leg, and supports the stylets. Put them all together, and they become the beak or rostrum of the bug. I don't suppose they have to think about how it all works anymore than we do! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2017)
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; labrum; stylets; labium; beak; rostrum

File.Name: hb17d_hb11-002.jpg
Notes: 38459105852 Here is another tough-looking Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Neither the common name nor scientific name are lovely, but they are handsome flies - albeit tough and gnarly. All insects have been scarce this week as we move towards winter, and this was my only keeper photo of a fly. I got this photo last Saturday - the day after Fly Day Friday. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2017)

File.Name: hb18a_hb18-011.jpg
Notes:
26734416589 This is the same group of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I showed last week in this photo. They look different now, more honey-colored for one thing. I thought there was no late-season sun in this north-slope place, but I was wrong. I managed to get this photo with some sunlight filtered through the oak trees. These mushrooms are growing from the stump of a fruit tree in the garden at home. Here they are before the winter rains in this (so far) dry year. I guess these early mushrooms are like a preseason football game - they get your interest up! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2017)

We're still waiting for real rain here. Our little "storm" produced some wind and a chill but no rain to speak of. Well, if the rain won't come to us, we'll go to the rain - to Oregon! Just two photos today, we're heading off to Oregon on Sunday to spend Thanksgiving with our kids and grandkids. We'll find some mushrooms too - hopefully for the table, see this photo and this photo from a few years ago! I'll return with photos in about two weeks.
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains

File.Name: hb18b_hb17-002.jpg
Notes: 37794409784 Our still-waiting-to-be-a-mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia, Oxyopidae) on a tall cactus plant in the garden is still waiting. I got my first photo of her on October 6 when her egg sac was brand new. Then I got this photo of her on October 28 for the end of Arachtober. Now here's my latest photo taken yesterday showing that she still there after six weeks on her egg sac. What devoted moms they are! She is hard to reach at about eight feet high in this spiny Prickly Pear (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus plant, so I brought in a ladder for this shot. Darn - I'll probably miss the hatch because we're heading off to Oregon tomorrow morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2017)

File.Name: hc04a_hc02-164.jpg
Notes:
27066140309 Here is a mass of ducks and geese taking wing at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. The literal upraising started in the back and worked its way towards us in layers. I believe the ducks are mostly Northern Pintails (Anas acuta) and the geese are White Geese - a mix of Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) and Ross's Geese (Anser rossii) that are hard to distinguish. This wonderful refuge is located south of Willows, California. Stopping there is always a highlight of the drive back from our Thanksgiving time in Oregon - see last year's photos here. There are many thousands of birds - 388,000 waterfowl at the last survey on November 20, and more still coming. I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge - from inside the car, a reasonable requirement at the refuge. See my previous few photos, which form a sequence. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, Glenn and Colusa Counties, California, 2 December 2017)

We got back from our trip to Oregon yesterday, and I still have most of my 500+ photos to sort out. Yep, lots of mushrooms - just as well since it's still dry here at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. The climate scientist Daniel Swain said today that "To date, Southern California has experienced one of its driest starts to the Water Year on record, and strikingly persistent West Coast ridging is now expected to last at least two weeks." David Swain started the talk about the Ridiculously Resilient Ridge in this post to his excellent California Weather Blog. Darn, we've had enough drought years for a while. It's odd - we were under a NWS Red Flag Warning yesterday and tonight the Santa Ynez Valley just down the hill is under a Freeze Warning!
CommonNames: Northern Pintails; White Geese; Snow Geese; Ross's Geese; Ridiculously Resilient Ridge
ScientificName: Anas acuta; Chen caerulescens; Anser rossii
associatedTaxa: Anas acuta; Chen caerulescens; Anser rossii

File.Name: hc05a_hc02-001.jpg
Notes:
23997143137 I believe this pretty mushroom in the Oregon woods is Floccularia albolanaripes aka Armillaria albolanaripes. I first thought this mushroom was Stropharia ambigua, but see this photo from a few years and notice Jacob Kalichman's comment. This mushroom is growing in the same place as that previous one. We didn't find loads of edible mushrooms in the Oregon woods for our Thanksgiving dinner this year, but I did find a good variety. This was my first keeper photo from our Oregon trip. I'll be featuring many more mushrooms in the still-dry-at-home days ahead at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2017)

I had no time for new photos today, but it's been dry and windy, with that horrific wildfire in the hills behind nearby Ventura. The power has been flickering on and off because the main power lines run through the fire zone. We don't get those nasty Santa Ana winds here in the Santa Barbara mountains because we don't have a direct path to the desert. We do get more than our share of wind, especially the Sundowner Winds, but not this time. It's curious - there are wildfires nearby and just down the hill the Santa Ynez Valley is under a Freeze Warning tonight!
ScientificName: Floccularia albolanaripes; Armillaria albolanaripes; Stropharia ambigua
associatedTaxa: Floccularia albolanaripes; Armillaria albolanaripes; Stropharia ambigua

File.Name: hc05b_hc02-075.jpg
Notes: 23996697097 I found this rope tied around a tree trunk in the Oregon woods - bedecked with moss and Cladonia lichens. I wish I knew how old the rope is as a way of dating the lichens, a kind of lichenometry. I suspect the rope is pretty old - look how the middle loop has made an indentation in the tree bark. Hah, the rope must be synthetic or it would have rotted away long ago. I noticed this tree along the gravel road to our cabin, which my parents built in the early 1970s. I reckon that serves as an upper bound to its age. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2017)

File.Name: hc05c_hc02-091.jpg
Notes: 38830611492 You can't go to the Oregon woods in autumn without finding at least one banana slug! This is a large spotted greenish Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) making tracks in the Oregon woods. You can see its eye-stalks and feelers on the left. These Oregon slugs look very different from the equally large California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) that we have at home, compare this photo from last year. There is some confusion about their names, but the difference seems clear to me. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2017)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slugs

File.Name: hc05d_hc02-094.jpg
Notes: 24988786758 I found this soggy Leaf-footed Bug (Coreidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) walking v-e-r-y--s-l-o-w-l-y on a cold but sunny morning along the railing of our deck in the Oregon woods last week. You can tell what it is by the leaf-like protrusions on its hind legs. This might well be the same Western Conifer Seed Bug (Leptoglossus occidentalis) that I found at home in the Santa Barbara mountains a month or two ago, see this photo. I didn't find a lot of insects during our recent Oregon trip, though I did find a few. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2017)
CommonNames: Leaf-footed Bug; Western Conifer Seed Bug

File.Name: hc06a_hc02-030.jpg
Notes:
24019344777 I believe this is a Cross Spider (Araneus diadematus, Araneidae) hanging on her perfect web in the Oregon woods. I found this spider deep in the woods, but they are said to be introduced from Europe, see here. I'm not sure I believe it. It's a beautiful web, so happy Web Webnesday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2017)

It's been sunny and calm today, though chilly. There's no immediate sign of the nasty Thomas Fire burning to the east of us in Ventura, but now there is concern that the fire is moving towards Santa Barbara County - see this source. The winds are expected to increase tonight and tomorrow which could quickly change everything. This might be a night to stay awake.

File.Name: hc06b_hc02-123.jpg
Notes: 25012715038 This is the striking web of an Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the Oregon woods. Look at that interesting medallion design in the center! I suspect it's made in a shrub of native Red Alder (Alnus rubra, Betulaceae) showing a few early catkins. There were other webs nearby that looked more filled in, but this one caught my eye. Happy Web Webnesday! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2017)

File.Name: hc06c_hc02-003.jpg
Notes: 24018768137 I thought this was a rather small Millipede (Diplopoda, Myriapoda) when I first saw it, about 3/4 inch long, with two pairs of legs on each body segment. I didn't realize that it is actually two millipedes until I had time to look closer at the photo. I'm pretty sure they are mating! It's a surprisingly tender moment, face-to-face with many legs wrapped around each other. I believe these are Flat-backed Millipedes in the order Polydesmida. There's a very similar photo of mating millipedes at BugGuide. They aren't insects or arachnids, but they are arthropods, and I reckon they are worth posting to the Insect Hump Day group - so HIHD! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2017)

File.Name: hc06d_hc02-070.jpg
Notes: 25011650558 This is a galled cane of "Wild Blackberry" in the Oregon woods - which is actually non-native Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor aka Rubus armeniacus) in the Rosaceae plant family. That blackberry is pretty ubiquitous in Oregon along roads at least, so it's pretty shocking to learn that it's actually not a native. The gall looks like it should be soft and gooey, but it's actually quite hard. If you look close, you can see a couple of grubs on the gall - I'll make a note. I'm not sure if those grubs are the inducers of the gall. One possibility is that this is the work of the Thimbleberry Stem Gall Wasp (Diastrophus kincaidii, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), though it looks lumpier and is larger. More likely, it is a Crown and Cane Gall caused by Rhizobium radiobacter and R. rubi bacteria. It's not beautiful, but it is interesting. (Baker Wayside, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2017)
CommonNames: Himalayan Blackberry; Thimbleberry Stem Gall Wasp; Crown and Cane Gall
ScientificName: Rubus discolor; Rubus armeniacus; Rosaceae; Diastrophus kincaidii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rhizobium radiobacter; R. rubi
associatedTaxa: Rubus discolor; Rubus armeniacus; Rosaceae; Diastrophus kincaidii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rhizobium radiobacter; R. rubi

File.Name: hc07a_hc07-002.jpg
Notes:
27127916049 Fire on the mountain - again!

This is looking at the backside of the Thomas Fire which has devastated Ventura county. It's getting closer to us. Most of the attention in Santa Barbara is on the ocean side of the wildfire to the right, where the fire is crossing the county line and threatening Rincon and Carpinteria. I took a drive along East Camino Cielo road today which follows the crest of the east-west Santa Ynez Mountains. It was too smoky to see anything on the ocean side, but I got this photo looking east along the north slope of the mountains. That low spot on the ridge in the back is the Murrieta Divide, and the fire appears to be burning up Divide Peak. Darn, that's too close to the upper Santa Ynez River drainage which starts just below the divide. I was surprised to see so much fire activity here - the city of Ojai must be surrounded. Now I hope we don't get surrounded too - with fire heading north and fire heading west along the coast - leaving us in the middle. I figure if smoke can get to us then so can burning embers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 December 2017)

The winds weren't as bad as expected today, but they were still there. Judging from the smoke, I reckon the winds were blowing towards the ocean on the right in Ventura, but then forming an eddy and moving back towards the mountains in our direction. The air has been smoky - rated "Hazardous" and "Very Unhealthy" in Santa Barbara, but not so bad up here in the mountains. I don't like this - I'd rather be posting mushroom photos from Oregon!

File.Name: hc07b_hc02-133.jpg
Notes: 25032536118 I only found a few of these Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus formosus) in the Oregon woods during our recent trip - after Thanksgiving, and not enough to harvest in any case. Compare this photo from a few years ago! This fungus is still known as Cantharellus cibarius in older books, but the species has now been split into a number of new species - including the fine Cantharellus californicus in this photo from home last spring. The Oregon Chantrelles are beautiful and tasty, as well as the official state mushroom of Oregon. A few years ago we found a bounty of mushrooms for our Thanksgiving feast - see this photo. We did a taste test and placed this one last - after the Black Chanterelles, Hedgehog mushrooms, and White Chanterelles (in that order). I suspect our California Chanterelles would fare better. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2017)
ScientificName: Cantharellus formosus; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantharellus californicus
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus formosus; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantharellus californicus

File.Name: hc07c_hc02-029.jpg
Notes: 38903499521 This is a somewhat contorted White Chanterelle (Cantharellus subalbidus) mushroom growing in the Oregon woods. This is another one that I only found after Thanksgiving, and only this one. They look like ordinary chanterelles, but are white instead of orange. The "gills" are really thick folds of tissue rather than the thin gills of other mushrooms. They are edible and delicious - if you have the right one! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2017)

File.Name: hc07d_hc02-144.jpg
Notes: 25031677418 I only found a few of these Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week - after Thanksgiving, and not enough to harvest in any case. Compare this photo and this photo from 2014! They are absolutely delicious when there are enough of them to collect. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2017)

File.Name: hc08a_hc02-059.jpg
Notes:
38209447244 I found this impressive Conifer Coral Hericium (Hericium abietis) "mushroom" growing from a conifer log along the North Umpqua River. It's about the size of a volleyball. (Mushroom author David Arora reports one giant specimen weighing 100 pounds!) It's said to be edible and delicious, but I'd rather admire its cascades of spore-bearing "icicles". A cousin of this fungus is the Lion's Mane Mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) in this photo. That one is smaller and grows on hardwood trees like oak. These fungi used to be grouped with other tooth fungi in the family Hydnaceae, but now it's in its own group of Hericiaceae. Those darn mycologists! (Baker Wayside, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2017)

I have no update on the Thomas Fire tonight, though it's certainly on my mind. It was too smoky to see anything to the east or south today, and news is scarce. The fire seems to stand at 132,000 acres and just 10% contained. That's 200 square miles since Tuesday! The most recent fire map I've seen is this one. If you zoom in, you can see that the fire is especially active to the north - near the Murrieta Divide and Matilija Canyon and going up Highway 33 into the backcountry. The good news is that firefighters seem to be holding a line along Highway 150 near Carpinteria. I'm worried that the fire has come into the upper Santa Ynez River drainage, but it's too smoky to see.
CommonNames: Conifer Coral Hericium; Lion's Mane Mushroom
ScientificName: Hericium abietis; Hericium erinaceus; Hydnaceae; Hericiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hericium abietis; Hericium erinaceus; Hydnaceae; Hericiaceae

File.Name: hc08b_hc02-143.jpg
Notes: 27148049209 I found this strange and beautiful Coral Mushroom in the genus Ramaria in the Oregon woods last week, like something under the ocean. It's about the size of a paperback book. These fungi are easy to recognize and hard to identify - I won't try to guess which one it is. I always seem to find these in Oregon during our Thanksgiving visit, but this year I really had to hunt before I found just this one. I found a good variety of mushrooms this year, but the numbers were down. I didn't notice at the time, but there's a little slug in there - I'll add a note. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2017)

File.Name: hc08c_hc02-048.jpg
Notes: 38924253231 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on the frond of a fern in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure what kind of fly it, and I won't try to guess. I was actually surprised to find any insects in Oregon, but it was actually fairly warm, at least during our first week there. It did cool down after that, but it never did freeze. HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2017)

File.Name: hc08d_hc02-131.jpg
Notes: 25052541688 I found this good-sized fly on conifer bark near an old clearcut in the Oregon woods. It has metallic colors on its abdomen, so I guess it's some kind of Blow Fly in the family Calliphoridae, Diptera - maybe genus Calliphora? It seemed odd to find it in the wilds, but there it was. It's actually pretty well camouflaged. HFDF! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2017)

File.Name: hc09a_hc09-001.jpg
Notes:
38061873045 This is the morning sun looking ominous through an oak tree in the woods with smoke from the Thomas Fire in the air. This photo is straight from my camera with some cropping - I did nothing to enhance the color. (I tried, but everything made it less dramatic!) It did clear up a bit later in the day. Yep, I had my "N95 Particulate Respirator" mask on this morning. Beautiful but eerie. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2017)

*Sigh* It's hard to find real information about the fire. The fire map seems to show increasing activity on the western edge of the fire which has been threatening the upper Santa Ynez River drainage, but it doesn't seem to have crossed into Santa Barbara County yet. The city of Ojai looks just about surrounded! The situation looks better along the coast at Rincon and Carpinteria, though I worry about the fire burning down from the top of the mountains. I guess it all depends on the wind now. One new development is that our part of the Los Padres National Forest is now closed. There's a map of the closure here.

File.Name: hc09b_hc02-055.jpg
Notes: 24082831627 Here are a pair of little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family in the Oregon woods. I believe they are growing on a small Red Alder (Alnus rubra, Betulaceae) tree on the edge of the North Umpqua River. These "mushrooms" have tiny "eggs" inside, which are really spore sacs called peridioles. The mushrooms are "splash cups" - raindrops knock the eggs out of the nest to scatter the spores. The cap on the right still has a cover, and it looks for all the world like a tiny baked pie! There are many different kinds of these cute fungi, so I'll leave it at that. (Baker Wayside, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2017)

File.Name: hc09c_hc02-017.jpg
Notes: 27169887059 Here is a triptych of different ascomycete mushrooms from the Oregon woods last week. On the left is a Fluted Black Elfin Saddle mushroom, distinctive with a fluted stem and black cap. I've always called this mushroom Helvella lacunosa, but that species has now been split up. This one might be Helvella vespertina which is said to favor conifers like Douglas Fir. I guess the mushroom one in the middle is the Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle (Helvella maculata). It looks similar to the first one except for the color of the cap. The mushroom on the right is different. It might be the might be the Hooded False Morel (Gyromitra infula). It's growing on buried wood. I wouldn't want to eat any of these mushrooms, but the one is the right might be downright poisonous, containing a substance that metabolizes into MMH (monomethylhydrazine) - rocket fuel! All of these mushrooms are ascomycetes rather than more typical basidiomycetes. They have a different way of producing spores, and a different "look" that makes them recognizable. There's more info on the web, e.g. the discussions of Ascomycota and Basidiomycota at Wikipedia. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2017)
CommonNames: Fluted Black Elfin Saddle; Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle; Hooded False Morel
ScientificName: Helvella lacunosa; Helvella vespertina; Helvella maculata; Gyromitra infula; ascomycetes; basidiomycetes
associatedTaxa: Helvella lacunosa; Helvella vespertina; Helvella maculata; Gyromitra infula; ascomycetes; basidiomycetes

File.Name: hc09d_hc02-008.jpg
Notes: 38946818451 Here are a pair of odd Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushrooms growing under (mostly) conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet, but I haven't tried them. They look a bit like narrow Chanterelles, but they are related to the Coral mushrooms I showed yesterday. I only found a few this year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2017)

File.Name: hc10a_hc10-017.jpg
Notes:
38086226175 I found this fragment of a leaf made of ash caught on a winter-dead fern frond. This is part of the ash fall from the Thomas Fire which is burning out of control to the east of us. The leaf would fall apart with the slightest touch. The color cast is due to all the smoke in the air, see this photo from yesterday. I find something poignant about these remnants of a forest that is no longer there. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2017)

The wildfire is coming, sending a flurry of ashes ahead. I reckon it's still about 14 miles away, this fire has been moving very fast. According to a press conference that just ended, the fire has burned over 200,000+ acres so far making it the fifth largest fire in California history. That's over 300 square miles. Everything depends on the wind now. Only these two photos today, and I don't know if I'll be able to post tomorrow. We spent the day working around the house and getting our evacuation stuff organized. We're not under evacuation orders yet - not even a warning, though that might change later tonight. It's kind of liberating to go through the motions of packing to evacuate and realize how little is really important.

File.Name: hc10b_hc10-027.jpg
Notes: 38256392224 I found this tiny twig on our deck railing this afternoon. It's nothing but ash that ended here up here from the Thomas Fire which is burning out of control east of us. I mushed one end of the twig with my ballpoint pen to show what it's made of - it falls apart at the slightest touch. It also has a 1.0 mm ball, so it gives scale. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Stick". The wildfire is coming. It's only Sunday here in California, but I don't know if I'll be able to post tomorrow, so here it is. HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2017)

File.Name: hc11a_hc11-020.jpg
Notes:
38996236631 This is a fragment of a chaparral leaf made of ash caught in a spider web on a leaf of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). This is part of the ash fall from the Thomas Fire which is burning out of control to the east of us. The ash leaf would fall apart with the slightest touch. It almost looks like a Ceanothus leaf - C. megacarpus or C. cuneatus? I find something poignant about these remnants of a forest that is no longer there. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2017)

Today was a quiet fire day - a welcome change after yesterday. We're still at home and not under evacuation orders or warning. The fire has slowed down, though that could change in a moment depending on the wind. The smoke was even better today. We already did our packing yesterday, so I went for as walk today with my camera and found ... lots of ashes. We've basically had no rain for the water year starting on September 1. It's the driest water year on record, at least since records started on San Marcos Pass in 1966. (You can see our rain history here.) It's been 200+ days since our last real rain, and it might not rain again until the new year. *Sigh* But there's always something to photograph in the woods - today it was lots of ashes. Well, if ashes fall from the sky, photograph ashes!
Locality: You can see our rain history here.) It's been 200+ days since our last real rain
ScientificName: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; C. megacarpus; C. cuneatus
associatedTaxa: Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; C. megacarpus; C. cuneatus

File.Name: hc11b_hc11-014.jpg
Notes: 38995837481 These are dead seedheads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) strung together by spiderwebs which have caught ashes from the Thomas Fire which is still burning out of control to the east of us. Nothing in the woods holds the fire ashes intact as well as spider webs. I don't know what kind of spider makes these webs, but I find something similar on dormant stems of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae), see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2017)
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: hc11c_hc11-006.jpg
Notes: 38995599351 Here are ash fragments from the Thomas Fire caught in a spider web in a still summer-dormant plant of Deerweed. For some reason, these webs collect ash from the wildfire like nothing else - but they do a good job of collecting dewdrops too, see this photo. It's probably the web of a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) or a little Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae). (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2017)

File.Name: hc11d_hc11-022.jpg
Notes: 25124413748 Here is a desiccated liverwort growing on a vertical sandstone rock surface in the canyon - with ash from the Thomas Fire. I believe this one is the Orobus-seed Liverwort (Targionia hypophylla, Marchantiophyta). It looks dead, but it's just dormant - ready to come back to life with a bit of rain, like in this photo from a different December. That's the problem. Every other year since I started posting my photos, I've found liverworts, mushrooms, and other creatures that the rain brings in the woods by this time of year. Not this year. We've basically had no rain for the water year starting on September 1. It's the driest water year on record, at least since records started on San Marcos Pass in 1966. (You can see our rain history here.) The ashes from a December wildfire is another consequence of the general dryness. It's been 200+ days since our last real rain, and it might not rain again until the new year. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2017)

File.Name: hc12a_hc12-009.jpg
Notes:
25148963438 There are still new flowers on this shrub of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - but it's getting pretty "long in the tooth" as they say. This shrub has been flowering since (at least) October 12, see this photo of the same shrub. Other shrubs haven't even started to flower yet, and a few more shrubs have shriveled buds that won't develop. We've basically had no rain for the water year starting on September 1. It's the driest water year on record, at least since records started on San Marcos Pass in 1966. It's an odd year - and a dangerous one. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2017)

The monster Thomas Fire is still moving slowly west along the Santa Ynez Mountains - towards us on San Marcos Pass. Now it's moved past Toro Canyon into Romero Canyon, see this live map. We're still at home. The fire stands at 234,000 acres and 20% contained. Politics and wildfire is too much - I'd rather be chasing liverworts and mushrooms!

File.Name: hc12b_hc07-020.jpg
Notes: 27241956139 Here are still-fresh flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is a perennial/subshrub that loves to grow along roads. That's been it's downfall this year because an over-zealous county road crew wiped out everything along the mountain roads last summer - at least in our part of the mountains. I got this photo on the far end of East Camino Cielo where the road crews never got to - where I had driven to get this photo of the then-new Thomas Fire which was threatening the city of Ventura. Now it's threatening Santa Barbara. The shrub in my photo may well have burned by now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 December 2017)
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc12c_hc07-014.jpg
Notes: 38132704265 These are new catkins on a shrub of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. The actual flowers aren't open yet, but they are getting close - see this photo for the actual flowers. I got this photo last Thursday on the far end of East Camino Cielo - where I had driven to get this photo of the then-new Thomas Fire which was threatening the city of Ventura. Now it's threatening Santa Barbara. The shrub in my photo may well have burned by now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 December 2017)

File.Name: hc12d_hc12-006.jpg
Notes: 24154703627 This is the underside of a leaf of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The leaves of Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) are very similar. I'm not sure, but I think that this might be the same kind of leaf as the burned ash leaf in my previous photo. I hope this leaf - and our house - doesn't share the same fate. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2017)

File.Name: hc13a_hc13-010.jpg
Notes:
38328605394 This is another beautiful gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. It's my understanding that the galls start to grow in late spring and early summer, and adult wasps emerge in November and December - right about now. I've only seen a few of these pretty galls this year. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad years, but the wasps that induce them do. These galls are particularly beautiful, but why would a gall - the induced home of tiny wasp larvae - be so darned pretty? Doesn't that just attract the attention of predators? I don't think of these as plant pests - they are more like decorations! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2017)

Today seemed to be a pretty quiet day for the Thomas Fire that is burning in our mountains to the east of us. There is concern that winds will kick up Friday and Saturday. Backfires are planned. We're still at home. Life goes on.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: hc13b_hc12-018.jpg
Notes: 38158416635 Insects and wildflowers are scarce in the woods as we wait and wait for our usual winter rains ... but it's a peak time for galls! This one is growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) which is the host for many different galls. I'm sure it's the gall of a tiny Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but I don't think I've ever noticed it before. It's probably an old gall of the Urn Gall Wasp (Phylloteras cupella) - see the account at BugGuide. It's small, only about 1/8 inch high. Like I've said before, there's always something to photograph in our woods - but sometimes you have to really hunt for it! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2017)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Cynipid Wasp; Urn Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Phylloteras cupella
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Phylloteras cupella

File.Name: hc13c_hc12-003.jpg
Notes: 39007447382 Here are more ash fragments of chaparral leaves caught in a spider web on an old flower stalk of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). This is part of the ash fall from the Thomas Fire which is still burning out of control to the east of us. The intact ash leaf would fall apart with the slightest touch, breaking up into smaller and smaller fragments, finally becoming dust. The big leaf kind of looks like another Ceanothus leaf - compare this photo of a living leaf. I find something poignant about these ashy remnants of a forest that is no longer there. Nothing in the woods holds the fire ashes intact as well as spider webs, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2017)

File.Name: hc13d_hc09-022.jpg
Notes: 38327083534 This is the web of an Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in between dead seedheads of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) - with ashes from the Thomas Fire. This web is small, just a few inches across. It might be the web of a tiny Trashline Orb Weaver (genus Cyclosa) spider, but there's no sign of the usual "trashline" that's visible in this photo so I'm not sure. I got this photo last Sunday - a day with ominous skies with the wildfire poised to start its run into Santa Barbara County. It's still burning and causing new flurries of ash. Nothing in the woods holds the fire ashes intact as well as spider webs, so happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2017)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver; Black Sage; Trashline Orb Weaver

File.Name: hc14a_hc14-011.jpg
Notes:
25194951798 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with it's head buried in a single flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). I actually wasn't sure if the spider was even alive, so I got a few photos and then poked it with a grass stem - it's alive! It's also a male with enlarged palps. I got this photo after I poked it. I believe the spider is a Ground Crab Spider in the genus Xysticus, see this photo. Insects and spiders are scarce in our woods right now. Everything is waiting for our winter rains which are late this year - instead of rain we have the terrible Thomas Fire burning out of control in the mountains east of us! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2017)

Today seemed to be another pretty quiet day for the Thomas Fire from our vantage up here in the mountains to the west. The wildfire stands at 242,500 acres and 30% contained - now the 4th largest fire in California history, eclipsing the Zaca Fire a few years ago in the backcountry. Hah, it took the Zaca Fire eight weeks to burn that much, and the Thomas Fire has done it in not much more than eight days! The winds were calm today, but there is concern that they will kick up tonight and Saturday. A firefighter from San Diego was killed in the line of duty. A large backfire happened in San Ysidro Canyon today. We're still at home. Life goes on.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; California Buckwheat; Ground Crab Spider

File.Name: hc14b_hc14-024.jpg
Notes: 27284091659 This photo is odd for a couple of reasons. It shows a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). It's odd because these insects are predators and this one has its proboscis fully inserted into the flower. My first thought was that this is starvation behavior because insect prey are so scarce right now - but I really don't know. This photo is also odd because of the plant. I usually find this shrub by the river in late summer, but I found this one today along Stagecoach Road up in the mountains. I suspect it's here because of seed carried by road-working equipment. (This wouldn't be our only transported plant, see this photo.) It might be flowering unseasonably late because the plants were cut down in early summer by a county road crew, and are flowering as soon as they can. It's an odd but nice photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2017)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc14c_hc14-023.jpg
Notes: 39060929971 This is a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) sticking its rear end up in the air on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). I usually find this shrub by the river in late summer, so it's odd to find the flowers now in this place along Stagecoach Road up in the mountains - but here it is. You can see a bit of the assassin's proboscis below its head, but it's not looking for prey - see my [Next] photo. This photo shows the insect's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2017)
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc14d_hc09-011.jpg
Notes: 39060741161 I guess this is some sort of Plant Bug (Heteroptera, Hemiptera), maybe family Miridae but maybe not. It's perched on a late-season flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers are only 1/2 inch or less across, smaller than my thumbnail, see

File.Name: hc15a_hc15-022.jpg
Notes:
25214135708 I went back to the same place and found this Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii, Reduviidae) on the same flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) where I found it yesterday - see this photo. I usually find this shrub in flower by the river in late summer, but here it is along Stagecoach Road up in the mountains today. You can see the bug's spike-like proboscis folded under its head. These insects are normally predators, see this photo, though my photo yesterday shows its proboscis deep in a flower. Go figure! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2017)

There was some guarded good news about the Thomas Fire at the usual 4:00 news conference today - apparently the fire is holding in San Ysidro Canyon, and the fire is starting to run into the scars of the earlier Tea Fire and Zaca Fire which will slow it down. There is concern about the winds which are forecast to pick up later tonight. The fire stands at 252,500 acres. We're still at home. Life goes on.
ScientificName: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus renardii; Reduviidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc15b_hc15-001.jpg
Notes: 39080209681 I found this mystery nest on a twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) today - some nice lichens too. The nest is about one inch long. The upper part in my photo feels rough, like excelsior, and the white lower part feels smooth, like down. I think this might be the pupal cocoon of a Western Tussock Moth (Orgyia vetusta, Lymantriidae, Lepidoptera) - and the lower white part is what's left of the female moth that emerged! The female moths are born without wings and are really just living sacks of eggs that mate with a winged male, lay their eggs, and die. Compare this photo at BugGuide. I may have another photo here showing the same thing, though I didn't realize what it was at the time. Of course, I might be wrong about this. It's interesting whatever it is. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2017)
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Orgyia vetusta; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: hc15c_hc15-018.jpg
Notes: 39049661262 I found this odd pile of little rocks in a shrub of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It was about head high in the shrub, cemented together into a solid mass. Actually, I found it last August - see this photo. It hasn't changed much in nearly half a year. This might be the work of a Resin Bee in the family Megachilidae, maybe genus Dianthidium or Anthidiellum - but it's so big! I occasionally find small nests that look like the work of a terrestrial caddisfly, see this photo - but I've never found anything this big, maybe spanning two inches along the branch. I'm not sure what it is. That's a lot of rocks to carry up to this branch! I'll keep checking in now and then to look for changes. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2017)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Megachilidae; Dianthidium; Anthidiellum
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Megachilidae; Dianthidium; Anthidiellum

File.Name: hc15d_hc09-023.jpg
Notes: 38198444135 I found this tiny fly perched on the tip of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The fly is about 1100 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 4.3 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I suspect it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, Diptera. Compare this photo and note the nearly spherical heads and skinny necks. After I noticed one of the flies, I noticed a few more perched on other mugwort leaves. These insects would perch on a leaf, then fly of, and return a moment later - typical behavior for a predator, though I never noticed any prey. I usually find these flies later in the winter when the willows flower, so this one seems early. I've been saving this photo since last Saturday for HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2017)

File.Name: hc16a_hc16-syp-e-mobo-c.jpg
Notes:
39067912622 This is a view of the Thomas Fire burning in the mountains behind Santa Barbara taken by an automated HPWREN webcam located on top of Santa Ynez Peak. The URL for this webcam is hpwren.ucsd.edu/cameras/SYP.html. The area around the antennas was also burned by the Whittier Fire last July, see this photo. I captured this webcam image around 1:00 pm today. I got other nice images later in the day, but they all have more smoke which obscures the details. The smoke plume has the white cap of a pyrocumulus cloud. Yep, wildfires can make their own rainclouds, see this photo for more info! We live in the low pass between the smoke and the first ridge towards the camera. The fire is still about 9 miles away, but that's too close for comfort. (West Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 December 2017)

Damn, it's been a busy day! We actually had a few raindrops last night. I got up in the middle of the night and saw that it was cloudy and calm - good. I checked the MODIS/VIIRS heat map and it seemed calm - very good! I went back to bed. I found out later that the calm was temporary and the heat map had stopped updating during the night. There were 40-60 mph winds all day, and the wildfire is dangerously out of control. The fire stands at 267,500 acres and 40% contained. There are 8,450+ firefighters and 973 fire engines on the job - though that's the whole fire, which stretches all the way to the upper Sespe and Fillmore. There have been 16,000 people evacuated in Santa Barbara and another 34,000 placed under voluntary evacuation. The winds are slowly diminishing tonight, but then Santa Ana winds will kick up again in Ventura. Fortunately, we don't get those winds in Santa Barbara, and we might actually get a few days of calm. For some reason, we remain out of any evacuation zone. We're still at home, but it's a dangerous situation.

(Update - I just heard on KEYT Channel 3 TV that we are now under Voluntary Evacuation. I'm not surprised. We're ready to evacuate if we really need too.)

File.Name: hc17a_hc17-009.jpg
Notes:
38238899295 This looks like a nut or a gall that some critter has gotten into, but I believe it is actually the husk of an odd insect - a small Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Has this one been parasitized? It feels hard like it has a shell. I found this one on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It seems to match the species Allokermes essigii in these photos that I found on the web. Unlike some other scale insects, I usually find these alone or in small groups. They don't seem to do any damage to the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2017)

Just three photos today, because that's what I got. It's amazing that the firestorm of the Thomas Fire yesterday only claimed 15 structures and only two homes. Thank you firefighters! The fire seems quiet today - even the MODIS/VIIRS heat map looks quiet in our end of the wildfire. It will hopefully stay calm for a couple of days when another blast of north winds are expected. Hah - it was mostly calm today except for the occasional gust - as though to remind us of who's in charge! The firefighters are working to hold the fire east of Gibraltar Road and south of East Camino Cielo. There was lots of air traffic over our house today - hopefully to knock the fire down while the winds oblige. The highway has been closed for a few days to let the fire trucks use it, but it's open again this evening. I feel cautiously optimistic, but it all depends on the wind.

I want to put in a good word for our local KEYT Channel 3 TV which has had non-stop coverage of the Thomas Fire. They deserve awards. And here's a cockpit video of a C-130 plane doing a drop on the fire!
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Allokermes essigii

File.Name: hc17b_hc17-015.jpg
Notes: 38410497454 This is another beautiful gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). If you compare the similar gall in this photo on the same shrub, you can see the variation in color - usually they are red, but this one is at least half green. The pointed beak of these galls is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2017)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: hc17c_hc17-003.jpg
Notes: 25253399558 Here are open seedpods - and seeds - of a dead-looking Prickly Phlox (Linanthus californicus aka Leptodactylon californicum, Polemoniaceae) plant in the woods. Some plants are starting to green up, see this photo, but other plants look quite dead. I was surprised to find intact seeds after the wind ripped through everything yesterday, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2017)

File.Name: hc18a_hc18-017.jpg
Notes:
25280823898 This is a small Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae,Neuroptera) insect on a yellowed leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They have amazing eyes, though this one got away from me before I could get closer. It seems that I mostly see these at night on the bathroom window screen, but I not sure if they are properly nocturnal. Sure, this photo could be better, but I'm happy to find any insects in the woods right now. We're having an unprecedented "winter drought" without our usual winter rains. Everything in the woods is waiting ... or burning! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)

The winds are down and the Thomas Fire remains quiet - except for a large backfire in the upper Sespe River area 20+ miles away. Fire crews have held the fire east of Gibraltar Road and south of East Camino Cielo, and now they're eliminating hot spots while they can. I feel cautiously optimistic, but it all depends on the winds that might kick up on Wednesday afternoon and undo everything. The fire stands at 270,500 acres. We're still at home. Life goes on.

File.Name: hc18b_hc18-002.jpg
Notes: 27368249639 This is a tiny fly (Diptera) perched on a single floret of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae). See this photo for a different insect on a whole flower head of the same kind. The fly is only about 375 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 1.5 mm, about 1/16 inch. (See this photo for how I figured.) I believe that's a winged aphid to the screen-right of the fly. I have no idea what kind of fly it is. Hah - the thing about these tiny flies is that they look just like larger flies - but tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)

File.Name: hc18c_hc18-013.jpg
Notes: 38264790725 I believe these are tiny scale-like larvae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of a leaf of an interesting oak that I'm still not sure of - maybe Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or Quercus wislizeni. (See my [Previous] photo for another photo and some discussion.) One possibility for ID is Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi), see p.44 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. I usually find these as shiny disk-like pupae that lack the white decorations, see this photo. You can see a couple of them on the very left. The oak is interesting too. Note that it doesn't have the "hairy armpits" of Coast Live Oak, see the leaf in the last link. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)
ScientificName: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Tetraleurodes stanfordi
associatedTaxa: Aleyrodidae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Tetraleurodes stanfordi

File.Name: hc18d_hc18-024.jpg
Notes: 39115205282 I believe this is the gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but it's not growing on the usual Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Instead, this gall is on an interesting oak that I'm still not sure of - maybe Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei). Another possibility is Quercus wislizeni - the names have been regarded as synonyms. This is an interesting native shrub or small tree that has leaves that are toothed or not, shiny and smooth underneath, and thin acorn scales. See my [Next] photo for a look under the leaf. It's interesting that this gall occurs on both this oak and Coast Live Oak see this photo. These galls are similar but different from the galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Shreve Oak; California Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Andricus quercuscalifornicus
