File.Name: hc19a_hc19-025.jpg
Notes:
39140299122 I found this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the inside of the sliding glass door to the upstairs porch this afternoon. I believe it is the species Thiodina hespera aka Colonus hesperus with those conspicuous dark areas around its eyes which makes them look much larger. I have another photo here. Many jumping spiders look alike, but this one is distinctive. Hah - I had to chase this one for a while. It jumped away before I got the photo I really wanted, but this one will do. It's nice to have interesting spiders come to me! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2017)

Today was another favorable day for controlling the Thomas Fire which has been burning to the east of us, but there was some wind, mostly from the south. I think of this as our rain wind, and there is a dry low-pressure system that's due to come through the area tomorrow. We probably won't get any rain, but we're expected to get sundowner-type north winds. When the fire in your fireplace burns down to ashes, you can take a bundle of newspaper and "fan the flames" by waving the papers back and forth - actually you fan the ashes to bring up the flames. It works! This is the concern with the Thomas Fire - it seems quiet now, but the winds might stir it up into flames again. On the other hand, the firefighters really have knocked the fire down. We'll see what happens...

File.Name: hc19b_hc19-002.jpg
Notes: 39139940452 Here are dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) waiting for a gust of wind before they head off into the world. These shrubs produce huge numbers of seeds. They are everywhere now. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2017)

File.Name: hc19c_hc18-006.jpg
Notes: 39139722532 Here is an odd flower of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - odd because of when it is flowering. I'm not sure if this is the first of the new or the last of the old. I suspect it's an old one that's flowering unseasonably late because the plants were cut down in early summer by a county road crew, and are flowering as soon as they can. This one is dusted with ashes from the Thomas Fire. You can see one of the real tubular flowers poking out on the left at about the 10 o'clock position. If you look close, you can see that the real flower is green and the rest of it is showy red bracts. I have found new flowers of Woolly Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) this early, but not this year - they are still waiting for real rain which is late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)

File.Name: hc19d_hc18-012.jpg
Notes: 38289917225 Here's a closer photo of (I believe) the tiny scale-like larvae of a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the underside of an oak leaf - see this photo from yesterday. They are tiny, less than a millimeter across. One possibility for ID is Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi), see p.44 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF), a document worth downloading. I believe the actual critters are shiny black disks as in this photo, but they decorate themselves with a waxy white exudate. Each one seems to sit in a little puddle. The oak is interesting too, see my photo from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)

File.Name: hc20a_hc20-007.jpg
Notes:
24326067477 These are old tumor-like aphid galls on leaves of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). The galls are caused by Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The galls are rather pretty when fresh, see this photo, but they look abandoned now. Specialized aphids used to live inside those pockets on the leaves, but no more. It's curious that there are also galls of the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) that look very similar - I don't understand this. Is there a connection? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2017)

The National Weather Service was right about the wind today. It started before noon today and has been blowing all afternoon with 40 mph gusts. I've been obsessively checking the MODIS/VIIRS heat map for the Thomas Fire, and our end of the fire still looks quiet. The fire stands at 272,000 acres and 60% contained, which makes it the second largest wildfire in California history, not too far behind the Cedar Fire in San Diego County at 273,246 acres in 2003. There is still some recent activity in the north near the upper end of the Sespe and Matilija creeks - we're not done with this yet! A speaker at the usual 4:00 news conference was downright optimistic that the fire lines on our end would hold and all evacuations would end tomorrow! I reckon wind-blown ash will be a problem in town tonight.

We're under a NWS Wind Advisory, a Red Flag Warning, and right next to a Freeze Warning for tonight! Well, it is winter - or it will be at 8:28 Thursday morning when the exact moment of the Winter Solstice happens. It's the official first day of winter, the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow, and maybe our fire will no longer be such a concern. Happy Solstice!
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids; Manzanita Leaf Fungus; Winter Solstice
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Exobasidium vacinii
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Exobasidium vacinii

File.Name: hc20b_hc20-026.jpg
Notes: 38310068915 This is the gall of a Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on the interesting oak that I believe is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or maybe Quercus wislizeni. See this photo for some more info about the oak. The galls start as smooth swellings on the twig, but in time they develop fissures and the gall capsules somehow rise to the surface, burst through, and drop to the forest floor - kind of like a boil? I also find these galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), see this photo. Well, both are "Black Oaks" with thin acorn scales, so it's right that they have the same galls. I found this gall on the small trees in this photo, though it grows more commonly as a shrub. Note the small buds along the stem. These oaks aren't properly deciduous, but they are semi-deciduous - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring. Like I said, they are interesting oaks! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2017)
CommonNames: Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp; Shreve Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: hc20c_hc19-008.jpg
Notes: 39185731701 This is the web of a tiny Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider on a dead stem of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera, Lamiaceae) with last summer's seedheads. This web is small, just a few inches across. Note that it doesn't have many ashes compared to other webs - compare my [Previous] photo taken at the same time. That might be because these spiders take their webs down and rebuild them pretty often, maybe every night. I'm not sure whose web this is. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2017)

File.Name: hc20d_hc19-006.jpg
Notes: 39185496451 Here are ash fragments of chaparral leaves caught in a spider web on the ground. I'm pretty sure the web belongs to a Funnel Web Weaver (Agelenidae) spider - you can see its funnel opening to the left of center. The ashes are from the Thomas Fire which has been burning out of control to the east of us for two weeks now, and which seems to be finally winding down. We haven't had ashes in the air for some time, but nothing in the woods holds the fire ashes that are already there intact as well as these spider webs. I find something poignant about these ashy remnants of a forest that is no longer there. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2017)

File.Name: hc21a_hc21-018.jpg
Notes:
25342707658 I believe this is a small Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira, Araneidae) tucked into its thimble-shaped retreat that is decorated with the bodies of its prey. I found this one in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae) that's done flowering. I have another photo of probably the same spider taken last October for Arachtober! The spider is in the same pose, and I wonder if it will sit here all winter. These spiders typically build a proper orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side, but this one had no orb web. I can see a hint of a cat face on its abdomen - more visible in my earlier photo. Yeah, I had to use my on-camera flash to get this photo, as the spider was lost in the shadows of the contrasty sunlight today. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2017)

Yesterday was a test by wind - and it seems that the Thomas Fire is now out, at least on our end of the mountains. There is still some recent fire activity in the north near the upper end of the Sespe and Matilija creeks - we're not done with this yet, but it quiet closer to home. After the fire's big push towards Santa Barbara last Saturday, it almost seems like an anti-climax that it's over. All evacuations have been lifted, including our evacuation warning. It was cold last night - cold enough for frost if there was any moisture in the air to freeze.

File.Name: hc21b_hc21-008.jpg
Notes: 38331437795 I believe this is the fertile form of the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans). It resembles the more common Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia - those small orange disks which are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. I found this one on a bare stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). This is only the second time that I've found this lichen up here in the mountains. (The first time is here.) It really looks like something that belongs in the ocean. There's not much to photograph in he woods as our extended dry autumn moves into winter. Insects, plants, mushrooms - everything is waiting for rain. Since here's not much to photograph, I have to look closer at what there is - and I am finding new things! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2017)
CommonNames: Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Golden-Eye Lichens; Chamise
ScientificName: Teloschistes flavicans; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes flavicans; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: hc21c_hc20-029.jpg
Notes: 39179719362 Here are two photos of individual galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) under the leaves of a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They just are variable in the amount of hairs they have - sometimes they are quite naked, like this. These galls typically form colonies that grow together to make a furry mass as in this photo. They covered some trees last year, see this photo! But they've been very scarce this year, and I've only found a few singletons like the ones in my photo. It seems odd to think of galls as having good and bad years - but they do. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2017)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: hc21d_hc15-028.jpg
Notes: 38330943505 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working the flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) growing along Stagecoach Road in the mountains. The flower is an odd one to find at this time of year in the mountains, see this photo for some discussion. I don't usually photograph Honey Bees, but all insects are scarce right now - I was happy to find this one. Did you realize that bees have such hairy eyes? This photo also shows the bee's rear, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2017)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc22a_hc22-013.jpg
Notes:
39228756961 There are still a few late-season flowers on native California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The wonderful large pollen grains seem to explode from the stamens like popcorn. The plants are just about done now, but there are still a few flowers to keep the hummingbirds going. I have trouble spelling this one - it's "Fuchsia" not "Fuschia". (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2017)

It was cold again today, which may be why I found no insects. Fortunately the ashes of the Thomas Fire are cold too. All evacuations have been lifted. According to the Inciweb incident report, the fire is now the largest fire in California history at 273,400 acres. It's getting hard to get new photos every day, because our end of the forest is still closed. I may go back to posting Oregon mushrooms from Thanksgiving - which I was doing before the fire interrupted everything.

File.Name: hc22b_hc22-007.jpg
Notes: 38520885794 There are still a few late-season flowers on the native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea, Asteraceae), though the plants themselves are looking pretty sad. The purple-tipped pistils look like they've been dipped in sugar. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2017)

File.Name: hc22c_hc22-017.jpg
Notes: 24366758447 This is looking close at fresh flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Most of the buckwheat flowers are finished by now, but the pink stamens show that these flowers still have something to offer insects. There is still a slight dusting of ashes on the flowers from the recent Thomas Fire. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2017)

File.Name: hc22d_hc18-003.jpg
Notes: 38352623515 Here are a pair of tiny flies (Diptera) in the woods. By counting pixels, I know that both flies are tiny - less than 2 mm long. The fly on the left is on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The fly on the right is on a single floret of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae), see this photo for a different shot. I don't know what kind of fly either is. Hah - the thing about these tiny flies is that they look just like larger flies - but tiny. These are the only flies I've managed to photograph this week, so HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2017)
CommonNames: flies; Coast Live Oak; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: hc23a_hc23-025.jpg
Notes:
25384600078 This is another shot of the interesting oak in our mountains that I believe is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei) or maybe Quercus wislizeni, see this photo for some more info. These oaks aren't normally deciduous, but they are semi-deciduous like Scrub Oaks (Q. berberidifolia) - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring. But in this odd year I'm noticing that some shrubby plants like this one are being genuinely deciduous. I take this as a sign of our unprecedented "winter drought" without our usual winter rains so far - we haven't had an inch of rain since last March! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2017)

Looking off into the distance to the east, I could see some smoke today from what's left of the Thomas Fire - probably backfire work near Highway 33 in the backcountry. It's all quiet on the western edge of the fire where we are. What a great gift for the holidays!
ScientificName: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Q. berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus wislizeni; Q. berberidifolia

File.Name: hc23b_hc23-004.jpg
Notes: 25384239358 This is an infestation of Scale Insects (Coccoidea, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The stem and the scale insects and that one leaf seem to be covered with a powdery black fungus. If you look close, you can see little orange specks here and there, e.g. on the leaves on the left. These might be the " free-moving crawlers" that "develop thick protective layers of wax or other inert substances, often in elaborate shapes - so it's hard to see the actual insect underneath" - as BugGuide puts it. I didn't notice them until I got the photo up on my screen, I'll try to get back for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2017)
ScientificName: Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Coccoidea; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: hc23c_hc23-009.jpg
Notes: 38542865594 I guess these are galled leaves of a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae), but I have no idea what critter made the galls. Most galls seem to have little long-term effect on the leaves they occupy, this these galls appear to be terminal. The leaves are swollen almost beyond recognition. I'll make a point of looking for new galled leaves when the fresh leaves appear later this spring. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2017)

File.Name: hc23d_hc23-010.jpg
Notes: 39222358702 These are new catkins on a shrub of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family growing on the pass. The actual flowers aren't open yet, but they are getting close - see this photo for the actual flowers. Note the dense mat of hairs under the leaves which gives the shrubs a two-tone look. What's odd is that these shrubs never did flower last year when we had normal rainfall, but this year is a drought so far and here are the flowers. I reckon these shrubs which form their flower buds early are responding to last year's rain. I hope the gamble pays off. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2017)

File.Name: hc24a_hc23-018.jpg
Notes:
24411553467 Here are ripe Christmas berries on native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The ripe berries are edible, but they are quite astringent (unless you're a bird!) and need cooking. They are beautiful and appropriate for the holidays. They seem to be having a good year, despite the drought conditions. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2016)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all

File.Name: hc24b_hc24-002.jpg
Notes: 39244671512 I found this handsome Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on fallen oak leaves this morning. We have a trio of large butterflies that I mostly see on warm days in the middle of winter, including the Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) and the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica). (Photos https://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/32579400465/">here and here.) I've seen all three of then lately, but this is the first photo of any of them that I've managed to get. Now what are they doing here in the mountains at this time of year? (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)
CommonNames: Red Admiral; Mourning Cloak; California Tortoiseshell
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis antiopa; Nymphalis californica

File.Name: hc24c_hc24-028.jpg
Notes: 39244252602 Here are ants tending an interesting aphid on a willow stem. The ants are family Formicidae - I'm not sure what kind they are, so I'll leave it at that. The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I believe the aphid is a Giant Willow Aphid - species Tuberolachnus salignus, Lachninae, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It is rather large - as big as the ants which are not small. I first thought I had something unusual, but it turns out the aphids are "non-native, introduced from Europe around 1872" - see BugGuide. Hah - the tubercle on the aphid's back looks a Sirius satellite radio antenna! I saw the ants collect drops of honeydew from the aphid's behind. They were also placing tiny aphids on the leaves, but I don't know that they were the same kind. That struck me as odd because the willows are about to lose their leaves - so won't they lose the aphids as well? (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)
CommonNames: ants; aphid; willow; Arroyo Willow; Giant Willow Aphid
ScientificName: Formicidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Tuberolachnus salignus; Lachninae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Tuberolachnus salignus; Lachninae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: hc24d_hc24-005.jpg
Notes: 38395862745 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) trying to warm up on a bare patch of sandy soil this morning. These wasps look threatening enough, but I've never been stung, even by the ones that nest under our eaves at home. I found this one and a few other insects today - a sure sign that it is warming up a bit. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)

File.Name: hc25a_hc25-022.jpg
Notes:
24434862347 Here are ants tending aphids on a willow leaf that's naturally curved to make a neat arch. The ants are family Formicidae, maybe species Formica moki. The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I'm not sure about the aphids, but I don't think they are the Giant Willow Aphids in my [Previous] two photos. If you look at my previous photo, you can see that the aphids are all different sizes, but they all look dark with black spots. That makes me pretty sure that these green aphids are something different, and it's just a coincidence that they are out at the same time. It is curious that the ants are placing their aphids on the old leaves - the willows are starting to lose their leaves - so won't they lose the aphids as well? (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2017)

Ants and aphids for Christmas? Well, why not!
I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
CommonNames: ants; aphids; willow; Arroyo Willow; Giant Willow Aphids

File.Name: hc25b_hc25-013.jpg
Notes: 24434684897 Here are ants tending lots of interesting aphids on a willow stem. The ants are family Formicidae - I'm not sure, but maybe they are species Formica moki? The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I believe the aphids are Giant Willow Aphids - species Tuberolachnus salignus, Lachninae, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. See this photo for more info and see my [Previous] photo for a closer look at a single aphid. This whole colony is a bit more than an inch long. Note that the aphids are all different sizes, but they all look dark with black spots. That makes me pretty sure that the aphids in my [Next] photo are a different kind. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2017)

File.Name: hc25c_hc24-025.jpg
Notes: 39269129582 Here is another photo of ants tending an interesting aphid on a willow stem. The ants are family Formicidae - I'm not sure, but maybe they are species Formica moki? The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I believe the aphid is a Giant Willow Aphid - species Tuberolachnus salignus, Lachninae, Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. See this photo for more info. The aphid in the back has the shepherd's prize - a drop of sweet honeydew harvested from the aphid's behind. This really is an example of mutual aid - the ants care for the aphids and protect them from predators, and the aphids feed the ants. It's agriculture. Hah - that tubercle on the aphid's back still looks a satellite radio antenna! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)

File.Name: hc25d_hc23-002.jpg
Notes:
39291072371 These are early flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The flower buds are really the stand-out feature of this plant, along with the folded leaves like taco shells. The pretty buds form in September and then wait fully half a year to open, see this photo from last March. This is my photo for the Macro Mondays group, with the theme of "Member's Choice - Bokeh". HMM! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2017)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: hc26a_hc26-024.jpg
Notes:
24459764837 This is looking down on a most peculiar spider. When I found it, the spider was rocking its web back and forth just like the genus Tmarus in this photo which I found nearby - and maybe that's what it is. It's got the same blue feet! On the other hand, I'm not sure its front legs are long enough, and it's been wrapping a prey - do Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) do that? Look close at its extended arms - is it really clutching the broken ends of its web?? Like I said, most peculiar! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2017)

The weather is continuing to be not too cold but too dry. We are having an unprecedented "winter drought" without our usual winter rains so far - we haven't had an inch of rain since last March. I'm worried.

File.Name: hc26b_hc26-006.jpg
Notes: 38615477304 The Grateful Dead had the "wall of sound" - here's a wall of mushrooms! This is looking closer at the large mound of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I show in my [Previous] photo. Usually when I find these large clusters, the mushrooms are dusted with white spores, see this photo. The mushrooms in his photo are dusted with ashes from the Thomas Fire instead! It's so odd to find these mushrooms now before the winter rains in this (so far) dry year. Among many more important reasons, we need some rain to wash off the mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2017)
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; before the winter rains

File.Name: hc26c_hc26-002.jpg
Notes: 24459227937 I was surprised to find this large mound of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from a tree stump in the woods today, probably California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. The ferns are native Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta, Polypodiaceae). It's the color - not the taste - that these mushrooms are named for. It's so odd to find these mushrooms now before the winter rains in this (so far) dry year. We are having an unprecedented "winter drought". We haven't had an inch of rain since last March, and the woods feel very dry - yet somehow, there are mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2017)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; California Bay; Coastal Wood Fern

File.Name: hc26d_hc26-011.jpg
Notes: 38614945814 Here is one more photo of ants tending an interesting aphid on a willow stem - they are so interesting that I've been going back to this place every day lately. The ants are family Formicidae, maybe species Formica moki. The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I believe the large aphid is a Giant Willow Aphid - species Tuberolachnus salignus, see this photo for more info. Now look closer at the two small aphids - the brown one near the large aphid's rear end, and a green on on the bottom of the stem directly below it. I guess the green aphid is one of the ones that other ants are tending, see my [Previous] photo. A least I guess that brown one is an aphid - but it really looks different! Well, what do I know? I'm just the photographer, so I can leave it to others to figure out what I've photographed - comments will be appreciated. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2017)

File.Name: hc27a_hc27-007.jpg
Notes:
38465255335 I found this hairy gall on the underside of a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today, and I believe it is the work of a well-named Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. This one seems larger than the usual galls I find like this one - maybe 3/4 inch across, though most of that is hair. There is a small capsule inside that holds the actual gall - the wasp-induced home of its larva, nurtured by the oak. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2017)

The weather is still dry. We are having an unprecedented "winter drought" without our usual winter rains so far - we haven't had an inch of rain since last March. I'm worried - what if the rain . . . doesn't come??
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus fullawayi; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: hc27b_hc27-024.jpg
Notes: 38464998725 I found this odd capsule on a leaf of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's about 1300 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 5 mm, about 3/16 inch. By the same reckoning, the exit hole is 1.2 mm. (See this photo for how I figured.) It's about the size of a medicine pill. I believe this is the pupal case of an insect, but I'm not sure what kind. (A little parasitic wasp comes to mind.) I've found something similar before, see this photo and this photo. It's interesting that this one is tied down with lines of webbing. I suppose that means that I could also post it to the Web Wednesdays group, but I already have two photos this week - and enough is enough! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2017)

File.Name: hc27c_hc26-016.jpg
Notes: 38634595104 This is webbing under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, but I don't think it's the work of a spider. I believe it's the work of the same little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) that I show in this photo - at least they both skeletonize the leaf in the same way. (Look at the tip of the leaf.) I have another photo of the same kind of nest here. You can see the caterpillar's frass ("poop") under the web. There also seems to be a colony of wool-producing critters in the lower-right that I believe are Woolly Oak Aphids in the genus Stegophylla of the Aphididae - see this photo for more info. There's a few ashes from the Thomas Fire in there too. It's the web that catches your eye, so Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2017)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; caterpillar; Woolly Oak Aphids
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera; Stegophylla; Aphididae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lepidoptera; Stegophylla; Aphididae

File.Name: hc27d_hc24-013.jpg
Notes: 38464462945 This is a spider web - obscured by white ashes from the Thomas Fire, brown flower crumbs from a nearby Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), and dandelion-like seeds of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis, Asteraceae). It's interesting that this web is so cluttered while theOrb-Weaver Spiders (Araneidae) keep their webs neater - see this photo. I believe this web is the work of a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis, Linyphiidae), see this photo with less clutter. The spider is still in there, but it's impossible to see in this photo. I'll post a close photo tomorrow - for BBBT. We haven't had ashes in the air for some time now, but nothing in the woods holds the fire ashes that are already there intact as well as spider webs. Happy Web Webnesday! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)
CommonNames: Chamise; Coyote Bush; Orb-Weaver Spiders; Bowl and Doily Spider

File.Name: hc28a_hc28-009.jpg
Notes:
38484127285 Lizard weather! This Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) was posed for just a moment on the exposed root of an oak tree. The lizard is shedding - you can see a flap of old skin behind its head. It did get into the mid-70s today - that's warm for the end of December, and lizards and butterflies both were out. I only see juvenile lizards in winter. I reckon it takes longer for the BIG critters to warm up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. A lizard that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big. You can see the "third eye" that these lizards have behind the lizard's real eyes - I'll make a note, see this photo for some info. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)

We're having lizard weather instead of rainy winter weather. We are having an unprecedented "winter drought" without our usual winter rains so far - we haven't had an inch of rain since last March. I'm worried - what if the rain . . . doesn't come??

File.Name: hc28b_hc28-015.jpg
Notes: 39331709712 I was surprised to spot this California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly in the oak leaves today! I take this as proof that these butterflies overwinter as adults and get frisky on warm days like today. My usual butterfly book An Introduction to Southern California Butterflies agrees. It's not hot, but it did get into the mid-70s today - that's warm for the end of December. These butterflies are instantly recognizable by their "falcate" forewings - curved like a sickle on the trailing edge. That green grass-like plant in the foreground is the dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae), see this photo. I've been seeing these butterflies for the last few days, but this is my first photo - not quite in focus and too tight a crop, but still a welcome photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)

File.Name: hc28c_hc28-004.jpg
Notes: 38483519155 Here is an ant tending its aphids on a willow leaf. This is too tight of a crop, but I like the photo - it shows the tenderness of the ant despite its ferocious appearance. The tiny aphids are cute! The ant is family Formicidae, maybe species Formica moki. The willow is a native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis, Salicaceae) by the creek which still has some leaves. I'm not sure about the aphids, but they are not the Giant Willow Aphids that I've been showing lately. The relationship between ants and aphids really is an example of mutual aid - the ants care for the aphids and protect them from predators, and the aphids feed the ants by making honeydew. It's agriculture. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)
CommonNames: ant; aphids; willow; Arroyo Willow; Giant Willow Aphids
ScientificName: Formicidae; Formica moki; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Formica moki; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: hc28d_hc24-014.jpg
Notes: 39360696811 I'm pretty sure this is a Bowl and Doily Spider (Frontinella communis aka F. pyramitela, Linyphiidae) perched on the upper deck of its web. I found it in the web I showed yesterday that's obscured by ashes from the Thomas Fire and other clutter, and you can see some ashes in this photo. (See this photo for a different web with less clutter.) It's a surprisingly small spider for such a large web. My photo does show the spider's rear end, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2017)

File.Name: hc29a_hc29-006.jpg
Notes:
38672173934 Here is another "wall of mushrooms" shot of a large clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from a tree stump in the woods today, probably a stump of California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae), see this photo. The mushrooms look orange-stained, but they definitely have white spores - you can see several white spore deposits, e.g. just to the right of center. I was really looking for flies on these mushroom like this one which produce hoards of maggots. I didn't see any flies, but there's a tiny wasp on the front of the cap with the spore print. I do have a hankering to photograph mushrooms, and I may have to go back to my photos of Oregon mushrooms from Thanksgiving. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2017)

We haven't had an inch of rain since last March, and the woods feel very dry - yet somehow these mushrooms are here. We actually do have a chance of rain in the middle of next week. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: hc29b_hc29-026.jpg
Notes: 25510359818 This is furry white foliage of native Southern Hawkweed (Hieracium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family - with ashes from the Thomas Fire. It's been a few weeks since the ashes fell from the sky, but this plant is as good at holding them as the spider webs I've been showing, most recently in this photo. I can't explain why this plant seems to have new leaves when we've had no rain yet this winter, but here it is. The plants get yellow flowers in mid-summer, see this photo, but the pretty foliage pretty much disappears by then. These are plants of cool rocky places, and I found this one growing from a crack in the sandstone bedrock. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2017)

File.Name: hc29c_hc29-019.jpg
Notes: 38502118625 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the woods today. Maybe it is family Muscidae? Even flies have been scarce in the dry winter woods lately, so I was happy to find this one. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2017)

File.Name: hc29d_hc28-040.jpg
Notes: 25510075318 I happened to notice this small Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera) land with its prey on a dead stem in the woods yesterday - otherwise I never would have noticed it. It's small - less than 1/2 inch long. Its prey is probably a different fly, though I can't make it out. I was hard to get an acceptable shot with this contrasty light from the sun behind, but the fly got away when I tried to move to the other side of the plant. So it goes, but I do like those amazing eyes in this photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2017)

File.Name: hc30a_hc30-011.jpg
Notes:
38690712324 (Maybe this is Hyptiotes diversus in the family Uloboridae of the Cribellate Orb Weavers? That explains a few mysteries such as why it's legs are too short and it has a web. Thanks Paul, I'll get back to this.)

Here's a close shot of a remarkable little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging on its web in a shrub of Southern Bush Monkeyflower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). Maybe this spider is genus Tmarus, probably Tmarus angulatus. It's only about 1/4 inch long. What is most peculiar is that it seems to be clutching the broken ends of its web! I don't know how it manages to move along the web, but it does. I only noticed this spider because it was bouncing up and down on its web - that attention-getting behavior is NOT good camouflage! These odd spiders seem to wrap their prey (see this photo), and this one seemed to come from a bit of web shaped like a pie slice of an orb web. I'm starting to think this is something else and not genus Tmarus at all - but what else could it be? Very peculiar! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)

We're having an unprecedented "winter drought" without an inch of rain since last March, but we do have a chance of rain towards the end of next week. It sounds like the NWS has some confidence that we will get some wet weather, but not much confidence in rainfall amounts and timing. We'll see what happens.
CommonNames: Cribellate Orb Weavers; Crab Spider; Southern Bush Monkeyflower
ScientificName: Hyptiotes diversus; Uloboridae; Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus; Tmarus
associatedTaxa: Hyptiotes diversus; Uloboridae; Thomisidae; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Tmarus; Tmarus angulatus; Tmarus

File.Name: hc30b_hc30-024.jpg
Notes: 38690507784 I'm pretty sure this is an adult Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper - those distinctive little stubs are all the wings it will ever get. The colors of these grasshoppers sure is confusing. This one is dark , but others are quite tan - compare this photo. (Hah - and compare this photo at BugGuide by Alice Abelia of a completely black individual! This one is on a stem of late-season California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) that still has flowers. Hah - I haven't seen any grasshoppers for some time now, and this might be the last one in the woods before real winter weather arrives! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)

File.Name: hc30c_hc30-027.jpg
Notes: 39398163191 This is a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) with its proboscis in a still-fresh flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae). Maybe it's the species Scaeva pyrastri in the tribe Syrphini? Most of the buckwheat flowers are finished by now, but the pink stamens show that these flowers still have something to offer insects. The buckwheat flowers seem to be lasting longer on the shrubs in this odd year without our usual winter rains. We are having an unprecedented "winter drought" - we haven't had an inch of rain since last March. That's not bad for the buckwheat though. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Scaeva pyrastri; Syrphini
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Scaeva pyrastri; Syrphini

File.Name: hc30d_hc30-020.jpg
Notes: 39367848932 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods - admiring its shadow. This fly is smaller than a house fly. It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but I don't know that it is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2017)

File.Name: hc31a_hc31-008.jpg
Notes:
38540287495 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) working a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) - see this shot a few photos back. Look how the fly has its proboscis in the open flower - though its proboscis is nowhere near long enough to reach the goodies in the flower. I'm not sure what it's getting. I'm also not sure what kind of fly it is, though I don't think it is an ordinary one. Maybe it's another Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)

Yep, the mountain roads are open again after being closed by the Thomas Fire. I went up East Camino Cielo today as far as Gibraltar Road, where Camino Cielo is closed but Gibraltar is open down to Santa Barbara. I got my photos today near the top of "FAA Peak" - the FAA station on the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. There's still a chance of rain in the works for Friday . . . but that's still a few days away. I hope it happens, we really need it.

File.Name: hc31b_hc31-021.jpg
Notes: 38710590014 I opened one of the galls on the Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) in my [Previous] photo, and here's what I found inside - aphids! I guess these are the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni, Aphididae) which are said to cause these galls - or are there different kinds of aphids that make the galls on different species of manzanita? One difference is that I found these galls today, while the galls on Bigberry Manzanita are long finished, see my photo two shots back. I'm also not sure what I see inside this gall. What are those white granules - maybe aphid pupae that go with the exuviae that seem to litter the floor, or some kind of crystalline exudate? Mysterious! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae

File.Name: hc31c_hc31-015.jpg
Notes: 38539751865 This is Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis, Ericaceae) with a heavy infestation of aphid galls - but it's different from the shrub in my [Previous] photo for several reasons. It's a different manzanita which has stems with long soft hairs, different leaves and berries, it forms burls, and it doesn't flower so early. For some time I've thought that the red tumor-like growths on this shrub were caused by the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) that look very similar, but I finally opened one up today and sure 'nuff there were aphids inside, see my [Next] photo. I guess these galls are caused by the same Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni, Aphididae) - or are they? I find these galls on Eastwood Manzanita later in the year when the galls on Bigberry Manzanita are already done, so maybe they are something different? And what about the galls of the fungus that look so similar - is there a connection? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Fungus; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Exobasidium vacinii; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Exobasidium vacinii; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae

File.Name: hc31d_hc31-025.jpg
Notes:
39387889762 I found these new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) today near the top of "FAA Peak" - the automatic FAA radio station on the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. It's in full flower despite our near-complete lack of rain in this odd rain year. Yeah, the flowers look wilted. You can see a hole in one of the flowers where an impatient insect chewed its way in before the flower was ready to open. You can also see dry tumor-like lumps on the leaves to the right which are the remains of aphid galls caused the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni, Aphididae) . Specialized aphids used to live inside those pockets on the leaves, see this photo for the fresh galls and this photo for the aphids that cause them - or at least their exuviae (shed skins). I find these galls on this shrub in early summer, they're done by now. Now see my [Next] photo of a different manzanita. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2017)

It's interesting that there are Bigberry Manzanitas on the other side of San Marcos Pass along West Camino Cielo road, but very few - and they look terrible. I don't know why there is such a difference.
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae

File.Name: g101a_g101-023.jpg
Notes:
24008383862 This is looking closer at the Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum, Libellulidae, Odonata) dragonfly in my [Previous] photo. This isn't just a crop - I got this close to the dragonfly before it reluctantly took wing to fly just a few inches. I guess it's grounded by the cold weather and wind today. Anytime I can make out the tiny ommatidia that make up the compound eyes of a dragonfly, I know I've done something right! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2016)

It was cold and windy today - still icy on my trail at noon today. The wind from the south - that's from the ocean in our east-west mountains - may be the first sign of weather for the new year. We have a chance of rain on Sunday night, and then a better chance of real rain later in the week. It's about time! Actually I look forward to a few rainy days to work up the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society.

File.Name: g101b_g101-021.jpg
Notes: 23489489673 I believe this is another beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata perched in the gravel by our mailbox. I guess it's grounded by the cold weather and wind today, but it's remarkably well-camouflaged on the ground. I only noticed it because it lifted up when I got too close, and came right to the same place. These are our usual resident winter dragonflies which come out when the sun is shining. It's neat that I found one yesterday (still in 2015) and this one today (in 2016) - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2016)

File.Name: g101c_g101-009.jpg
Notes: 24115888935 These are flowers-to-be of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The tassels still aren't quite open yet - showing their stamens - but they're getting close. (I've seen this particular shrub before - it's staminate on this dioecious shrub.) The leaves are interesting, green above and white-tomentose below, which makes them easy to spot from a distance or a moving car. Last year, the male catkins never opened, though the female shrubs still produced seed. Even the unopened catkins are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2016)

File.Name: g101d_g101-014.jpg
Notes: 24115479275 This is a classic "oak apple gall" of a California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae), about the size of a squished tangerine. What a beautiful surface texture this gall has. There don't seem to be any exit holes, but there are already new galls forming in the woods. I don't understand the timing of these galls. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2016)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g101e_fc30-027.jpg
Notes: 24032716621 This is another photo of the bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) that I found working still-new flowers of native Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the Santa Ynez River a couple of days ago. See this photo and this one. Looking at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Peleteria. It's a handsome fly that seems unusual for the new year, but here it is - along with the flower! HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 30 December 2015)

File.Name: g101f_fc27-020.jpg
Notes: 23747350139 Here is another photo of the tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae) in the order Diptera that I found a few days ago on a leaf of a flowering Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in the mountains. See this photo of the same fly. It has remarkable green iridescent eyes and interesting patterns on its wings. I guess this fly is genus Trupanea of the tribe Tephritini. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 December 2015)
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Trupanea; Tephritini
