File.Name: g102a_g102-002.jpg
Notes:
24030182192 I believe these are some kind of brown-spored Inocybe mushrooms coming up in the garden, which is also close to Coast Live Oak trees . They have no particular odor. They certainly qualify as "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) - of little interest except here they are in this (so far) dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)

There is finally a good chance of real rain next week! The NWS discussion calls for rain most days of the week, and maybe a good bit of it. The bad news is that I'm catching Julie's cold. Julie was in Portland before Christmas with the grandkids, and she came down with the cold when she got home. Grandkids and airplanes both have a way with colds! I was feeling kind of *blah* today, and maybe it shows in my photos. On the other hand, it was a pretty *blah* day - cold and overcast with gathering clouds, not many photo-ops.

File.Name: g102b_g102-006.jpg
Notes: 24030022912 This is the cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I've been showing lately, here and here. Here it is in context, growing from an old stump of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) in the garden that flowered faithfully for many years and grew as tall as our upstairs deck. The shrub finally died a few years ago, but it's supporting new life in its death. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: g102c_g102-004.jpg
Notes: 23509602294 This is our other cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that's coming up from a still-sprouting stump of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. I can't get closer to these mushrooms because they are protected by a thicket of blackberry brambles! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: g102d_g102-009.jpg
Notes: 23842035930 These are old leaves of deciduous native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing deep in our canyon showing some "fall color". These plants have maple-like palmate leaves which are quite different from our common Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the background. Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess they're a relict plant in our mountains from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change, but our ongoing drought has been a real test. I've never found the berries here. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)

File.Name: g102e_g102-018.jpg
Notes: 23841760710 This is a healthy-looking plant of native Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta, Polypodiaceae) in the woods near home, growing under a tree of California Bay. These ferns have been looking pretty ragged in our dry autumn, but they're starting to freshen up. The expected rain next week will do them some good! (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)

File.Name: g102f_g102-017.jpg
Notes: 23841589900 This is a mass of Lichens growing on mostly dead stems of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see a bit of the needle-like foliage in the lower-right. I don't know why this shrub attracts so many more lichens than any other, but it does. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2016)

File.Name: g103a_g103-001.jpg
Notes:
24136046146 I found this ornate pupal husk of an insect sticking out of the oak mulch this morning and moved it to this sandy place for a photo. I believe it's the perfect husk of a Robber Fly (Asilidae, Diptera), see the images here at BugGuide. I actually found another one of these is nearly the same place last October - see this photo. Adult flies are diverse enough, but when you factor in their maggots and pupa, the diversity goes off the charts! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2016)

It was really a crummy day for new photos - cloudy with gale-force winds as our new storm moves in. The winds were from the south/east so I looked for places on the north/west that had some shelter. The NWS is calling for a good chance of real rain all week starting later tonight. Yahoo! I'll do what I can for new photos, but the recent archives are in good shape.

File.Name: g103b_g103-008.jpg
Notes: 24161543035 This is a brand new frond of a California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a sandstone outcrop with moss and lichen. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive - but if you look close, you can see that it is coming from a rhizome. You can also see that the new frond already has the buds of new sporangia. This one is growing just below an old leaf of Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus, Fagaceae) and just to the left of a twist of fallen Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) bark. These are all creatures of our most shady woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2016)
CommonNames: California Polypody; Resurrection Ferns; Tanbark Oak; Madrone
ScientificName: Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Polypodium californicum; Polypodiaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: g103c_g103-018.jpg
Notes: 24078476861 This is a large colony of the locally uncommon native Narrowleaf Sword Fern (Polystichum imbricans, Dryopteridaceae) growing with moss and lichen on a sandstone wall in the Santa Ynez Mountains. The old dead thatch below the green fronds seems distinctive. It's an unusual fern in our mountains, and this colony is lower than the only other colony I know of near the top of La Cumbre Peak. I've found the same fern growing on exposed rock in Oregon. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 January 2016)

File.Name: g103d_g103-010.jpg
Notes:
23533941583 They don't make 'em like this any more...

This is a beautiful old stonework drain along the shady part of East Camino Cielo road, the uphill section of a culvert that goes under the road. I don't know how old this road is, but this craftsmanship has the look of the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) work in the 1930s. Those fitted sandstone blocks have become a home for lichens and moss. This is something you miss driving by in your car! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2016)

File.Name: g103e_g103-014.jpg
Notes: 24052032662 This is the fantastic foliage of an uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family, growing along East Camino Cielo road. I only find this plant in a few shady places where the sun never shines. I've never seen a berry, though I have seen flowers - see this photo . I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on against climate change, though the plant extends south into Baja. The foliage is holly-like and beautiful - though very thin, like paper. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2016)

File.Name: g103f_g103-011.jpg
Notes: 23533160193 This is a new rosette of succulent Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family growing in the shady woods near home. If you look close, you can see tiny mosses, liverworts,and seedlings on the sandstone bank. The rains that are due this week will do them all some good! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2016)

File.Name: g104a_g104-018.jpg
Notes:
24075562032 I believe this is a short-winged Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper (Melanoplinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera), though it beats me what it's doing out in the woods in January! Maybe they too are waiting for some decent rain? See the comment on this photo at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)

We're all waiting for rain, but it hasn't happened yet. Despite the NWS probabilities, our storms last night and today crapped out. There's still a 70% chance of rain later tonight and a 100% chance (!) of heavy rain tomorrow, but there's nothing on the radar map yet and I can see stars. We need the rain. As far as I can see, we're well into our fifth year of extreme drought with just 22% of normal rain so far since September. The woods are suffering.

File.Name: g104b_g104-002.jpg
Notes:
23556175643 I guess this is the tiny gall of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). You can see the exit hole where the wasp got out. I wonder if that's the scar of another gall on the midrib just to the left?

This might be the same critter that makes the interesting "catkin galls" when the tree flowers later in the spring. Those would be the spring "sexual generation" of both male and female wasps, while the gall in this photo would be the summer "asexual generation" that produces only parthenogenic females. (Sorry, but galls are complicated like that.)

This gall is quite tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the gall is about 770 pixels across - that's only about about 3 mm across, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I don't find these galls very often, but they are tiny and hidden on the underside of the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)
CommonNames: Two-horned Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g104c_g104-011.jpg
Notes: 24156714976 Here are a pair of galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Yeah, there's a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia) stuck in the shrub under the galls, but don't be confused. These galls have interesting textures and patterns, different from gall to gall. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: g104d_g104-014.jpg
Notes: 23814468399 Here are lichens growing over old galls on a dead stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe they are the galls of the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. They are "detachable galls" that often stay attached. The gray lichens might be the Fringed Rosette Lichen (Physica tenella) - I'm guessing of course! They are fringed with rhizines - those long hairs on the underside that can act as a holdfast to anchor the lichen down. The galls aren't much more than pea-size, so the lichens are tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Fringed Rosette Lichen
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Physica tenella
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Physica tenella

File.Name: g104e_g104-016.jpg
Notes: 23814068009 Here are a pair of the "pinched form" galls of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae in the Hymenoptera family) growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like little yarmulke (kippah) caps - or maybe inverted rice bowls. The one on the left might have a fungus infection. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)
CommonNames: Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: g104f_g104-007.jpg
Notes: 23813836919 Here are dried out mosses and lichens growing on top of a large sandstone boulder in the canyon today. We're all waiting for rain, but it hasn't happened yet. Despite the NWS probabilities, our storms last night and today crapped out. There's still a 70% chance of rain later tonight and a 100% chance (!) of heavy rain tomorrow, but there's nothing on the radar map yet. We need the rain. As far as I can see, we're well into our fifth year of extreme drought with just 22% of normal rain so far. The woods are suffering. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2016)

File.Name: g105a_g105-031.jpg
Notes:
23574736904 Here are lichens on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods, already reviving after the rain today. It's remarkable how fast they go from nothing to this. The trees are smiling. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

After a couple of false starts, we finally did get some real rain today! We've had a bit over 2-1/4 inches today, and we might get as much again tomorrow. We're still under 5 inches for the year, less than 40% of average. I won't say this drought is over until we get to average - at least.

File.Name: g105b_g105-001.jpg
Notes:
24176140596 This is a close photo of remarkable green lichens growing on a fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods, revived by the rain today. That's a fallen apple leaf providing that nice background color. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

I don't know what kind of lichen it is, but it appears to be foliose and quite different from any other I've found. I believe the green caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae, but they look full of green algae too. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, and I've acquired a few new books, but there's a lot to learn. I just flipped through Stephen Sharnoff's "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and I couldn't find it. Oh well, mostly I'm just enjoying lichen diversity!
Locality: spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae

File.Name: g105c_g105-015.jpg
Notes:
24175775766 This is a universe of tiny Lichens growing on a fallen twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. The lichens are revived by the rain today. I can't count how many different lichens there here, let alone ID them! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

I have fat new copies of "A Field Guide to California Lichens" and the massive "Lichens of North America" sitting on my desk, but I've only glanced at them so far. Meanwhile I'm just enjoying their diversity!

File.Name: g105d_g105-019.jpg
Notes: 23833719089 These are tiny white mushrooms growing on a finger-sized oak twig in the soggy woods today. They are tiny, but well-formed, with short off-center stems. (I turned the stem around to show the gills.) Maybe they are Marasmiellus candidus or kin, marasmioid at least? The largest one is about 1/8 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

File.Name: g105e_g105-013.jpg
Notes: 23574414113 This is a large colony of native Giant Wild Rye grass (Elymus condensatus, Poaceae) in the rain today. These large grasses have been hanging on through our drought, but maybe they've made it through the worst of it! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

File.Name: g105f_g105-022.jpg
Notes: 24118357291 This is a large crack in a sandstone wall near our house, inhabited by moss and powdery lichens - all refreshed by the rains today. It's a beautiful place, overhung by Tanbark and Coast Live Oaks. But I don't trust that rock! We've had a couple of large boulders come down in the past, including one than crushed a friend's van. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2016)

File.Name: g106a_g106-049.jpg
Notes:
24222186175 This is a hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else?) encircling a little Mycena mushroom in the woods today. There seems to be some confusion about the proper name. Slugs have teeth?? Well, sort of. It does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch the leaves and mushrooms that it consumes. They remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! You can clearly see it's "pneumostome" - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this hole on the slug-right side. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. The upper tentacles in front are eyestalks with sensitive dark "pupils" and the lower pair are feelers and tasters. I'm always happy to find these large slugs, a sign that our woods are coming back to life after a long drought. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)

I've always wondered how do these large slugs survive from one year to the next, especially in a drought year? Do they dehydrate into almost nothing like pipe cleaners, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time.

Rain, rain, rain! It was pretty exciting today, with some thunder and brief intense thunderstorms, followed by periods of calm - even some sun. There's been no runoff, so it's all gone into the ground. We've had another 1-1/4 inch of rain today, and it's still coming down. It's expected to quiet down down tomorrow, so I'm looking forward to getting out in the woods to see what I can find.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Mycena
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Mycena

File.Name: g106b_g106-042.jpg
Notes:
24139294691 We're having banana slug weather!

This is a hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else?) in the woods today! It's the first one I've found this year. If you look close, you can see that it's munching a little Mycena mushroom on the right. This was the only one I found today. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Mycena
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Mycena

File.Name: g106c_g106-022.jpg
Notes: 24113292282 These are the first opening flowers I've seen this year on native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family, in the rain today. The trees have had buds for months, see this photo from last October. Have the trees been waiting for the rain or is this just coincidence? (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)

File.Name: g106d_g106-003.jpg
Notes: 24221199985 Here is another shot of the tiny white mushrooms growing on a finger-sized oak twig in the soggy woods today, with my pen for scale. They are tiny, but well-formed. I first thought with this photo that they have short off-center stems, but not I'm not sure. I still suspect they are marasmioid kin of Marasmiellus candidus. They seem to be a bit over 1/8 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)

File.Name: g106e_g106-004.jpg
Notes: 23592700854 Here is another shot of the Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I've been showing lately, looking beautiful in the rain today. The rain really bring out their honey-like colors. It's the color - not the taste - that these mushrooms are named for. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)

File.Name: g106f_g106-030.jpg
Notes:
23592512814 This is an odd Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing in the woods. They are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. The spore sacks quickly disintegrate, but the old rinds last in the woods for years. I suspect this is a new one with remnants of the inner spore sack still attached. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2016)

I rarely find them with the spore sack intact, but see this photo!

File.Name: g107a_g107-012.jpg
Notes:
24216784776 I found these fairly large mushrooms in the woods today growing in the oak mulch along the road in our canyon. I'm sure they are genus Cortinarius with rusty-brown spores and spore-stain hairs on the stalk. These are the first ones I've found this year, and I won't try to identify them. (Every year I have to relearn all my mushrooms!) They have slimy caps and dry stems, and the base is swollen but doesn't seen especially bulbous. Whatever they are, this is one of the first "real mushrooms" I've found in the woods this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

No more rain today, but it was gloomy and cold and breezy all morning, and then increasingly windy as the day went on. Our next chance of rain comes on Saturday. We ended up with 4-1/4 inches of rain from our recent storms. That's welcome, but we're still only at 57% normal rainfall for the year. We need lots more to bring us up to normal.

File.Name: g107b_g107-025.jpg
Notes: 23947079650 I found this hefty Agaricus mushroom poking up through the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon this morning, see my [Previous] photo. Here it is with bits of asphalt from the road! I believe this is Agaricus bitorquis which is said to favor roadsides and to be delicious. It has a dry cap that slowly stains brown with handling, and a flaring ring that appears to sheath the base of the stalk. At least I know what to look for now! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

File.Name: g107c_g107-020.jpg
Notes:
23613961724 I found this hefty mushroom poking up through the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon this morning. I suspected right away that it's an Agaricus species, see my [Next] photo. I believe it is Agaricus bitorquis which is said to favor roadsides and to be delicious. I don't think I've ever found it here before. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

Now I suspect that this mushroom that I found along the same road two months ago is the same mushroom.

File.Name: g107d_g107-016.jpg
Notes: 24133864402 Here is one branch of a new frond of native Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) in the Polypodiaceae plant family, with raindrops. At least the frond is newly revived by our recent rains. I guess the name comes from the color of the stems. They've had a tough time in our drought summer, but this one seems to be recovering just fine. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

File.Name: g107e_g107-040.jpg
Notes: 23613494134 I found this neat little clump of fresh mushrooms coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods today. I'm not sure, but I suspect they are more Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms. The white mycelium with root-like rhizomorphs holding the soil together at the base seems pretty distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

File.Name: g107f_g107-007.jpg
Notes:
24158789871 Here is a group of little Mycena mushrooms in the fallen oak leaves this morning, probably the same kind of mushroom the Banana Slug was munching in my [Previous] photo. There are *many* different kind, so I won't try to guess. Of course the oaks are Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I really enjoy photographing the oak mulch after a rain. Sometimes the mushrooms are just an excuse! (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2016)

Hah - I just noticed that there's a single stalk of a tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungus poking up behind the mushroom on the very right!
ScientificName: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Macrotyphula juncea
associatedTaxa: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Macrotyphula juncea

File.Name: g108a_g108-005.jpg
Notes:
24155459522 This is peaking "under the skirts" of a little Mycena mushroom growing in fallen oak leaves this morning. Beautiful, but I had to break the mushroom stalk to get the photo. The gills look so "organic" up inside! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)

Today was a beautiful day - chilly but sunny, with just a whisper of breeze. That is changing now as clouds are moving in and we have a good (80%) chance of rain tomorrow. It sound like this will be another small storm, but it might be enough to bring the Banana Slugs back from wherever they go!
Locality: 80%) chance of rain tomorrow.  It sound like this will be another small storm

File.Name: g108b_g108-017.jpg
Notes: 24155276742 I found these small mushrooms in the woods today growing under (mostly) native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I can see a hint of pink or flesh-colored spores on the cap to the right, so I suspect they are in the Entoloma (Entolomataceae family - but I won't try to guess what kind. They have a neat "wheel spoke" pattern on their caps. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)

File.Name: g108c_g108-037.jpg
Notes: 23636185543 I believe these are genus Clitocybe mushrooms with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe Clitocybe gibba? (It's also aka Infundibulicybe gibba - those darn mycologists!) They are said to be edible and excellent, but hard to recognize and best avoided. I found these growing in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. I think I've seen these before this season, see this photo from Dec. 29 along the same trail. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)
ScientificName: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g108d_g108-014.jpg
Notes: 23635795223 I'm pretty sure this is a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, maybe the Field Dog Lichen (Peltigera rufescens)? I found this one today growing on a sandstone rock with mosses. The "dog teeth" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. These pelt lichens are common in the Oregon woods, but they are quite uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one trail. On the other hand, they almost disappear into a dark crust when dry, so they would be easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)

File.Name: g108e_g108-033.jpg
Notes: 24154043202 This is a tiny world of mosses, liverworts, lichens, and even one seedling plant growing on sandstone that is slowly becoming soil. I don't know anything about mosses, but the lichens are genus Cladonia, maybe Cladonia chlorophyta or C. pyxidata? . The liverworts might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), and they already have the asexual buds known as gemmae which will become little "splash cups" when they mature. Some plants seem to produce them, others don't. I love this tiny diverse world that the rains bring! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)
ScientificName: Cladonia; Cladonia chlorophyta; C. pyxidata; Asterella californica; gemmae
associatedTaxa: Cladonia; Cladonia chlorophyta; C. pyxidata; Asterella californica; gemmae

File.Name: g108f_g108-045.jpg
Notes:
23633437324 This is a dying shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along my trail today. It looks like it was cut off suddenly since mid-summer, with new flower buds still intact. This shrub still has a few green leaves, but it's fading away. Drought stress, or some other cause because it happened to quickly? Stress can easily lead to blight. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)

See this photo for more dying manzanitas - with interesting comments. I looked today, and those manzanitas seem to have recovered. Maybe the rains will bring this one back too?

File.Name: g109a_g109-007.jpg
Notes:
24284082735 Here are more tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves above our retaining wall at home. They are as thin as toothpicks. That twig in the background has the tiny white marasmioid mushrooms in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2016)

I was feeling *blah* today with a cold that kept me up all night. I didn't go far, but I did my duty to get a few new photos! It was drizzly all day, but no real rain, just a bit under 1/10 inch total. Our next chance of rain is next Wednesday.

File.Name: g109b_g109-010.jpg
Notes: 23988230400 This is a nice cluster of little Mycena californiensis mushrooms growing in the fallen leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. See my [Previous] photo for some info about the ID. These mushrooms are small, beautiful, and very common in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2016)

File.Name: g109c_g109-003.jpg
Notes: 23656811413 This the broken stem of a Mycena californiensis mushroom showing how it exudes a red juice from the base. I believe that this and the colored gill edges (this photo) and the habitat under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees are enough to pin down the species in Michael Kuo's "Key to 122 Mycenoid Taxa in North America" at www.mushroomexpert.com. I tip my hat to Jacob Kalichman for suggesting this name here. I've seen these mushrooms for years, and never thought to break a stem. Mushrooms have secrets! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2016)

File.Name: g109d_g109-014.jpg
Notes: 23656223983 Here is a mass of little Mycena mushroom growing in what used to be a bramble of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) under a large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. The drought has taken care of the bramble, but the oak leaves and mushrooms are still there. I believe the mushrooms are Mycena californiensis with colored gill edges (this photo) and the broken stem exuding a red juice from the base (see my [Next] photo). Those two traits go right to the species in Michael Kuo's "Key to 122 Mycenoid Taxa in North America" at www.mushroomexpert.com. I tip my hat to Jacob Kalichman for suggesting this name here. There are many thousands of them, more than I've seen for a few years. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2016)
ScientificName: Mycena; Rubus ursinus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis
associatedTaxa: Mycena; Rubus ursinus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mycena californiensis

File.Name: g109e_g109-017.jpg
Notes:
23987112130 Trees grow down as well as up!

This is a small (<1 foot diameter) Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree on an embankment along our driveway that's slowly being undermined. It's exposing its roots. I suppose one of these years it will come down and block the road, but I've been saying that for 30+ years now! Note that the soil under the tree is still dry. Our El NiÒo rains dropped 4+ inches of rain this week, but that's still not enough to get down where the tree roots are. I hope there is more coming. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2016)

File.Name: g109f_g108-024.jpg
Notes:
23654133144 This is another photo of what appears to be a dying shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing along my trail yesterday. It still has a few green leaves, but they are getting crowded out by dead leaves. See this photo from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2016)

I saw something like this on a larger scale two years ago come March, see this photo. It's interesting because that site seems to now be completely recovered, at least it doesn't stand out. (It's across the same canyon on private land. I haven't been actually been there.) I now wonder if this is a blight that kills all the leaves and then allows them to recover? There's something similar on the Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) trees along the North Umpqua River in Oregon, see my photos here and here. I suppose time will tell.

If you know of other places where these manzanitas are doing poorly, please let me know. A grad student contacted me about these shrubs, and I'll forward any observations.
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: g110a_g110-012.jpg
Notes:
24224732841 This is another shot of the large hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else?) "on the prowl" in the woods this morning. (Thanks for the phrase Barbara!) When I first find one, they usually tuck themselves in. Then if I wait a few minutes, they usually relax and start exploring. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. This is only the second one I've found this year. I guess the drizzly weather today brought it out. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2016)

It turned out to be drizzly all day today which caught me (and maybe the NWS) by surprise. That brings us close to 1/4 inch for this little storm. Hah, as I was typing my [Previous] post, email came in with the text and photos I've been expecting for the next Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter. I'll probably be homebound for the next few days as I work on it. The recent archives are in good shape.
Locality: and maybe the NWS) by surprise.  That brings us close to 1/4 inch for this little storm.  Hah
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus

File.Name: g110b_g110-008.jpg
Notes:
24010963870 Here is a large hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else?) poking it eye-stalks out in the oak mulch along our driveway this morning. When I first found it, it retracted everything - just like a politician! I waited a few minutes, and it started "putting out feelers" before it started moving around. You can clearly see it's "pneumostome" - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this hole on the slug-right side. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I regard these slugs as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them. This is only the second one I've found this year. I guess the drizzly weather today brought it out. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2016)

*Sigh* I think we all have "slug moments" when we just want the world to go away. I've been feeling that way today as I'm expecting a bunch of text and photos in my email for the next Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter. I'll probably be homebound for the next few days as I work on it.
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus

File.Name: g110c_g110-004.jpg
Notes:
23677867424 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushrooms in the woods, and these are a contender! I noticed these tiny 'shrooms on a dead twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the mulch above our retaining wall. (That's my window into the wood mulch, putting the micro-world at eye level.) I put my pen in the photo for scale. My pen has a 0.7 mm ball which is close to the size of the smallest mushrooms, with the largest about double that. I have no idea what kind of mushrooms these are, but they are remarkably well-formed. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2016)

I'm still waiting to find more BIG mushrooms! The drizzly weather today should help.

File.Name: g110d_g110-027.jpg
Notes: 24197520672 These are younger caps of striking Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) than my [Previous] photo. These eyes have eyelashes! They are growing with other lichens on a living twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. The orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. These are mature apothecia that have been around for a while, with newer ones in the background. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2016)
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia

File.Name: g110e_g110-024.jpg
Notes: 23678727393 These are striking Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a living twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. The orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the fungus and algae. These are mature apothecia that have been around for a while, with newer ones in the background. (See my [Next] photo.) (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2016)
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia

File.Name: g110f_g110-021.jpg
Notes: 23937395729 These are brown Agaricus mushrooms with dark blackish-brown spores staining the gills. They are downright unattractive. I guess they are the "California Agaricus" (Agaricus californicus) mushroom, which is mildly poisonous to most people. I found these today growing with the weeds near the Lake Cachuma rest stop. They resemble edible agaricus species when fresh, but their smell and taste (if you nibble) definitely put you off. If you find agaricus mushroom in a weedy place, these are probably the ones. Watch out! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2016)

File.Name: g111a_g111-007.jpg
Notes:
24033094300 This is a wonderland of tiny Mycena californiensis mushrooms against a forest of toothpick-thin Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) fungi growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves above our retaining wall at home. If you look close near the center-bottom, you can even spot one of the tiny white mushrooms I showed yesterday in this photo. What a fantastic little micro-world/myco-world this is! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)

I got a reprieve from my newsletter duties today because of a delay in getting materials, so I took a walk instead. I'll probably be homebound for the next few days - unless I get lucky again!
ScientificName: Mycena californiensis; Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Mycena californiensis; Macrotyphula juncea; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g111b_g111-045.jpg
Notes: 24032710690 I found this family of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) in the woods today growing from the bark on a long-dead fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Look close to see the tiny buttons below the little mushroom on the left! My first thought was Tubaria furfuracea, but I usually find that one on wood chips. My second thought is the Deadly Galerina (Galerina autumnalis) - which really is deadly, with amanita-toxins. Well, don't eat any LBMs growing from wood, that's easy! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tubaria furfuracea; Galerina autumnalis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tubaria furfuracea; Galerina autumnalis

File.Name: g111c_g111-010.jpg
Notes: 23960341259 This is yet another shot of the same Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) that I've been showing lately, looking beautiful after the rain yesterday. These mushrooms have orange gills, but you can see the natural white spore prints they make on the lower caps. Don't always judge spore color by gill color! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)

File.Name: g111d_g111-032.jpg
Notes: 24327771195 I believe this is a Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) mushroom growing from a fallen oak branch in the woods. It has a toothy grin! It appears to be a gilled mushroom, but it's actually a polypore in the fungal order Polyporales. It's growing from a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This mushroom is at least a year old, though it seems to have fresh "gills" - see this photo of the same fungus a year ago. It's interesting that it has a green top suggesting that algae are living there. I wonder if this mushroom is "lichenizing" those algae? (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)
ScientificName: Lenzites betulina; Polyporales; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lenzites betulina; Polyporales; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g111e_g111-035.jpg
Notes: 23699130844 Here are two different shaggy lichens growing from the same trunk of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. I'm just starting to learn the lichens, so I may well be wrong - but I believe they are both genus Ramalina. I believe the one on the left is the Dotted Ramalina(Ramalina farinacea) with thin branches and lots of powdery soredia along the margins but no apothecia. And I believe the one on the right is the Western Strap Lichen (Ramalina leptocarpha) with broader branches and lots of apothecia but no soredia. The latter are 4 or 5 inches long, the former only about half that size. Lichens are another world that will take me a while to figure out. Please let me know if I'm wrong. That's how I learn! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Dotted Ramalina; Western Strap Lichen
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramalina; Ramalina farinacea; Ramalina leptocarpha
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Ramalina; Ramalina farinacea; Ramalina leptocarpha

File.Name: g111f_g110-019.jpg
Notes: 24244590151 I can't resist posting one more shot of the large hand-sized California Banana Slug "on the prowl" in the woods yesterday. There seems to be some confusion about the proper name for this slug, but I'll call it Ariolimax californicus because this is California - or is it A. columbianus or A. dolichophallus or something else? The green plant is native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae). These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I regard them as "environmental indicators" of the kind of place I want to live, and I love finding them near home. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2016)
ScientificName: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax californicus; A. columbianus; A. dolichophallus; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: g112a_g111-017.jpg
Notes:
24051368040 This is looking under the cap of the mushroom I found in the woods near home yesterday, see my [Previous] photo. I first uncritically thought it was a brown-spored Agaricus species - until I turned it over. I'm pretty sure it's some kind of white-spored Lepiota or Leucoagaricus. The latter makes sense because the name means "white agaricus" which I first thought it was. Looking on the web, Leucoagaricus erythrophaeus seems a possibility. That one is said to stain pinkish-orange, which this one certainly does - but there are others. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)

No time for new photos today, and these two are enough for now. I've been sitting at my computer all day working on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I reckon it will take me another day to get the draft together. I'd rather be out taking photos, but the volunteer work we do for non-profits is important!

File.Name: g112b_g111-012.jpg
Notes: 24051066680 I found this mushroom in the woods near home yesterday. I first uncritically thought it was a brown-spored Agaricus species - until I dug it up, see my [Next] photo. I'm pretty sure it's some kind of white-spored Lepiota or Leucoagaricus. The latter makes sense because the name means "white agaricus" which I first thought it was. Looking on the web, Leucoagaricus erythrophaeus seems a possibility. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)

File.Name: g113a_g111-021.jpg
Notes:
24070518010 This is another photo of the Cortinarius mushrooms I found a few days ago, see this photo. They have rusty-brown spores and spore-stain hairs on the stalk. You can see that it clearly does have a bulbous base, which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. The species Cortinarius fulmineus comes to mind. Every year I have to relearn all my mushrooms, but in this fifth drought year (so far) I have to even relearn what to look for! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2016)

Just this one photo today. I've been sitting at my computer all day working on the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, and I'm almost done. Tired as well. Hopefully I can send it out to the board tomorrow and be done with it. I may even be able to get out for a few new photos!

File.Name: g114a_g114-006.jpg
Notes:
24277586322 This is a small group of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump/burl of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. These aren't new mushrooms, but this is the first time I've noticed them in this place. The white powder that looks like a dusting of snow is the natural spore deposit of these white-spored mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2016)

I did finish my newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society today, and I even got out late in the afternoon (as the light was failing) for a few photos! I sent the newsletter out for comments/corrections and so far the changes are minor and easy. I'll have to take it to the printer in Solvang tomorrow, and that will be the end of it. What can I say, the volunteer work we do for our non-profits is important!
Locality: as the light was failing) for a few photos!  I sent the newsletter out for comments/corrections and so far the changes are minor and easy.  I'll have to take it to the printer in Solvang tomorrow
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: g114b_g114-003.jpg
Notes: 24385528615 This is a large cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. I also see leaves of Tanbark and Coast Live Oak in this interesting woodland. These aren't new mushrooms, but this is the first time I've noticed them in this place. The white powder is the natural spore deposit of these white-spored mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2016)
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: g114c_g114-009.jpg
Notes: 24017535039 These are tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" that are drying up and fading away in the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves above our retaining wall at home. They are as thin as toothpicks, becoming thinner as they dry out. Compare this photo of the same mushrooms a few days ago. We actually had a trace of rainfall yesterday, as another small storm washed out going around Point Conception, not enough to measure. *Sigh* We've seen that weather pattern a lot this year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2016)

File.Name: g115a_g115-017.jpg
Notes:
23779061333 This is a beautiful new sprout of perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, coming up under a drought-stressed shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) near Lake Cachuma today. They remind me of new rhubarb stalks. This is the first sign of life I've seen this year on these plants. Last year, we had the first open flowers by January 26, see this photo - but we also had good early rains last year. This year will be a bit slower. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 January 2016)

My newsletter is done and delivered to the printers, yahoo! Free at last, but it was still a pretty crummy day for new photos. We actually had a trace of rain this morning, just enough to get my seat cushions on the porch wet, but not enough to measure, as another small storm washed out going around Point Conception. *Sigh* We've seen that weather pattern a lot this year. We have another chance of the same thing tomorrow. I hope to get back to my usual six photos tomorrow.
ScientificName: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g115b_g115-007.jpg
Notes: 24037714759 These are little Cladonia lichens growing from a detached piece of mossy soil - like a little "floating island" community, think of the movies Avatar or Castle in the Sky. Maybe they are the Common Powderhorn (Cladonia coniocraea)? I notice that the mosses in the background already have sporophyte reproductive stalks. I'll try to get photos tomorrow when I have more time. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2016)
ScientificName: Avatar; Castle in the Sky; Cladonia coniocraea
associatedTaxa: Avatar; Castle in the Sky; Cladonia coniocraea

File.Name: g115c_g115-013.jpg
Notes: 24405290395 This is another odd Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing on mossy bare ground in the chaparral by Lake Cachuma today. This one has its inner spore sack still intact - though is it full of spores or sand? These are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. It's uncommon to find the spore sacks which usually quickly disintegrate, but the old outer rinds last in the woods for years. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 15 January 2016)

File.Name: g116a_g116-001.jpg
Notes:
23799985854 "Me and my shadow..."

This is a lovely Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a just a moment on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) by the creek this morning. This was my first keeper photo today, underexposed but still presentable. These are the flies I've seeing all winter, but I also noticed a new furry Bee Fly (maybe Bombylius, Bombyliidae, Diptera) today and even a a little Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly! Both were on the wing, so I didn't get photos - but now I know there's something out there to photograph! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)

Darned if we didn't get another spritz of rain this morning - not enough to measure, but it got my seat cushions wet again. Even the meteorologist who writes the NWS forecast is starting to get a sense of humor about this weather pattern:

ITS LIKE A SCENE OUT OF GROUNDHOGS DAY... AS ANOTHER
REPEAT OF THE PREVIOUS STORM IS SET TO OCCUR AGAIN
ON SUNDAY EVENING INTO MONDAY MORNING. LIGHT RAIN
EXPECTED FOR AREAS N OF POINT CONCEPTION...WITH ONLY
A SPRITZ OF RAIN POSSIBLE FOR LOS ANGELES AND VENTURA COUNTIES...
There's a better chance of rain next Tuesday.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak; Bee Fly; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombylius; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: g116b_g116-032.jpg
Notes:
24059719879 There's lots of life on this fallen trunk of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today!

First you notice the brown mushrooms coming out from under the bark that may be Tubaria furfuracea or Galerina autumnalis, see this photo. Then you notice the neat crustose lichens on the bark, see my [Previous] photo. Then you might notice the thin grass stem which is in fact native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae), see my first photo today, here. Then you might notice that dark hole in the lower-right where some critter (a beetle?) got out of the wood. And if you look real close, you can see two tiny gnats (Diptera) "doing it" on the third mushroom cap from the right! I wish I noticed those tiny flies at the time for a closer shot, but nope. Life on a log - remarkable! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; crustose lichens; Thin Grass; beetle; gnats
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tubaria furfuracea; Galerina autumnalis; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tubaria furfuracea; Galerina autumnalis; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Diptera

File.Name: g116c_g116-038.jpg
Notes: 24344709341 These are handsome crustose lichens on an arm-sized fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. I believe the main light-colored ones are a Rim Lichen in the genus Lecanora because of the rim of "lecanorine" thallus-like growth around the apothecia of the lichen. The apothecia are the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen - little disk fungi (Ascomycetes). The thallus is the main body of the lichen, and these lichens have their cups rimmed with that tissue. There's a different little orange crust lichen making spots on the top of this branch which is quite pretty. If you didn't know better, you'd think the white patch is just how the bark is. The diversity of lichens is wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)
CommonNames: crustose lichens; Coast Live Oak; Rim Lichen

File.Name: g116d_g116-005.jpg
Notes: 24059158459 This is the small-leaved Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant in our canyon growing on a mossy sandstone outcrop. It's extending its reach. This one is different from the more common California Liverwort (Asterella californica). I noticed in another photo that it's starting to form sporophytes, but that photo wasn't good enough to post. I'll try to get a close photo tomorrow. I'm not sure what kind of liverworts they are, but I've had Targionia hypophylla suggested for a name. They dry out very quickly, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)

File.Name: g116e_g116-011.jpg
Notes:
23797965534 Yucca leaves do not a prison make!

This is the succulent rosette of a Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family growing under the leaves of a Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae). It's been here for a couple of years, see this photo. This plant flowered last year. You can still see a couple of old flower stalks. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)

These woodland yuccas seem different from the chaparral plants, with longer and thinner leaves than the plants that grow in the direct sun. I suspect it has something to do with our fire history, though our canyon hasn't burned since the Refugio Fire in 1955.
CommonNames: Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Chaparral Yucca
ScientificName: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: g116f_g116-040.jpg
Notes: 23799144043 This is looking into a small grassland of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the canyon under the oak trees. You probably wouldn't want it for a lawn, but it's a real native grass that sometimes forms a grassy understory in our woods like this. These are places worth finding. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2016)

File.Name: g117a_g117-040.jpg
Notes:
24083088089 I found this Bumblebee (Bombus, Anthophila, Hymenoptera) today huddled on a glandular flower stalk of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). At first, I thought it might be spider prey, but I saw it move! I guess it was just grounded by the cool and cloudy weather today. The bee moved in the creaky way that I do when I first get up in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)

It was mostly overcast today, but quite lovely for photos - cloudy-bright rather than cloudy-gloomy. We do have another small storm moving in tonight, but it doesn't sound like we'll get much rain. We have a better chance on Tuesday, but again not much rain. *Sigh*
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: g117b_g117-018.jpg
Notes: 24368135171 Here are brand new catkins of a White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia) tree in the Betulaceae plant family growing in Kinevan canyon today. These are mostly male staminate catkins, but you can also see a few old and new pistillate cones if you look close. Very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)

File.Name: g117c_g117-037.jpg
Notes: 24424101436 I believe this is a new exploring stem of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family advancing like a snake into the woods. Look how it's reaching out with its tendrils, and has already grabbed that stem behind! I suppose this could also be Marah fabaceus, but that species is smaller and more in the backcountry. We have both species up here in the mountains, but time will tell. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)

File.Name: g117d_g117-003.jpg
Notes: 24341797902 This is a lovely little "floating island" community of mosses and Cladonia lichens growing from a detached piece of soil. Think of the movies Avatar or Castle in the Sky! Maybe the lichens are the Common Powderhorn (Cladonia coniocraea)? These detached pieces of mossy soil seem to go on for many years. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)
ScientificName: Avatar; Castle in the Sky; Cladonia coniocraea
associatedTaxa: Avatar; Castle in the Sky; Cladonia coniocraea

File.Name: g117e_g117-007.jpg
Notes: 24449777415 This is looking very close at the small-leaved Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in our canyon growing on a mossy sandstone outcrop. You can see what I believe are tiny sexual sporophytes on some of the leaves. That one just above and to the right of center has some detail. I've never seen these before, but to be honest I couldn't see them today except with the magnifying glass of my macro lens. The sporophytes are 160 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to just 0.63 mm! (See here for how I figured.) No wonder I've never noticed them before. I'm not sure what kind of liverworts they are, but I've had Targionia hypophylla suggested for a name. They dry out very quickly, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)

File.Name: g117f_g117-011.jpg
Notes: 24341115412 I believe these are California Liverworts (Asterella californica) producing bumpy tongue-like structures on the "leaves" that are the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. Later in the year the same liverworts might producing umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction, see this photo. Bryophyte reproduction is really complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2016)

File.Name: g118a_g118-018.jpg
Notes:
24177275090 These are tiny white mushrooms growing from a twig - wearing raindrop caps reflecting the woods! Even that tall mushroom in the back has a raindrop cap, though it's harder to make out. I didn't have my pen for scale, but I've already done that in this photo of the same mushrooms a week ago. They are tiny. That one on the right is just 290 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to just over 1 mm across. (See here for how I figured.) In the dry weather a few days ago these mushrooms shriveled up, but now they are revived - maybe genus Marasmius? (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

We had another "small storm" today. We only received 0.15 inches of rain, but it took its time and got everything wet. Tomorrow is forecast to bring much the same. We're still below half of normal rainfall.

File.Name: g118b_g118-035.jpg
Notes: 24472477715 I found these new Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods today growing in oak leaves along East Camino Cielo road. They are edible and tasty, though a bit mushy - I didn't collect these for the table. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan like the one on the left with just a hint of color. Compare this photo! We also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar, and I've confused them before. See this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

File.Name: g118c_g118-043.jpg
Notes:
23845335593 Here are the first flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've seen this year. This is different from last year when I found them on November 16, see this photo! I got this photo today during the only moment of sunshine I saw all day, at least until I got home. This is a small woody sub-shrub, usually a foot or less high. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

Sure, I tried to get a closer photo of the flowers, but they are all hopelessly overexposed. I'll get back to it tomorrow. *Sigh* You'd think I would know about photographing deep red flowers by now.

File.Name: g118d_g118-047.jpg
Notes: 24103822409 These are blushing new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family, with raindrops. They are growing on the pass near Painted Cave, so the flowers are slowly working their down from the summits where I first found them early this year - in early October, see this photo! These flowers are coming from previously aphid-galled leaves, you can still see dark scars of the galls, see this photo. It's interesting that we don't seem to have a mass flowering of these shrubs this year. It's more like a trickle. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

File.Name: g118e_g118-014.jpg
Notes: 24471393685 Here are the first new flowers of non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family, smothered with raindrops. It's a common weed along the roads in the woods, but I don't think it causes any problems. You can add it to your salads. The flowers are tiny, with divided petals. These are the first new flowers of any kind that I've found in our canyon this year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

File.Name: g118f_g118-038.jpg
Notes: 23842943414 Here are new plants of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family - with raindrops. They are hardly recognizable at this stage because they lack the umbrella-like "perfoliate leaves" that surround the flower stems, but those will come. These plants have three differently shaped leaves - strap-like at first, then spade-like, finally umbrella-like. They grow like a weed in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

File.Name: g119a_g119-018.jpg
Notes:
24383928112 This is looking closer at the first flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've seen in our mountains this year, in the rain today. If you look close, you can see the real tubular flowers, not just the brilliant red bracts that first get your attention. This is a small woody sub-shrub, usually a foot or less high, see my photo yesterday for the whole plant. There's something about deep red. I managed to overexpose many of my photos again today! I really don't understand how the luminance histogram can look good, while the red histogram is off the chart! Fortunately, I bracketed my exposure, and this one looks OK. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2016)

Despite my expectations of another small storm, we had some real rain today, about 7/10 inch so far! I'll be looking for new mushrooms tomorrow.

File.Name: g119b_g119-027.jpg
Notes: 24409533301 New mushrooms in the rain today! I believe these are the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens) mushroom growing along the road in our canyon. You can see the amber-colored stains on the stem of the mushroom on the right that I handled. They have a sweet almond-like smell, and David Arora descibes them as "delectably delicious" - that is, if they are really that species! Having been bitten before, I'm wary of all our woodland agarics! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2016)

File.Name: g119c_g119-021.jpg
Notes: 24123938269 This is looking closer at a new frond of native Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a rocky hillside in the mountains, in the rain today. See my [Previous] photo for the whole plant. It has small spine-tipped ("mucronate") leaflets. This is a tough fern that grows in exposed places in full sun, and somehow manages to survive our hot dry summers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2016)

File.Name: g119d_g118-045.jpg
Notes: 23863252654 This is a whole plant of native Bird's Foot Fern (Pellaea mucronata) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a rocky hillside in the mountains, in the rain yesterday. See my [Next] photo for a closer look at the spine-tipped ("mucronate") leaflets. It's a tough fern that grows in exposed places in full sun, and somehow manages to survive our hot dry summers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2016)

File.Name: g119e_g119-007.jpg
Notes: 23863097194 This is a lovely native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the rain today. This is another one of the plants I can photograph from the shelter of our front porch. (See my [Previous] photo.) This is a young tree, maybe 20 feet tall, very beautiful. Those leaves... that bark! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2016)

File.Name: g119f_g119-014.jpg
Notes: 24123366209 These are new leaves on a shrub of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden in the rain today. It's one of the plants I can photograph from the shelter of our front porch. We rescued this plant from a slide along Paradise Road many years ago, and it's done very well here. We never water it. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead. Maybe so! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2016)

File.Name: g120a_g120-040.jpg
Notes:
24485939166 I found this stunning lichen on a fallen tree branch in Kinevan Canyon today, and I posed it on this mossy rock to bring it up to eye level. I believe it is the fertile form of the Powdered Orange Bush Lichen (Teloschistes flavicans). It resembles the Golden-Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) in this photo, but it lacks the ciliate apothecia. I'm not sure what tree this fell from, it could be oak or bay in this place. I walked through the dark mossy woods of Kinevan Canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

There's lots more happening in this photo. You can see neat little crustose lichens on the branch to the left, small orange disk fungi (Ascomycetes) in the lower right, and a proper mushroom in the lower-left - not to mention all the beautiful moss! See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of the mushroom.

It was a lovely day for lichens and mushrooms. The mountains had a cloud cap, but it was "cloudy bright" not "cloudy gloomy". I suspect all the mushrooms were here before the recent rains. It takes a few days for new ones to appear.
CommonNames: lichen; Powdered Orange Bush Lichen; Golden-Eye Lichens; crustose lichens; disk fungi; mushroom

File.Name: g120b_g120-045.jpg
Notes: 24216231540 I found these real "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing on a mossy rock growing along Kinevan Creek today. I suspect they do some damage to the mosses, you can see some discoloring at the base of the stalks. The gills start pallid, but become brown in age. I suspect they are some kind of Galerina in the Cortinariaceae family of mushrooms, but I didn't collect them for ID. They might be quite poisonous, but they are not hazardous to admire! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g120c_g120-035.jpg
Notes: 23884750973 This is looking into the spoke-like wheels of tiny mushroom caps that have become a hotel for tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects. I believe the mushrooms are Marasmiellus candidus or kin, growing on a broken-off bit of a hardwood stump that was unrecognizable. The tiny insects are all different sizes and colors. Really cool! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g120d_g120-023.jpg
Notes: 24428982511 These are strange antler-like fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin). I don't think the count as "mushrooms" as they are not at all fleshy, but they are fungi. I'm sure these are growing from buried wood under the oak and bay leaves. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. The white dusting on the black stems is from asexual spores (conidia). Later on they form sexual spores from "perithecia" at the top of the fruiting body. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g120e_g120-050.jpg
Notes: 24143196799 I found this pallid Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today, growing in mostly California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) leaves. It clearly has a bulbous base, which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. I believe this is Cortinarius regalis. Cortinarius mushrooms are usually yellow or lavender, at least when fresh, but this one is different. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis
associatedTaxa: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius regalis

File.Name: g120f_g120-026.jpg
Notes: 23882510584 This appears to be a thin band of white fungus mycelium moving down a rock and munching the lichens and moss in its path. The fungus appears to have tiny fruiting bodies. I'm not sure if that's what this is, but that's what it looks like! Try a web search on "Lichenicolous fungus". (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g121a_g121-012.jpg
Notes:
23902920424 Here are three photos of a tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) on blushing red flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today - the same flower cluster that I show in my [Previous] photo. Maybe they are a Pteromalid Wasp in the family Pteromalidae in the family Chalcidoidea that seem to favor the manzanita flowers? I'm sure these are all the same wasp, but the antennae sure look different between these different photos. None of my photos was quite good enough to go big, so I went with a triptych of smaller shots. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2016)

It was breezy today, which got in the way of my photos. Then the haze started thickening into clouds. This may be the first sign of our next weather system - another small storm, expected on Friday night and Saturday.
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pteromalidae; Chalcidoidea
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pteromalidae; Chalcidoidea

File.Name: g121b_g121-008.jpg
Notes: 24235096340 These are blushing new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Some shrubs start with white flowers, but others like this one start pink and are quite beautiful. Vive la diffÈrence! You can see holes were impatient insects drilled into the flowers. I saw interesting flies and bees working the flowers today, but they mostly didn't want to pose, and there was a steady annoying breeze that got in the way. See my [Next] photo! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2016)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Vive la diffÈrence!
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Vive la diffÈrence!

File.Name: g121c_g121-033.jpg
Notes: 24504199466 Here are two photos of the first open flowers I've seen this year of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. These are male, staminate flowers, with pollen-bearing stamens. The lady, pistillate flowers will come later in the year on separate shrubs of this dioecious shrub. They are beautiful, with the "string-of-pearl" flowers hanging like ornaments. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2016)

File.Name: g121d_g121-002.jpg
Notes:
24162262309 This is a tiny mite (Acari) worrying the carcass of a tiny dead fly (Diptera) on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. It's not the best photo, taken in low light this morning - but it shows what's happening. I reckon the mite is being a scavenger, not a predator? The mite is about 300 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot. That comes to just over 1 mm long, see here for how I figured. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2016)

I thought twice about posting this less-than-perfect photo, but what the heck! It might be something unusual.

File.Name: g121e_g120-002.jpg
Notes: 24447554861 These are tiny mushrooms of Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Look how the leaf is peppered with tiny stems that might become more mushrooms if the weather obliges with more rain. They're small, only a few millimeters across, and the stems are tough and wiry. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g121f_g120-004.jpg
Notes: 24447406571 Here are two photos of what must be tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from an oak leaf. (I turned the leaf around.) At first I thought they might be something else because I don't usually see their rooting mycelium like this. These are small, about 1/2 inch tall, and thinner than a toothpick. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2016)

File.Name: g122a_g122-020.jpg
Notes:
24255551310 Here is the first new sprout I've seen this year of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. I've been looking for them because I found the first ones already in flower last year on December 28, see this photo. Well, we had good early rains last year, but not this year. The new stems of these plants come up mushroom-like in the leaf mulch. It probably wont be too long before they produce their first flowers. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. See this photo and read the interesting comments. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2016)

We have a "likely" chance of rain tonight and Saturday, but I'm not sure how much we'll get. It sounds like another small storm that will crap out as it comes around Point Conception. Darn, this is not what we need!
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: g122b_g122-024.jpg
Notes: 23924007783 This is a new/old flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family flowering out of season from a crack in the sandstone bedrock. It may be Hazardia squarrosa ssp. grindelioides? It seems odd to find it flowering now in January, but here it is. All the other flowers of its kind were finished a couple of months ago. Winter is a time when our first new flowers and last old flowers overlap. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2016)
ScientificName: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Hazardia squarrosa; grindelioides
associatedTaxa: Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Hazardia squarrosa; grindelioides

File.Name: g122c_g122-010.jpg
Notes:
24254723640 These are interesting mushrooms growing with mosses and Cladonia lichens on the dead stump of a native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). I can't identify them, but they really remind me of the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis aka Craterellus tubaeformis) that we find in Oregon on dead conifer wood, see this photo. They have cross-veins like a chanterelle, but the caps and stems are different, so I'm sure this is something else. It's rather small, less than an inch tall. If you have any thoughts about this mushroom, please leave a comment or email. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2016)

Thanks Jacob, this might well be a Lichen Agaric in the genus Xeromphalina. (See comment below.)
CommonNames: Cladonia; Bigberry Manzanita; Funnel Chanterelle; Winter Chanterelle; Lichen Agaric
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Cantharellus infundibuiformis; Craterellus tubaeformis; Xeromphalina
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Cantharellus infundibuiformis; Craterellus tubaeformis; Xeromphalina

File.Name: g122d_g122-008.jpg
Notes: 23921806774 I believe these are new mushrooms of the Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin). They look "fluffier" and more typical than the early mushrooms I've been finding, e.g. in this photo. I found these growing in a mulch of fallen oak and manzanita leaves. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2016)

File.Name: g122e_g122-028.jpg
Notes: 23922672433 These are little dark mushrooms with grayish gills, but I'm sure they will end up with flesh-colored or pink gills - in the pink-spored family Entolomataceae. I think I can see just a hint of pink color in the cap to the right which, will become more pronounced as the caps mature. The caps of younger mushrooms are "umbilicate", with a little belly button-like bump or "umbo" in the center, like a sombrero hat. (Think of umbilical cords.) The bumps flatten out as the caps expand. I found these growing in a mulch of fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves. Edibility is unknown, best avoided. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2016)

File.Name: g122f_g121-011.jpg
Notes: 23921029724 This is a little dark fly (order Diptera) taking nectar from the cup of an urn-like flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. It looks like "just a fly" - but I guess it's more interesting because it's here in the woods drinking from a native flower. Is that a tiny mite (Acari) on the fly's left eye? This was a tricky exposure - the flower is too light and the fly is too dark! I also saw interesting tachinid flies and furry bee flies on the flowers, but this is the only one I could get a photo of. HFDF! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 January 2016)

File.Name: g123a_g123-009.jpg
Notes:
24546380456 Here are three shots of a beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms poking up through the fallen oak in our canyon today, the first I've seen this year. This may be a related species because the cap isn't especially velvety or frosted as they sometimes are, but that may just be an environmental thing. The widely-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! The photo on the bottom-left shows a tuft of the copious white mycelium at the base of the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)

*Sigh* We just had a tinkle of rain last night, a few hundredths of an inch. And then the wind kicked up... It's a good thing I found lots of mushrooms today, because anything else would have been rock 'n rolling in the wind!

File.Name: g123b_g123-017.jpg
Notes: 24545982786 I first thought these were pink-spored Pluteus petasatus mushrooms in the Pluteaceae family of mushrooms, but maybe I was just being lazy since I have found that one nearby. Jacob Kalichman his comment below tells me that it's an untypical Psathyrella uliginicola aka Coprinopsis uliginicola and rightly points out the veil tissue on the margin of the cap. Thanks Jacob, live and learn - again and again! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)
ScientificName: Pluteus petasatus; Pluteaceae; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola
associatedTaxa: Pluteus petasatus; Pluteaceae; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola

File.Name: g123c_g123-029.jpg
