Notes: 24276307360 I believe these are the Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota magnispora) mushroom - the one that David Arora calls Lepiota clypeolaria var. "nabiscodisca". I found these in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They are quite beautiful, but possibly poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)
ScientificName: Lepiota magnispora; Lepiota clypeolaria; "nabiscodisca"; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepiota magnispora; Lepiota clypeolaria; "nabiscodisca"; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g123d_g123-005.jpg
Notes:
24571431965 This looks suspiciously like an Amanita mushroom with free gills, a ring on the stalk, and a slightly bulbous base that may hide a volva - and maybe it is, see below. But it might also be some kind of Lepiota or Leucoagaricus. The species Leucoagaricus erythrophaeus seems a possibility, but the cap lacks dark fibrils. That one is said to stain pinkish-orange, which this one certainly does - but there are others. I wouldn't care to eat this one! I found these today on the wild side of our garden, growing in a mulch of fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)

That's what I first thought. Now I'm thinking it might be The Blusher (Amanita rubescens)? The soft white gills, reddish stains, and lack of a volva seem right. These mushrooms seem to come up both in autumn/early winter and again later in the spring, with the spring forms being larger and more distinctive.
ScientificName: Amanita; Lepiota; Leucoagaricus; Leucoagaricus erythrophaeus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Amanita rubescens
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Lepiota; Leucoagaricus; Leucoagaricus erythrophaeus; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Amanita rubescens

File.Name: g123e_g123-039.jpg
Notes: 24571168255 I found these Agaricus mushrooms this morning growing under a clump of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae). They might well be the Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens) mushroom, but the stem of the one I picked didn't change color as I handled it. Compare this photo. They have a sweet almond-like smell, and David Arora descibes them as "delectably delicious" - that is, if they are really that species! Having been bitten before, I'm wary of all our woodland agarics! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)
ScientificName: Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; if
associatedTaxa: Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Agaricus albolutescens; if

File.Name: g123f_g123-032.jpg
Notes: 24275430660 This is a cluster of (I believe) Clitocybe mushrooms with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk - maybe Clitocybe gibba aka Infundibulicybe gibba. They are said to be edible and excellent, but hard to recognize and best avoided. I found these growing under leaves of our interesting dry-land native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae), in the mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees today. Sorry about the contrasty light, but that's how it was today - when the sun finally came out. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2016)
ScientificName: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe; Clitocybe gibba; Infundibulicybe gibba; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g124a_g124-031.jpg
Notes:
24487608792 This is the first fully open flower I've seen this year of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The flowers remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats and skirts in the air. The glandular Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) will be the next to flower. There are buds, but no flowers yet. Maybe tomorrow. It's interesting how our native gooseberries usually start flowering so close together. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2016)

The winds died down this morning, so I went into the higher mountains looking for bees and bee flies on the manzanita flowers. I was also looking for the sun! It was colder than its been this morning, still in the 30s when I got up this morning. It turned out to be a good day for gooseberries. There's always something!
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry
ScientificName: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; en pointe; Ribes amarum
associatedTaxa: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; en pointe; Ribes amarum

File.Name: g124b_g124-040.jpg
Notes: 24487375272 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It was hiding in the back of this shrub growing on a steep embankment so I couldn't get too close - but close enough for a record photo! Last year I got my first photo of them on January 9, not too far away. The hummingbirds will enjoy them! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2016)

File.Name: g124c_g124-043.jpg
Notes: 24299767550 I believe these are odd galled flower buds on a shrub of native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are probably the bud galls of a tiny Gall Midge - genus Rhopalomyia, family Cecidomyiidae, order Diptera. The shrub has just produced it's first flower, see my [Next] photo, so there are buds available. It's so interesting that the galled "flower" seems to be producing deformed stamens - if that's what those tiny dark things in the throat of it are. Life is resilient! The tiny atypical leaves surrounding the flower are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2016)
ScientificName: Ribes speciosum; Grossulariaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ribes speciosum; Grossulariaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: g124d_g124-007.jpg
Notes: 24299557810 These are tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). If you look close, you can make out a third one "in the hole". I believe these thrips are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae. I went up into the higher mountains today hoping to get a photo of the interesting furry bee flies and wild bees that are working the flowers, but these were the only critters I could capture in a photo. They are tiny - only a mm or two long. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2016)
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: g124e_g124-016.jpg
Notes: 24486558472 This is a large flowering shrub of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. These are male, staminate flowers, with pollen-bearing stamens. The lady, pistillate flowers will come later in the year on separate plants of this dioecious shrub. The flowers are attractive, like ornaments, though this particular shrub looks pretty bedraggled. If you click on the photo to enlarge it and squint at the left edge, maybe you can make out the swarm of tiny gnats! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2016)

File.Name: g124f_g124-013.jpg
Notes: 23967713783 I can't identify this striking little Foliose Lichen covered with monochrome apothecia - the fruiting bodies of the fungus part of the lichen. I found these covering the dead twigs of a Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, Garryaceae) today, and I don't think I've seen them anywhere else. See this photo of the same lichen - with the host plant misidentified. There are a few other lichens here too. Lichens seem to love company! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2016)
ScientificName: Foliose Lichen; apothecia; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae
associatedTaxa: Foliose Lichen; apothecia; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae

File.Name: g125a_g125-009.jpg
Notes:
24535516171 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched for a moment on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). I'm pretty sure this is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera, the first I've managed to capture this year. It has brilliant red "underwings" that makes them easy to spot in the air, but they almost disappear on the ground with wings folded like this. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. This isn't the best photo, but it's not bad for my first encounter with these moths this year! See this photo for a clearer shot of the snout-like head. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)

It felt warmer today, I took off my warm outer shirt for the first time in weeks. It almost felt like spring! On the other hand, it's expected to be colder tonight and maybe frosty.
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: g125b_g125-025.jpg
Notes: 24249798389 This is a handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). I won't try to guess what kind of bee this is, but it has rather long antennae. I also saw interesting large flies working the flowers, but couldn't get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)

File.Name: g125c_g125-036.jpg
Notes: 24508807602 These are the first new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've seen this year. Last year I first found the first flowers on December 31, and the year before on January 7. We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains. This one is distinctive for its early white flowers and mostly alternate leaves. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow - but I don't expect that in this (still) drought year. The individual flowers are intricate, like little origami boxes - I'll post a closer photo tomorrow. I got this photo on the way to town today, near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass". (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)

File.Name: g125d_g125-001.jpg
Notes: 23989706563 I found this small but beautiful mushroom growing from a fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning above our retaining wall. It's small, an inch or less tall. *Gulp* Maybe it's a pink-spored Deer Mushroom or Fawn Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus)? "Once bitten, twice warned" - I was bitten on the last mushroom I thought was a Pluteus that turned out to be Psathyrella or Coprinopsis - see this photo. This one still has mostly white gills, but I think I can see a hint of pink (really flesh) color if I sit back from my computer screen. I know where it lives. I'll check back in a day or two to see if it really has pink spores. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Deer Mushroom; Fawn Mushroom
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pluteus cervinus; Pluteus; Psathyrella; Coprinopsis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Pluteus cervinus; Pluteus; Psathyrella; Coprinopsis

File.Name: g125e_g125-007.jpg
Notes: 23989550283 Here are last year's seeds of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family - with a new spider web. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)

File.Name: g125f_g125-015.jpg
Notes: 24616044725 This is the first new sprout I've seen this year of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Beautiful, and full of hope for the new year. I saw lots more of them. It's so interesting how all the plants come up at the same time, presumably without communicating. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2016)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: g126a_g126-022.jpg
Notes:
24529939092 This is the first butterfly of 2016 - a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon)! These are small butterflies with no strong markings below, but they are real Blues (subfamily (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera). You can even see a tiny slice of blue color on its upper wings showing just behind its head. This one is "puddling" - taking moisture - on the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon today. It 's a new year! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)

It never did get very cold last night, and today was another spring-like day. I can't complain about the weather, but ... where's the rain?? Weather pundits are still saying the El NiÒo rains are coming, see this article in the L.A. Times this morning. We'll see.

File.Name: g126b_g126-015.jpg
Notes: 24637901735 I found this small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus, Araneidae) with a tiny fly for prey in the woods this morning. The spider seems to have a bump of its back which reminds me of this spider - Araneus bispinosus. There was little breeze today, but that breath of air was just enough to rock this small spider back and forth on its web. There was nothing to do except up the ISO a notch and take lots of photos and try to catch it at the apex of its swing. It could be worse! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)

File.Name: g126c_g126-010.jpg
Notes:
24611290916 This is another photo of the mushrooms I first thought were pink-spored Pluteus. Now I believe they are an atypical Psathyrella uliginicola aka Coprinopsis uliginicola or C. lignicola (name proposed), thanks Jacob! I still think they look Pluteus-like, compare this photo from yesterday. And isn't that a hint of a pink spore deposit on the stem?? These are growing from dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)

Yep, I'm confused.
ScientificName: Pluteus; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola; C. lignicola; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Pluteus; Psathyrella uliginicola; Coprinopsis uliginicola; C. lignicola; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g126d_g126-006.jpg
Notes: 24010369813 I noticed these tiny white mushrooms growing on a mossy rock near home today. They are also white-spored - you can see a dusting of white spores on the moss below some of the caps. They are tiny - tooth-size, and they resemble teeth! I believe they are genus Marasmius - marasmioid at least. Are they killing the moss? (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)

File.Name: g126e_g126-001.jpg
Notes: 24269050169 The Honey Mushrooms (Armillaria mellea aka Armillariella mellea) in this photo have turned into a lake of dark slime. I believe this is the work of the maggots of Drosophilid Flies (Drosophilidae, Diptera). The maggots are basically aquatic, and turn the mushrooms into liquid mush. You can see a few on the surface if you look close, but I would need a spoon to dig deeper. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)
ScientificName: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Drosophilidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Armillaria mellea; Armillariella mellea; Drosophilidae; Diptera

File.Name: g126f_g126-026.jpg
Notes:
24268616549 This is the handsome new foliage of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon today. This plant is perennial, so this is a new sprout and not a seedling. We had a 'dozer scrape the sides of our road a while back before the El NiÒo rains come, Hah! (I was outvoted ;=) Anyway, I'm happy to see that some of the perennials survived. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2016)

Weather pundits are still saying the El NiÒo rains are coming, see this article in the L.A. Times this morning. We'll see.

File.Name: g127a_g127-018.jpg
Notes:
24658310595 Here is a comparison photo of both male staminate (left) and female pistillate (right) catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. See my [Previous] two photos for full shots of each. The males are more dangly and show their stamens, and the ladies are more compact and show their pistils. Both male and female have just started to flower at this place at about the same time. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. Are they wind pollinated? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)

It was a beautiful day, with nary a breath of breeze. Our next chance of rain comes on Saturday night and Sunday. Weather pundits are still saying the El NiÒo rains are coming, see this article in the L.A. Times this morning. We'll see.

File.Name: g127b_g127-010.jpg
Notes: 24031118373 These are female pistillate flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. The catkins are less dangly than the male staminate plant in my [Previous] photo, and they look quite different. You can see the fertile pistils sticking out from the joints of the catkins. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. Are they wind pollinated? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)

File.Name: g127c_g127-004.jpg
Notes: 24657668825 These are male staminate flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. The long dangly catkins make for interesting "string-of-pearl" flowers hanging like ornaments from the shrub. They have just started to flower at this place. See my [Next] photo for the lady pistillate flowers. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants. Are they wind pollinated? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)

File.Name: g127d_g127-007.jpg
Notes: 24657326875 Here are two different galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera growing on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They look so different, but both are grape-sized and have a little beak opposite their point of attachment. I'm not sure why they look so different, but these are the work of a species, not the species themselves. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: g127e_g127-032.jpg
Notes: 24030165183 Here is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing in a weedy place where the sun never shines at this time of year. I'm sure they are genus Panaeolus or Psathyrella, but I won't try to guess which one. The gills are still pallid in these young caps, but they will darken with age. They are not exciting mushrooms, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)

File.Name: g127f_g127-033.jpg
Notes: 24630559856 Here are crustose lichens growing on a sandstone outcrop in the woods, like a patchwork quilt. How many different lichens do you see? (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2016)

File.Name: g128a_g128-007.jpg
Notes:
24385340900 Here are more tiny thrips (order Thysanoptera) on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). Look how they are clustered around the throat of the flower. I'm sure there are many more inside! I believe they are Predatory Thrips in the genus Aeolothrips of the family Aeolothripidae. Are they really predatory? On what?? They are tiny - only a mm or two long. I went back into the higher mountains today hoping to get a photo of the interesting flies and wild bees that are also working the flowers, but these were the only critters I could capture in a photo - again. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 January 2016)

Today was nice enough, but the breeze kicked up in the afternoon and some interesting clouds showed up. This may be an early sign of our next weather system, due this weekend. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: g128b_g128-020.jpg
Notes: 24052572604 Here are lots of new flowers on a branch of Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These deciduous shrubs are beautiful as they start to leaf out and flower at the same time. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 January 2016)

File.Name: g128c_g128-016.jpg
Notes: 24384925440 Here are lots of new flowers on a shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family near Arroyo Burro saddle higher in the mountains. It looks like the white balance is off, but this is how they are here - a bit more blue than usual. Even though some of these shrubs have been flowering for 4+ months already (see this photo from September 7!), the plants are really just coming into their own now. They are one of our longest flowering natives. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 January 2016)

File.Name: g128d_g128-020.jpg
Notes: 24051916214 These are "rolled leaves" on leaves of an interesting oak in our mountains that I believe is Shreve Oak (Quercus parvula var. shrevei, Fagaceae). The leaves are shiny and smooth on both upper and lower surfaces, quite different from our usual oaks. I'm pretty sure the "rolled leaves" are real galls and not just mechanically rolled. I find the same galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), see this photo which also shows the inside of an old gall of the same sort. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 January 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus parvula; shrevei; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: g128e_g128-035.jpg
Notes: 24679552525 I'm pretty sure this is new growth of native Purple Needle Grass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family. It's growing on the red conglomerate soil of the Sespe Formation along Painted Cave Road - a non-marine sedimentary deposit with these characteristic "redbeds". I really like this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2016)

File.Name: g128f_g128-038.jpg
Notes: 24585861791 This is new growth of native Soap Plant aka Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. Lots of them! They look like daffodils coming up in someone's winter garden, but these are native perennials growing from hairy bulbs, see this photo from nearby. I'm always happy to find them. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2016)

File.Name: g129a_g129-018.jpg
Notes:
24606550081 This is a triptych of photos showing a tiny Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). See my [Previous] photo and look close! In the first photo, it's digging in; then the weevil noticed me and back out; and finally it moved away. I got the photo on the right at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. It's about 645 pixels from the tip of its butt to the tip of its snout, which comes to 2.5 mm - and much of that is snout. (See here for how I figured.) Like I said, it's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

Today started nice enough, but it turned breezy and cloudy. I hope this is an early sign of a weather change as our next weather system moves in. It sounds like we have a chance of good rain on Sunday. Bring it on!

File.Name: g129b_g129-016.jpg
Notes:
24404316450 Here are new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). If you look very close on the top of the flower just to the right and above center, you can see the tiny Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) in my [Next] photo. I first noticed this tiny weevil in my viewfinder. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

Some of the new flowers are pure white like these. Others are blushing pink like the ones in this photo. Vive la diffÈrence!

File.Name: g129c_g129-023.jpg
Notes: 24404026880 I noticed this bristly Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae, Diptera) high in a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) this morning. It was a few feet away up in the shrub, so this is a very tight crop. This is not the best photo, but its an interesting fly. That's how it's been lately - interesting flies that won't get close. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

File.Name: g129d_g129-013.jpg
Notes: 24699170705 I'm pretty sure this is the thimble-like nest of a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. You can see the spider inside if you look close. You can also barely make out the spider's (out of focus) proper orb web in the foreground. These interesting spiders make two different webs - an real orb web with a cobwebby retreat nearby, usually decorated with the bodies of their prey. It's tough to show both webs at once. This one is in a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)
CommonNames: Labyrinth Spider; Orb Weavers; Bigberry Manzanita

File.Name: g129e_g129-035.jpg
Notes: 24403348690 These are the first new flowers of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family, probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus). Young plants have woolly-white leaves, see this photo. I need to wait for seed pods and take my Jepson manual down to the river to sort this out, but - hah! - I've been saying that for years! Last year I found the first flowers on January 21, so these are right on schedule. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 January 2016)

File.Name: g129f_g129-002.jpg
Notes: 24330922349 Here are the first flowers I've found this year of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Last year I found the first flowers on 18 January and the year before on 21 January so I guess they are right in schedule. I found these in a sunny place. Our plants in the shady woods will have to wait a while. The new year ticks on... (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

File.Name: g130a_g130-006.jpg
Notes:
24093515293 I noticed this neat little Plume Moth (Pterophoridae, Lepidoptera) hanging from a stem of non-native Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis, Lamiaceae) on the edge of our garden today. There are both nocturnal and diurnal kinds of these moths - night and day, many kinds. Their outstanding feature is their "cruciform" (cross-like) wings. This one has raindrops on its legs. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2016)

We were in a "cloud cap" today - gloomy at home, but clear just down the mountains towards town. That's a good rain sign. Then it mostly cleared up, with winds from the north-west. That's NOT a good rain sign, as our best storms come from the south. Hopefully that will change tonight. We still have a 100% chance of good rain tomorrow. I hope this storm doesn't miss us like so many others have this year.

File.Name: g130b_g130-003.jpg
Notes: 24719835605 This is the pad of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning, wearing a halo of spider webs with rain drops. It reminds me of the aureola or nimbus or glory around the head of a saint in early Christian art. I suspect the webs are the work of small Cobweb Spiders (Theridiidae). We didn't actually have rain this morning, but there was enough drizzle to soak the chair cushions and leave a bit in the rain gauge. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2016)

File.Name: g130c_g130-013.jpg
Notes: 24091326204 I noticed these tiny sporophytes of a moss (Bryophyta) growing on a concrete utility-box cover in our canyon today. I'm not sure if this is a native moss as I haven't seen them anywhere else, though there are lots of other native mosses in the woods. They are pretty, with raindrops reflecting the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2016)

File.Name: g130d_g130-014.jpg
Notes: 24090930964 Here are little dried up clumps of Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica), a familiar Jelly Fungus in the Tremelliales family, growing on a fallen oak branch that's covered with a resupinate Crust Fungus (Steraceae and allies). My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two." We did have a bit of drizzle this morning, so I was looking for new mushrooms today, but I only found these. Nah, not enough moisture yet. We're still stuck in year 5 of our drought with just 45% normal rainfall. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2016)
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Jelly Fungus; Crust Fungus

File.Name: g130e_g129-009.jpg
Notes: 24092004663 This is the first new flower of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've found this year. You can see one of real flowers poking out just left of center, the rest of it is showy red bracts. I first thought it was too early for these, but I checked my records and darned if I didn't find the first one last year on the same day, January 29! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

File.Name: g130f_g129-011.jpg
Notes: 24090459534 Here are more new flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing in a different place from the ones I showed before in this photo. The flower heads seem more open, but they come from the same soft white stems. Maybe the difference is that I got the previous photo in the rain! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2016)

File.Name: g131a_g131-027.jpg
Notes:
24376143209 There's been a lot of water under the bridge since I've seen this much water under the bridge where West Camino Cielo Road crosses San Jose Creek at the lower end of Kinevan Canyon! The creek was dry yesterday, but it's cranking after our 3-3/4 inches of rain this morning! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2016)

We had a few hours of genuinely hard rain this morning! I believe this storm brings us up to 11.42 inches for the rain year - still only 66% of normal, but at least we're over the 1/2 mark. There were a few minutes there when the rainfall rate hit 0.12 inch per minute - which comes to 7.2 inch per hour! Pretty exciting. The rain stopped shortly after noon, but then the winds kicked up - gusts up to nearly 50 mph for a while, though they've started to back off now. For a while we were under four NWS weather alerts - "Flood Advisory", "Flash Flood Warning", "Severe Thunderstorm Warning", and "High Wind Warning", as well as the usual "Hazardous Weather Outlook". It's all over now except for the wind. It's turned cold again, about 35∞ right now with a serious wind chill. *Sigh* Now we're due for a week of dry and warmer weather. I'll be looking for mushrooms tomorrow!

File.Name: g131b_g131-020.jpg
Notes: 24447917910 This is looking down San Jose Creek today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road. The creek was dry yesterday, but it's cranking after our 3-3/4 inches of rain this morning! You can see alder, sycamore, and bay trees - and even a young Redwood (Sequoia sempervirens, Cupressaceae) on the right! That one must be introduced, but I don't know where it came from. Are there others in Kinevan Canyon? (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2016)

File.Name: g131c_g131-023.jpg
Notes: 24114772884 This is looking up San Jose Creek today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road. The creek was dry yesterday, but it's cranking after our 3-3/4 inches of rain this morning! You can see a little White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia, Betulaceae) tree with catkins in the foreground, and behind it on the right an Incense Cedar (Calocedrus decurrens, Cupressaceae) tree. I believe the cedar was introduced long ago, though they are native in the backcountry and they've been seeding and proliferating in Kinevan canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2016)
ScientificName: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae
associatedTaxa: Alnus rhombifolia; Betulaceae; Calocedrus decurrens; Cupressaceae

File.Name: g131d_g131-034.jpg
Notes: 24648847911 Here are two photos of an impressive waterfall as viewed from along San Marcos Road (the "old pass road") this afternoon. I didn't believe it would have water, but it sure does! I've sat in that little notch at the top when the falls are dry, but there's no way to get there when they are cranking like this. I don't know a name for these falls, but Jack Eliott calls them Hankins Falls . I think "Arrowhead Falls" would also be a good name. Darn, I don't even have a name for this creek which I've driven past hundreds of times. I guess it's the West Fork of Maria Ygnacio Creek? There are several of these large waterfalls visible from town after a good rain, but they usually stop flowing soon after the rain stops. (San Marcos Road, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 January 2016)

File.Name: g131e_g131-009.jpg
Notes: 24648439231 Here are lichens and mosses on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the rain today. Look how even the dead twigs in the upper-left are covered with lichens. It's remarkable how quickly they come back. I got this photo from under cover on our upstairs porch this morning. It was raining too hard even for me to go out! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2016)

File.Name: g131f_g131-001.jpg
Notes:
24647963921 A hard rain's a-gonna fall!

This is the view from under the eaves of our upstairs porch this morning showing raindrops with a California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) plant in the background. We had a few hours of genuinely hard rain this morning, leaving us with 3-3/4 inches by noon! I believe this brings us up to 11.42 inches for the rain year - still only 66% of normal, but at least we're above 1/2. It rained hard enough to produce some run off, but I never made it to the river to see if there's a flow. A nice day... but then the wind kicked up. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2016)

File.Name: g201a_g201-028.jpg
Notes:
24647489492 Here are two photos of what I guess is the Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It's a large beautiful mushroom with a wonderful smell that is probably edible though I haven't tried them yet. Our woodland agaricus species are a risky bunch, so I'm wary. I only found this one today which seems odd because I usually find them in groups. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)

Wind, wind, wind... It sucks the moisture from the air and from that critical mulch layer that the mushrooms need to fruit. The lichens in the trees are already shriveled up. We had 50 mph winds yesterday, and close to that again today. But despite the drying winds, I found a few new mushrooms today! I'm sure they were already present before our good rains yesterday, but the rain helped them poke up through the oak mulch. The wind seems to be slowly calming down now, so maybe tomorrow will be a nicer day. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)

File.Name: g201b_g201-021.jpg
Notes: 24738464446 These are fairly large Tricholoma mushrooms with "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. I regard the reddish-brown spots on the gills and stems as distinctive. They are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I used to regard them as part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, though that species is now considered to be European. Maybe they are Tricholoma dryophilum? Edibility is unknown, not recommended for the table. I wouldn't be interested anyway with that farinaceous smell and taste. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tricholoma pessundatum; Tricholoma dryophilum
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tricholoma pessundatum; Tricholoma dryophilum

File.Name: g201c_g201-011.jpg
Notes:
24468572510 I have to chuckle because this red-pored bolete mushroom is so small! Compare the oak leaf to the side, which are usually about an inch long. Well, it's been another one those years so far, but just maybe that will change?

Small as it is, I believe this is a tiny "Satan's Bolete" (Rubroboletus eastwoodiae aka Boletus eastwoodiae aka Boletus satanas) mushroom in the woods today. Compare this photo! Or maybe it's Rubroboletus pulcherrimus without the bulbous base? This one is still too small to tell. Sure, I replanted it it to hopefully reconnect with its mycelium and grow up! This is the first of any kind of bolete mushroom I've found in the woods so far this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)
ScientificName: those; Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus satanas; Rubroboletus pulcherrimus
associatedTaxa: those; Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus satanas; Rubroboletus pulcherrimus

File.Name: g201d_g201-015.jpg
Notes: 24763503805 I found this different Russula mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), compare my [Previous] photo. This one isn't as deeply colored as Russula mairei in the "Russula emetica" group, but it's not white either. I don't know what it is, and I didn't nibble. It is rather pretty with those rosey colors. Russulas seem to form a continuum between white and deep red in our mountains, and it seems wrong to guess. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016).
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica

File.Name: g201e_g201-013.jpg
Notes: 24645410942 I found these Russula mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Maybe they are Russula cremoricolor or Russula albidula? I didn't nibble, but I'm pretty sure they taste "acrid" - like slightly "off" wasabi. (I've nibbled before.) It looks like some other critter has been nibbling on the larger mushroom on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula cremoricolor; Russula albidula
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula cremoricolor; Russula albidula

File.Name: g201f_g201-053.jpg
Notes:
24467447930 Creeks go up, creeks come down...

This is another photo looking up San Jose Creek today from the bridge on West Camino Cielo Road. Compare my photo from yesterday! It's the same creek, but different light - shady-dull rather than cloudy-bright. The day before yesterday, the creek was dry. That's the way it is with our creeks, at least until the water table get more fully recharged. They flood one day in the rain, and drop to almost nothing the next day. But watch out for those floods! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2016)

File.Name: g202a_g202-022.jpg
Notes:
24784954645 New flower-like foliage on a whole plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. Yep, it's a foliage, the tube-like flowers have yet to appear, but the reddish bracts do look like flowers. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. The reddish bracts are a bit overexposed, but I still like this photo on the plant illuminated by a sunbeam. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

Today is Groundhog Day, midway between solstice and equinox. The days are getting longer and the sun is a bit higher, we are starting to get a bit of sun in the afternoon on our upstairs porch. The old story goes that if a groundhog sees it's shadow on this day, then we're due for six more weeks of winter - until the equinox. (I did a stumper about this many years ago.) We don't have groundhogs in California, but if any gopher stuck its head up today, it would depend on when he did it. It was sunny this morning, but cold and frosty - with calm winds, very nice after a few windy days. Then the wind kicked up, and clouds started to blow past, and darned if we didn't get a bit of rain! So when did the gopher poke its head out? I'll go for six more weeks of winter. We need it.
Locality: I did a stumper about this many years ago.) We don't have groundhogs in California
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Groundhog Day
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: g202b_g202-005.jpg
Notes: 24157627343 Here are the first flowers I've seen this season of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The flowers are late this year - I found the first ones on December 19 last year - but the rains have been late too. The flowers look a lot like those of the Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in this photo, but the whole plant is glandular - new stems, leaves, peduncles, even the flowers themselves. I first noticed the flowers a couple of days ago, but the wind and rain made me wait for a photo. I got this shot in the shade this morning. I'll get a better photo in the sun sometime soon. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

File.Name: g202c_g202-018.jpg
Notes: 24758060336 This is the unopened partial veil of a Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescen) mushroom in the woods today. The veil covers the gills until the cap starts to expand, then tears free around the outside edge to form the "ring" on the stem - see my [Previous] photo. I can't account for that black stain on the stem that looks burned. Maybe it's an invading fungus on this fungus? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

File.Name: g202d_g202-028.jpg
Notes: 24157265283 This is a cluster of what I believe are Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) mushrooms pushing up a "mushrump" of fallen leaves in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. These caps aren't so richly colored as the mushroom I showed yesterday in this photo, but they have the same important characteristics. They are large beautiful mushrooms with a wonderful smell that is probably edible though I haven't tried them yet. Our woodland agaricus species are a risky bunch, so I'm wary. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

File.Name: g202e_g202-001.jpg
Notes: 24416139679 This is a small but beautiful mushroom growing in the oak mulch above our retaining wall. It's only about an inch tall. The mushroom has white gills, but maybe it's a pink-spored Pluteus mushroom like the Deer Mushroom or Fawn Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus)? Compare this photo of the same kind of mushroom a week ago. That one disappeared before I could verify the spore color, but I get to try again. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

File.Name: g202f_g202-031.jpg
Notes: 24156789123 I believe this is a Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, maybe the Field Dog Lichen (Peltigera rufescens)? This one is growing on a sandstone rock with mosses. Note the tiny mushroom growing down from the lowermost lobe of the lichen. This lichen has a few "dog teeth" which are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. These pelt lichens are common in the Oregon woods, but they are quite uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one trail, but they almost disappear into a dark crust when dry, so they would be easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2016)

File.Name: g203a_g203-036.jpg
Notes:
24805538785 This is a plain Echo Azure (Celastrina echo) aka Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly taking a sip from the nectar disk of a new flower of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of the flowers. These small butterflies have no strong markings below, but they are real Blues (subfamily (Polyommatinae, Lepidoptera), and this one looked quite blue when it took wing. I reckon it's male as the ladies are less blue above. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2016)

Apparently the names have changed and the true Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly is now regarded as only occurring in the eastern U.S. and Canada, see the link in NatureShutterbug's comment below. I'll probably keep calling them Spring Azures for many a year!

It was a nice enough day today, but still a bit breezy. It was cold this morning, but I didn't see any ice. The sun is starting to skim over the tops of the oak trees and give us a bit of sunlight on the upstairs porch in the afternoon, which is a bit of welcome warmth when I come home every day.
Locality: Celastrina ladon) butterfly is now regarded as only occurring in the eastern U.S. and Canada
CommonNames: Echo Azure; Spring Azure; Bigpod Ceanothus; Blues; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Celastrina echo; Celastrina ladon; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Celastrina echo; Celastrina ladon; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: g203b_g203-041.jpg
Notes:
24805224885 This is looking close at the new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that are starting to flower in our mountains. Them are surprisingly intricate, like little origami boxes, with a blue nectar disk at the base. See my [Next] photo. In a good year, the shrubs cover the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow - but I don't expect that in this (still) drought year. I got this photo on the way back from town today, near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass". (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2016)

(I reckon "San Marcos Pass" covers the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp, and Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle.)
Locality: I reckon "San Marcos Pass" covers the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp

File.Name: g203c_g203-022.jpg
Notes: 24778557366 These are new flowers on a climbing vine of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year. We also have its cousin Marah fabaceus which grows more in the backcountry with smaller more yellow flowers. This one also grows as a fire-follower higher in the mountains, and it's confusing where they intersect. But M. macrocarpus has 8-12 seeds in its spiky fruit, and M. fabaceus has just four. These plants are monoeciouswith separate male and female flowers on the same plant. If you look close, you can spot a female pistillate flower near the bottom of the flower stalk that goes off to the right in the center. (Foothills, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2016)
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah fabaceus; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; monoecious

File.Name: g203d_g203-026.jpg
Notes: 24508826950 Here are pretty new flowers of native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) in the (Anacardiaceae) plant family. The leaves are thick and plane, unlike the folded taco-shell leaves of the native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in my [Previous] photo. These shrubs are strictly coastal. I found this one at about 800 feet elevation today, which is about their upper limit. (Foothills, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2016)

File.Name: g203e_g203-014.jpg
Notes: 24804153715 These are beautiful new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The buds will wait on the plant for months to flower, until March or April. The foliage is beautiful too, with thick succulent leaves, folded like hard taco shells. Compare my [Next] photo of its cousin Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia). That one grows lower in the mountains, more a plant of the coast. This one favors the higher mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2016)

File.Name: g203f_g203-006.jpg
Notes:
24436204669 Is this tree half-dead or half-alive?

This is looking into the crown of a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family that is slowly succumbing to (I guess) and infestation of Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). It's odd that one tree succombs and another right next to it survives. You can see several of the large cones high up in the tree if you look close. This particular tree is growing by an old homestead along West Camino Cielo, and I suspect it was originally planted. The native trees in the mountains are also dying, at least some of them are dying. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2016)

File.Name: g204a_g204-006.jpg
Notes:
24529751840 This is a raft of tiny flies (Diptera) floating in our plastic trash can/rain barrel today in deep shade. They went 'round and 'round in the breeze this morning. I didn't do anything with the lighting, no flash, but I had to turn down my exposure a couple of stops. The white balance is what it is. I really can't explain anything in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2016)

It was quite nice today, though it feels uncommonly dry after we had so much rain just a few days ago. I have commitments tomorrow. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: g204b_g204-015.jpg
Notes: 24731636261 Here is another photo of the odd flower-like galled buds on a shrub of native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I believe they are the bud galls of a tiny Gall Midge - genus Rhopalomyia, family Cecidomyiidae, order Diptera. See my [Previous] photo for the real flowers of this shrub. The tiny atypical leaves surrounding the gall are quite beautiful. They don't seem to be common. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2016)
ScientificName: Ribes speciosum; Grossulariaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ribes speciosum; Grossulariaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: g204c_g204-011.jpg
Notes: 24196635614 The new flowers of native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family have been slow coming since I found the first one almost two weeks ago, see this photo. Finally, the beautiful new flowers are starting to appear. This plant is growing down a steep embankment, and I don't want to venture down until the flowers are better developed! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2016)

File.Name: g204d_g204-034.jpg
Notes: 24197612703 Here are flowering stems of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. tomentosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The glabrous Bigleaf Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. macrophyllum) that grows on Sycamore and Cottonwood trees (among others) along the river seems much more common, see this photo. I only know a few places were this more pubescent variety grows, but maybe it's mostly hidden high in the oak trees? (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 February 2016)

File.Name: g204e_g203-032.jpg
Notes: 24798043546 This is a bee-utiful butt shot of a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) digging into a new flower of native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) in the (Anacardiaceae) plant family. It appears to be going for nectar, but it also has loaded pollen baskets. HBBBT! (Foothills, Santa Ynez Mountains, 3 February 2016)

File.Name: g204f_g124-005.jpg
Notes: 24195905864 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a tiny thrip (Thysanoptera) disappearing into a flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). Hah, sure this is a deep crop. I believe the thrip is a Predatory Thrip (Aeolothrips, Aeolothripidae), see this photo for more. I got this photo more than a week ago, but I've been saving it for. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 January 2016)

File.Name: g205a_g205-020.jpg
Notes:
24818372806 Here are three photos of different Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) snuggling into new flowers of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. See my [Previous] photo. The bees are snuggling for nectar, but look how their tails are touching the stigma of the flowers. I can't guarentee that these are the same kind of bee. There were also similar-sized black bees with white faces working the flowers, and I'm suspicious of the one on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2016)

I had an appointment in the Santa Ynez Valley this afternoon which got in the way of new photos. Just three posts today, but there are actually six if you count individual shots, so I reckon I did my duty!

File.Name: g205b_g205-016.jpg
Notes: 24217382743 Here are two photos of Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on a newly flowering shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. That bee on the left sure looks like it regurgitated something that disappears in the next photo I got. I can't be absolutely certain that these are the same kind of bee, but they look similar except for the wing color which may be a matter of lighting. There were also similar-sized black bees with white faces working the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2016)

File.Name: g205c_g204-002.jpg
Notes: 24217150433 Here is another raft of tiny flies (Diptera) floating in our plastic trash can/rain barrel yesterday. See my [Previous] photo. I can also see a tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insect in the lower left. They go 'round and 'round in the slight current, but mostly the clusters of flies stay together. Maybe that's the work of surface tension and those slight depressions that the insects make in the water, like a rubber sheet demo of general relativity? I didn't do anything with the lighting, no flash, but I had to turn down my exposure a couple of stops. The white balance is what it is. I guess the background is the blue sky, though I got this photo in deep shade. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2016)

File.Name: g206a_g206-002.jpg
Notes:
24236459984 "Me and my shadow..."

I finally got a photo of a furry little Bee Fly (genus Bombylius, Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on an oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon this morning! I've been trying for a while. You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. I believe this is a larger female fly. I see both these larger ones and also smaller ones that I guess are male, and sometimes I see them flying off together. See this photo. I'll post a photo of the smaller male fly tomorrow. I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

It was a beautiful day today, warm and calm - really too warm for February. We got up to 70∞ on the upstairs porch this afternoon, and it was no doubt warmer on the sunnier side of the canyon. The forecast is for 80s on Monday and Tuesday. I fear that our hopes for the much-hyped El NiÒo rains are evaporating.

Notes:
24864106855 "Me and my shadow..."

I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

File.Name: g206c_g206-015.jpg
Notes: 24568013560 I guess this is the Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron aka Xerocomellus chrysenteron, or kin) mushroom in the woods today. It's a typical "bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills. This one has blue-staining gills, but it's not one of those mushrooms. They should be common right now, but this is the first and only one I've seen this year so far. The cap of this one is brown and cracked, but with only a hint of red in the cracks. Sure, it could also be B. truncatus. This one is growing in a place with deep oak mulch, with new sprouts of mugwort and wood mint. If I'm right about the ID, it's said to be edible but mushy and insipid. It's not one of the choice boletes. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)
ScientificName: Boletus chrysenteron; Xerocomellus chrysenteron; those; B. truncatus

File.Name: g206d_g206-030.jpg
Notes: 24567832610 These are fairly large Tricholoma mushrooms with "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. I believe they are they are Tricholoma dryophilum, which I used to regard as part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. These are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Edibility is unknown, not recommended for the table. I wouldn't be interested anyway with that farinaceous smell and taste. See this photo of the same mushrooms. With those reddish-brown stains on the caps, I would almost take them for Cortinarius mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

File.Name: g206e_g206-023.jpg
Notes: 24234804494 Here are mosses, liverworts, and ferns (oh my!) growing on a still-damp sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. The fern is a small Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis, Polypodiaceae). I believe the liverworts are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica) - which already are producing sporophytes! I have no idea what the mosses Bryophyta are. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

associatedTaxa: Pentagramma triangularis; Polypodiaceae; Asterella californica; Bryophyta

File.Name: g206f_g206-035.jpg
Notes: 24862788245 Here is a little patch of moss (Bryophyta) - with sporophytes - growing on a concrete utility-box cover in our canyon today. I'm not sure if this is a native moss as I haven't seen them anywhere else, though there are lots of other native mosses in the woods. I'm posting this one for the Growing in the gaps group. Life finds a place! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

File.Name: g207a_g207-004.jpg
Notes:
24886786025 Here is a tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa) - a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae). I found it sitting in the inner circle of its web in the "cactus patch" of our garden this morning. It felt dead calm, but the tiny breezes and low shade light made this a challenging shot. I had to take *many* photos to get this keeper. They have a remarkable "tubercle" on their abdomens, like a bottle gourd. You can see a bit of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white webs above and below. Usually these spiders decorate them with the bodies of their prey, but this one is just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2016)

Today was sunny and calm, with moments of wind. At least we don't get those nasty Santa Ana winds like Ventura County just to the south of us, where they are have had fire weather today with 40+ mph winds. That's because we don't have a direct path to the desert in Santa Barbara County. We do get our share of wind, especially the Sundowner Winds, but not today. It was quite warm, into the upper 70s, and tomorrow is expected to be even warmer, into the 80s. Warm and dry is expected for the rest of the month, so our chances of El NiÒo rains are evaporating.

I did watch a bit of Superbowl 50. I'm not a fan and I don't care who wins, but I have to marvel at the photography and computer stuff. Now if we could just get that kind of technology working on local insects...

File.Name: g207b_g207-019.jpg
Notes: 24768477322 Here are two shots of galled flowers of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The shot on the left shows a galled flower bud next to a normal one, note the abruptly swollen ovary of the flower. I sacrificed the one on the right, and plucked it from a stem and posed it on leaves after removing a few of the red sepals. The petals and stamens look totally different from normal, compare my [Previous] photo on the left. I'm not sure whose gall this is, as Ron Russo doesn't describe it in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). Life is full of surprises when you look close! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2016)

File.Name: g207c_g207-017.jpg
Notes: 24590570430 Here are comparison shots of two similar but different native Gooseberry shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family. On the left is the Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum), and on the right is the Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum). The one on the right is glandular throughout , but the other isn't. The one on the left has spiky ovaries that the other lacks. The one on the right looks a little rougher and stubbier. They have differently colored stamens, though I'm not sure that's always true. The important key difference is the presence of glands. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2016)
CommonNames: Gooseberry; Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Bitter Gooseberry

File.Name: g207d_g207-011.jpg
Notes: 24518109299 Here are female pistillate flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Garryaceae plant family. These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen - see this photo for a comparison. Those are the polished pistils sticking out. I assume they are wind pollinated, but last year the males never opened, and the ladies did just fine producing seed on their own. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2016)

File.Name: g207e_g207-009.jpg
Notes: 24767424792 This is new foliage on a native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, aka Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea in the Adoxaceae. This shrub is deciduous and just starting to get its spring leaves. Sure, this shrub has opposite leaves. These shrubs are already fully leafed out and probably starting to flower down in the foothills, but they are just starting up here in the mountains. These shrubs have always been associated with music. The pithy stems are easy to hollow out, and make good flutes and clapper-sticks. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2016)

File.Name: g207f_g206-007.jpg
Notes:
24256842394 "Me and my shadow..." (part 3)

This is one more shot of a furry little Bee Fly (genus Bombylius, Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on an oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon yesterday morning. Compare my [Previous] two photos, which I believe are both the larger females. I believe this one shows the smaller male fly. I see both the larger ones and these smaller ones, sometimes flying off together. See this photo for both at once. I guess this patch of oak leaves that gets morning sun is their "hookup" place. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

File.Name: g208a_g208-030.jpg
Notes:
24790255702 I found this striking Becker's White (Pontia beckerii) butterfly today working new flowers of (I believe) the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family along the edge of Paradise Road on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River today. I think of this butterfly as a desert species, but here it is. One of its host plants is Prince's Plume (Stanleya pinnata) in the Brassicaceae which I've found growing nearby - see this photo. I guess we're becoming more desert-like every year as our drought continues into year 5. Despite predictions of El NiÒo rains this year, we continue to be dry with just 59% normal rainfall so far. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 February 2016)

Yipes, it was 89∞ at the weather station in Paradise today, where I was! I was overdressed with my usual winter sweatpants. It's already time to find my shorts before I venture out tomorrow.
CommonNames: Becker's White; Santa Barbara Milkvetch; Prince's Plume

File.Name: g208c_g208-008.jpg
Notes:
24611960490 These are new flowers of a native Milkvetch aka Locoweed (Astragalus) in the Fabaceae plant family, probably the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus). They are spilling onto Paradise Road, which was repaved last summer. These are beautiful native wildflowers, growing with the weeds right along the road. Now why are they here? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 February 2016)

This was my stumper for the currant newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society which I put together last month:

"Soon it will be time to get outside and enjoy the wildflowers that will appear if the El NiÒo rains appear as expected. The best wildflower fields are in the backcountry on Figueroa Mountain and beyond, far from human disturbance. But it's convenient that many wildflowers are abundant right along the roads. Introduced weeds are also common, but there are many natives like poppies and lupines that thrive along our highways despite traffic and mowing and scraping. Wildlife follows. What environmental factors make the roadsides so attractive for native wildflowers and wildlife?"

Fortunately I don't have to answer this one for a few months until the next issue comes out!

File.Name: g208d_g208-017.jpg
Notes: 24280337533 These are the first fully open flowers I've seen this year on native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family, growing near the river in Paradise. The new flowers are surprisingly intricate. There was a breeze today, so I couldn't get closer - but see this photo from last year. I thought they were starting to flower a month ago in

File.Name: g208e_g208-012.jpg
Notes: 24611473360 I found this pretty new flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) by the river today. I'm not sure if it's one of the last flowers of the past year or one of the first new flowers of this year! These shrubs always seem to have at least a few flowers. Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida, Papaveraceae) is another native shrub like that. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 February 2016)

File.Name: g208f_g208-014.jpg
Notes: 24788728772 This is new growth of a perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, arising phoenix-like from last years dead stems. These plants are fire-followers, in the case growing on the old burn of the White Fire a few years ago. They are fire-followers, but they sometimes linger for decades. There's no sign of flower buds yet, but I reckon it won't be long. Last year I found open flowers in late January, see this photo, but we had good early rains that year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 February 2016)

File.Name: g209a_g209-021.jpg
Notes:
24561438199 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I won't try to guess what kind of bee it is - there are so many! The flowers seem especially pretty. They've been flowering since early September, see this photo, but they are really just coming into their own. In a good year, these shrubs will flower for 6+ months. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

Yipes again, it was 89∞ again at the weather station in Paradise today. I stayed up in the mountains, but it was still pretty warm - up to 80∞ on our upstairs porch and no doubt warmer in the sunnier places where I was. *Sigh*

File.Name: g209b_g209-011.jpg
Notes: 24633384920 Here is a whorl of the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers already look a bit ratty, but that's how they are. Like all of our Salvias, the flowers have two stamens and a forked style. We have black, white, and purple sages in our mountains, and a few more to boot. They are lovely plants, all with fragrant foliage and edible seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

File.Name: g209c_g209-001.jpg
Notes: 24902125316 This is the first new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) that I've seen this year. Yep, flower and foliage look pretty sad. The blackberries have really taken a hit in these still-ongoing drought years. If you look close, it's interesting that our shrubs are "perfect" with both stamens and pistils, but the Jepson Manual says that they are mostly unisexual and dioecious with separate male and female plants. It's curious that the plants on the west side of the pass are mostly dioecious. I don't understand this difference. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

File.Name: g209d_g209-014.jpg
Notes:
24901721286 This is a new vine of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. It's climbing over a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). They have exuberant growth, coming from the enormous "man-root" of the plant. See Gerry Queener's photo (of a related species) here! Many years ago they had a large root on display at the Santa Barbara Botanic Garden that flowered for several years despite being out of the ground! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

We also have this plant's cousin Marah fabaceus which grows more in the backcountry with smaller more yellow flowers. This one also grows as a fire-follower higher in the mountains, and it's confusing where they intersect. But M. macrocarpus has 8-12 seeds in its spiky fruit, and M. fabaceus has just four. These plants are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same plant.

File.Name: g209e_g209-024.jpg
Notes: 24901360716 This is a cheerful yellow flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. You have to remember not to overexpose these flowers. I sometimes half-joke that there are always flowers on Bush Poppy *somewhere* in the mountains, and even in these in these drought years that's been true. The Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) in this photo is another shrub that almost always has a flower or two. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

File.Name: g209f_g209-015.jpg
Notes: 24834205141 This is beautiful new growth of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Beautiful, and full of hope for the new year. Fabulous foliage indeed! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2016)

File.Name: g210a_g210-026.jpg
Notes:
24654122890 Spider love! Just count the legs and divide by two...

I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transferring genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigynum. It's a tender moment... and complicated! Mating is difficult for these predators who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. As I understand it, the male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates his spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up, see my [Previous] photo. He then transfers his sperm to the female's epigynum. If you look very close, you can see the female's epigynum and the odd spine on the male's palp that seems to be engaged with it. Spider foreplay? (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

It was a good day for mating spiders! It was also another too-warm day, 80∞ on our upstairs porch and up to 88∞ by the river. *Sigh*
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; spermaphores; palps; epigynum
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; palps; epigynum; spermaphores; palps; epigynum

He will then transfer his sperm to the female's epigynum, see my [Next] photo. Mating is difficult for these predators who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

File.Name: g210d_g210-015.jpg
Notes:
24317866404 This is a female Sheetweb Spider (family Linyphiidae) on her web in a shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek today. That may look like blue sky in the background, but it's actually the steel bridge railing behind her. You can see her "lady parts" - her shiny brown epigynum at the base of her abdomen. Yep, it looks male-like, but that's how it is with spiders. After a few minutes she ducked under a nearby leaf. I followed her and got my [Next] photos! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

The epigynum is sclerotized like the hard shell of a beetle. They last even when the critter is gone. This is what makes spider and insect genitalia so important for taxonomy.
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; epigynum; sclerotized
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; epigynum; sclerotized

File.Name: g210e_g210-003.jpg
Notes: 24921924346 This is a new web of a Sheetweb Spider (family Linyphiidae) on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Yeah, it's a bit overexposed as photos of spider webs so often are. I think you can see the spider just below the center. I had better luck photographing these spiders today, see my [Next] few photos! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

File.Name: g210f_g210-031.jpg
Notes: 24652516360 I believe these are small Agaricus bitorquis mushrooms on the edge of the chipseal road in our canyon. They're not open, and they may not ever open with the hot and dry weather we've been having lately. See this photo for a larger open one. These mushrooms are said to favor roadsides and to be delicious. It has a dry white cap that slowly stains brown with handling. It's a pity that its drying out. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

File.Name: g211a_g211-018.jpg
Notes:
24969893605 I found this small diurnal (daytime) moth on the first flowers I've seen this year of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure it's a Crambid Snout Moth (family (Crambidae, order Lepidoptera), but it looks different from the more common Pyrausta dapalis moths that have red underwings. It's a pretty little moth, almost butterfly-like. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2016)

I had a meeting I couldn't get out of at midday, which cut into my photo time today. I got this photo on the way back from my beer run to town, near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass". I reckon "San Marcos Pass" covers the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp, and Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle. Today was another warm one, but a step cooler that it's been. It was 78∞ on our upstairs porch and up to 87∞ by the river. *Sigh* Our next chance of (a small) rain is next Wednesday.
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Crambidae; Lepidoptera; Pyrausta dapalis

File.Name: g211b_g211-015.jpg
Notes:
24674145090 Here are new flowers and old seed capsules of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't often see them together. The seed pods live up to the species name! See this photo for a closer shot of the tiny (but plentiful) flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2016)

I got this photo on the way back from my beer run to town today, near the intersection of Highway 154 and Painted Cave Road at about 1500 feet elevation, right on the lower edge of what I consider to be "the pass". I reckon "San Marcos Pass" covers the area from the Trout Club to the San Marcos Christian Camp, and Lizard Mouth to Knapp's Castle.

File.Name: g211c_g211-009.jpg
Notes: 24342108693 Here are the two webs of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers showing both its orb web on the left and its separate cobweb-like home web of the right. This web is spun in a shrub of Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in our garden, and the spider's house is decorated with old lavender buds. They use what ever plant material is handy, compare this photo on a different shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2016)

File.Name: g211d_g211-004.jpg
Notes: 24968637025 I found this tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa) - a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) - in the garden this morning. Here it is, sitting in the inner circle of her web. They are lovely little spiders with a remarkable "tubercle" on their abdomens, like a bottle gourd. You can see a bit of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white webs above the spider. This spider is small, less than 1/4 inch long. Maybe it's Cyclosa conica? Usually these spiders decorate their webs with the bodies of their prey, but this one is just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2016)
ScientificName: Cyclosa; Araneidae; stabilimentum - those thick white webs above the spider.  This spider is small, less than 1/4 inch long.  Maybe it's Cyclosa conica
