File.Name: g211e_g210-012.jpg
Notes: 24875179761 This is a "double butt shot" of a female Sheetweb Spider (family Linyphiidae) on her web with a little fly (Diptera) that I can't identify. This lady spider is one of the few I found yesterday that wasn't mating. I found it in a shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

File.Name: g211f_g210-021.jpg
Notes: 24875179761 Here is one more photo of spider love! Just count the legs and divide by two... I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transferring genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigynum. See my [Previous] three photos. This is also a pretty nice butt shot, so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2016)

File.Name: g212a_g212-005.jpg
Notes:
24358755114 This is a small spider with its prey in the "cactus patch" of our garden this morning. I believe the spider is a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) based on its web and the shape of its abdomen. I'm pretty sure its prey is not a bee, but an unfortunate Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) with no sign of a "wasp-waist". Last year, we had an infestation of sawfly wasp larvae eat all the new leaves and catkins of the large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck - see this photo, and [Previous] to it. The tree is still looking pretty sad, but at least it has buds for the new year. If this is the same sawfly that did the damage, I'm not sad. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2016)

Today was another warm one, almost 80∞ on our upstairs porch and up to 87∞ by the river. *Sigh* Our next chance of (a small) rain is next Wednesday. The woods feel real dry. This is definitely year 5 of our drought.
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Sawfly Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g212b_g212-011.jpg
Notes: 24693625730 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (The flowers are also tasty!) Last year I found the first flowers on January 12, so these are late - no doubt because we had our first real rain at the end of January this year. Yeah, the white balance is a little funky, but they are flowering in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, and they should look like this. If you look close, you can see the they have six stamens - four longer and two shorter, as most of this family. This is one of the first proper wildflowers that I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g212c_g212-023.jpg
Notes: 24693392700 This is a closer look at the new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family in my [Previous] photo. The flowers are related to avocado and sassafras. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with four anthers each, with two orange glands at the base of each, finally surrounded by an outer circle of six more fat stamens with four anthers each. The whole thing is contained within a circle of (usually) 6 sepals. They are strange and wonderful flowers for such a common tree in our California woods. If you haven't looked close at the flowers of this tree, I recommend you do so! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g212d_g212-021.jpg
Notes: 24895354161 Here is a brand new cluster of open flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are surprisingly intricate, see my [Next] photo for a closer shot. Sure, you can use the leaves as "bay leaves" for cooking - but not too much! They are flowering at home too, but these were in the sun. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g212e_g211-001.jpg
Notes: 24620499389 I believe this is a tiny Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera that I happened to notice on a dead stem on the wild edge of of garden yesterday. It must be less than 1/4 inch long. It has the whiskers and scooped-out top of its head that are characteristic of the family. It also has an odd humped back, and a chunkier abdomen than I expect. Maybe it's a gravid female? Like a dragonfly, this critter took off and returned to this perch a few times before it disappeared into the woods. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2016)

File.Name: g212f_g206-006.jpg
Notes:
24357380144 "Me and my shadow..." (again)

This is one more shot of a furry little Bee Fly (genus Bombylius, Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on a fallen oak leaf on the sunny side of our canyon a week ago, but I've been saving this for the Fly Day Friday group. You can see the fly's body under its hairy coat. I'm not sure if this is a larger female or a smaller male. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2016)

File.Name: g213a_g213-012.jpg
Notes:
25012224155 I found this little moth caterpillar pretending to be a twig in the woods this morning! I never would have noticed it, but it was waving a bit when there was no wind, so it caught my eye. It's not tiny, an inch or more long. I guess from the shape that it's the Looper or Inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera). Pretty good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2016)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but still unseasonably warm and dry. *Sigh* We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight and tomorrow, but it's calm so far.

File.Name: g213b_g213-013.jpg
Notes: 24644362029 This is another tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa) - a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) - in the garden this morning. Here it is, sitting in the inner circle of its web. They are lovely little spiders with a remarkable "tubercle" on their abdomens, like a bottle gourd. You can see a bit of the spider's stabilimentum - those thick white webs above and below the spider. This spider is small, less than 1/4 inch long. Maybe it's Cyclosa conica? This one is beginning to decorate its web with the bodies of its prey, like a pirate.
Locality: Araneidae) - in the garden this morning. Here it is

File.Name: g213c_g213-026.jpg
Notes: 24893643172 This mushroom is pushing up a very different "mushrump" through the chipseal road in our canyon! Compare my [Previous] photo of a mushroom pushing up a pile of oak leaves. I'm pretty sure this is an Agaricus bitorquis mushroom making a hole in our asphalt. I guess that's a seedling of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae or Montiaceae plant family to the left. I didn't pick this mushroom yet because I want it to grow up and I want better light than deep shade, but see this photo of a different specimen. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2016)
ScientificName: Agaricus bitorquis; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae
associatedTaxa: Agaricus bitorquis; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Montiaceae

File.Name: g213d_g213-006.jpg
Notes: 24715580810 I believe this is a deeply yellow-stained Amber-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus albolutescens) mushroom. It has a sweet almond smell, and the stem did stain amber/brown when I handled it. If I'm wrong, then cf. the Agaricus sylvicola group. Despite the yellow colors, I don't believe it's the Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) because it doesn't quickly bruise yellow and lacks that foul phenol-like smell. This mushroom is pushing up a "mushrump" of fallen leaves in deep leaf mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. (That's mushroom-author David Arora's felicitous word.) See my [Next] photo for a very different mushrump! It seems too hot and dry for mushrooms, but I found a few today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2016)
CommonNames: Amber-Staining Agaricus; Yellow-Staining Agaricus; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g213e_g213-033.jpg
Notes: 24715221130 Here are two shots of new flower catkins on native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today, showing the separate male staminate catkins (left) and female pistillate (right). These shrubs are dioecious with separate male and female plants, and the ladies look quite different from the gentlemen. Both are the same species, and they need each other. These catkins look wind-pollinated, but I'm sure that both male and female catkins produce nectar and are host to many insects - see this recent photo for an example. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2016)

File.Name: g213f_g213-017.jpg
Notes:
24714971660 "Leaves of three, let it be.
Leaves of four, eat some more!"
- Homer Simpson

Here are lovely new leaves of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family, in our canyon. It looks positively dripping with skin-irritating oil! This shrub isn't really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. (I'm not.) It really is a beautiful plant, and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2016)

File.Name: g214a_g214-010.jpg
Notes:
24406535874 Here are two shots of a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera carrying of a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) as prey. I found it this morning on new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's rather small, about the size of a manzanita flower, not much more than 1/4 inch. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The striped legs and the hyaline wings are remarkable. I've found this one before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

It was another unseasonably warm day, with some wind to boot. The wind never got up to advisory levels, but it was still blowing about 20 mph. The woods are feeling very dry. We don't have the usual burgeoning new growth that I expect as we move into spring.

*Sigh* I'll be busy for a day or too and probably won't be able to get out for new photos - but the recent folder is in good shape so I'll keep posting. I'm doing a presentation for the Hollister Ranch Conservancy on local insects next Saturday, and it's time to start picking photos. This has been in the back of my mind for a while, and it's time to bring it up front. The problem of course is too many photos, but I guess that's a good sort of problem to have!

File.Name: g214b_g214-002.jpg
Notes: 25010344776 This is a marvelous little caterpillar hanging from the spine of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. The caterpillar looks translucent, made of gummy bear stuff. I don't know whose caterpillar it is and I don't much care! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

File.Name: g214c_g214-020.jpg
Notes: 24918480502 These are the first flowers I've seen this year of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. I did see a hummingbird working the flowers today, but I couldn't get a photo. They appear to be classic hummingbird flowers with a long floral tube, very beautiful and not common. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: g214d_g214-043.jpg
Notes:
24405017554 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of the well-named native Padre's Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family. *Sigh* Apparently this plant has been recently renamed as Primula clevelandii, maybe var. patula with those blue anther tips? I guess all I can do as a naturalist and not a botanist is sway with the nomenclature breezes!

I really thought twice about posting this photo. It's taken in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, the white balance is wrong, and it was breezy... but what the heck - it's a record photo and I had to lie down in Stagecoach Road to get it - on a Sunday afternoon with Cold Springs Tavern traffic! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

File.Name: g214e_g214-005.jpg
Notes: 24404701314 This is the beautiful new foliage and even a few flower buds of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. It won't be too long before the flowers open, but I actually think the foliage is this plants best part. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

File.Name: g214f_g214-026.jpg
Notes: 24408143013 Here are two shots of a pretty little mushroom coming up in a mulch of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) leaves and Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri, Pinaceae) needles. My first thought was genus Cortinarius subgenus Telamonia, but there no sign of rusty-brown spores and spore-stained hairs on the stalk. Maybe it's too hot and dry for it to make spores? This one seems to have a viscid cap. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

File.Name: g215a_g215-007.jpg
Notes:
24426091554 This is another photo of the remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera that I found yesterday carrying a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) as prey, see this photo. I found it on new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's rather small, about the size of a manzanita flower, not much more than 1/4 inch. Looking at BugGuide, I think it might be species Pselliopus spinicollis in the subfamily Harpactorinae. The striped legs and the hyaline wings are remarkable. I've found this one before, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

Just three photos today, as I've been sitting at my computer all day working with photos all day and I'm weary. I'm doing a presentation for the Hollister Ranch Conservancy on local insects next Saturday, and I've been selecting photos from the past few years. At first I thought about organizing everything by insect family, but then I thought better. This won't be a university crowd of entomologists! My slide show will be more of a media blitz of *many* good photos in an hour with minimal talk. I think it will be a dynamite show.

It finally made it up to 90∞ by the river at Paradise today, and up into the low 80s on the upstairs deck at home. Too warm, but we have a good chance of rain on Wednesday night. Warm and calm, and I've been sitting at my computer all day! I'll try to get out tomorrow.

File.Name: g215c_g212-002.jpg
Notes: 24429533903 This is another photo of the first new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the canyon, seethis photo. Yep, the flower looks pretty ratty, like some insect nibbled it in the bud, and the blackberries have really taken a hit in these still-ongoing drought years. You can clearly see that our plants are "perfect" with both stamens and pistils, but the Jepson Manual says that they are mostly unisexual and dioecious with separate male and female plants. It's curious that the plants on the west side of the pass are mostly dioecious. I don't understand this difference. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g216a_g214-036.jpg
Notes:
24782870050 The greening of the Santa Ynez Valley...

Actually, it's been greening for a while and it's probably starting to dry out in this photo. You can see a bit of Grass Mountain on the left and Figueroa Mountain on the right, taken from a place along Stagecoach Road. That's all private ranch land in the foothills. We're expecting a bit of rain tomorrow night that I hope will help it stay green. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2016)

Just three photos again today, as I've been sitting at my computer all day working with photos all day and I'm weary. I'm doing a presentation for the Hollister Ranch Conservancy on local insects next Saturday, and I've been selecting photos from the past few years. I've got it down to about 500 photos for a one hour show at about 7 seconds for photo. There wont be time to say much more than "bee fly" - which suite me fine! I think it will be a dynamite show.

File.Name: g216b_g212-014.jpg
Notes: 24710471209 Here is a single flower head of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. It shows the green tubes of the real flowers hiding in the bright red bracts that make up the conspicuous part of the flower head. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g216c_g212-013.jpg
Notes: 24710226169 This is a whole plant of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing in the mountains. I reckon it's properly called a subshrub with stems that are woody at the base, but the whole plant is only about one foot tall. It's handsome, and unlike our other Castilleja species. The actual flowers aren't what you expect, see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2016)

File.Name: g217a_g217-018.jpg
Notes:
24472217803 Here are ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on new growth of not-quite-flowering native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The ant-aphid relationship is complicated. The ants actually seem to place the aphids on the new plant stems to start a new colony, protect them from predators like wasps and Syrphid flies, and harvest the honeydew that the aphids produce. That sure sounds like "animal husbandry" to me!

I've been tied to my computer for two days now, but enough is enough! My slide show is in good shape, so I ventured outside today to get a few photos before our newest storm moved in. We've only had 1/10 inch so far, but I hope more is coming. The forecast is for 1-2 inches tonight, but it sure looks like most of the rain is moving south of us on the radar map. *Sigh* We'll see what happens.
Locality: Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The ant-aphid relationship is complicated. The ants actually seem to place the aphids on the new plant stems to start a new colony

File.Name: g217b_g217-012.jpg
Notes: 25098607115 I found this tiny Fly (Diptera) on a grass blade in the woods today. I like to think it's a Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae - mostly because I like the family name! But I really don't know. The fly is about 530 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2 mm. See here for how I figured. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2016)

File.Name: g217c_g217-002.jpg
Notes: 24730697259 This is another shot of small spider with its prey in the "cactus patch" of our garden from this morning. I believe the spider is a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) based on its web and the shape of its abdomen. I'm pretty sure its prey is not a bee, but an unfortunate Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) with no sign of a "wasp-waist". Last year, we had an infestation of sawfly wasp larvae eat all the new leaves and catkins of the large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree that's closest to our upstairs deck - see this photo, and [Previous] to it. The tree is still looking pretty sad, but at least it has buds for the new year. If this is the same sawfly that did the damage, I'm not sad! I actually saw half a dozen of the dead wasps in the spider's web, so it's having good luck hunting.
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Sawfly Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g217d_g217-027.jpg
Notes: 24471183543 Here are two photos of what I believe is the Cracked-Cap Bolete aka Red-Cracked Bolete(Boletus chrysenteron aka Xerocomellus chrysenteron, or kin) mushroom in the woods today. I actually found several of them today. This one seems to have a red cap with yellow cracks, though sometimes it's the other way around. It's a typical "bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills. It has rather large pores that slowly stain blue when bruised, but it's not one of those blue-staining mushrooms. If I'm right about the ID, it's said to be edible but mushy and insipid. It's not one of the choice boletes. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2016)

File.Name: g217e_g217-005.jpg
Notes: 24979516472 This is new foliage of native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing on the hillside above our retaining wall at home. These particular plants haven't flowered for a few years, I guess because of the drought. But they are still alive and might do better this year. These large basal leaves will completely shrivel up by the time they flower in summer, leaving a bare asparagus-like stalk about two feet tall. See this photo for the flowers. It's interesting that I find what I believe is the same orchid species in Oregon in flowering mid-summer, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2016)

File.Name: g217f_g217-011.jpg
Notes: 25097519245 Here new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a perennial mustard with an edible root ("the tooth"), but please don't dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (The flowers are also tasty!) I just found the first new flowers a few days ago on February 12 (see this photo), and suddenly there are lots of them on the hillside above our retaining wall. How do these wildflowers know to all start flowering at the same time?? (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2016)

File.Name: g219a_g219-014.jpg
Notes:
25112716556 This is an absolutely gorgeous Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on the new flowers of non-native succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae) in the garden this morning. The butterfly must be newly emerged to look so brilliant, and indeed this is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2016)

We did get a inch of rain on Wednesday night! I apologize for missing photos yesterday, but it's been a busy week, and we went out for dinner with old friends last night to make it even busier! Just four photos today, as my mouse hand is starting to ache from too much mousing. I was mostly computer-bound today, finalizing my slide show for the Hollister Ranch crowd, and I'll miss tomorrow also when I present it in the evening. I trimmed the show considerably, and I reckon it will be good.

File.Name: g219b_g219-003.jpg
Notes: 24770910499 This is a tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera. I guess it's a male with those plumose antennae. Is it looking at me? I got this photo in a shady place on a cloudy morning with some breeze, white balance is what it is - or isn't! These might be the tiny flies that sometimes dance in the sunbeams in great numbers, see this photo. The fly is about 530 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2 mm. See here for how I figured. Yeah, they look mosquito-like, but they aren't interested in us. It's not much of a fly, but HFDF anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2016)

File.Name: g219c_g218-009.jpg
Notes: 24842627600 These are striking galls on new spring leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose gall they are, but I've been calling them "Painted Toenail Galls". Ron Russo doesn't describe them in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. They look different as they age (don't we all!) - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2016)

File.Name: g219d_g218-002.jpg
Notes: 24842284720 Here are brand new pollen-bearing staminate catkins on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree by our mailbox in the canyon. This is the first tree I've seen in flower this year in the mountains, though I've been seeing them along the highway on the way to town for a while. (That's not counting the oddball tree I found in flower early last November, see this photo!) I looked, but I didn't see any galled catkins yet. The catkins hang like decorations on the trees, and whole trees suddenly change color, very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2016)

File.Name: g221a_g221-015.jpg
Notes:
24816884939 I found this handsome Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly in the woods today. At first they look plain brown, but when you look closer you see that they really have a palette of many colors! Up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make the eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it with that stubby little tail. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2016)

Today was a beautiful almost-spring day, and the breeze held off until the afternoon. My presentation on local insects at Hollister Ranch yesterday went well, though my voice started to give out towards the end. I haven't talked that much at one time since I retired from teaching! I hope I'm done with distractions for a while.

File.Name: g221b_g221-012.jpg
Notes: 24888376230 This is a brilliant little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) curled up like a cat on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I guess this one is some kind of the genus Mecaphesa. It's quite small, maybe 3/8 inch across. At first I thought it might be a brown form of Diaea livens, see this photo, but those spiders are translucent green. This one is equally handsome! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2016)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Diaea livens
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Mecaphesa; Diaea livens

File.Name: g221c_g221-035.jpg
Notes: 25090373471 Here are the first new male staminate flowers of (I think) native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon. The female pistillate flowers are lower on the stem, maybe not open yet. This is a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2016)

File.Name: g221d_g221-028.jpg
Notes: 24552684684 Here are two photos of what I believe is the Vulgar Milk Cap (Lactarius argillaceifolius var. megacarpus) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. At first I thought it was a Tricholoma mushroom, but the bleeding white latex gives it away as a Lactarius. It's a very solid mushroom, large and hefty. My book says it's "possibly poisonous" - but you really wouldn't want to eat it anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2016)
CommonNames: Vulgar Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g221e_g221-018.jpg
Notes:
25182903955 Mushrooms are eating our road!

I believe this is an Agaricus bitorquis mushroom coming up through the asphalt of our chipseal road. I have an earlier shot of the same mushroom in this photo. There are more coming up - all through the road. Now why would a mushroom come up through a road? How did a spore originally get down in there?? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2016)

File.Name: g221f_g220-004.jpg
Notes:
24551517324 This is kind of an oddball plant. I believe it is native Brittlebush (Encelia farinosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. That's a desert plant, but here it is growing on an ocean bluff at Bulito Beach on Hollister Ranch ranch near Point Conception! Maybe this one has been introduced along the train tracks, or maybe it's genuinely native? There seems to be a fair amount of it. It sure isn't a typical location for this plant! (Bulito Beach, Hollister Ranch, 20 February 2016)

I got this photo before my presentation on local insects at "the ranch" yesterday. The slide show went well, though my voice started to give out towards the end. I haven't talked that much at one time since I retired from teaching!

File.Name: g222a_g222-032.jpg
Notes:
24837899199 I found this beautiful peach-colored Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) sitting on its web in a just starting to flower shrub of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this is a Six-spotted Orb Weaver (Araniella displicata), though this one seems to have eight spots around its abdomen. The spider is quite small, only about 1/4 inch long. Its web seems well placed! I'll post a better photo of the new flowers tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)

It was warm today, up to almost 80∞ on the upstairs porch. Finally the sun is getting high enough to give us some sunlight, though the garden is still in the shade most of the day. I reckon the vernal equinox is about when we start really getting sunlight - and then we'll wish for shade! Today started windy, then calm, and now the winds are kicking up again.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Holly-Leaved Redberry; Six-spotted Orb Weaver
ScientificName: Araneidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Araniella displicata
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Araniella displicata

File.Name: g222b_g222-004.jpg
Notes: 25205257045 I believe these are female (left) and male (right) Cobweb Spiders (Theridiidae) perched just a few inches apart on a dead twig of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). The colors seem so different because the photo of the higher female has a dark blackberry patch behind it, while the lower male has light-colored sandstone behind. You can see the enlarged male palps on the right, and the larger-bodied female on the left. These photos aren't quite to scale - use the thickness of the stem as a ruler. I first noticed this pair of spiders yesterday, and they're still keeping their distance today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)

File.Name: g222c_g222-020.jpg
Notes: 24909250780 Here are two shots of different large yellow Crab Spiders (Thomisidae) on new flowers of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. I believe that both are the Goldenrod Crab Spider (Misumena vatia) that lacks a white ridge across its face. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)
CommonNames: Crab Spiders; Southern Tauschia; Goldenrod Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae; Misumena vatia
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae; Misumena vatia

File.Name: g222d_g222-016.jpg
Notes: 24909101160 Here is a whole plant of native Southern Tauschia (Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family that's just starting to flower. If you look close in the upper-left quadrant, you can see the two yellow Crab Spider in my [Next] photo. I really think that this plant's outstanding feature is the foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)

File.Name: g222e_g222-025.jpg
Notes: 25178119606 Here is a cascade of new flowers of native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Sure enough, there was a hummingbird guarding the flower patch. These are classic hummingbird flowers, with a long floral tube that most other insects can't get into. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)

File.Name: g222f_g222-038.jpg
Notes: 24908522130 These are the first new flowers I've seen of native Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. The "ssp. mollis" refers to the long white non-glandular hairs on the petioles and stems. This is our other native manzanita, different from the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in many other photos, for example here. A big difference is that these make burls, while the other one doesn't. There are many more differences in the flowers and fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2016)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: g223a_g223-013.jpg
Notes:
25200834956 This is a remarkable little diurnal (daytime) moth resting on a dead twig. I believe it is an Annaphila astrologa moth in the family Noctuidae. I can understand the species name as it seems to have an astrological pattern on it's cryptic upper wings, like an antique astrolabe. See my [Previous] photo for some discussion about its identity. I found a few of these today, in different places. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)

Today was hazy with high clouds and a breeze. I finally had to bump up my ISO a notch. I did my best for new photos, and got a few nice ones!

File.Name: g223b_g223-031.jpg
Notes: 25108190902 Here are two photos of a remarkable little diurnal (daytime) moth working the first new flowers of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. At first I thought it was Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) that has dark upper wings and orange under wings, see this photo. It's about the same size - less than 1/2 inch - but the upper wings are darker with a more complex pattern. After consulting my "Annaphila astrologa moth in the family Noctuidae. It's a striking moth with butterfly-like colors. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)
CommonNames: Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush; Crambid Snout Moth
ScientificName: Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Annaphila astrologa; Noctuidae
associatedTaxa: Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Annaphila astrologa; Noctuidae

File.Name: g223c_g223-006.jpg
Notes: 25226145415 These are lovely new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family - with a pair of Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterflies enjoying nature's bounty. I reckon these are the first "real flowers" of the shrub that I've seen this year - not counting those oddball flowers I found last October. The individual flowers look like corals or sea anemones that belong in the ocean. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)

File.Name: g223d_g223-027.jpg
Notes: 24857874989 This is a "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on new flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it is a Western Boxelder Bug (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - though the closest Boxelder tree is about 20 miles away down the river past Buellton. We have *lots* of different red and black true bugs, and it's hard to keep them straight. You can see the bug's remarkable mouthparts - a thin "stylet" for sucking, supported by a helper "labium" that almost looks like another leg. These are the first flowers of Ceanothus cuneatus that I've seen this year. It looks a lot like Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus), though it has opposite leaves - not really visible in this photo, but take my word for it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)
CommonNames: Buck Brush; Western Boxelder Bug; Bigpod Ceanothus
ScientificName: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus megacarpus
associatedTaxa: Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Rhamnaceae; Boisea rubrolineata; Rhopalidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus megacarpus

File.Name: g223e_g223-012.jpg
Notes: 24598485693 Here are the first new flowers of native Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. These are male staminate catkins. The female pistillate flowers look like tiny acorn cups and come later. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)

File.Name: g223f_g223-029.jpg
Notes: 24929349590 This is the "gall" - a knobby burl anyway - that forms where parasitic Bigleaf Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. macrophyllum, Viscaceae) grows from a branch of native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae). I like this photo, shot high-key - meaning a bit over exposed, but not too much. If you look close you can see that the sycamore is starting to leaf out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 February 2016)
ScientificName: Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron leucarpum; macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: g224a_g224-013.jpg
Notes:
24615396094 This is a lovely Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly nectaring on a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum, Grossulariaceae) by the creek. I've been seeing them flying up and down the canyon for the past couple of weeks, but this is the first one I've found at rest. Just gorgeous! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

We're still having lizard weather, but it was a few degrees cooler today. There's no rain in the forecast. We are well into our fifth year of drought with just over 50% normal rainfall.

File.Name: g224b_g224-005.jpg
Notes: 25127406782 Here are a pair of (I guess) Cobweb Spiders (Theridiidae) having a domestic dispute over an insect in the web - which is probably a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). That's the female on the left and the male on the right, and you can clearly see the difference in the palps, which are actually sexual organs in the males. Curiously, the male and female spiders seem to be sharing the web. Is this typical spider behavior? (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g224c_g224-001.jpg
Notes:
25152197331 Lizard weather!

This is a mature Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) thoroughly enjoying the sun on a stump in a blackberry patch. It's expression is like a definition of contentment, with just a hint on an "Are you looking at me?" attitude. I reckon this one is male, and it already has mating colors. It's been warm this week - too warm, lizard weather for sure. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g224d_g224-017.jpg
Notes: 25152069971 This is another Agaricus bitorquis mushroom pushing up through the asphalt of the chipseal road in our canyon! There are about half a dozen of the mushrooms, all along the road. They are said to be edible and delicious, but there's something unappetizing about eating a mushroom with asphalt! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g224e_g224-015.jpg
Notes: 25218621806 Here are drying up liverworts (Marchantiophyta) and mosses (Bryophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods. I believe the liverworts are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). Here they are with new sporophytes. *Sigh* The moisture is giving out in our woods. We are well into our fifth year of drought. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g224f_g224-026.jpg
Notes: 25151605651 There's nothing quite like oaks in full flower! This is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree with dangling staminate catkins, like ornaments. I still haven't seen any of the catkin galls like this photo shows. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g225a_g225-013.jpg
Notes:
24635620284 Here are tiny new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers are 4-merous, unlike their mostly 5-merous Ceanothus kin in my [Previous] two photos. They are quite pretty the way they cluster around the leaf nodes. If you notice a scrub oak in the woods with surprising red berries instead of acorns, this is the one! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2016)

Today's photos are mostly new shrubs and trees just starting to flower. It's a busy time in the woods, but curiously I found more flowers than insects today. It was another warm day, really too warm for February. *Sigh*

File.Name: g225b_g225-023.jpg
Notes: 25239638316 These are the first new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road. It seems odd to find these flowers before the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in my [Previous] photo, but here they are. The flower clusters are deeper blue and in tighter clusters. This is no doubt the most handsome Ceanothus species in our part of the mountains, though they get deeper colors out towards Vandenberg AFB, in Alice Abelacountry. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2016)

File.Name: g225c_g225-031.jpg
Notes: 24897874799 These are the first new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing on the pass. Actually I've been seeing them for a while lower in the mountains, but I still haven't seen them along East or West Camino Cielo roads. The flower clusters are airy and blue, but sometimes almost white. The leaves are one-veined below. Compare the Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in my [Next] photo. I can distinguish them with a glance. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2016)

File.Name: g225d_g225-019.jpg
Notes: 24634600494 Here are lovely new urn-like flowers on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the woods today. Like the maples in my [Previous] photo, these trees start flowering up high and slowly work their way down, so this is a crop. Flowers are still scarce in these trees, and I suspect that many trees will not flower in this fifth drought year. I hope they manage to hang on, as they are no doubt our finest native tree. I've been seeing the flowers for the last week, but this is the first photo I like. I'll call it a "first flower" for the record. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2016)

File.Name: g225e_g225-007.jpg
Notes: 24897392509 Here are unfolding new flower clusters with old seeds on a deciduous tree of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae plant family. So far the flowers are high up in the trees, so this is a tight crop. The flowers will come down and I'll get better shots, but I wanted to document the occasion. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2016)

File.Name: g225f_g224-007.jpg
Notes: 25238621216 This is another shot of (I guess) a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) with prey - which is probably a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). See my photo yesterday showing both male and female spiders arguing over the prey. The lady won the argument. It's also a nice "double butt shot" - HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2016)

File.Name: g226a_g226-020.jpg
Notes:
25195228791 These are striking new berries of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the mountains. The spiky berries look like the ocean mines in old movies. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. Tasty! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2016)

I was really looking for flies today for the Fly Day Friday group, but I had no luck. Maybe I was distracted by all the new flowering plants I found. I can't say that the mountains are full of life - actually it feels quite dry - but the native plants are doing their thing.

File.Name: g226b_g226-005.jpg
Notes: 25194274881 Here are the first new flowers of native Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in a rocky place on the pass. It's a good-sized shrub, 3-6 feet tall. Hopefully there will be many more flowers once it gets going. So many different lupines, from finger size to shrubs! (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2016)

File.Name: g226c_g226-028.jpg
Notes: 25287057665 Here are new flowers of the Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. Flowers and bracts are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. Note that the flower tubes are quite red on top, whereas the flowers of Castilleja affinis are greenish, compare this photo. These are the first flowers I've noticed this year in our mountains. I found these plants in an unlikely place - a rocky outcrop in full sun. Usually I find them hiding in shady places. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2016)
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis

File.Name: g226d_g226-026.jpg
Notes: 25193796261 These are the first new flowers of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family that I've found this year. The feathery seed plumes that follow the flowers later in summer are probably more impressive, but without the flowers there will be no seeds! See this photo for the seed plumes. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2016)

File.Name: g226e_g226-031.jpg
Notes: 24659559923 This is the first flowering plant of native Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a distinctive native growing on the red conglomerate soil of the Sespe Formation along Painted Cave Road - a non-marine sedimentary deposit with these characteristic "redbeds". The plant has tightly clustered leaves and tight flower heads. These plants are sometimes known as "Biscuit Root" because of their edible roots. I like to nibble on the ripe seeds - they have a spicy taste that stays with you. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2016)

File.Name: g226f_g226-009.jpg
Notes: 25167723722 Here are the first new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can make out all the key characters in this photo. The leaves are opposite and toothed, white-tomentose underneath, with corky stipules, and white flowers. We have several different white-flowered Ceanothus species that cover the mountains, at least in a wetter year - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits. All the white flowers look similar, but the foliage is different. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2016)
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: g227a_g227-003.jpg
Notes:
25207733942 "Two sleepy lizards
with nothing to say,
And too much in love to break away..."
- didn't Hoagy Carmichael and Frank Loesser write that?

These are a pair of mature Blue-belly Lizards aka Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) thoroughly enjoying the morning sun on a stump in a blackberry patch near home. The one on the left has the blue mating colors of a male, which the one on the right is lacking. I like to think they are a couple enjoying a quiet moment in the sun after mating, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)

It's been warm all week - too warm, lizard weather for sure. It turned breezy this afternoon.
ScientificName:
Two sleepy lizards
with nothing to say,
And too much in love to break away..."; Sceloporus occidentalis
associatedTaxa:
Two sleepy lizards
with nothing to say,
And too much in love to break away..."; Sceloporus occidentalis

File.Name: g227b_g227-016.jpg
Notes: 24698479553 I found this handsome mushroom in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) - "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens). It's small, but it seems surprisingly fresh given the warm dry weather we've been having lately. It has a "pizza face" cap and a bulbous base with no visible volva. All parts of the mushroom blush orange- pink with handing. It's said to be edible, but it's definitely not recommended because of the risk of confusing it with a poisonous species. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens

File.Name: g227c_g227-028.jpg
Notes:
25231147271 Here are two shots of a large "Satan's Bolete" mushroom that I believe is really Suillellus amygdalinus. At first I figured it was Rubroboletus eastwoodiae or Rubroboletus pulcherrimus aka Boletus eastwoodiae or Boletus satanas. But it has golden flesh that turns blue almost instantly when exposed, and it has a non-bulbous stalk that is not reticulated. See this photo for a follow-up shot. Thanks for the tip Michelle!

Whatever it is, it's a hefty mushroom that has a orange/red cap and pores, about the size and heft of a baking potato. The mushrooms stain dark blue when you scratch or handle them, and I find it irresistible to leave messages for anyone who might find them. But don't believe everything you read on mushrooms! These mushrooms are possibly poisonous, but maybe not? Don't eat them - but you have to admire them! (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)
ScientificName: Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Rubroboletus pulcherrimus; Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus satanas
associatedTaxa: Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Rubroboletus pulcherrimus; Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus satanas

File.Name: g227d_g227-015.jpg
Notes: 25323308105 This is a sad-looking mushroom in the Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus ) group - maybe Cortinarius trivialis? It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. It should be a very slimy mushroom, but this one is dried out from the warm weather we've been having. The debris stuck to the cap indicates slime, and I can see hints of a slime veil on the stem. Both cap and stem once were slimy, which places them in the subgenus Myxacium. See this photo for what they should look like. This is the only one I've found this year, growing in a place where I've found them in previous years. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Cortinarius trivialis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Myxacium

File.Name: g227e_g227-005.jpg
Notes: 24692244524 This is the exuvia - the shed skin after a molt - of a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the woods. You can see the spider's fangs! Not the best photo, but a pretty cool shot anyway. I'm not sure why I noticed this, but I did. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)

File.Name: g227f_g226-032.jpg
Notes: 25321961495 Here are new galls and old leaves on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe these are the galls of the Fuzzy Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera. They are "detachable galls" that often stay attached to the stems for years. It's curious that these are galls common in some years, but I didn't find any last year. The galls are stem galls. These oaks are "semi-deciduous" - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once in the spring, soon. The wasps are attuned to this schedule, and leaf galls won't start until the new leaves appear. (San Marcos Pass, 26 February 2016)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: g228a_g228-002.jpg
Notes:
25337530386 I went back to the red-pored "Satan's Bolete" mushroom that I posted yesterday and took Michelle's advice in a comment on the photo. Sure enough, the mushroom does have golden flesh that turns blue almost instantly. I tried to get a photo of the yellow flesh, but it turned blue before I could get back to my camera! It's like blood, only the opposite color-shift - I see blue veins, but bleed red. So maybe this one is actually Suillellus amygdalinus and not Rubroboletus eastwoodiae aka Boletus eastwoodiae or B. satanas like I first thought. That would explain a few annoying discrepancies, like why this mushroom has a non-bulbous stalk that is not reticulated. I put my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. I have a photo of the real Satan's Bolete here. Thanks Michelle! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)

*Sigh* It was yet another lovely day, but a bit overcast. This is getting boring!
ScientificName: Suillellus amygdalinus; Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Boletus eastwoodiae; B. satanas
associatedTaxa: Suillellus amygdalinus; Rubroboletus eastwoodiae; Boletus eastwoodiae; B. satanas

File.Name: g228b_g228-016.jpg
Notes: 25336549616 I found this different Russula mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It looks red enough to be Russula mairei in the "Russula emetica" group - maybe now renamed Russula silvicola? All these recent name changes for mushrooms and plants are really confusing! Anyway, here it is - whatever it is! I nibbled a piece, and it tastes "acrid" - hot and spicy, like slightly "off" wasabi - a pleasant nibble, but probably poisonous in quantity. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola
associatedTaxa: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula mairei; Russula emetica; Russula silvicola

File.Name: g228c_g228-014.jpg
Notes: 24735648963 I found these "mystery mushrooms" in the woods today. I don't know what they are, but they look like little Amanitas - without a sign of of a ring or volva. Could they be "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta)? If so, they sure don't look typical, compare this photo from yesterday. Dry weather does sometimes play tricks with mushrooms! These were growing right where a sandstone outcrop meets soil, in a mix of Live oak and Manzanita. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)

File.Name: g228d_g228-004.jpg
Notes: 24994167619 This is a new flowering shrub of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family, the first I've posted this year. I've been seeing them along the highway on the way to town, and I found the first flower in our canyon yesterday, but this is a better photo. If you look close, you can see a little Katydid nymph (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) in the upper-left and a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the upper-right. Insects have been scarce lately, but they're out there! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)
ScientificName: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Thomisidae

File.Name: g228e_g228-012.jpg
Notes: 25361428495 This is a nice natural garden of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. They are not having a great time in this fifth drought year, but they are hanging on. Isn't that what we all do? (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: g228f_g228-006.jpg
Notes: 25267925731 This is another whole plant of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing in the mountains. I reckon it's properly called a subshrub with stems that are woody at the base, but the whole plant is not much more than about a foot tall. It's handsome, and unlike our other Castilleja species has soft white foliage. Curiously, the right half of this plant looks vigorous, the left half not so good. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2016)

File.Name: g229a_g229-002.jpg
Notes:
24766374733 This is a tiny spider stretched out on a stem in the woods after I spooked it from its orb web. I believe it's a male because on its enlarged palps. It's web was small, like the web of a "Trash Line Weaver" (Cyclosa), but the spider lacks the gourd-like "tubercle" on its abdomen, so it must be something else. I guess it's an Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneidae) - or maybe a Long-Jawed Orb Weaver in the family Tetragnathidae? (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2016)

I wasn't even looking for new photos today, as Julie and I are heading off to Death Valley tomorrow and I have things to do! There are reports of a fabulous wildflower year in Death Valley, and I hope we're still in time for the "super bloom". To get us ready for the trip, it was warmer than it's been for a while at home today, into the mid-80s on the upstairs porch. We'll be back next weekend.
CommonNames: Trash Line Weaver; Orb-Weaver Spider; Long-Jawed Orb Weaver

File.Name: g229b_g229-001.jpg
Notes: 24762192344 Here are the first new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've found this year. The glandular flower heads might well be more impressive than the actual flowers! They are low-growing hummingbird flowers, see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2016)

File.Name: g229c_g229-010.jpg
Notes: 25299296061 Here are new flowers of (I believe) native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in our canyon. The shaggy male staminate flowers are most visible on the right, and the more delicate female pistillate flowers are on the left . This is a remarkably tough grass-like plant that grows in the shaded woods, but also in glaring sun. It's not at all like a typical wet-land sedge, except for the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2016)

File.Name: g229d_g229-014.jpg
Notes: 25273663782 This is a fine specimen of an Agaricus bitorquis mushroom, posed on a bridge post. This one was growing in hard-packed sandy soil along our road - not IN the road like this one! I collected this one for our spaghetti tonight, but it turned out to be wormy. Too bad, because they are said to be fine eating. These are large hefty mushrooms. We have a dozen or more of these along (and in) our road. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2016)

File.Name: g229e_g229-004.jpg
Notes: 25365287556 Here are two shots of a pallid Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. It has a bulbous base, which places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. This might be Cortinarius regalis, but all these Cortinarius mushrooms look the same as they age. The reddish-brown gills are stained with fallen spores, and are distinctive. Compare this photo. It's not beautiful, but I did find it today in our drought-dry woods. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2016)

File.Name: g229f_g227-008.jpg
Notes: 25272872902 These are little liverworts (Marchantiophyta) fading to black as they dry out on a sandstone outcrop in the woods. I believe the liverworts are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). Well, it's not really black - you can see some blue color as a sign of life. These liverworts might even revive in the next rain - next week or next year! *Sigh* The moisture is giving out in our woods. We are well into our fifth year of drought. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2016)

File.Name: g306a_g306-001.jpg
Notes:
24949189763 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

We got home from Death Valley in light rain last night to the sounds of happy Chorus Frogs! I'm happy that the frogs are still out there after so many drought years. We ended up with 4.07 inches of rain overnight! I found this hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) after the rain this morning - from the edge of our front porch, but not the foundation of our house. They might be the Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae). Many of them have their wings stuck in the rain-wet oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2016)

D'oh! I have to report for Jury Duty in Santa Barbara early tomorrow morning. Well it's a duty, I can't think of a better system, and I have no good excuse - except for missing a few days of new photos! I have lots of Death Valley photos to post so it's no problem.

File.Name: g306b_g304-045.jpg
Notes:
25482469931 Here are masses of Desert Gold (Geraea canescens, Asteraceae) wildflowers growing up into the hills along the Beatty Cutoff road in Death Valley a couple of days ago. The wildflowers started along Badwater Road, but they seem to be shifting north and higher. The flower fields look impressive from a distance, but seem sparser up close. The challenge is to find all the other wildflowers hiding under these dominates. I'll get to those tomorrow! (Beatty Cutoff, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)

We couldn't get a room in Furnace Creek, so we ended staying in Beatty, Nevada - just outside the park. We stayed at the Atomic Inn, which was clean but basic. The local food was better than expected. "Mel's Diner" for breakfast and "Mama Sara's" for dinner were both satisfactory - as Nero Wolf would say, and that means really good!

File.Name: g306c_g304-012.jpg
Notes: 24948248243 Here are Desert Gold (Geraea canescens, Asteraceae) wildflowers in Death Valley a couple of days ago. I got this photo near the 25 mile marker along the Badwater Road. The real challenge is to find all the other wildflowers hiding under these dominates. I'll get to those tomorrow! (Badwater Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)

File.Name: g306d_g304-005.jpg
Notes: 25278998610 This is a sea of Desert Gold (Geraea canescens, Asteraceae) wildflowers washing up against the mountains in Death Valley a couple of days ago. What else can I say? I got this photo near the 25 mile marker along the Badwater Road. This seemed to be the main wildflower hotspot in the valley, though the higher section along the Beatty Road was also starting to pop. Many areas are still quite bare, unless you look very close. (Badwater Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)

File.Name: g306e_g303-016.jpg
Notes: 24947366453 This is looking out over the floor of Death Valley from a place along the road to Beatty, Nevada. The yellow wildflowers in the foreground are probably the yellow Golden Evening-Primrose (Camissonia brevipes, Onagraceae). There's also a belt of Desert Gold (Geraea canescens, Asteraceae) visible in the distance at a lower elevation. The difference in wildflowers from middle to low elevations is striking, but there's always something yellow. (Daylight Pass Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 3 March 2016)
ScientificName: Camissonia brevipes; Onagraceae; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Camissonia brevipes; Onagraceae; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae

File.Name: g306f_g303-017.jpg
Notes:
25480610541 Here are new wildflowers growing along the road to Beatty, Nevada in Death Valley. They are probably the yellow Golden Evening-Primrose (Camissonia brevipes), the purple Notched-leaf Phacelia (Phacelia crenulata), with a few white Gravel Ghost (Atrichoseris platyphylla) and Cryptantha flowers. Wildflowers often line the roads, with fewer beyond. (Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 3 March 2016)

I find this very interesting - how wildflowers, and not just weeds, collect along roadsides. This was my stumper for the currant newsletter of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society which I put together recently:

"Soon it will be time to get outside and enjoy the wildflowers that will appear if the El NiÒo rains appear as expected. The best wildflower fields are in the backcountry on Figueroa Mountain and beyond, far from human disturbance. But it's convenient that many wildflowers are abundant right along the roads. Introduced weeds are also common, but there are many natives like poppies and lupines that thrive along our highways despite traffic and mowing and scraping. Wildlife follows. What environmental factors make the roadsides so attractive for native wildflowers and wildlife?"

Fortunately I don't have to answer this one for a few months until the next issue comes out!
CommonNames: Golden Evening-Primrose; Notched-leaf Phacelia; Gravel Ghost
ScientificName: Camissonia brevipes; Phacelia crenulata; Atrichoseris platyphylla; Cryptantha
associatedTaxa: Camissonia brevipes; Phacelia crenulata; Atrichoseris platyphylla; Cryptantha

File.Name: g307a_g307-004.jpg
Notes:
25574964206 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

There's a moment of golden light when the sun first tries to break through on a rainy day. I got this photo of new leaves and catkins on a tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) at just such a moment. A few minutes later, the clouds closed back in and it was raining again. I'm especially glad to see this tree with new leaves because it's the tree closest to our upstairs porch that was half defoliated by caterpillar-like larvae last year, see this photo and this one. I believe the grubs are larvae of Sawfly Wasps ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). The tree never did recover from the attack, and was looking pretty sad a few weeks ago with lots of bare stems. I'm relieved to see all the new growth. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2016)

We had more rain last night and today! We're now up to 1.4 inches of rain today, bringing our two day total to almost 5.5 inches and giving us 17.9 inches for the year. That's still only 66% of our normal rainfall for the year, but it's looking better. And there's a chance of more rain on Friday.

We actually had a bit of slushy snow early this morning as I was getting ready to drive down to Santa Barbara for jury duty. I grumbled but did my duty. After we watched their video, the clerk came out and told us that our case had just been settled, and we were free to go - with full credit for doing our duty. Woohoo! This is the second time this has happened to me, I guess the imminent threat of a real trial is good motivation to cop a plea. I've served on three juries so far. I'm not afraid of the service, but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: g307b_g304-043.jpg
Notes: 25481759672 This pollen-dusted Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae, Lepidoptera) caterpillar has eaten its way to the end of its branch. What next? I'm pretty sure that the stem is a Brown-Eyed Evening-primrose (Camissonia claviformis aka Chylismia claviformis, Onagraceae). I'm not sure, but the caterpillar sure looks like a White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata) that we also have at home. Compare this photo, which is on the stem of a California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) - another plant in the same Onagraceae plant family. (Beatty Cutoff, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)
CommonNames: Sphinx Moth; Brown-Eyed Evening-primrose; White-Lined Sphinx Moth; California Suncup
ScientificName: Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Camissonia claviformis; Chylismia claviformis; Onagraceae; Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Sphingidae; Lepidoptera; Camissonia claviformis; Chylismia claviformis; Onagraceae; Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae

File.Name: g307c_g304-025.jpg
Notes: 25507493871 These are flowers of the Death Valley Blazing Star (Mentzelia reflexa) in the Loasaceae plant family. Look close - the flowers are full of tiny insects, mostly wasps and aphids. Despite the countless wildflowers in Death Valley, insects were rather scarce on the flowers. (Badwater Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)

File.Name: g307d_g303-042.jpg
Notes: 25599875735 This is a beautiful flower of native Desert Five-Spot (Eremalche rotundifolia) in the Malvaceae plant family - with lots of tiny red Mites (Acarina, Arachnida). Despite the countless wildflowers in Death Valley, insects and other arthropods were rather scarce on the flowers. (Badwater Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 3 March 2016)

File.Name: g307e_g303-023.jpg
Notes:
25480681562 A tale of two phacelias...

Here are photos of two different Phacelia plants in the in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. The flowers look quite similar, but look at the leaves. The plant on the left is the Notched-leaf Phacelia (Phacelia crenulata) with pinnatifid leaves. The plant on the right is the Caltha-leaved Phacelia (Phacelia calthifolia) with roundish leaves. P. crenulata seems to grow at higher elevations than P. calthifolia - with Golden Evening-Primrose (Camissonia brevipes) instead of Desert Gold (Geraea canescens, Asteraceae). Both are quite pretty. (Daylight Pass Road and Beatty Cutoff, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 3 March 2016)
Locality: Daylight Pass Road and Beatty Cutoff
CommonNames: Notched-leaf Phacelia; Caltha-leaved Phacelia; Golden Evening-Primrose; Desert Gold
ScientificName: Phacelia; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Phacelia crenulata; Phacelia calthifolia; P. crenulata; P. calthifolia; Camissonia brevipes; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phacelia; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Phacelia crenulata; Phacelia calthifolia; P. crenulata; P. calthifolia; Camissonia brevipes; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae

File.Name: g307f_g304-032.jpg
Notes: 24968417604 Here are two shots of Gravel Ghost (Atrichoseris platyphylla) in the Asteraceae plant family, showing flowers and buds on the left and the whole plant on the right. The odd leaves lie flat on the ground, with a single stem rising from the middle. The flowers look a lot like genus Malacothrix. I believe the difference is the lack of a pappus. This specimen was growing on an alluvial fan south of Badwater, but I also found it growing along the Beatty Road. (Badwater Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 4 March 2016)

File.Name: g308a_g308-001.jpg
Notes:
25262988719 This is a beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for a moment on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the creek. I found it close to its Dudleya host plants. I've been seeing them for a couple of weeks, but this is the first one I've managed to get a photo of. They are quite camera shy! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)

It was windy this morning and afternoon, but there was a slot in-between when the wind died down allowing me to get a few photos. I have errands in Solvang and Goleta on opposite sides of the mountains tomorrow, so photo-ops will be limited. I'll get back to my Death Valley photos tomorrow.
CommonNames: Sonoran Blue; Coast Live Oak; Dudleya

File.Name: g308b_g308-007.jpg
Notes: 25537537681 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek this morning. It's the first one I've managed to capture a photo of this year. These butterflies have a brown trailing edge on their hindwings, unlike our other duskywings with white trim. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)

File.Name: g308c_g308-029.jpg
Notes: 25003009753 This is a tiny Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) insect on the edge of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The hopper is about 1250 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 4.9 mm, about 3/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm always surprised by how pretty these little insects are up close. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g308d_g308-017.jpg
Notes: 25536702821 This is looking close at the lovely urn-like flowers of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree in the woods today. The flowers are a bit puffier than the similar manzanita flowers. They are like those those candy Easter eggs that you can look inside of. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)

File.Name: g308e_g308-014.jpg
Notes: 25261295619 This Cortinarius mushroom has both a slimy cap and stem, so I reckon it's subgenus Myxacium. It's actually difficult to pick up because its so slimy! This is probably in the Cortinarius cylindripes group. The stem looked Blewitt-blue when I found it, though the color washed out in my photo. It also has blue/violet colors on its slimy cap. Sure, this one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Myxacium; Cortinarius cylindripes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Myxacium; Cortinarius cylindripes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: g308f_g308-023.jpg
Notes: 25628391885 I believe this is "The Blusher" (Amanita novinupta aka Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods today. These mushrooms sure are different from 'shroom to 'shroom - compare this photo! This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) with California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) plants. The stem did "blush" pink with handling in the photo on the left, but when I first found it (on the right) I thought it was an Agaricus. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2016)
CommonNames: The Blusher; Coast Live Oak; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Amanita novinupta; Amanita rubescens; Quercus agrifolia; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Agaricus

File.Name: g309a_g309-016.jpg
Notes:
25634074446 This is looking into a new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, with a troop of fat stamens and three reddish pistils looking out from the large carpels. Some flowers only have two carpels. This is the first open flower that I've seen this year, though I reckon they've been flowering for a while. Usually the flowers point down towards the earth, but I propped this one up with a stick. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, probably long-lasting fire-followers in this place that burned about 15 years ago. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2016)

I had errands on both sides of the mountains today. I stopped near Lake Cachuma on my way to Solvang and found lots of new flowers. I guess Death Valley photos will have to wait another day. They'll keep. Today's photos from home are always the best!

File.Name: g309b_g309-013.jpg
Notes: 25633799996 Here are pretty blue flowers of native Tuberous Skullcap aka Danny's Skullcap (Scutellaria tuberosa) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I wonder who Danny is? I found this little mint in clear spot in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma today. It's said to be a fire-follower, and this place did burn about 15 years ago. They are small plants, just 2 or 3 inches high. They look like an escaped garden ground cover plant, but they are native. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2016)

File.Name: g309c_g309-007.jpg
Notes: 25029098484 This is the first new flower of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family that I've seen this year. It's an elegant onion-like plant with these pretty flowers that many kinds of butterflies love. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2016)

File.Name: g309d_g309-003.jpg
Notes:
25028801574 This are new flowers of what I believe is native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family. The stems and leaves are almost glabrous, with just a bit of "peach fuzz" on them. See this photo for a comparison with the species. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 17 February 2015)

This variety is not recognized in the new Jepson manual except as a synonym for the species, but maybe it should be? It seems distinct and localized. It is recognized in my old Munz manual.

File.Name: g309e_g309-010.jpg
Notes: 5632916856 Here are new flowers of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family. They are pretty little flowers that look like garden violets, but they are native. Did you notice that fat little Springtail (order Collembola) insect in the throat of the flower on the left? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2016)

File.Name: g309f_g309-026.jpg
Notes: 25566007281 Here are beautiful new leaves and flowers of native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. It's a striking shrub or small tree with odd-pinnate leaves. I expect it to find it near the river, but there's also an odd shrub up here in the mountains. Last year, I found the first flowers on March 7, so this one is right on schedule. (Santa Ynez Valley, 9 March 2016)

File.Name: g310a_g310-003.jpg
Notes:
25569715412 Here is a nice shot of a mature Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on our retaining wall this morning. It has mating colors. It also has a pair of Ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) between it's left shoulder and eye in what I believe is the "sweet spot" (for ticks) is the lizard's ear tympanum. Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that usually get me in the summer, though I've already been "ticked" a few times this year. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)

It started as a beautiful day today, but it got cloudy and the wind kicked up, maybe a harbinger of our next storm expected tomorrow. I went to Paradise by the river, but the first river crossing is still closed by a gate. There didn't seem to be much water in the river, but I guess the Forest Service wants to avoid hassles (and lawsuits). It was warm today, up to 75∞ on our upstairs porch. That's 35∞ warmer than it was a few days ago when we had some slushy snow!
Locality: and lawsuits).  It was warm today
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Ticks

File.Name: g310b_g310-002.jpg
Notes:
25057446504 Welcome to a tiny Katydid nymph's world!

This is the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a spine of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. The nymph only about 3/16 inch long. I'm pretty sure it's a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) as the Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs are more ornate. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of the nymph. I like this photo. I guess this also counts as a "butt shot" - so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)
CommonNames: Katydid; Prickly Pear; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia

File.Name: g310c_g310-005.jpg
Notes: 25060903133 Here is a "butt shot" of a tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) on a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. The nymph only about 3/16 inch long. I'm pretty sure it's a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) as the Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs are more ornate. Those tiny "glochid" spines at the base of the larger spines are really nasty if you brush against them! See my [Next] photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)
CommonNames: Katydid; Prickly Pear; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydid
