File.Name: g310d_g310-007.jpg
Notes: 25594541561 I'm sure this pretty green Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is Diaea livens. It's perched for just a moment on a new leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin, Caprifoliaceae), but it scooted off up it's line before I could get a better shot. I'm happy with this one photo, at least the eyes are in sharp focus! They are one of our most beautiful spiders, and early spring seem to be their time. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Diaea livens; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: g310e_g310-034.jpg
Notes: 25567919952 This is a fat yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a new flower cluster of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae). It has an hairless abdomen and a band across its eyes, so maybe it's a White-banded Crab Spider (Misumenoides formosipes)? Is so, it's small for the species, but it is still early in the year. There's plenty of time for it to bulk up with prey! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Holly-Leaved Redberry; White-banded Crab Spider
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Misumenoides formosipes
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae; Misumenoides formosipes

File.Name: g310f_g310-022.jpg
Notes:
25059411633 These are the odd "flowers" of native California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the Plantaginaceae plant family growing in a meadow near the first crossing of Paradise. I don't reckon they are anyone's favorite wildflower, but they are native and deserve some respect - at least a photo! Didn't I see the same flowers in Death Valley last week? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2016)

I still don't understand why the beautiful Penstemon plants are now considered to be in the same Plantaginaceae plant family.

File.Name: g311a_g311-006.jpg
Notes:
25085250024 Here is a large healthy vine of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula, Caprifoliaceae) climbing over a just-starting-to-flower Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae), with a flowering Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the right. This is the view from the cover of our upstairs deck this morning, in the rain. This honeysuckle hasn't looked so vigorous for a few years, and I expect lots of flowers when their time comes later this spring. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2016)

We had another 1-1/4 inch of rain today! There's been no "March Miracle" rain this year (yet!) like there was in 1991 when 21.56 inches of rain fell on the pass in the month, but the outlook does look better. These late-season March rains are good for all the wildflowers and especially good for the summer flowers.
Locality: yet!) like there was in 1991 when 21.56 inches of rain fell on the pass in the month
CommonNames: California Honeysuckle; Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: g311b_g311-019.jpg
Notes: 25084640254 Here are roadside natives and weeds along San Marcos Road (the "old pass road") after the rain stopped this afternoon. The flower in the center is native Succulent Lupine (Lupinus succulentus) in the Fabaceae plant family, surrounded by native Popcorn Flower (probably Plagiobothrys nothofulvus, Boraginaceae) and California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus, Onagraceae). There is also weedy Filaree (Erodium, Geraniaceae) and other common weeds. This road was scraped last summer as a preparation for the mighty El NiÒo rains that never appeared - though we're looking better this month. That may explain the suncup, which I was surprised to find here. Most of these plants are fire-followers, though a freshly scarped road works just fine to get them going. (Santa Barbara foothills, Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 March 2016)
CommonNames: Succulent Lupine; Popcorn Flower; California Suncup; Filaree
ScientificName: Lupinus succulentus; Fabaceae; Plagiobothrys nothofulvus; Boraginaceae; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae; Erodium; Geraniaceae

File.Name: g311c_g311-015.jpg
Notes: 25414100980 Here is a line of Agaricus bitorquis mushrooms growing along the edge of - and through! - the asphalt of the chipseal road in our canyon. For some reason, we have more of this mushrooms this year than ever before. Several of these mushrooms are turned over. I'm not sure if some critter is nibbling on them or if it's just natural exuberance. They are said to be edible and delicious, but there's something unappetizing about eating a mushroom with asphalt! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2016)

File.Name: g311d_g310-010.jpg
Notes: 25087839503 This is a new gall on a new spring leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I'm not sure whose gall it is, but I've been calling them "Painted Toenail Galls". Ron Russo doesn't describe them in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) book. This is the first time I've seen one so young. They look different as they age, but don't we all! Compare this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2016)

File.Name: g311e_g310-018.jpg
Notes: 25083622084 This is a well-named native Padre's Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii aka Primula clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family. (See the comment about the recent name change in this photo by Jim Morefield.) There are small masses of these flowers up the hill, but I like this close shot best. I especially like that beautiful deep-purple crinkly tissue at the base of the anthers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2016)

File.Name: g311f_g306-006.jpg
Notes: 25592665802 This is "just a fly" (Diptera) perched on a smooth new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. It might be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) with that bristly behind, but I see no signs of a subscutellum so maybe not. Just a fly - but a handsome one! HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2016)

File.Name: g312a_g312-028.jpg
Notes:
25622422082 Can it be?? I think this might actually be a tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) laying its eggs on the leaf bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon. Actually, the photos show it on two different leaf buds on the same branch, which accounts for the difference in lighting. The wasp is about 790 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 3.1 mm, less than 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) It has the right hump-backed appearance to be a Cynipid, and it seems to have a separate ovipositer below its "bung hole" - see the photo in the middle. I'm not sure about this, but if I'm right I've waited years to get this photo sequence! I've been collecting photos of insect galls for many years - see my Gall Album. I suppose this could also be a parasitic wasp laying its egg on a gall wasp larvae that's already in there, but I prefer to think I'm right! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

It was breezy this morning, and it became quite windy as the day went on, with gusts to 30 mph. I guess that's to be expected after yesterday's rain. The wind made photos difficult, so I bumped up the ISO a notch for most of my photos today. Clouds are moving over tonight, maybe the first sign of our next (slight) chance of rain tomorrow night. I can't complain about the recent rainfall - this has been a good month so far!
CommonNames: Cynipid Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: g312b_g312-012.jpg
Notes: 25743231585 Here are a pair of black and white tiny Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) in the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the canyon. Neither photo was quite good enough to stand alone, but they make a nice pair. There seem to many different kinds of these spiders in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

File.Name: g312c_g312-017.jpg
Notes: 25442036240 This is another tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. This one is a bit larger than the last one I found, maybe 1/4 inch long. I'm pretty sure this is a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) as the Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymphs are more ornate. New insects are out there, but small and easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)
CommonNames: Katydid; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Microcentrum californicum; Scudderia

File.Name: g312d_g312-001.jpg
Notes:
25115599573 I believe this is a furry little Bee Fly (genus Bombylius, Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) on the wild edge of our garden this morning. Compare the bee fly in my [Previous] photo. That one looks more like a military jet and this one looks more like a cargo helicopter. It flies like one too! Yeah, it's a good "butt shot" - but I got more of those today to save for next Beautiful Bug Butt Thursday group. HBBB anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

Wouldn't you know it - I've been looking for interesting flies all week for the Fly Day Friday group, but I had little luck. And today on Saturday, I find *lots* of interesting flies! Of well, I saved a few for Friday.
ScientificName: Bombylius; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombylius; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g312e_g312-007.jpg
Notes: 25646915351 This is a different little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) than the fly in my [Next] photo, with a more slender body, but similar antennae and proboscis. It is perched near the tip of a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the woods. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

File.Name: g312f_g312-008.jpg
Notes: 25741666275 This is a bristly little fly (Diptera) perched on a dead stem in the woods this morning. I believe this one might really be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) because it has a "subscutellum" - that extra bump below the rear part of its thorax, behind the wings. See this page at BugGuide. (And whatever happened to Flicker notes??) Maybe this is the same kind of fly I showed yesterday? It's not the best photo, but it does show a diagnostic feature. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

File.Name: g313a_g313-001.jpg
Notes:
25653210972 I found this tiny - but perfectly formed - fly (Diptera) this morning on a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I don't have to guess, but if I had to I would guess it's a Drosophilid Fly (Drosophilidae). It really is tiny, about 540 pixels across in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to about 2.1 mm, just 5/64 inch long - a notch over 1/16 inch. (See here for how I figured.) (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)

I was really a lousy day for new photos - mostly cloudy, chilly, and breezy - but I got a few. It's been feeling like rain all day, and we did get ghost-like traces of drizzle today - like virga almost missing us. Now in the evening, there really is some light rain, but I'm not expecting much from this storm.

File.Name: g313b_g313-004.jpg
Notes: 25747928636 This is a large Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera trying to hide among the new leaves of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) in the canyon today. I've seen a few this year, but this is the first one I've managed to get a photo of. It's about the size of a blackberry leaf, an inch or two long. There are many different kinds, so I won't try to guess. I dig their faces! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)

File.Name: g313c_g313-009.jpg
Notes: 25142726554 I found this pair of very different tiny Weevil beetles (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on the wild side of our garden this morning, just a few feet apart. The one on the left looks typical, but the one on the right looks different. I believe the one on the right is a Broad-nosed Weevil (subfamily Entiminae) on a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I'm not sure where to start looking for the one on the left, but I found it on a new leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). Both are tiny, about 1/8 inch long. Neither are great photos, but together like this they say something about the diversity of the weevils. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)
CommonNames: Weevil; Broad-nosed Weevil; Wild Blackberry; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Entiminae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Entiminae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: g313d_g313-027.jpg
Notes: 25146395213 Here are new flower clusters of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia, Rhamnaceae) in a rare moment of sunlight today. If you look close, you can see a few galled flowers in the clusters - those reddish flowers that stay closed. I still don't know whose galls they are. The galled flowers stay on the plant long after the real flowers are done. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)

File.Name: g313e_g313-012.jpg
Notes: 25772427635 Here are two native flowers in the Saxifragaceae plant family that I usually find growing together in shady places where the sun never shines. On the left is California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica), see my [Previous] photo for the foliage. The different stages of this flower are striking, I'll have to get back to this place when it's not so breezy. The flowers on the right are Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria), which have a pair of small opposite leaves at the base of the flower stem. Neither plant is common in our mountains, but both are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)

File.Name: g313f_g313-017.jpg
Notes: 25141660864 Here is a neat colony of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They are growing in their usual habitat - a shady sandstone bank where the sun never shines, with mosses and liverworts. (There are also a few non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media, Caryophyllaceae) plants, pesky weeds.) These are just starting to flower. See my [Next] photo for the pretty flowers, but the foliage and habitat is equally handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2016)
ScientificName: Saxifraga californica; Saxifragaceae; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Saxifraga californica; Saxifragaceae; Stellaria media; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: g314a_g314-054.jpg
Notes:
25680044722 Here are lots of fantastic tiny wasp galls on staminate catkins of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are the spring galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, which will make different summer galls after they mature. The galls are only a few mm long. Catkins seem such an ephemeral place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. The tiny gall insects change the behavior of the abscission as well as the stamens. It's interesting that these are the first catkin galls I've seen this year, though the trees have been flowering for some time. I guess the gall wasps were late this year and almost missed the show. They are now coming out in numbers on this late-flowering tree! I'll try to get a better photo tomorrow, without the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2016)

It was gloomy and windy at home this morning, then sunny and windy when I went on an errand to the Santa Ynez Valley this afternoon. We did get another 1/10 inch of rain last night. Not much from this fading storm, but every bit helps in our fifth drought year. That brings our our total to 19.28 inches, still just 69% of normal. That's better than we've got in the past four years, so maybe El NiÒo isn't a total bust for us? I got a few photos despite the wind.

Happy Pi Day - 3.14.16. You won't see that again!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: g314b_g314-046.jpg
Notes:
25679198522 This is the real "California Poppy" in our neck of the woods - Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see that this one lacks the "receptacle rim" or collar below the flowers that the real California Poppy (E. californica) has. See my [Previous] photo and this one for a comparison shot. These flowers are usually more yellow than the others. This one often grows as a fire-follower or on disturbed ground. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2016)

BTW, don't trust the usual histogram display on your camera when photographing such bright yellow, orange, or red flowers! Look at the separate RGB histograms, and get the red color within bounds. Your photo will look dull on the camera display, but it will come out right. Believe me, this is lesson hard-learned!

File.Name: g314c_g314-039.jpg
Notes: 25773809726 Here are real California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica, Papaveraceae) flowers showing the large "receptacle rim" that these flowers have - that disk below the flowers, buds, and seed pods. Compare my [Next] photo of the Tufted Poppy (E. caespitosa). I believe these flowers are all introduced from seed packets. They are the state flower of California - pretty but kind of weedy. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2016)

File.Name: g314d_g314-042.jpg
Notes:
25168830014 Roadside wildflowers or roadside weeds?

Here are lots of new flowers of California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica, Papaveraceae) growing along busy Highway 154 near San Lucas Ranch. The flowers are pretty and native to California, but I'm pretty sure that they are all planted here. Compare my [Next] two photos. This plant is considered a weed around the world, e.g. in Chile and Australia. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2016)

File.Name: g314e_g303-022.jpg
Notes: 25704007461 I believe this is the Little Gold Poppy (Eschscholzia minutiflora, Papaveraceae) growing on an alluvial fan in Death Valley two weeks ago. The Mojave Gold Poppy (E. glyptosperma) looks similar, but this one has leafy stems that the other one lacks. It's a pretty little plant, like a miniature California Poppy. Compare my next few photos. (Daylight Pass Road, Death Valley National Park, Inyo County, California, 3 March 2016)
CommonNames: Little Gold Poppy; Mojave Gold Poppy

File.Name: g314f_g314-036.jpg
Notes: 25772715306 This is the beautiful coiled "fiddlehead" of a new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family in the woods today. It was a crummy day for new photos, gloomy and windy, but this one came out pretty nice - and surprisingly bright. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2016)

File.Name: g315a_g315-041.jpg
Notes:
25704373742 This is the first Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly I've found this year - surely an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's perched for a moment on a new leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

I'm following my usual rule of "butterflies first" - though I think that interesting spiders really ruled the day, see my [Next] few photos. It was sunny and warmer today, 70∞ on the upstairs porch, warm enough to bring out a sweat in the sun! There was an annoying breeze all day, just enough to keep everything wiggling. The rest of the week is forecast to be dry, and I hope that's not the end of the rain this year. One of these breaks in the weather will be the end.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: g315b_g315-044.jpg
Notes:
25194439004 "Reach out and touch someone..."

Here are a pair of (I guess) Cobweb Spiders (Theridiidae). I reckon that's the skinnier male on the left and the larger female on the right. I also reckon they're thinking about mating, but it's taking them a while! I first found them in the morning sharing a web, then checked back in the afternoon and they had gotten this far. Well, they're holding hands at least! Spider sex must be difficult between these consummate predators that usually attack anything that moves. I found these on their web in a patch of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

It was a good day for interesting spiders!

File.Name: g315c_g315-047.jpg
Notes: 25197952393 This must be a tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) with that odd bump on its abdomen. I guess it's spinning a new orb web because it was walking back and forth along its webbing, like a tightrope walker. This one seems darker and has a more sharply defined abdominal bump than the usual Cyclosa spiders I see at home, compare this photo. This one was rigging its web between plants and dead stems of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

File.Name: g315d_g315-014.jpg
Notes: 25703168912 This is a tiny black and white Jumping Spider (Salticidae) showing it's chelicerae and fangs! It's a tiny spider, perched on a dead leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. Not the best photo - too contrasty and a bit over-exposed, not to mention the focus - but I like the fangs! If these spiders were larger, they would be truly frightening. This might be the same kind of spider I show on the left in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

File.Name: g315e_g315-031.jpg
Notes: 25728741541 This is a neat little "forest" of tiny sporophytes of (I guess) the California Liverwort (Asterella californica, Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. It's interesting that the liverwort plants in the lower-left corner are producing the asexual buds known as gemmae instead of sporophytes. These plants can do either, and it always helps to have a backup strategy! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

File.Name: g315f_g315-024.jpg
Notes: 25196851773 Rodents have been nibbling this pair of Bolete mushrooms that I found in the woods today. Maybe they are Boletus dryophilus because I also see signs of the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus on the right that usually devours these mushrooms. I was hoping for more mushrooms today, but I didn't find them. Maybe their time is done - despite the rain? (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

File.Name: g316a_g316-035.jpg
Notes:
25849839095 Here is the first new flower of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family fully open along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains today! This deciduous shrub has everything - gorgeous white flowers, edible nuts (here), and pretty autumn leaves (here)! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. They are pretty common on San Marcos pass - both in woodland and chaparral. They are stunning flowers, worth hunting for. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south.

It was warm today, up to 80∞ on the upstairs porch when I got home in the afternoon. I'm afraid that the woods will dry out fast if this weather continues.

File.Name: g316b_g316-023.jpg
Notes:
25728569512 This is native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. It's beautiful and the flower buds look fully formed, but they are not quite open yet. I went back up West Camino Cielo road today looking for two new flowers that I thought might be open with the warm weather we've been having. This one, no. The one in my [Next] photo, yes! I'll give this one a few more days. It will be worth the wait! (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

The other seedling plants in the photo are mostly Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus, Asteraceae) with a few obscure plants of Fairy Mist (Pterostegia drymarioides, Polygonaceae) visible near the top.

File.Name: g316c_g316-024.jpg
Notes: 25548458000 This is the work of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. They build a distinctive orb web with a separate little house suspended by a tangle of cobwebs off to the side. They decorate their houses with the bodies of their prey, pirate fashion! The photo on the left is looking through the orb web at the house. On the right, the spider actually came out of its house for a moment to wrap a little prey. That moment was fast and furious, and then the spider retreated into its shelter. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

File.Name: g316d_g316-012.jpg
Notes: 25727874782 Here are two photos of different caterpillar-like larvae that I found in the woods today. I can't identify either of them. The one on the left is certainly a true caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae). But the one on the right might be the larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) because it sure seems to have single eyepots. (Thanks Alice!) That one is on a catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Doesn't the catkin look like bunches of bananas up close! Insects are out there, but many are still in the forming stage. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

File.Name: g316e_g316-048.jpg
Notes: 25221885263 These are the first open flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The flower buds are really the stand-out feature of this plant, along with the taco-shell-like folded leaves. The pretty buds form in September and then wait half a year until now to open, see this photo. This plant's cousin Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) grows lower in the mountains and has similar flowers, see this photo. It has smaller flat leaves without the fold. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

File.Name: g316f_g316-050.jpg
Notes: 25217680724 This is a new plant of beautiful native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) growing in Kinevan Canyon today, catching a beam of sunlight in the shady woods along the creek. This one looks good, but the flower buds are often eaten by deer or grubs that I can't identify. They won't flower for a few months, but see this photo from last June. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2016)

File.Name: g317a_g317-019.jpg
Notes:
25874007115 This is another little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly - surely an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) - on a flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) today. I was pre-focused on the flower waiting for tiny bees, when this butterfly flew into my photo! All it took was a slight focus readjustment. Luck favors the ready! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2016)

Today was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, only about 75∞ on the upstairs porch instead of 80∞. It was also quite breezy - no real wind, but a steady 10 mph breeze that kept everything in motion. I kept my ISO bumped up to 400. We're suddenly getting some sun in the garden! The sun has finally climbed high enough in the sky to clear the tree tops to the south. That and the breeze kept me in the garden at home today. Why go anywhere when it's so nice right here?
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g317b_g317-003.jpg
Notes:
25847246646 This is NOT a caterpillar Lepidoptera, though it is is caterpillar-like!

I'm sure this is the larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). It may be genus Periclista - see this photo by Alice Abelia. There are a couple of sure signs that it's not a proper caterpillar. First, it has too many "prolegs", those little stumps in the back that are separate from its three pairs of true legs in front on the left. I count eight pairs, but caterpillars have no more than 5 pairs. Second, it has single eyes instead of the usual circle of eyespots that caterpillars have. Finally, those "forked hairs" seem pretty distinctive. I don't know of any caterpillar that has hairs like that. See this page. I found this one munching a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2016)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Periclista; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; "Symphyta"; Hymenoptera; Periclista; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: g317c_g317-017.jpg
Notes: 25777833021 This is a Looper or Inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae, Lepidoptera) on a new stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus, Fabaceae). It's trying to disguise itself as a dead twig - which would be great camouflage if it wasn't on a green herbaceous stem! This one seems to have just two pair "prolegs" on the left, those little stumps in the back that are separate from it's three pairs of true legs in front on the right. Compare my [Next] photo of a larvae of a Sawfly Wasp ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera) in my ]Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2016)
CommonNames: Looper; Inchworm; Geometrid Moth; Wild Sweetpea; Sawfly Wasp

File.Name: g317d_g317-007.jpg
Notes: 25872238995 Here are beautiful bug butt shots of two different "Trash Line Weavers" (Cyclosa, Araneidae) in the mint patch of the garden this morning. Look at the photo on the left. The spider's butt is on the right, but these spiders also have an odd bump on their abdomen which is visible on the left. Compare this photo of what might be a different species. These tiny spiders decorate their stabilimentum with the dead bodies of their prey, pirate fashion. The spider on the right just grabbed a tiny prey, which he'll surely add to the row. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2016)

File.Name: g317e_g315-017.jpg
Notes:
25750971112 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of a woodlouse that can roll up in a ball, so I guess it's genus Armadillidium, probably Armadillidium vulgare. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda, Crustacea. They are known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names. This one is kind of pretty with those yellow markings! These are critters that the rain brings out, and we had a bit of rain the night before I got this photo. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2016)

Pill bugs aren't bugs? Of course they are, but they're not insects. I reckon the word covers all arthropods, and if it's in BugGuide, it's cool!
ScientificName: woodlouse; Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea
associatedTaxa: woodlouse; Armadillidium; Armadillidium vulgare; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea

File.Name: g317f_g312-023.jpg
Notes: 25776549831 This is a beautiful bug butt shot of the tiny nymph of a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon. It seems to have a face on its butt - or turn signals? Maybe it's the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum)? I have another photo of the whole nymph here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2016)

File.Name: g318a_g318-004.jpg
Notes:
25774633452 Here are masses of Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family growing on a bare hillside above Sage Hill Campground in Paradise. This is the real "California Poppy" in our neck of the woods, more yellow than the real California Poppy (E. californica), see my photos here and here. These bare-looking hills were burned by the White Fire in May 2013, but that's where the wildflowers are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2016)

It was cooler and breezier today, and I think I know why. There was marine layer fog creeping up the mountains and advancing up the Santa Ynez Valley as far as Lake Cachuma this morning, so we had "fog winds". That's my name for the turbulent winds that happen on top of the temperature inversion that the marine layer brings. We expect it to get colder as we go higher, but this is reversed in an inversion with coastal fog. This might be the first time its happened this year, but it will become increasingly common (and annoying) as we move into Spring.

*Sigh* I just noticed that the marine layer fog is creeping over the mountains tonight.

File.Name: g318b_g318-009.jpg
Notes: 25800143531 This is a mass of native Padre's Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii aka Primula clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family. (See the comment about the recent name change in this photo by Jim Morefield.) These are growing in a little grassy potrero in a native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii,Fagaceae) woodland in Paradise today. (These trees do that.) It was breezy, but OK for group shots. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2016)

File.Name: g318c_g318-017.jpg
Notes: 25894897065 Here is a pretty new flower cluster of native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family, growing (untypically) in the mountains along East Camino Cielo road - with Cachuma Saddle in the background to establish it's provenance. They are beautiful shrubs with odd-pinnate leaves and interesting flowers, see my [Previous] photo. I usually find these shrubs along the river, but there are a few up here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2016)

File.Name: g318d_g318-018.jpg
Notes: 25265716353 This is a close shot of new flowers of native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family, growing (untypically) in the mountains along East Camino Cielo road. The flowers look strange to me, but probably that's just because it's an unusual family in our part of California. They have bisexual flowers with two petals and two stamens with a little capitate pistil in the center. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2016)

File.Name: g318e_g318-020.jpg
Notes: 25868406096 Here are new flowers of native Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha, Ranunculaceae) on a plant that's climbing over the native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala, Oleaceae) in my [Next] two photos. The flowers have been around for a while, I just haven't got around to photographing them before. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2016)

File.Name: g318f_g314-035.jpg
Notes: 25893684355 This is a tiny but perfectly formed fly (order (Diptera) perched on a rain-drop-encrusted leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) a few days ago This fly really is tiny, only about 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, about 3/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is. It has the eyes of a Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae) but the gray body of a Drosophilid Fly (Drosophilidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2016)

File.Name: g319a_g319-028.jpg
Notes:
25895367186 This is a beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for a moment on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). What's most interesting is that she seems to be ovipositing on the leaf! The host plant of these pretty butterflies is the Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae) which grow nearby, so I can't explain this photo. Maybe she's just "making frass"? (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

(Update, 21 March
I just looked it up, and I believe this is a male, as the females are even deeper blue with orange spots on the hindwings as well. This reverses the usual pattern with blues and ducks that the males are more brightly colored than the females. This male is NOT ovipositing!)

The fog monster knocked on the door this morning, but it didn't come in. There was marine layer fog on both the ocean and valley sides of the mountains, but it didn't quite get to us, and there were no fog winds to speak of. That might change tomorrow, as there's already a hint of fog in the air.

The vernal equinox happens tonight at 9:30 PDT, just an hour away. That's already tomorrow in England where UTC starts, but still tonight here in California. Happy equinox! The seasons change, life goes on...

File.Name: g319b_g319-012.jpg

This one is perched for a moment on a new leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

File.Name: g319c_g319-015.jpg
Notes: 25825875761 Here is a little Damselfly (Zygoptera, Odonata) perched on fallen oak leaves in the canyon today, munching its tiny prey - maybe a winged aphid? This is the first one I've managed to photograph for some time. Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

File.Name: g319d_g319-005.jpg
Notes:
25619949640 This Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) is thoroughly enjoying a moment in the sun this morning! It has blue and cyan mating colors. It also has a fat Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) in its ear tympanum. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

Apparently these are an immature form of the same ticks that usually get me in the summer, though I've already been "ticked" a few times this year. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web.
Locality: me!).  I don't quite understand the details
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Tick

File.Name: g319e_g319-023.jpg
Notes: 25619551510 This is looking closer at the first flowers of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon today, see my [Previous] photo. You can see that the pretty flowers are not quite in whorls. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

File.Name: g319f_g319-021.jpg
Notes: 25619352550 Here are (almost) the first flowers of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon today. I added the qualifier because I found them a few days ago, but had to wait until today to shed some light on them. (D'oh, and then I overexposed them a notch!) These are a perennial lupine, see my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2016)

File.Name: g320a_g320-005.jpg
Notes:
25828316262 It's officially spring!

This is a spring-like tendril of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or Manroot (Marah macrocarpus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing over a not-quite-flowering shrub of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina, Lamiaceae) in the woods today. The coiled tendrils are neat! If you start at that flat place to the right of center, you can see that they twist clockwise to the right and counterclockwise to the left. Today is the first day of spring, though the Spring Equinox actually happened last night at 9:30 PDT. But that was already tomorrow in England where UTC starts, and that's where the start of our seasons is reckoned from. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)

The marine layer fog climbed up to about 2,000 feet in the mountains today - leaving us clear on the pass. It was a beautiful day, though it looked pretty gloomy down in town. The weather might change tomorrow, with a slight chance of drizzle or light rain continuing through Monday night. *Sigh* It sounds like that will be followed by wind. Julie and I want to head out to the Carrizo Plains next week, but that will depend on the wind.

File.Name: g320b_g320-011.jpg
Notes: 25948890725 This is a closer shot of flowering Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for a whole plant and more info. I'll been waiting for these flowers! I reckon this is a contender for the finest spring wildflower in our mountains, along with the Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus, Styracaceae) flowers two photos [Back]. Both of these should be in cultivation. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)

File.Name: g320c_g320-020.jpg
Notes: 25922544126 Here are two photos of flowering Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in Jepson, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)

File.Name: g320d_g320-022.jpg
Notes: 25922349736 Here are more new beautiful flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few scant weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains, though the flowers in my next two photos are a contender! The shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)

File.Name: g320e_g320-029.jpg
Notes: 25947418645 Here are a pair of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road today, no doubt lingering fire-followers from the Gap Fire back in 2009. It's hard to find the right exposure to photograph these "red, all too red" plants and flowers! This shot is a compromise between too-bright and too-dark. The flower heads are mostly leafy bracts with a few real flowers tucked in. If you look close, you can see the exerted pistils stuck out like little tongues and the stamens curled up inside the hood-like upper lip of the flowers. The foliage isn't always so dark red. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in this place. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g320f_g320-033.jpg
Notes: 25947120475 This is any unusually floriferous native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's like a whole field of poppies on a single shrub! I've been watching these plants this winter, and there were *always* a few flowers one shrub or another. That's impressive after five years of drought now. I found this one out along West Camino Cielo road near Lizard's Mouth. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)

File.Name: g321a_g321-012.jpg
Notes:
25343160374 I found another beautiful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched for a moment on a fallen oak leaf in the woods today. I looked it up, and I believe this is a male, as the females are even deeper blue with orange spots on the hindwings as well. This reverses the usual pattern with blues and ducks that the males are more brightly colored than the females. And it also solves a small puzzle - the butterfly in this photo is another male, it's NOT ovipositing! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

It was really a crummy day for new photos, but I managed to get a few nice ones. It was chilly and cloudy - but bright enough to cast a faint shadow in this photo. It was also increasingly breezy as the day went on, becoming downright windy tonight. Julie and I are hoping to head out to the Carrizo Plains in a couple of days, but that will depend on the wind. Wind blows... for closeup photographers anyway!

File.Name: g321b_g321-007.jpg
Notes:
25674617470 Here are two shots of a Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) hanging on a dead stem in the woods today. It's probably a Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. (What, me worry?) This one was quite active, marching along the stem and finally dropping off. A few minutes before, I had found a tick on my hand and brushed it off. Maybe it's the same one? I then noticed it moving towards me, and I moved away! I've already been ticked a number of times this year, but this was the first time I found one before it found me! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

If you look close at the photo on the right, you can see an odd scale-like "thing" below its left hind leg - and then a couple more bumps under its body. Reminds me of the poem, originally by Jonathan Swift ("On Poetry: A Rhapsody", 1733):

"So, naturalists observe, a flea
Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
And so proceed ad infinitum."

File.Name: g321c_g321-020.jpg
Notes:
25974667045 Here are two shots of what I guess is a tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spider with that odd bump on its abdomen. I believe this one is a male with those enlarged palps. It was hanging on a dead stem of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

This one seems darker than the usual Cyclosa spiders I see at home, compare this photo. The shape of the abdominal bumps does seem similar though. It was a challenge to get these photos in the breeze today! As usual, I took *lots* of photos and only kept the best. You sure couldn't afford do that with film!

File.Name: g321d_g321-001.jpg
Notes: 25345727943 These are the first flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon at home, though I've been seeing them elsewhere in the mountains. The flowers are quite variable, sometimes deeply colored, but sometimes almost white like these. This plant is climbing down the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

File.Name: g321e_g321-017.jpg
Notes: 25853386522 Here are the first open flowers I've seen this year on native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Like most other native sumacs, the flower buds stay on the plants for some time before they finally open. This shrub isn't really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. (I'm not.) It really is a beautiful plant, and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

File.Name: g321f_g321-018.jpg
Notes:
25973906365 This is a new plant of the violet form of native Cobwebby Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the canyon today. They have wonderful summer flowers that many insects like, see this photo. These plants seem to grow as true biennials, forming leaves their first year, flowers in their second year, and then... they're done. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2016)

This is not a weed! If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds and such - make sure you know the difference between the native and weedy thistles. The non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up, and it would be a shame to pull the natives by mistake. If in doubt, leave it be!
Locality: Cirsium vulgare) is easily confused with the natives when it's just coming up
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: g322a_g322-013.jpg
Notes:
25998719415 This is a handsome Black-crowned Night-Heron bird perched in a non-native pine tree at our friend Gabrielle's house in Santa Barbara this morning. Gabrielle runs the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society , see this photo. Her home is also a bird sanctuary! I asked her about this, and she said that her home was a SB Wildlife Care Network site back in 1991, and they raised a number of these birds from chicks that had fallen from their nests. The original birds are gone, but this new generation keeps coming back. She occasionally gives them food scraps from the raptors she cares for, but otherwise they're on their own. Bravo! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2016)

I don't usually photograph birds because so many others do it so well! I couldn't resist this one so perfectly perched. It was challenging to get new photos today with the rock 'n roll winds blowing over the mountains, but I managed to get a few. The wind does seem to be dying down a bit this evening and our NWS Wind Advisory runs out tonight. Julie and I are still hoping to get out to the Carrizo Plain on Thursday/Friday, but it will only be frustrating if it's windy. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: g322b_g322-006.jpg
Notes: 25369670903 This is a large Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera) trying to hide under the long spines of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia, Cactaceae) cactus in the garden this morning. The cactus might be a Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis) that we collected years ago from the Santa Monica Mountains. This was almost the only insect I saw this morning as it was chilly and very windy. I dig the shadows of its long legs on the cactus pad. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2016)
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Prickly Pear; Coastal Pricklypear
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia littoralis
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia littoralis

File.Name: g322c_g322-002.jpg
Notes: 25877321552 Here are new flowers on native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The glandular bracts of the flower heads may be the outstanding feature of these plants, see my [Previous] photo. They are low-growing hummingbird flowers, see this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2016)

File.Name: g322d_g322-003.jpg
Notes: 25697399560 This is a new glandular flower head of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. There are no flowers yet on this one, but see my [Next] photo. I really think these very glandular bracts are the best part of this plant. Yep, the plants smell good too, and the leaves make a decent tea. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2016)

File.Name: g322e_g322-015.jpg
Notes: 25368769903 These are new flowers on a shrub of Fremontia (Fremontodendron, Sterculiaceae) growing above the retaining wall on the wild side of our garden. We planted this shrub many years ago from a nursery specimen, and it's done well with no summer water. It might be the Mexican Flannelbush (F. mexicanum) with glabrous sepal pits and unbranched habit? We are said to have native F. californicum in our mountains near Gibraltar Dam, but I've never found them there. It is pretty common in Rose Valley along Highway 33 above Ojai to the east. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2016)
ScientificName: Fremontodendron; Sterculiaceae; F. mexicanum; F. californicum

File.Name: g322f_g322-032.jpg
Notes: 25696759850 This is a whole shrub of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family with lots of flowers. See this photo for a closer shot of the beautiful flowers. That's Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae, Boraginaceae) in the foreground, another interesting native that will flower later in the year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2016)
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: g323a_g323-003.jpg
Notes:
25387048854 Finally, here are two photos of a beautiful female Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched in the canyon today near her host plant Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata, Crassulaceae). If you compare the males in this photo and this photo, you can see that the female is more brilliantly colored above, with deeper colors and extra orange patch on her hind wing. This seems to reverse the usual pattern with blues and ducks that the males are more brightly colored than the females. On the other hand, she is quite plain underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2016)

The wind did ease off today, though there were some gusty moments in the afternoon. Julie and I are heading out to the Carrizo Plain tomorrow. We'll return on Friday night and I'll post new photos on Saturday. Hopefully we'll still find some wildflowers, but they are fading fast this year.

File.Name: g323b_g323-012.jpg
Notes:
25390190143 The is the first caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've found this year, on new leaves of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. I always think that they have radio towers on their backs. These are the same web-spinning caterpillars that I find in summer, for example this photo. Apparently they form their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer, then form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They re-emerge in the following spring, get fat on their host plants, and then pupate into butterflies. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them.

This caterpillar is on Bush Monkey Flower, but I mostly find them on native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae). The old Scrophulariaceae plant family has been split up, but I wonder if the butterflies know something that the botanists have missed?
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Southern Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon

File.Name: g323c_g323-001.jpg
Notes: 25385710164 This is a mating pair of tiny Broad-nosed Weevils (Entiminae, Curculionidae, Coleoptera) on a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). Maybe they are the species Thricolepis inornata? (Thanks Boris!) These critters are tiny, about 930 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 3.6mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. (See here for how I figured.) They are humping for hump day Wednesday - at least they're in the right position, but I can't see any intimate connection between them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2016)

File.Name: g323d_g323-017.jpg
Notes: 25897426492 This is a tiny Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera, the first I've seen this year. It's perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) - with its proboscis extended. This one is missing its right antenna. These insects are very thin, like tiny walking sticks. It's not the best photo, but it is a record shot. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2016)

File.Name: g323e_g323-018.jpg
Notes:
25384978544 Here are two more shots of a tiny Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) walking across the underside of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I believe it's a Western Black-legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. (What, me worry?) This one was quite active, marching across the leaf and then vanishing under it. This might be the same tick I showed two days ago in this photo, as I found it about a foot away from the previous one. Hah, the photo on the right is also a nice "butt shot", so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2016)

(I'm posting this on Wednesday night, but it is already Thursday by UTC or GMT!)
Locality: I'm posting this on Wednesday night

File.Name: g323f_g323-008.jpg
Notes: 25384814804 Here are new florets of the perennial native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and look like they already have seeds when they are just starting to open. It's said to be a good forage grass. It's seems quite common in the mountains - once you notice it. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2016)

File.Name: g326a_g326-002.jpg
Notes:
26059965275 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I really wasn't looking for new photos today, but I noticed this bee-like Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae, Hymenoptera), probably in the genus Abia, right away! I've been trying to get a decent photo of these wasps for some time. One indication that it's a wasp and not a bee is that it has no pollen-collecting hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. I got this photo in the mint patch of the garden at home this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2016)

Tomorrow I'll post some closer photos of some the wildflowers we found in the Carrizo Plain National Monument over the last two days. That was a quick trip with no time to really explore, but I found quite a few. The winds were polite. In fact it didn't get windy until we got home - in the middle of a NWS Wind Advisory! I guess that says something about where we live.

File.Name: g326b_g325-002.jpg
Notes:
25456219973 Here are zillions of wildflowers on the edge of Soda Lake along Simmler Road yesterday morning. In the distance you can see the proper lake bed and the power lines across it that service the Topaz Solar Farm that now occupies much of California Valley beyond. Most of these flowers are composites (Asteraceae). The whiter flowers are Munz's Tidy-tips (Layia munzii) and the oranger ones are probably the Alkali Goldfields (Lasthenia ferrisiae). The shrubs are probably all Iodine Bush ( Allenrolfea occidentalis, Chenopodiaceae). It's quite a landscape! (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

We had a great trip, but too short to really explore. We stayed the night at the California Valley Motel. It's an odd place that looks closed when you drive by - but our room was decent and everything worked just fine. Bring your own food, coffee, and cups. The closest alternative is about an hour away!
CommonNames: Munz's Tidy-tips; Alkali Goldfields; Iodine Bush
ScientificName: Asteraceae; Layia munzii; Lasthenia ferrisiae; Allenrolfea occidentalis; Chenopodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Asteraceae; Layia munzii; Lasthenia ferrisiae; Allenrolfea occidentalis; Chenopodiaceae

File.Name: g326c_g325-029.jpg
Notes: 25455788753 I reckon this is the damnedest mirage I've ever seen! It looks to be a lake reflecting the Temblor Mountains in the background, as well as the closer hills and yellow wildflowers close to the shore. But not in the Carrizo Plain in late March! It's all an illusion, but we had to walk out there to make sure! This is the dry salt pan of a smaller pond to the south of Soda Lake, but it sure looks wet - I can almost see ducks floating on the water! Yeah, it's full of wildflowers - mostly Munz's Tidy-tips (Layia munzii), I'll have closer photos tomorrow. The soda of Soda Lake is mostly Sodium Sulfate. It was mined in past years. The dry lake is probably stinky black mud under the crust, but the surface is completely dry. I got this photo along Simmler Road near Soda Lake, with the Temblor Mountains in the background. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g326d_g325-023.jpg
Notes: 25965418102 Here are Julie and our dog Brodie looking over a field of (I believe) Leafstem Tickseed (Leptosyne calliopsidea aka Coreopsis calliopsidea, Asteraceae). They knock your eyes out! I got this photo along Simmler Road near Soda Lake, with the Temblor Mountains in the background. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g326e_g324-035.jpg
Notes: 25452660214 Here are two photos of yellow composite (Asteraceae) covered hillsides in the Carrizo Plain National Monument. They look like they are both facing the same way, but they're not! The photo on left is looking towards the Temblor Mountains on the east side of the plains, the flowers are on the north-facing slopes of these west-facing mountains. But the photo on the right is looking towards the Caliente Mountains on the west side of the plains, the flowers are on the south-facing slopes of these east-facing mountains. (I think I got my east-west and north-south straight!) I know, it's confusing because I'm turned around between these two photos. I find this so interesting. It must have something to do with sun exposure. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

File.Name: g326f_g324-048.jpg
Notes: 25452172104 A river runs through it? Not in the dry Carrizo Plain except in your dreams! This is a "river" of blue Phacelia flowers in the in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family, maybe the Great Valley Phacelia (Phacelia ciliata), but we didn't drive down to find out. Those are the Temblor Mountains in the background. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

File.Name: g327a_g327-022.jpg
Notes:
25989928032 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I found this small slug-like caterpillar in the mint patch of the garden today. I guess from its shape that it's a larva of a Gossamer-Wing Butterfly (Lycaenidae, Lepidoptera) - Coppers, Blues, Elfins, and Hairstreaks - but I have no idea what kind. You can see into its body, which looks like it's beginning to split apart under the shell of its skin - time for a molt? (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2016)

(Update, 28 March: I was just searching my photos of flies and found this photo. Maybe this is the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera)? That would explain why it didn't match any of my caterpillar photos!)

I've been rapidly going downhill as I post these photos tonight with a cold. *Blah* I did my duty and got them all up, but I hope my comments make sense.

File.Name: g327b_g325-032.jpg
Notes: 25989542492 I believe this is the uncommon Recurved Larkspur (Delphinium recurvatum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I found this plant growing in alkali soil along Simmler Road near Soda Lake. That may be a big reason why it's so scarce - because it's habitat is scarce? They were not common this year, and I had to hunt for them. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g327c_g324-059.jpg
Notes: 25808221650 This is an odd native plant of Desert Candle (Caulanthus inflatus) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I haven't found these wonderful plants for a few years. It's a mustard with fat inflated stems topped with magenta flowers that almost look black. These were growing on the valley floor, a couple of miles south of KCL campground. You can also see a background of Common Hillside Daisy (Monolopia lanceolata, Asteraceae), San Joaquin Blazing Star (Mentzelia pectinata, Loasaceae), a Phacelia, and the usual Cheat Grass and Fiddleneck. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

File.Name: g327d_g324-070.jpg
Notes: 26014378681 I believe this is the San Joaquin Blazing Star (Mentzelia pectinata) in the Loasaceae plant family. The flowers are large and very beautiful. This one growing on the valley floor, a couple of miles south of KCL campground. The leaves feel like sandpaper. I believe the yellow composites in the photo are the Common Hillside Daisy (Monolopia lanceolata, Asteraceae) with different phyllaries than the Coreopsis/Leptosyne two photos back. There's also a blue Phacelia and the usual remnants of the old ranching days - Cheat Grass and Fiddleneck. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

Notes: 25987678822 This is looking closer at pretty flowers of (I believe) Munz's Tidy-tips (Layia munzii, Asteraceae). There are also a few Goldfields (Lasthenia) in the mix - as well as a Foxtail (Hordeum jubatum, Poaceae) in the lower-left that managed to sneak in. I got this photo in alkali soil along Simmler Road near Soda Lake. See this photo from yesterday. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g327f_g325-018.jpg
Notes: 26079909115 Here are lots of flowers of (I believe) Leafstem Tickseed (Leptosyne calliopsidea aka Coreopsis calliopsidea, Asteraceae). If you look close under the flowers, you can see the distinctive two-ranked phyllaries that used to place this is the Coreopsis genus. They knock your eyes out! They also seem to overload the sensor in my digital camera, there's something about too much yellow that causes my camera to choke. I got this photo in alkali soil along Simmler Road near Soda Lake. See this photo from yesterday. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g328a_g328-004.jpg
Notes:
26105811515 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a mint leaf in the garden this morning. The sun just come out on this chilly and gloomy morning, and the fly was still feeling chilled - it let me get closer than usual. It's a nice shot of the ommatidia that make up its compound eyes. No wonder they always see me coming! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2016)

It was gloomy, chilly, and windy all morning until it cleared, then it became sunny, chilly, and windy for the rest of the day. Despite the wind, I did get a few photos. I still feel *blah* with a cold - or is it just hay fever from all the oak pollen and catkin parts in the air? Our power will be off tomorrow from 8-5 for "scheduled maintenance". I'll do what I can for posting new photos.

File.Name: g328b_g325-061.jpg
Notes: 25500736674 These are beautiful flower heads of native Thistle Sage (Salvia carduacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in a patch of non-native grass on our way back from the Carrizo Plain last week. I was disappointed that the wildflowers at Shell Creek were already just about done, but these flowers made the stop worth while. I only found a few of them in one place. (Shell Creek Road, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g328c_g324-069.jpg
Notes: 26012275392 This is a fairly large Scarab Beetle (Scarabaeidae, Coleoptera) on blue Phacelia (Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae) flowers on the Carrizo Plain. The beetle is probably species Paracotalpa ursina. I'm not sure what kind of phacelia it is, but maybe it's theTansy-leaf Phacelia (Phacelia tanacetifolia) with pale flowers and long stamens? Compare the phacelia in my [Previous] photo. I didn't find many insects in the Carrizo Plain last week, which is surprising considering how many wildflowers there were. I really believe most wildflowers don't need pollinators, but every once and a while they help mix up the genes. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)
ScientificName: Scarabaeidae; Coleoptera; Phacelia; Boraginaceae; Hydrophyllaceae; Paracotalpa ursina; Phacelia tanacetifolia

File.Name: g328d_g324-025.jpg
Notes: 25501992543 There were whole fields of these pretty blue Phacelia flowers in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family near the south entrance to the Carrizo Plain. There are many different species and I won't try to identify this one. It has deep blue flowers and rather short stamens. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

File.Name: g328e_g325-028.jpg
Notes: 26037837071 These are new flowers of native Lemmon's Mustard (Caulanthus anceps aka Guillenia lemmonii) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flowers almost look like lilies - but these are all 4s and 6s and lilies are all 3s and 6s. Those are yellow/orange flowers of Leafstem Tickseed (Leptosyne calliopsidea aka Coreopsis calliopsidea, Asteraceae) in the background, and this mustard was growing in the middle of them. I got this photo in alkali soil along Simmler Road near Soda Lake. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 25 March 2016)

File.Name: g328f_g324-037.jpg
Notes: 26011442592 This is a yellow composite (Asteraceae) covered hillside in the Carrizo Plain National Monument, looking towards the Temblor Mountains on the east side of the valley. There are also orange California Poppies (genus Eschscholzia, Papaveraceae) visible on the left, but I didn't climb the hill to find out what kind they are. The orientation of these flower fields is interesting. They are only on the north-facing slopes of these west-facing mountains, but on the other east-facing side of the valley they grow on the south-facing slopes. See this photo. It must have something to do with sun exposure. (Carrizo Plain National Monument, San Luis Obispo County, California, 24 March 2016)

File.Name: g329a_g329-014.jpg
Notes:
25523460633 Here is another shot of no doubt the same little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) as my [Previous] photo yesterday. In today's photo, it is sucking pollen from a new flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). You can see its proboscis in the flower, but it's dark-colored just like one of the old anthers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2016)

Do you know that old Ron Cobb cartoon that shows a man wandering through a wasteland with a broken TV, holding the power cord out in front of him? (See it here.) I felt like that today, only with a computer rather than a TV! Our power was out all day for "scheduled maintenance". It was supposed to be back on by 5:00, and we finally got it around 6:30. Relief!

There as nothing to do today except take some photos, but it was windy all day again - which is pretty frustrating. The wind finally seems to settling down, so maybe tomorrow will be a nice photo day? It was also unseasonably cold today - just 35∞ in the early morning! It wasn't the best day to be stuck waiting for the power to come back on. Just this one photo today. I got a late start and I'm tired.
Locality: See it here.) I felt like that today

File.Name: g330a_g330-034.jpg
Notes:
25541872013 I suspect that the more you know about local insects and plants, the more confounding this photo will be!

This is a "Timema" insect on the left - no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. It's perched on a flower bud of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, with an open flower to the right. As far as I know, the usual host plants of these insects are native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) and Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae), so this is unusual! The insects do seem to get bolder (and more careless) during mating season - which is right now. See my [Previous] few photos for a bit more info about insect and plant. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)

Today was an interesting weather day. It was unseasonably cold again, though a bit warmer than yesterday. It was still about 40∞ when I got up, and it didn't get much above 50∞ later in the day. The big winds seem over, but it was still breezy today. It was curious. I was out along West Camino Cielo road, and the wind was blowing from the south. When I got home, the wind was blowing from the north. Sitting on the porch at home, I noticed higher clouds moving south and lower clouds moving north - at the same time! I'm not sure what that portends.

More photos from the Carrizo Plain? Well, I got 'em, but it seems time to move on. I'll save them for a windy day, along with all my Death Valley photos that I never got around to posting!

File.Name: g330b_g330-007.jpg
Notes: 26077995201 Here are an "almost mating" pair of unusual "Timema" insects - no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. They are atypical walkingstick insects. That's the green striped female on the left and the much smaller male on the right. I guess I scared the male off, or maybe they were already done? They also come in brown forms. They are on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), one of their preferred host plants. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are. They are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot. I rarely see them except during mating season (now), when they seem to become bolder. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g330c_g330-013.jpg
Notes: 25870971720 This is an unusual "Timema" insect - no doubt Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan. It's an atypical walkingstick. This one is a green striped female, an inch or so long. It was with a smaller male a few moments before, but I guess I scared him off. QuÈ l·stima, but see my [Next] photo. They also come in brown forms. This one is on a shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae), one of their preferred host plants. I'm not sure how uncommon these insects actually are. They are very well camouflaged and certainly hard to spot. I rarely see them except during mating season (now), when they seem to become bolder. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)
ScientificName: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: g330d_g330-028.jpg
Notes: 26143433445 Here are new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, with a Convergent Lady Beetle (Hippodamia convergens, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) for a color accent. These shrubs have beautiful flowers, but the shrubs are unusual, a singleton in their family here in California. The specimen map at Calflora shows them all around the central valley in California, but I suspect their distribution is highly localized. They are common here on the pass , both in woodland and chaparral, but I don't think I've seen them anywhere else in Santa Barbara County. There is just one record from San Luis Obispo county to the north and none from Ventura county to the south. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: g330e_g330-019.jpg
Notes: 25870530220 Here are new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. The variety inezianus isn't recognized in Jepson, but the plants do seem distinct - larger, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. Very beautiful. They are a fire-follower, though these are growing in a place that was cleared - not burned - a few years ago. Sometimes that's enough. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)

File.Name: g331a_g331-018.jpg
Notes:
25558823874 The stuff of nightmares...

I believe this is a Cobweb Spider (Theridiidae) from the tangled web and eye arrangement. It's sinking its sharp fangs into a tiny aphid (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - which gives a sense of scale. The spider's web was in a bunch of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae), which is probably where the winged aphid came from. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2016)

It was warmer today, up to 70∞ on the upstairs porch - 20∞ warmer than it was yesterday! But it was also breezy all morning, becoming downright windy this evening with a proper sundowner event. We're under a NWS wind advisory tonight. Funny thing, the windier it gets, the worse my cold gets! I'm sure it's just hay fever from all the oak pollen in the air, but that doesn't make me feel any better!
ScientificName: Theridiidae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: g331b_g331-004.jpg
Notes: 25890466980 This is the brown nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera). It really does have two antennae, but the other one is pointed right at my camera. It's perched on a dusty leaf of native Southern Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens aka Diplacus longiflorus, Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae) in the canyon today. This one is not our usual green katydid! I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2016)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Southern Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
