File.Name: g331c_g331-011.jpg
Notes: 25558475974 Here are the first new flowers of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing in our canyon at home. The skinny truncate leaflets and bi-colored flowers that are not at all whorled are distinctive. I think of it as a fire-follower, but it's happy on disturbed roadsides as well. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2016)

File.Name: g331d_g331-007.jpg
Notes: 25560121533 Maybe this little lupine is the south coast variant of Coulter's Lupine (Lupinus sparsiflorus ssp. inopinatus) in the Fabaceae plant family? That's just a guess, I'm not at all sure of the ID. The leaflets are short and fat and quite hairy, and the flowers are not at all whorled. They grow along the road in our canyon, and are just starting to flower. The Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus) comes to mind, but this one doesn't seem quite hairy enough, and the leaves are narrower - and this is not the desert. (At least not yet, but if the dought continues....) If you have an idea about this, please let me know. So many lupines! (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2016)
ScientificName: Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus sparsiflorus; inopinatus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus

File.Name: g331e_g327-003.jpg
Notes: 25557638474 Here are separate photos of male (left) and female (right) tiny "Trash Line Weaver" (genus Cyclosa, Araneidae) spiders in the mint patch of the garden. These are the spiders that have that odd bump on their abdomen. They were both on the same web! It was the lady spider's web, and she mostly sat in the center, but she came out to retrieve a tiny aphid as prey. The male was on the edge of the web, plucking the strings no doubt to get her interested. I watched them for a while, and she finally chased the male away. I saw him balloon away into parts unknown. I guess mating isn't any easier for these spiders than it is for us! Both photos also happen to be good "butt shots" so HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2016)

File.Name: g331f_g330-037.jpg
Notes: 25557462634 I can't resist one more shot of an uncommon "Timema" insect perched on a flower bud of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, with an open flower to the right. It's a goodbye "butt shot" as well, so HBBBT! I'm pretty sure the insect is Timema cristinae in the family Timematidae of the Phasmida clan, a chubby walking stick insect. See my [Previous] few photos for a bit for info about the insect and flower. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2016)
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Timema cristinae; Timematidae; Phasmida

File.Name: g401a_g401-002.jpg
Notes:
26113968011 This is looking closer at a beautiful flower head of native Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The brilliant red color is mostly from the bracts, but the actual flower tubes are also quite red on top - whereas the flower tubes of Castilleja affinis are greenish, compare this photo. Flowers and bracts are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. See my [Previous] photo for a whole group of plants. These handsome plants are growing on the hillside by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2016)

It was a beautiful day - warmer than yesterday and not breezy at all. I drove up to our mailbox and got this photo. I wasn't sure where to go next, so I engaged in a bit of "Flipism" - flip a coin! (This is from a classic Carl Barks story in Walt Disney's Comics and Stories #149 in February 1953. You can see a few bits of it here.) Heads I go left up East Camino Cielo; and tails I go right to the highway and maybe East Camino Cielo or Paradise, but that's another flip. It came up heads, so I went left. I'll go right tomorrow.

I don't know why, but as of yesterday I can do longer delete those bogus tags that Flicker adds to my own tags. They are sometimes hilarious, but more often just plain wrong. There is a workaround, but it's laborious. That is to add a tag (like [outdoor]) which is already there, and then delete it. But what a PITA! I hope Flickr fixes this, it's annoying.
ScientificName: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis; Walt Disney's Comics and Stories
associatedTaxa: Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja martinii; Orobanchaceae; Castilleja affinis; Walt Disney's Comics and Stories

File.Name: g401b_g401-005.jpg
Notes: 26154256266 This is a nice grouping of Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii aka Castilleja martinii) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. Flowers and bracts are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of a flower head. These pretty flowers are growing on the hillside by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2016)

File.Name: g401c_g401-011.jpg
Notes: 26153970716 Here is a whole plant of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family growing with Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei, Liliaceae aka Hesperoyucca whipplei, Agavaceae) on a sandstone outcrop in the mountains. I reckon it's properly called a subshrub with stems that are woody at the base, but the whole plant is not much more than about a foot tall. It's handsome, and unlike our other Castilleja species has soft white foliage. These plants have been flowering since January, but they are really just coming into their own now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 April 2016)
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: g401d_g401-016.jpg
Notes: 26087229852 This is a whole flowering plant of native Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family growing on the red conglomerate soil of the Sespe Formation along Painted Cave Road - a non-marine sedimentary deposit with these characteristic "redbeds". They are not common in our mountains. The plants have tightly clustered leaves and tight flower heads. These plants are sometimes known as "Biscuit Root" because of their edible roots. I like to nibble on the ripe seeds - they have a spicy taste that stays with you. That's Purple Needle Grass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family in the upper-left, but it's not making flowers yet. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2016)
CommonNames: Woolly-fruited Lomatium; Sespe Formation; Purple Needle Grass

File.Name: g401e_g328-006.jpg
Notes: 25574793414 This is a handsome little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) grooming itself on a new leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I have more shots of the same kind of fly here and here. I've also been seeing them high in the oak trees from our upstairs porch. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2016)

File.Name: g401f_g329-008.jpg
Notes: 25906494120 This is another shot of a tiny but perfectly formed fly (order (Diptera) perched on a mint leaf in the garden. See this photo. This fly really is tiny, only about 600 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot, which comes to 2.4 mm, about 3/32 inch. (See here for how I figured.) I'm not sure what kind of fly it is. It has the eyes of a Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae) but the gray body of a Drosophilid Fly (Drosophilidae). HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2016)

File.Name: g402a_g402-011.jpg
Notes:
25597556853 This is a little Buprestid beetle (Buprestidae, Coleoptera) on a new flower of a native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina, Lamiaceae) shrub in the woods today. It's the first such beetle I've seen this year on the first flower of the sage. I guess it's a Spotted Flower Buprestid in the genus Acmaeodera. There are lots of species, so I'll leave it at that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)

It was a nice enough day, but breezy in the morning. It did get warm enough to bring out a sweat!
CommonNames: Buprestid; Pitcher Sage; Spotted Flower Buprestid

File.Name: g402b_g402-025.jpg
Notes: 26199903515 This is the pretty flower head of a Gilia in the (Polemoniaceae) plant family. I'm not sure if it's Gilia capitata or Gilia achilleifolia, but it's one of those. (The stamens are about equal to the corolla lobes, and what the heck is the difference between hemispheric and spheric flower heads as the Jepson key asks?) This one is growing on a bank along West Camino Cielo road, though they are more common in the backcountry. The flowers have blue pollen, which always seems odd to me. Did you notice the two little "humping beetles" in the lower left? I didn't either until I got the photo up on my screen. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)
ScientificName: Gilia; Polemoniaceae; Gilia capitata; Gilia achilleifolia

File.Name: g402c_g402-016.jpg
Notes: 26106917502 I believe this pretty flower is Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in the Portulacaceae plant family, growing in place in the mountains that was cleared but not burned a few years ago. It usually grows as a fire follower, but sometimes clearing the chaparral is enough to trigger these plants. What beautiful colors it has, though you have to get down on your hands and knees to appreciate them. Apparently all plants that were formerly considered Calandrinia ciliata are now included in Calandrinia breweri? Well, I guess that makes ID easier. This one does seem different though, more prostrate and spreading. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)
ScientificName: Calandrinia breweri; Portulacaceae; Calandrinia ciliata; Calandrinia breweri
associatedTaxa: Calandrinia breweri; Portulacaceae; Calandrinia ciliata; Calandrinia breweri

File.Name: g402d_g402-020.jpg
Notes: 25596212043 I believe this is our other miner's lettuce - the Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora) in the Portulacaceae plant family. It's also known as Streambank Springbeauty, though that name is too "flowery" for my taste. It's a fire-follower, though this one is growing on soil that was cleared (and mulched?) but not burned a few years ago. This might actually be Claytonia parviflora ssp. grandiflora - the "large-flowered small-flowered miner's lettuce" - an oxymoron if ever there was one! The way that the leaves are clustered in the center with the flower stalks around the outside is typical, and quite different from the usual Claytonia perfoliata. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2016)
ScientificName: Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Claytonia parviflora; grandiflora; Claytonia perfoliata
associatedTaxa: Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Claytonia parviflora; grandiflora; Claytonia perfoliata
